text:
In this paper, we investigate the non-relativistic limit of the Einstein equation, which allows us to understand general relativity in the context of Newtonian mechanics. The results obtained provide a framework for understanding the gravitational field of slowly moving massive objects. 

The key ideas and methodology behind our work involve utilizing the post-Newtonian approximation method, which captures the effects of weak gravitation and slow movement for the metric tensor. By expanding the metric tensor in powers of v/c, we found that the lowest order approximation results in the Poisson equation for the gravitational potential. This result was further developed to include higher order corrections that involve the spatial and temporal derivatives of the gravitational potential.

The problem that our work attempts to solve is the applicability of general relativity to macroscopic phenomena, such as the motion of planets and stars in our solar system. While general relativity provides significant insights into the nature of gravity and the curvature of space-time, it is often difficult to apply to real-world scenarios without the aid of simplified models and approximations. Our work provides a mathematical framework for understanding the gravitational field of slowly moving massive objects in the non-relativistic limit, which is an important step towards developing more accurate models for macroscopic phenomena.

We present an analysis of the pulsation behaviour of the Delta Scuti stars 7
Aql (HD 174532) and 8 Aql (HD 174589) -- a new variable star -- observed in the
framework of STEPHI XII campaign during 2003 June--July. 183 hours of high
precision photometry were acquired by using four-channel photometers at three
sites on three continents during 21 days. The light curves and amplitude
spectra were obtained following a classical scheme of multi-channel photometry.
Observations in different filters were also obtained and analyzed. Six and
three frequencies have been unambiguously detected above a 99% confidence level
in the range 0.090 mHz--0.300 mHz and 0.100 mHz-- 0.145 mHz in 7 Aql and 8 Aql
respectively. A comparison of observed and theoretical frequencies shows that 7
Aql and 8 Aql may oscillate with p modes of low radial orders, typical among
Delta Scuti stars. In terms of radial oscillations the range of 8 Aql goes from
n=1 to n=3 while for 7 Aql the range spans from n=4 to n=7. Non-radial
oscillations have to be present in both stars as well. The expected range of
excited modes according to a non adiabatic analysis goes from n=1 to n=6 in
both stars.

Our research aimed to investigate smooth and starburst tidal tails in the Great Observatories Origins Deep Survey (GOODS) and Galaxy Evolution from Morphology and SEDs (GEMS) fields. Using data from the Hubble Space Telescope, we observed the morphology and structure of these galaxies to determine the properties of their tidal tails. We employed a combination of image stacking and modeling techniques to analyze the data, allowing us to detect and classify both smooth and starburst tails.

Our results revealed that the presence of starbursts in galaxies can lead to the formation of more prominent and complex tidal tails. Additionally, we found evidence of a relationship between the tidal tails' properties and the host galaxy's morphology, with early-type galaxies exhibiting more smooth tails and late-type galaxies showing higher star formation rates and more starburst tails.

The motivation for our research comes from the desire to understand the mechanisms and processes involved in galaxy interactions and mergers, which are crucial for galaxy evolution. By studying the properties of tidal tails in different galaxies, we hope to gain insight into the formation and evolution of these structures and their effects on galaxy evolution. Our methodology and results contribute to the growing body of research on galaxy formation and evolution and may have implications for our understanding of galaxy evolution over cosmic time.

In this paper, we investigate the orbital magnetization and its effects in spin-chiral ferromagnetic Kagome lattice. Our study focuses on understanding the influence of the orbital magnetization on the electronic and magnetic properties of the Kagome lattice via the spin-orbit coupling. The Kagome lattice is a two-dimensional material composed of interconnected triangles, with the ability to exhibit exotic electronic states.

We employ the tight-binding model and the Green's function formalism to analyze the electronic band structures of the Kagome lattice. Our study revealed that the orbital magnetization leads to a shift in the electronic bands and the appearance of band gaps. The appearance of the gaps is attributed to the destruction of the chiral symmetry of the Kagome lattice due to the spin-orbit coupling. We also observed that the orbital magnetization has a significant effect on the electronic and magnetic properties of spin-chiral Kagome ferromagnet. The applied magnetic field results in the changes of the magnetic moment, spin polarization, and the orbital angular momentum.

Our research aims to provide a better understanding of the effects of the orbital magnetization in spin-chiral ferromagnetic Kagome lattices. The study is motivated by the potential applications of these materials in next-generation electronic and magnetic devices.

In this study, we investigate a possible explanation for the presence of Theta+ particles in some experiments and their absence in others. Theta+ is a resonance state of a baryon that was first observed in 2002, but its existence has been controversial due to inconsistent experimental results. The motivation for this research stems from the need to understand the fundamental properties of the subatomic particles that make up matter in the universe.

Our key idea is that the differences in observed Theta+ production rates can be attributed to the different collision energies and conditions involved in the experiments. We used a theoretical framework based on a model that describes the interaction between subatomic particles, called the quark model. This allowed us to simulate the different energy ranges and collision conditions to investigate the factors that affect the production of Theta+.

Our results suggest that Theta+ production is highly dependent on the collision energy and strangeness content of the nucleons involved. Our simulations predict that Theta+ production is more likely to be observed at higher energies, where there is a higher concentration of strange quarks.

In conclusion, our study provides insight into the possible factors that may explain the variation in observed Theta+ production rates in different experiments. Further experimental studies are necessary to test these theoretical predictions and provide a better understanding of the fundamental properties of subatomic particles.

Context: Planets outside our solar system transiting their host star, i. e.
those with an orbital inclination near 90 degree, are of special interest to
derive physical properties of extrasolar planets. With the knowledge of the
host star's physical parameters, the planetary radius can be determined.
Combined with spectroscopic observations the mass and therefore the density can
be derived from Doppler-measurements. Depending on the brightness of the host
star, additional information, e. g. about the spin-orbit alignment between the
host star and planetary orbit, can be obtained.
  Aims: The last few years have witnessed a growing success of transit surveys.
Among other surveys, the MACHO project provided nine potential transiting
planets, several of them with relatively bright parent stars. The photometric
signature of a transit event is, however, insufficient to confirm the planetary
nature of the faint companion. The aim of this paper therefore is a
determination of the spectroscopic parameters of the host stars as well as a
dynamical mass determination through Doppler-measurements.
  Methods: We have obtained follow-up high-resolution spectra for five stars
selected from the MACHO sample, which are consistent with transits of
low-luminosity objects. Radial velocities have been determined by means of
cross-correlation with model spectra. The MACHO light curves have been compared
to simulations based on the physical parameters of the system derived from the
radial velocities and spectral analyses.
  Aims: We show that all transit light curves of the exoplanet candidates
analysed in this work can be explained by eclipses of stellar objects, hence
none of the five transiting objects is a planet.

Our work centers around understanding incoherent non-Fermi liquid (nFL) scattering in a Kondo lattice. The Kondo lattice model is a well-known theoretical framework used to describe strongly correlated materials, where a system composed of localized spins (describing magnetic impurities) in a metallic host conveys several intriguing properties such as heavy-fermion behavior and metal-insulator transitions. In this study, we focus on a scenario where the conduction electrons interact strongly with magnetic impurities through a Kondo exchange coupling, which leads to the formation of an nFL fixed point. However, the existence of nFL scattering in materials is still under debate, and the theoretical modeling of it is an unsolved problem in condensed matter physics.

Through detailed numerical analysis, we demonstrate that our Kondo lattice model can develop incoherent nFL scattering, indicating that this feature goes beyond the simple picture of coherent quasiparticles in traditional Fermi liquids. These findings reveal that the fractional power-law behavior, typical of nFL physics, stems from the presence of free chiral fluctuations in the model and its consequent renormalization. This study provides theoretical insights into nFL physics in strongly correlated systems, which could pave the way for understanding several exotic phases and phenomena that occur in such materials.

Photonic molecules, named by analogy with chemical molecules, are clusters of
closely located electromagnetically interacting microcavities or "photonic
atoms". As two or several microcavities are brought close together, their
optical modes interact, and a rich spectrum of photonic molecule supermodes
emerges, which depends both on geometrical and material properties of
individual cavities and on their mutual interactions. Here, we discuss ways of
controllable manipulation of photonic molecule supermodes, which improve or add
new functionalities to microcavity-based optical components. We present several
optimally-tuned photonic molecule designs for lowering thresholds of
semiconductor microlasers, producing directional light emission, enhancing
sensitivity of microcavity-based bio(chemical)sensors, and optimizing
electromagnetic energy transfer around bends of coupled-cavity waveguides.
Photonic molecules composed of identical microcavities as well as of
microcavities with various degrees of size or material detuning are discussed.
Microwave experiments on scaled photonic molecule structures are currently
under way to confirm our theoretical predictions.

Our work focused on understanding cascading on extragalactic background light (EBL) and its implications for high-energy gamma-ray observations. The motivation for this research lies in the fact that the EBL, which is the diffuse radiation field between galaxies and throughout the universe, absorbs high-energy gamma-rays, leading to a loss of energy and a distortion of the observed gamma-ray spectrum. Cascading, which is the production of electron-positron pairs from interactions between gamma-rays and EBL photons, further complicates this process.

We used a Monte Carlo simulation technique to model cascading and its effects on high-energy gamma-rays. Our results showed that cascading can significantly alter the gamma-ray spectrum, especially at high energies, leading to a reduction in the observed flux. This has important implications for the interpretation of gamma-ray observations, particularly in determining the sources of high-energy gamma-rays and their underlying physics.

Our work contributes to a better understanding of the processes involved in high-energy gamma-ray observations, and highlights the importance of considering cascading and EBL absorption in the interpretation of such observations. This can aid in the identification of the sources of high-energy gamma-rays, as well as in constraining models of the EBL and the universe's evolution.

The structural environment of substitutional Cr3+ ion in MgAl2O4 spinel has
been investigated by Cr K-edge Extended X-ray Absorption Fine Structure (EXAFS)
and X-ray Absorption Near Edge Structure (XANES) spectroscopies.
First-principles computations of the structural relaxation and of the XANES
spectrum have been performed, with a good agreement to the experiment. The Cr-O
distance is close to that in MgCr2O4, indicating a full relaxation of the first
neighbors, and the second shell of Al atoms relaxes partially. These
observations demonstrate that Vegard's law is not obeyed in the MgAl2O4-MgCr2O4
solid solution. Despite some angular site distortion, the local D3d symmetry of
the B-site of the spinel structure is retained during the substitution of Cr
for Al. Here, we show that the relaxation is accomodated by strain-induced bond
buckling, with angular tilts of the Mg-centred tetrahedra around the Cr-centred
octahedron. By contrast, there is no significant alteration of the angles
between the edge-sharing octahedra, which build chains aligned along the three
four-fold axes of the cubic structure.

In this work, we present a novel approach to calculate the effective gravitational force based on a quantum gauge theory in Euclidean space-time. Our key idea is to consider a class of gauge theories with nontrivial topology, which allows us to introduce a notion of gravitational Wilson line operators. The methodology we follow involves integrating out the matter fields, which in turn reduces the Wilson line operators to a set of classical geometric objects. We then compute the partition function of the gauge theory with these objects, perturbatively in the gravitational coupling. 

Our motivation for this research is rooted in the quest to reconcile quantum mechanics and gravity, which has been a challenging problem for decades in theoretical physics. The effective gravity is a useful tool in this context, as it can provide a framework to study quantum gravitational phenomena in non-perturbative regimes. Our approach is unique in that it provides a geometric interpretation of the effective gravitational force, which is crucial for understanding the underlying physics.

The problem we attempt to solve is a long-standing one and involves extracting meaningful gravitational effects from a quantum field theory. Our approach is based on a rigorous theoretical foundation and is characterized by a high degree of mathematical sophistication. We expect our work to find practical applications in the field of quantum gravity, as it can facilitate the study of black hole physics, gravitational waves, and other quantum gravitational phenomena.

In this work, we investigate the dependence of the estimated luminosities of Ultra-Luminous X-ray sources (ULXs) on spectral models. ULXs are point sources with X-ray luminosities that exceed the Eddington limit for a neutron star. The estimation of their luminosities is a crucial task for evaluating their physical properties, such as their black hole masses and the accretion rates. However, the luminosity estimation is significantly affected by the choice of spectral models. The primary objective of this work is to understand and quantify the impact of different spectral models on the luminosity estimation of ULXs.

We use a large sample of ULXs from the Chandra and XMM-Newton archives and analyze their spectra using different spectral models, including power-law, thermal, and Comptonization models. We employ a Bayesian framework to compare the goodness-of-fit of the spectral models and estimate the uncertainties of the luminosity measurements. Our results indicate that the choice of spectral models has a significant impact on the estimation of the luminosity of ULXs. We find that thermal models tend to overestimate the luminosity, while power-law models tend to underestimate it. We show that a Comptonization model provides a better fit to the data and yields a more accurate luminosity estimation.

The implications of our work are important as it contributes to a better understanding of the nature and physical properties of ULXs, which can have a significant impact on the field of high-energy astrophysics research.

In this paper, we present our work towards creating an exact adaptive algorithm for calculating the determinant of a rational matrix. The determinant of a matrix is a fundamental quantity in linear algebra and has numerous applications in various scientific fields. Our aim is to develop an algorithm that can accurately and efficiently compute the determinant of large rational matrices.

The key idea in our work is to utilize adaptive techniques that can take advantage of the structure of the rational matrix. This allows us to reduce the computational complexity of the algorithm and avoid potential numerical errors that may arise due to the rational property of the matrix. Additionally, we address the challenge of handling large matrix dimensions by implementing sophisticated data structures and optimizing the algorithm's memory usage.

Our methodology involves combining classic techniques such as Gaussian elimination and LU decomposition with more recent discoveries such as the sparse rational matrix algorithm. We further enhance the algorithm's efficiency by leveraging parallel computing techniques.

Through our experiments on a variety of randomly generated rational matrices, we achieved accurate and efficient computation of the determinant within reasonable time complexity. We compared our algorithm with existing methods, and the results show that our algorithm outperforms all of them, especially for large matrices.

Our work contributes to the field of linear algebra by providing a more efficient and reliable algorithm for computing the determinant of a rational matrix, which can be used in various applications.

We present an investigation into the shapes, orientation, and alignment of dark matter subhalos in the Milky Way galaxy. Our goal is to understand the nature of the dark matter distribution, a phenomenon which cannot be directly observed but which can have a significant impact on the motion of visible matter. By analyzing the Milky Way's subhalos, we can improve our understanding of the distribution of the invisible but vital component of the galaxy. 

Our results show that the shapes of the subhalos are generally triaxial and that their orientation is significantly affected by the tidal field of the Milky Way. Additionally, we find evidence for non-random alignments of subhalos, which we attribute to the correlation between the shapes and orientations of neighboring subhalos. Our findings suggest that the distribution of the dark matter subhalos in the Milky Way is not random and is shaped by the environment in which they reside.

We achieve these results by conducting a numerical simulation using a high-resolution N-body code to model the dark matter distribution in the Milky Way. Our analysis involves computing the shapes, orientations, and alignments of the subhalos identified in the simulation and comparing them to theoretical predictions. Our study provides a new insight into the dark matter distribution of the Milky Way and can have implications for a better understanding of the formation and evolution of galaxies.

A set of sufficient conditions to construct $\lambda$-real symbol Maximum
Likelihood (ML) decodable STBCs have recently been provided by Karmakar et al.
STBCs satisfying these sufficient conditions were named as Clifford Unitary
Weight (CUW) codes. In this paper, the maximal rate (as measured in complex
symbols per channel use) of CUW codes for $\lambda=2^a,a\in\mathbb{N}$ is
obtained using tools from representation theory. Two algebraic constructions of
codes achieving this maximal rate are also provided. One of the constructions
is obtained using linear representation of finite groups whereas the other
construction is based on the concept of right module algebra over
non-commutative rings. To the knowledge of the authors, this is the first paper
in which matrices over non-commutative rings is used to construct STBCs. An
algebraic explanation is provided for the 'ABBA' construction first proposed by
Tirkkonen et al and the tensor product construction proposed by Karmakar et al.
Furthermore, it is established that the 4 transmit antenna STBC originally
proposed by Tirkkonen et al based on the ABBA construction is actually a single
complex symbol ML decodable code if the design variables are permuted and
signal sets of appropriate dimensions are chosen.

We present an ergodic model for the expansion of spherical nanoplasmas, aiming to provide a fundamental understanding of the dynamics of such systems. We investigate the expansion of a nanoplasma, where a spherical metal nanoparticle is illuminated by an ultrafast laser pulse, causing a rapid increase in temperature and electron density, and eventually leading to the plasma explosion. We explore the evolution of the nanoplasma by analyzing the time-dependence of several key parameters, such as electron density, temperature, and size, to obtain insights into the underlying dynamics. Our model takes into account the influence of the surrounding medium, and incorporates the effects of ionization, recombination, and diffusion.

By applying this model, we are able to reproduce existing experimental data for different nanoparticle sizes and laser pulse durations, and we provide a consistent framework for interpreting experimental observations. Our approach also enables us to predict novel phenomena, such as the formation of a double shell structure during the expansion of the nanoplasma, which was independently confirmed in recent experiments. Our methodology is based on a combination of numerical simulations and analytical calculations, allowing us to gain a deeper insight into the complex processes involved in the nanoplasma expansion. The results of our work shed light on the underlying mechanisms of nanoplasma expansion, and provide a solid foundation for the development of future applications of nanoplasma technology.

In this study, we challenge the theoretical framework of the cosmological constant, which posits that empty space is pervaded by a constant energy density. We argue that this framework presents significant problems, as the predicted energy density is far greater than what has been observed in experiments.

To address this issue, we propose a modification to the cosmological constant, in which the energy density is allowed to vary with time and space. This approach is based on the idea that the vacuum energy density is influenced by the presence of matter and radiation in the universe, and therefore should not be constant.

To test our hypothesis, we analyze the observed large-scale structure of the universe, as well as the cosmic microwave background radiation. Our analysis shows that our modified cosmological constant framework provides a much better fit to these observations than the conventional framework.

Our study offers a new perspective on the nature of vacuum energy and its role in shaping the evolution of the universe. We hope that our work will inform the ongoing attempts to reconcile the theoretical predictions with the observed properties of the universe, and ultimately contribute to a deeper understanding of the fundamental laws of physics.

Hubble Space Telescope observations of Uranus- and Neptune-crossing object
(65489) Ceto/Phorcys (provisionally designated 2003 FX128) reveal it to be a
close binary system. The mutual orbit has a period of 9.554 +/- 0.011 days and
a semimajor axis of 1840 +/- 48 km. These values enable computation of a system
mass of (5.41 +/- 0.42) 10^18 kg. Spitzer Space Telescope observations of
thermal emission at 24 and 70 microns are combined with visible photometry to
constrain the system's effective radius (109 +10/-11 km) and geometric albedo
(0.084 +0.021/-0.014). We estimate the average bulk density to be 1.37
+0.66/-0.32 g cm^-3, consistent with ice plus rocky and/or carbonaceous
materials. This density contrasts with lower densities recently measured with
the same technique for three other comparably-sized outer Solar System binaries
(617) Patroclus, (26308) 1998 SM165, and (47171) 1999 TC36, and is closer to
the density of the saturnian irregular satellite Phoebe. The mutual orbit of
Ceto and Phorcys is nearly circular, with an eccentricity <= 0.015. This
observation is consistent with calculations suggesting that the system should
tidally evolve on a timescale shorter than the age of the solar system.

Millimeter interferometry provides evidence for the presence of mm to cm size
"pebbles" in the outer parts of disks around pre-main-sequence stars. The
observations suggest that large grains are produced relatively early in disk
evolution (< 1 Myr) and remain at large radii for longer periods of time (5 to
10 Myr). Simple theoretical estimates of the radial drift time of solid
particles, however, imply that they would drift inward over a time scale of
less than 0.1 Myr. In this paper, we address this conflict between theory and
observation, using more detailed theoretical models, including the effects of
sedimentation, collective drag forces and turbulent viscosity. We find that,
although these effects slow down the radial drift of the dust particles, this
reduction is not sufficient to explain the observationally determined long
survival time of mm/cm-sized grains in protoplanetary disks. However, if for
some reason the gas to dust ratio in the disk is reduced by at least a factor
of 20 from the canonical value of 100 (for instance through photoevaporation of
the gas), then the radial drift time scales become sufficiently large to be in
agreement with observations.

Short time-scale radio variations of compact extragalactic radio quasars and
blazars known as IntraDay Variability (IDV) can be explained in at least some
sources as a propagation effect; the variations are interpreted as
scintillation of radio waves in the turbulent interstellar medium of the Milky
Way. One of the most convincing observational arguments in favor of a
propagation-induced variability scenario is the observed annual modulation in
the characteristic time scale of the variation due to the Earth's orbital
motion. So far there are only two sources known with a well-constrained
seasonal cycle. Annual modulation has been proposed for a few other less
well-documented objects. However, for some other IDV sources source-intrinsic
structural variations which cause drastic changes in the variability time scale
were also suggested. J1128+592 is a recently discovered, highly variable IDV
source. Previous, densely time-sampled flux-density measurements with the
Effelsberg 100-m radio telescope (Germany) and the Urumqi 25-m radio telescope
(China), strongly indicate an annual modulation of the time scale. The most
recent 4 observations in 2006/7, however, do not fit well to the annual
modulation model proposed before. In this paper, we investigate a possible
explanation of this discrepancy.

A spectral method is described for solving coupled elliptic problems on an
interior and an exterior domain. The method is formulated and tested on the
two-dimensional interior Poisson and exterior Laplace problems, whose solutions
and their normal derivatives are required to be continuous across the
interface. A complete basis of homogeneous solutions for the interior and
exterior regions, corresponding to all possible Dirichlet boundary values at
the interface, are calculated in a preprocessing step. This basis is used to
construct the influence matrix which serves to transform the coupled boundary
conditions into conditions on the interior problem. Chebyshev approximations
are used to represent both the interior solutions and the boundary values. A
standard Chebyshev spectral method is used to calculate the interior solutions.
The exterior harmonic solutions are calculated as the convolution of the
free-space Green's function with a surface density; this surface density is
itself the solution to an integral equation which has an analytic solution when
the boundary values are given as a Chebyshev expansion. Properties of Chebyshev
approximations insure that the basis of exterior harmonic functions represents
the external near-boundary solutions uniformly. The method is tested by
calculating the electrostatic potential resulting from charge distributions in
a rectangle. The resulting influence matrix is well-conditioned and solutions
converge exponentially as the resolution is increased. The generalization of
this approach to three-dimensional problems is discussed, in particular the
magnetohydrodynamic equations in a finite cylindrical domain surrounded by a
vacuum.

In this study, we present the results of a search for muon neutrinos from northern hemisphere gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) with the Antarctic Muon And Neutrino Detector Array (AMANDA). The motivation for this research stems from the fact that GRBs are among the most powerful energetic phenomena in the universe, and they are believed to be associated with the collapse of massive stars or the merger of compact objects. Moreover, they are considered to be potential sources of high-energy neutrinos, particles that can provide invaluable information about the astrophysical processes that generate these bursts.

Our analysis of AMANDA data between 2000 and 2003 has yielded no evidence for a significant flux of muon neutrinos associated with northern hemisphere GRBs. However, we have set upper limits on the flux of neutrinos from these sources, which can provide important constraints on models of neutrino production in GRBs. Additionally, our analysis allowed us to estimate the sensitivity of future neutrino telescopes, such as IceCube, to detect muon neutrinos from GRBs.

Overall, this work presents important implications for the study of high-energy astrophysics and neutrino physics. Our results contribute to the ongoing efforts to understand the nature of GRBs and the mechanisms that generate high-energy particles in the universe.

We model energy dependencies of the quasi periodic oscillations (QPO) in the
model of disc epicyclic motions, with X-ray modulation caused by varying
relativistic effects. The model was proposed to explain the high frequency QPO
observed in X-ray binaries. We consider two specific scenarios for the geometry
of accretion flow and spectral formation. Firstly, a standard cold accretion
disc with an active X-ray emitting corona is assumed to oscillate. Secondly,
only a hot X-ray emitting accretion flow oscillates, while the cold disc is
absent at the QPO radius. We find that the QPO spectra are generally similar to
the spectrum of radiation emitted at the QPO radius, and they are broadened by
the relativistic effects. In particular, the QPO spectrum contains the disc
component in the oscillating disc with a corona scenario. We also review the
available data on energy dependencies of high frequency QPO, and we point out
that they appear to lack the disc component in their energy spectra. This would
suggest the hot flow geometry in the spectral states when high frequency QPO
are observed.

In this study, three empirical Free Core Nutation (FCN) models developed to
the present time, MHB2000, Malkin's and Lambert's ones, are compared on the
basis of representation of variations of the FCN amplitude and phase predicted
by these models. It is possible to conclude, that the model of the author
provides the most realistic representation of the FCN variations. However, the
specified models are based on representation about single FCN rotational mode.
At the same time, some results of processing of the VLBI observations made
during last years, specify possible presence of two close FCN periods. A
theoretical explanation to presence of a second FCN frequency FCN has been
given by G. Krasinsky in his theory of rotation of the Earth with two-layer
liquid core, ERA2005. In the present work, for more detailed studying this
phenomenon, the IVS time series of celestial pole offset, and also those
predicted by the ERA2005 theory, have been investigated by means of several
statistical methods which confidently show presence of two fluctuations in
nutational movement of an Earth's rotation axis with the periods about -452 and
-410 solar days.

In this work, we present a CCD BV survey of 42 open clusters with the aim of identifying their basic physical properties such as age, distance, and color magnitude diagrams. We obtained the data from small telescopes and CCD cameras at different locations, and we astrometrically calibrated them using Gaia DR2 data. We then constructed color magnitude diagrams, isochrones, and mass functions to infer the clusters' ages, distances, and masses. 

Our survey aims to solve the problem of incomplete, inconsistent, and inaccurate open cluster databases with the latest astrometric calibration and photometric measurement techniques. We expect these determinations to improve knowledge of the structure, evolution, and Galactic distribution of open clusters. 

Our results show that the majority of the surveyed clusters are young, with ages ranging from 10 to 500 million years. The distances range from 570 to 9830 pc, with uncertainties of about 5-10%. The masses range from 11 to 700 solar masses, with a median value of 100 solar masses. We also identified a significant number of unresolved binary systems and the presence of mass segregation in some clusters. These results will serve as a resource for further studies of open clusters and their parent molecular clouds.

In this article, we provide a commentary on the recent study by Kroy et al. (2020), which suggests a new method for predicting the minimal size of a barchan dune. The authors proposed that the size at which a barchan dune can form is related to the balance between the sand transport rate and the dune migration rate, based on the idea of frictional stability. We agree that this is an important topic in the field of aeolian geomorphology, as the formation and migration of barchan dunes can have significant impacts on the stability of desert ecosystems and the management of sand dune fields.

Although Kroy et al. (2020) provide a valuable contribution, we argue that their model oversimplifies the complex physical and environmental factors that regulate the formation and migration of barchan dunes. Additionally, the authors focused on the minimal size of barchan dunes, without considering the variability of dune morphology and the interplay between dune fields. We suggest that future research should incorporate more detailed and comprehensive measurements and simulations to refine our understanding of barchan dune dynamics.

In conclusion, we commend Kroy et al. (2020) for their innovative approach to predicting the minimal size of barchan dunes. However, we also advocate for more sophisticated models and empirical data to better capture the complexity of aeolian processes and improve the management of desert landscapes.

Thermally stimulated luminescence (TSL) and conductivity (TSC) are considered
using the classical insulator model that assumes one kind of the active trap,
one kind of inactive deep trap, and one kind of the recombination center.
Kinetic equations describing the model are solved numerically without and with
the use of the quasiequilibrium (QE) approximation. The QE state parameter qI,,
the relative recombination probability g, and a new parameter called
quasi-stationary (QS) state parameter q*=qIg are used for the analysis of the
TSL and TSC. The TSL and TSC curves and the temperature dependences of qI, q*,
g, the recombination lifetime, and the occupancies of active traps and
recombination centers are numerically calculated for five sets of kinetic
parameters and different heating rates. These calculation results show that:
(1) the upper limit of the heating rate for presence of the QS state appears at
higher heating rate than that for the QE state when the retrapping process is
present, and (2) the TSL (TSC) curves in the QS state have the properties
similar to those for the TSL (TSC) curves in the QE state. Approximate formulas
for calculation of the parameters qI and q* in the initial range of the TSL and
TSC curves are derived and used in the heating-rate methods, proposed in this
work, for determination of those parameters from the calculated TSL curves.

We present K-band imaging of fields around 30 strong CaII absorption line
systems, at 0.7<z<1.1, three of which are confirmed Damped Lyman-alpha systems.
A significant excess of galaxies is found within 6"0 (~50kpc) from the absorber
line-of-sight. The excess galaxies are preferentially luminous compared to the
population of field galaxies. A model in which field galaxies possess a
luminosity-dependent cross-section for CaII absorption of the form (L/L*)^0.7
reproduces the observations well. The luminosity-dependent cross-section for
the CaII absorbers appears to be significantly stronger than the established
(L/L*)^0.4 dependence for MgII absorbers. The associated galaxies lie at large
physical distances from the CaII-absorbing gas; we find a mean impact parameter
of 24kpc (H0=70km\s\Mpc). Combined with the observed number density of CaII
absorbers the large physical separations result in an inferred filling factor
of only ~10 per cent. The physical origin of the strong CaII absorption remains
unclear, possible explanations vary from very extended disks of the luminous
galaxies to associated dwarf galaxy neighbours, remnants of outflows from the
luminous galaxies, or tidal debris from cannibalism of smaller galaxies.

In this study, we analyze the stellar mass assembly history of galaxies over the last 8 billion years using data from the VIMOS VLT Deep Survey. Our main objective is to understand the growth and evolution of galaxies as a function of their stellar mass and cosmic time. We use a sample of more than 20,000 galaxies with stellar masses greater than 10^10 solar masses extracted from the survey. The mass growth of these galaxies is traced through their cumulative stellar mass density profiles as a function of cosmic time.

Our results show that the stellar mass assembly history of galaxies is strongly dependent on their initial mass. We find that the most massive galaxies (>10^11 solar masses) have already assembled a significant fraction of their mass at high redshifts (z>2), while smaller galaxies continue to grow in mass until the present day. We also find that the rate of mass growth decreases with increasing redshift, indicating that the assembly of galaxies has slowed down over time.

Our methodology involves the analysis of galaxy number counts, cumulative stellar mass density profiles, and the use of the stellar mass-halo mass relation to infer the evolution of dark matter halos. Our research seeks to shed light on the physical mechanisms responsible for the observed growth of galaxies over cosmic time.

Overall, our study provides a critical insight into the formation and evolution of galaxies and provides a foundation for future research in this field.

In this paper, we investigate the variation of fundamental constants and the relationship between these variations and the evolution of the Universe. The main motivation behind this research is to understand the behavior of nature's most fundamental quantities that are believed to be immutable. We explore both the theoretical and observational aspects of this study, and analyze how variations in these constants can have wide-ranging implications in our understanding of physical phenomena across space and time.

The key ideas explored in this paper center on the theoretical basis for the variation of fundamental constants, such as the fine structure constant, and how these variations could arise from fundamental physical processes. We also present an overview of current observational techniques used to measure these variations and highlight the challenges associated with these measurements. Finally, we discuss the implications of these variations in cosmology and the search for a unified theory of physics.

Our methodology includes a thorough literature review, theoretical modelling, and data analysis of observational results. We also consider the constraints provided by cosmological simulations and compare our results with existing models proposed in the literature.

Overall, this work aims to contribute to our understanding of the behavior of the Universe and to stimulate discussion on how our views of the Universe may change in light of any new discoveries.

The BL Lac object 3C 66A was observed in an extensive multiwavelength
monitoring campaign from July 2003 till April 2004. The spectral energy
distribution (SED) was measured over the entire electromagnetic spectrum, with
flux measurements from radio to X-ray frequencies and upper limits in the very
high energy (VHE) gamma-ray regime. Here, we use a time-dependent leptonic jet
model to reproduce the SED and optical spectral variability observed during our
multiwavelength campaign. Our model simulations could successfully reproduce
the observed SED and optical light curves and predict an intrinsic cutoff value
for the VHE gamma-ray emission at ~ 4 GeV. The effect of the optical depth due
to the intergalactic infrared background radiation (IIBR) on the peak of the
high-energy component of 3C 66A was found to be negligible. Also, the presence
of a broad line region (BLR) in the case of 3C 66A may play an important role
in the emission of gamma-ray photons when the emission region is very close to
the central engine, but further out, the production mechanism of hard X-ray and
gamma-ray photons becomes rapidly dominated by synchrotron self-Compton
emission. We further discuss the possibility of an observable X-ray spectral
variability pattern. The simulated results do not predict observable hysteresis
patterns in the optical or soft X-ray regimes for major flares on multi-day
time scales.

In our work entitled "Detection of GRB 060927 at z = 5.47: Implications for the Use of Gamma-Ray Bursts as Probes of the End of the Dark Ages," we aimed to study the characteristics of gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) and their potential as probes for understanding the end of the dark ages. The problem we attempted to solve was to determine if GRBs could provide insight into the formation of the first stars and galaxies, which occurred about 13 billion years ago, or if they could only illuminate the later universe at redshifts of about 5.

Our key idea was to study GRB 060927 in detail since it occurred at an unprecedented redshift of 5.47. We obtained data from several telescopes and analyzed it using spectroscopy, photometry, and modeling techniques. Our results showed that GRB 060927 was an exceptionally bright and energetic burst, lasting for 10 seconds and releasing an enormous amount of energy. We also found that its host galaxy was a young dwarf galaxy, suggesting that GRBs may have contributed to the reionization of the universe in its early stages.

Overall, our study highlights the potential of GRBs as probes of the early universe. Our methodology, using a combination of observational data and modeling, provides a promising approach for further investigations into the formation of the first galaxies and evolution of the universe.

In this research, we investigate the timeline and rate of cosmic acceleration. By analyzing the data obtained from type Ia supernova, we attempt to understand the acceleration of the universe. Our research uses data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, and the Supernova Legacy Survey, in combination with literature data, to establish a high-redshift sample. We fit a variety of models, such as the Chevallier-Polarski-Linder (CPL) and Jassal-Bagla-Padmanabhan (JBP) in order to provide a precise estimate of the transition redshift and rate. The methodology for analyzing the data used a maximum likelihood estimation to obtain a chi-square fit, with the error of the supernova magnitudes being considered. 

The results show that the transition redshift is approximately 0.77-0.81, which means that cosmic acceleration began roughly six billion years ago. Additionally, the acceleration rate was found to be 0.34-0.38, consistent with previous research. Our research provides further evidence to support the idea that standard Lambda Cold Dark Matter (ΛCDM) cosmology is consistent with the observations. 

The motivation of our research is the understanding of the universe's evolution from its early stages to the present day. Exploring the fundamental principles of our universe allows for a better understanding of nature and our place in it. Furthermore, an accurate estimate of cosmic acceleration provides valuable information to cosmologists in terms of understanding the future of the universe.

In this research, we showcase new object classification techniques for difference imaging that will help find more supernovae with less work. Difference imaging technology uses the subtraction technique to locate objects that exhibit variations in brightness between two images of the same region of the sky taken at different times. Supernovae are among these objects. However, the process of manually scanning through large amounts of image data to distinguish and identify supernovae from other objects can be limiting, time-consuming, and potentially error-prone.

Our research aims to address this limitation by presenting an automated object classification technique designed to enhance the identification of supernovae in difference imaging images. Specifically, we propose a combination of machine learning algorithms and image feature extraction techniques to simplify the object classification procedure. We carried out evaluations on various datasets using different classification algorithms to determine the suitability of our approach. 

The motivation behind this research is to develop a more efficient and accurate technique to identify supernovae in astronomical observations, which can potentially lead to deeper insights into their origins and properties. Our results show that the proposed object classification technique enhances the accuracy and efficiency of supernova identification, demonstrating its potential for use in large-scale astronomical surveys and the broader scientific community.

Ab initio wavefunction based methods are applied to the study of electron
correlation effects on the band structure of oxide systems. We choose MgO as a
prototype closed-shell ionic oxide. Our analysis is based on a local
Hamiltonian approach and performed on finite fragments cut from the infinite
solid. Localized Wannier functions and embedding potentials are obtained from
prior periodic Hartree-Fock (HF) calculations. We investigate the role of
various electron correlation effects in reducing the HF band gap and modifying
the band widths. On-site and nearest-neighbor charge relaxation as well as
long-range polarization effects are calculated. Whereas correlation effects are
essential for computing accurate band gaps, we found that they produce smaller
changes on the HF band widths, at least for this material. Surprisingly, a
broadening effect is obtained for the O 2p valence bands. The ab initio data
are in good agreement with the energy gap and band width derived from
thermoreflectance and x-ray photoemission experiments. The results show that
the wavefunction based approach applied here allows for well controlled
approximations and a transparent identification of the microscopic processes
which determine the electronic band structure.

In this work, we investigate the stability of polytropes, which are self-gravitating, spherical objects with a fixed ratio of pressure to density power law index. Our aim is to understand how these objects behave under small perturbations and whether they maintain their equilibrium configurations. We use the theoretical tools of linear perturbation analysis and numerical simulations to study the stability of polytropes with different indices and central densities.

Our methodology involves solving the equations of hydrostatic equilibrium and perturbing the density and pressure fields of the polytropes. We then solve the linearized equations of motion and calculate the growth rate of the perturbations. Additionally, we perform numerical simulations using the smoothed particle hydrodynamics method to confirm and extend our analytical results.

Our results indicate that the stability of polytropes depends strongly on the value of the polytropic index, with higher indices being more stable. We find a critical index above which the polytropes are stable for all central densities, and below which they are unstable to radial perturbations. We also identify modes of instability for low-index polytropes, which can lead to the collapse of the object.

Our work aims to provide a better understanding of the physics of polytropes and the conditions under which they can form and evolve in the universe. Our findings have implications for astrophysical objects such as stars, protostars, and white dwarfs, which can be modeled as polytropes.

We investigate the possibility that dark matter is made of heavy Dirac
neutrinos with mass in the range [O(1) GeV- a few TeV] and with suppressed but
non-zero coupling to the Standard Model Z as well as a coupling to an
additional Z' gauge boson. The first part of this paper provides a
model-independent analysis for the relic density and direct detection in terms
of four main parameters: the mass, the couplings to the Z, to the Z' and to the
Higgs. These WIMP candidates arise naturally as Kaluza-Klein states in
extra-dimensional models with extended electroweak gauge group SU(2)_L* SU(2)_R
* U(1). They can be stable because of Kaluza-Klein parity or of other discrete
symmetries related to baryon number for instance, or even, in the low mass and
low coupling limits, just because of a phase-space-suppressed decay width. An
interesting aspect of warped models is that the extra Z' typically couples only
to the third generation, thus avoiding the usual experimental constraints. In
the second part of the paper, we illustrate the situation in details in a
warped GUT model.

Bose-Einstein-condensed gases in external spatially random potentials are
considered in the frame of a stochastic self-consistent mean-field approach.
This method permits the treatment of the system properties for the whole range
of the interaction strength, from zero to infinity, as well as for arbitrarily
strong disorder. Besides a condensate and superfluid density, a glassy number
density due to a spatially inhomogeneous component of the condensate occurs.
For very weak interactions and sufficiently strong disorder, the superfluid
fraction can become smaller than the condensate fraction, while at relatively
strong interactions, the superfluid fraction is larger than the condensate
fraction for any strength of disorder. The condensate and superfluid fractions,
and the glassy fraction always coexist, being together either nonzero or zero.
In the presence of disorder, the condensate fraction becomes a nonmonotonic
function of the interaction strength, displaying an antidepletion effect caused
by the competition between the stabilizing role of the atomic interaction and
the destabilizing role of the disorder. With increasing disorder, the
condensate and superfluid fractions jump to zero at a critical value of the
disorder parameter by a first-order phase transition.

In this paper, we continue our work on the classification of finite-dimensional complex filiform Leibniz algebras. We build upon the results of our previous paper and provide a complete classification of these algebras of dimensions six and seven. Our methodology involves a thorough analysis of the structure of these algebras through a combination of algebraic, computational, and geometric techniques.

The main motivation for this research is the practical application of Leibniz algebras in fields such as physics and engineering. These algebras provide a powerful framework for modeling and analyzing complex systems, and a better understanding of their properties can lead to more efficient and accurate models.

The problem we attempt to solve in this paper is the classification of filiform Leibniz algebras of low dimensions. The classification of these algebras is particularly challenging due to their nontrivial structure and the large number of isomorphism classes. However, our results show that a complete classification is possible, providing new insights into the algebraic and geometric properties of these structures.

In conclusion, our work contributes to the ongoing development of the theory of Leibniz algebras and demonstrates the potential of these structures in practical applications. Our methodology and results may also be useful in other areas of algebraic research and provide a foundation for future work on these intriguing and complex algebraic structures.

Theoretical analysis of interplay between the condensates $<\bar{q}q>$ and
$<qq>$ in vacuum is generally made by relativistic effective potentials in the
mean field approximation in 2D, 3D and 4D models with two flavor and $N_c$
color massless fermions. It is found that in ground states of these models,
interplay between the two condensates mainly depend on the ratio $G_S/H_S$ for
2D and 4D case or $G_S/H_P$ for 3D case, where $G_S$, $H_S$ and $H_P$ are
respectively the coupling constants in a scalar $(\bar{q}q)$, a scalar $(qq)$
and a pseudoscalar $(qq)$ channel. In ground states of all the models, only
pure $<\bar{q}q>$ condensates could exist if $G_S/H_S$ or $G_S/H_P$ is bigger
than the critical value $2/N_c$, the ratio of the color numbers of the fermions
entering into the condensates $<qq>$ and $<\bar{q}q>$. As $G_S/H_S$ or
$G_S/H_P$ decreases to the region below $2/N_c$, differences of the models will
manifest themselves. Depending on different models, and also on $N_c$ in 3D
model, one will have or have no the coexistence phase of the two condensates,
besides the pure $<qq>$ condensate phase. The $G_S-H_S$ (or $G_S-H_P$) phase
diagrams in these models are given. The results also implicate a real
constraint on two-flavor QCD-analogous NJL model.

We measure the obscured star formation in z~1 early-type galaxies. This
constrains the influence of star formation on their optical/near-IR colors,
which, we found, are redder than predicted by the model by Bruzual & Charlot
(2003). From deep ACS imaging we construct a sample of 95 morphologically
selected early-type galaxies in the HDF-N and CDF-S with spectroscopic
redshifts in the range 0.85<z<1.15. We measure their 24 micron fluxes from the
deep GOODS-MIPS imaging and derive the IR luminosities and star formation
rates. The fraction of galaxies with >2 sigma detections (~25 muJy} is
17(-4,+9)%. Of the 15 galaxies with significant detections at least six have an
AGN. Stacking the MIPS images of the galaxies without significant detections
and adding the detected galaxies without AGN we find an upper limit on the mean
star formation rate (SFR) of 5.2+/-3.0 Msol yr^-1, and on the mean specific SFR
of 4.6+/-2.2 * 10^-11 yr^-1. Under the assumption that the average SFR will
decline at the same rate as the cosmic average, the in situ growth in stellar
mass of the early-type galaxy population is less than 14+/-7% between z=1 and
the present. We show that the typically low IR luminosity and SFR imply that
the effect of obscured star formation (or AGN) on their rest-frame
optical/near-IR SEDs is negligible for ~90% of the galaxies in our sample.
Hence, their optical/near-IR colors are most likely dominated by evolved
stellar populations. This implies that the colors predicted by the Bruzual &
Charlot (2003) model for stellar populations with ages similar to those of z~1
early-type galaxies (~1-3 Gyr) are most likely too blue, and that stellar
masses of evolved, high-redshift galaxies can be overestimated by up to a
factor of ~2.

In this study, we present the design and fabrication of a very high-Q superconductor electromechanical clock. Our aim was to address the need for a clock that could maintain its accuracy over time without being affected by environmental factors such as temperature changes or vibration. To achieve this, we utilized superconductor materials that exhibit zero electrical resistance and high magnetic properties, resulting in a stable and precise timekeeping mechanism. 

To make this clock, we employed a resonant circuit composed of a superconducting coil and a mechanical resonator made of quartz crystal. This circuit oscillates at a specific frequency that corresponds to the time unit, making the clock run accurately. The output signals were converted from mechanical to electrical signals and measured using a cryogenic amplifier. 

The motivation for this research was to develop a clock with extraordinary precision and accuracy, which could be useful in various scientific and industrial applications where precise timekeeping is necessary. Moreover, our work could contribute to the development of superconducting technologies, which could lead to significant advances in different fields such as power transmission and medical imaging. 

In conclusion, we successfully designed and fabricated a very high-Q superconductor electromechanical clock that showed exceptional accuracy with unprecedented stability and precision. Our work demonstrated the advantages of using superconductor materials in clockmaking and seems promising for further research in high-precision timekeeping.

In this paper, we investigate the occurrence of difermion condensates in vacuum in 2-4D four-fermion interaction models. We introduce the concept of a non-local four-fermion interaction in 2-4D spacetime, which allows us to study the formation of condensates in the vacuum state. Our methodology involves the use of a mean field approximation and numerical simulations to analyze the relevant parameters and their effects. Our main goal is to understand the role of the four-fermion interaction in the emergence of difermion condensates in vacuum, as well as to explore the dynamics of these condensates.

Our work builds upon previous studies in the field, but focuses specifically on the effects of the four-fermion interaction, which has not been extensively studied in this context. Our approach allows us to make predictions about the behavior of difermion condensates under different conditions and to identify the key factors that influence their formation and dynamics. Through our simulations, we demonstrate the emergence of stable difermion condensates in 2-4D vacuum space, and analyze their properties in detail.

Our results suggest that the formation of difermion condensates in vacuum is a robust phenomenon, which can occur in a wide range of conditions and is strongly influenced by the strength of the four-fermion interaction. Our work contributes to the understanding of the dynamics of these condensates and may have implications for the study of particle physics and cosmology.

In this paper, we present a study focused on the understanding of transients from initial conditions in N-body simulations using Lagrangian perturbation theory. N-body simulations are one of the primary tools in cosmology to explore the evolution of large-scale structures in the Universe. However, the precise reproduction of observed galaxy clustering statistics remains a challenge. A major source of systematic errors comes from the initial conditions, which are typically generated using linear perturbation theory assumptions. We propose a novel approach that employs Lagrangian perturbation theory to compute the initial conditions for the simulations, thereby accounting for non-linear effects that can have a significant impact on the final clustering statistics. Our proposal is based on the idea that high-order corrections can be systematically included and give rise to non-Gaussianities in the initial conditions. The methodology is tested on a set of numerical experiments, where we demonstrate that our approach leads to improved results in the clustering statistics even on large scales. Our work aims to contribute to the development of more accurate and reliable simulation tools that can be used to test cosmological models and explore the fundamental properties of the Universe.

We review the recently discovered interplay between chaos and symmetry in the
general inhomogeneous solution of many string-related Einstein-matter systems
in the vicinity of a cosmological singularity. The
Belinsky-Khalatnikov-Lifshitz-type chaotic behaviour is found, for many
Einstein-matter models (notably those related to the low-energy limit of
superstring theory and M-theory), to be connected with certain
(infinite-dimensional) hyperbolic Kac-Moody algebras. In particular, the
billiard chambers describing the asymptotic cosmological behaviour of pure
Einstein gravity in spacetime dimension d+1, or the metric-three-form system of
11-dimensional supergravity, are found to be identical to the Weyl chambers of
the Lorentzian Kac-Moody algebras AE_d, or E_{10}, respectively. This suggests
that these Kac-Moody algebras are hidden symmetries of the corresponding
models. There even exists some evidence of a hidden equivalence between the
general solution of the Einstein-three-form system and a null geodesic in the
infinite dimensional coset space E_{10} / K(E_{10}), where K(E_{10}) is the
maximal compact subgroup of E_{10}.

In this work, we explore the topic of symmetries in differential geometry through a computational approach to prolongations. Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that symmetries play a crucial role in many areas of mathematical physics and mechanics, with applications ranging from fluid dynamics and elasticity to relativistic mechanics and quantum field theory. Our main goal is to develop a computational framework for computing the prolongations of Lie symmetries of a given differential equation, which essentially amounts to determining the highest-order derivatives that can be expressed in terms of the dependent and independent variables and their lower-order derivatives. 

Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed computational approach, which is based on the use of symbolic manipulation software and Lie symmetry algorithms. We show that this approach can be applied to a wide range of differential equations, from simple linear equations to highly nonlinear systems. Moreover, our approach allows us to obtain explicit formulas for the prolongations of Lie symmetries, which can be used to determine the Lie point symmetries of the equation. This, in turn, provides valuable insight into the underlying symmetry structure of the equation and its physical implications. Overall, our work contributes to a better understanding of the role of symmetries in differential geometry, and provides a powerful computational tool for investigating these structures in various contexts.

The influence of three well-known disaccharides, namely trehalose, maltose
and sucrose, on some structural and dynamical properties of lysozyme has been
investigated by means of molecular dynamics computer simulations in the 37-60
wt % concentration range. The effects of sugars on the protein conformation are
found relatively weak, in agreement with the preferential hydration of
lysozyme. Conversely, sugars seem to increase significantly the relaxation
times of the protein. These effects are shown to be correlated to the
fractional solvent accessibilities of lysozyme residues and further support the
slaving of protein dynamics. Moreover, a significant increase in the relaxation
times of lysozyme, sugars and water molecules is observed within the studied
concentration range and may result from the percolation of the hydrogen-bond
network of sugar molecules. This percolation appears to be of primary
importance to explain the influence of sugars on the dynamical properties of
lysozyme and water.

In this research, we address the problem of identifying the locations of distributed antenna systems (DAS) with multiple carrier frequency offsets (CFOs). The DAS is an evolving technology that offers efficient wireless communication by deploying multiple antennas. However, it poses a significant challenge in identifying the actual location of antennas, especially when multiple CFOs are present.

The motivation behind this research is to address the growing demand for accurate identification of the DAS location. Accurate identification of DAS locations is crucial for optimizing wireless communication systems. However, existing methods of identifying DAS locations require a significant amount of prior information about the system, which is not always available. Thus, a new approach is needed to overcome this challenge.

To overcome this problem, we propose a blind identification technique that uses the power distribution of the received signals to estimate the carrier frequencies and locations of DAS antennas. We have developed an algorithm that estimates the CFOs using the second-order statistics of the received signals.

Our methodology employs simulations and experiments with varying levels of noise and signal strengths. The results of our experiments demonstrate that our proposed algorithm accurately identifies the locations of DAS antennas with multiple CFOs. Our approach offers a practical solution for the accurate identification of DAS locations with multiple CFOs, which is crucial for optimal wireless communication performance.

In this study, we present an analysis of thermally stimulated luminescence and conductivity in the absence of the quasiequilibrium approximation. The motivation for this work stems from the fact that the traditional quasiequilibrium assumption, which assumes the carrier distribution to be in equilibrium with the temperature, is invalid in many systems. Our aim is to develop a more accurate method for analyzing such systems. 

We employ a generalized Master equation approach to model the energy distribution of traps in the system and derive expressions for the luminescence and conductivity. We find that in the absence of the quasiequilibrium assumption, the luminescence and conductivity show highly non-linear behavior. Specifically, we observe non-monotonic dependencies on trap concentrations, energetic distributions, and temperature. Furthermore, we find that our results are in good agreement with experimental data and that our approach provides a clearer understanding of the dynamics of the system. 

In conclusion, this study presents a novel method for analyzing luminescence and conductivity in non-equilibrium systems and demonstrates our approach's effectiveness in describing the dynamics of such systems. We expect that our findings will be valuable for future studies of optoelectronic materials and devices.

It has been demonstrated that quantum vacuum energy gravitates according to
the equivalence principle, at least for the finite Casimir energies associated
with perfectly conducting parallel plates. We here add further support to this
conclusion by considering parallel semitransparent plates, that is,
delta-function potentials, acting on a massless scalar field, in a spacetime
defined by Rindler coordinates (tau,x,y,xi). Fixed xi in such a spacetime
represents uniform acceleration. We calculate the force on systems consisting
of one or two such plates at fixed values of xi. In the limit of large Rindler
coordinate xi (small acceleration), we recover (via the equivalence principle)
the situation of weak gravity, and find that the gravitational force on the
system is just Mg, where g is the gravitational acceleration and M is the total
mass of the system, consisting of the mass of the plates renormalized by the
Casimir energy of each plate separately, plus the energy of the Casimir
interaction between the plates. This reproduces the previous result in the
limit as the coupling to the delta-function potential approaches infinity.

We describe a numerical method to compute Casimir forces in arbitrary
geometries, for arbitrary dielectric and metallic materials, with arbitrary
accuracy (given sufficient computational resources). Our approach, based on
well-established integration of the mean stress tensor evaluated via the
fluctuation-dissipation theorem, is designed to directly exploit fast methods
developed for classical computational electromagnetism, since it only involves
repeated evaluation of the Green's function for imaginary frequencies
(equivalently, real frequencies in imaginary time). We develop the approach by
systematically examining various formulations of Casimir forces from the
previous decades and evaluating them according to their suitability for
numerical computation. We illustrate our approach with a simple
finite-difference frequency-domain implementation, test it for known geometries
such as a cylinder and a plate, and apply it to new geometries. In particular,
we show that a piston-like geometry of two squares sliding between metal walls,
in both two and three dimensions with both perfect and realistic metallic
materials, exhibits a surprising non-monotonic ``lateral'' force from the
walls.

We investigate the ground-state magnetic long-range order of
quasi-one-dimensional quantum Heisenberg antiferromagnets for spin quantum
numbers s=1/2 and s=1. We use the coupled cluster method to calculate the
sublattice magnetization in dependence on the inter-chain coupling. We find
that for the unfrustrated spin-1/2 system an infinitesimal inter-chain coupling
is sufficient to stabilize magnetic long-range order, which is in agreement
with results obtained by some other methods. For s=1 we find that a finite
inter-chain coupling of about 10% of the in-chain coupling is necessary to
stabilize magnetic long-range order. Furthermore, we consider a quasi
one-dimensional spin-1/2 system, where a frustrating next-nearest neighbor
in-chain coupling is included. We find that for stronger frustration also a
finite inter-chain coupling is necessary to have magnetic long-range order in
the ground state, where the strength of the inter-chain coupling necessary to
establish magnetic long-range order is related to the size of the spin gap of
the isolated chain.

We present a novel approach to estimate the ${\bar q}q$ condensate for light quarks beyond the chiral limit within models of QCD. This work aims to tackle the pressing problem of modeling QCD in the low-energy regime, in which perturbative methods no longer suffice due to the non-perturbative dynamics. Specifically, we focus on estimating the ${\bar q}q$ condensate, a fundamental quantity in QCD, that is responsible for the spontaneous breaking of chiral symmetry. Our proposed method is based on the Banks-Casher relation, which characterizes the relation between the density of low-lying modes of the Dirac operator and the ${\bar q}q$ condensate. In order to go beyond the usual chiral limit, we use a modified Dirac operator, which allows for non-zero quark masses. We perform numerical simulations to test our approach, and obtain results for the ${\bar q}q$ condensate for up and down light quarks that agree well with lattice QCD data. The motivation for this research is to gain a better understanding of the non-perturbative regime of QCD, a frontier that is still largely unexplored and remains one of the greatest challenges in theoretical physics.

Data from {\it Chandra} observations of thirty nearby galaxies were analyzed
and 365 X-ray point sources were chosen whose spectra were not contaminated by
excessive diffuse emission and not affected by photon pile up. The spectra of
these sources were fitted using two spectral models (an absorbed power-law and
a disk blackbody) to ascertain the dependence of estimated parameters on the
spectral model used. It was found that the cumulative luminosity function
depends on the choice of the spectral model, especially for luminosities $>
10^{40}$ ergs/s. In accordance with previous results, a large number ($\sim
80$) of the sources have luminosities $> 10^{39}$ ergs/s (Ultra-Luminous X-ray
sources) with indistinguishable average spectral parameters (inner disk
temperature $\sim 1$ keV and/or photon index $\Gamma \sim 2$) with those of the
lower luminosities ones. After considering foreground stars and known
background AGN,we identify four sources whose minimum luminosity exceed
$10^{40}$ ergs/s, and call them Extremely Luminous X-ray sources (ELX). The
spectra of these sources are in general better represented by the disk black
body model than the power-law one. These ELX can be grouped into two distinct
spectral classes. Two of them have an inner disk temperature of $< 0.5$ keV and
hence are called ``supersoft'' ELX, while the other two have temperatures
$\gtrsim 1.3$ keV and are called ``hard'' ELX. The estimated inner disk
temperatures of the supersoft ELX are compatible with the hypothesis that they
harbor intermediate size black holes, which are accreting at $\sim 0.5$ times
their Eddington Luminosity. The radiative mechanism for hard ELX, seems to be
Inverse Comptonization, which in contrast to standard black holes systems, is
probably saturated.

This is the second paper studying the QSOs in the spitzer QUEST sample.
Previously we presented new PAH measurements and argued that most of the
observed far infrared (FIR) radiation is due to star-forming activity. Here we
present spectral energy distributions (SEDs) by supplementing our data with
optical, NIR and FIR observations. We define two sub-groups of ``weak FIR'' and
``strong FIR'' QSOs, and a third group of FIR non-detections. Assuming a
starburst origin for the FIR, we obtain ``intrinsic'' AGN SEDs by subtracting a
starburst template from the mean SEDs. The resulting SEDs are remarkably
similar for all groups. They show three distinct peaks corresponding to two
silicate emission features and a 3mic bump that we interpret as the signature
of the hottest AGN dust. They also display drops beyond 20mic that we interpret
as the signature of the minimum temperature (about 200K) dust. This component
must be optically thin to explain the silicate emission and the slope of the
long wavelength continuum. We discuss the merits of an alternative model where
most of the FIR emission is due to AGN heating. Such models are unlikely to
explain the properties of our QSOs but they cannot be ruled out for more
luminous objects. We also find correlations between the luminosity at 5100A and
two infrared starburst indicators: L(60mic) and L(PAH 7.7mic). The correlation
of L(5100A) with L(60mic) can be used to measure the relative growth rates and
lifetimes of the black hole and the new stars.

We investigate the conditions of entanglement for a system of two atoms and
two photon modes in vacuum, using the Jaynes-Cummings model in the
rotating-wave approximation. It is found, by generalizing the existing results,
that the strength of entanglement is a periodic function of time. We explicitly
show that our results are in agreement with the existing results of
entanglement conditions under appropriate limits. Results for the two-atom and
two-photon system are generalized to the case of arbitrary values for the
atomic energies, corresponding to photon modes frequencies. Though it is
apparently a generalization of the existing work, we have considered for the
first time both the resonant and nonresonant conditions and found a general
equation which could be true for both cases. Moreover, we show that periodicity
of the entanglement is a distinct feature of resonant system. Considering the
two atoms and two photons system, in detail, we setup an approach which could
be generalized for many particle systems and the resulting master equation can
also be analyzed.

In this work, we investigate the absence of the fifth force problem in a model governed by a spontaneously broken dilatation symmetry. The key idea behind this research is to propose a solution to the long-standing problem of reconciling the existence of a fifth force with the fundamental principles of nature. To this end, we introduce a new theoretical framework that involves the spontaneous breaking of dilatation symmetry, which we show is capable of providing a natural explanation for the absence of the fifth force.

Our methodology involved a combination of analytical calculations and numerical simulations, which allowed us to investigate the properties of the proposed model in detail. Our results demonstrate that the model is capable of accurately reproducing the observed behavior of the universe, and that it provides a natural explanation for the observed absence of a fifth force.

The problem that this work attempts to solve is a significant one, as the existence of a fifth force would imply the breakdown of some of the most fundamental principles of nature. By proposing a solution that is both theoretically sound and experimentally viable, our research represents an important step forward in our understanding of the fundamental laws that govern the universe.

Aims: We describe a fast, robust and automatic detection algorithm, TRUFAS,
and apply it to data that are being expected from the CoRoT mission. Methods:
The procedure proposed for the detection of planetary transits in light curves
works in two steps: 1) a continuous wavelet transformation of the detrended
light curve with posterior selection of the optimum scale for transit
detection, and 2) a period search in that selected wavelet transformation. The
detrending of the light curves are based on Fourier filtering or a discrete
wavelet transformation. TRUFAS requires the presence of at least 3 transit
events in the data. Results: The proposed algorithm is shown to identify
reliably and quickly the transits that had been included in a standard set of
999 light curves that simulate CoRoT data. Variations in the pre-processing of
the light curves and in the selection of the scale of the wavelet transform
have only little effect on TRUFAS' results. Conclusions: TRUFAS is a robust and
quick transit detection algorithm, especially well suited for the analysis of
very large volumes of data from space or ground-based experiments, with long
enough durations for the target-planets to produce multiple transit events.

Deep space laser ranging missions like ASTROD I (Single-Spacecraft
Astrodynamical Space Test of Relativity using Optical Devices) and ASTROD,
together with astrometry missions like GAIA and LATOR will be able to test
relativistic gravity to an unprecedented level of accuracy. More precisely,
these missions will enable us to test relativistic gravity to
$10^{-7}-10^{-9}$, and will require 2nd post-Newtonian approximation of
relevant theories of gravity. The first post-Newtonian approximation is valid
to $10^{-6}$ and the second post-Newtonian is valid to $10^{-12}$ in the solar
system. The scalar-tensor theory is widely discussed and used in tests of
