relativistic gravity, especially after the interests in inflation, cosmological
constant and dark energy in cosmology. In the Lagrangian, intermediate-range
gravity term has a similar form as cosmological term. Here we present the full
second post-Newtonian approximation of the scalar-tensor theory including
viable examples of intermediate-range gravity. We use Chandrasekhar's approach
to derive the metric coefficients and the equation of the hydrodynamics
governing a perfect fluid in the 2nd post-Newtonian approximation in
scalar-tensor theory; all terms inclusive of $O(c^{-4})$ are retained
consistently in the equation of motion.

In this work, we present a comprehensive study of accretion disk illumination in Schwarzschild and Kerr geometries, focusing on the development of fitting formulae to accurately describe the photon emissivities and fluxes. The goal of our research is to provide a reliable and efficient framework for modeling the behavior of accretion disks around black holes, which are believed to be among the most energetic and dynamic phenomena in the universe.

To achieve this, we begin by formulating the relevant physics equations governing the radiation emission and absorption processes in the accretion disk. We then use a combination of analytical solutions and numerical simulations to derive the necessary parameters for our fitting formulae, taking into account the effects of general relativity and other relevant factors such as disk thickness and temperature.

Our results demonstrate that our fitting formulae are highly accurate and efficient for modeling accretion disk illumination in both Schwarzschild and Kerr geometries, outperforming existing models in terms of speed and precision. Additionally, we identify several key features of these accretion disks that have important implications for our understanding of black hole behavior and the wider astrophysical landscape.

Overall, our work provides a valuable tool for researchers studying accretion disks and black holes, and represents a significant step forward in our ability to model and understand these fascinating systems.

Spectral variability is the main tool for constraining emission models of BL
  Lac objects. By means of systematic observations of the BL Lac prototype PKS
2155-304 in the infrared-optical band, we explore variability on the scales of
months, days and hours. We made our observations with the robotic 60 cm
telescope REM located at La Silla, Chile. VRIJHK filters were used. PKS
2155-304 was observed from May to December 2005. The wavelength interval
explored, the total number of photometric points and the short integration time
render our photometry substantially superior to previous ones for this source.
On the basis of the intensity and colour we distinguish three different states
of the source, each of duration of months, which include all those described in
the literature. In particular, we report the highest state ever detected in the
H band. The source varied by a factor of 4 in this band, much more than in the
V band (a factor ~2). The source softened with increasing intensity, contrary
to the general pattern observed in the UV-X-ray bands. On five nights of
November we had nearly continuous monitoring for 2-3 hours. A variability
episode with a time scale of ~24 h is well documented, a much more rapid flare
with t=1-2 h, is also apparent, but is supported by relatively few points.

We report on follow-up observations of the GRB 060927 using the ROTSE-IIIa
telescope and a suite of larger aperture ground-based telescopes. An optical
afterglow was detected 20 s after the burst, the earliest rest-frame detection
of optical emission from any GRB. Spectroscopy performed with the VLT about 13
hours after the trigger shows a continuum break at lambda ~ 8070 A produced by
neutral hydrogen absorption at z~5.6. We also detect an absorption line at 8158
A which we interpret as SiII at z=5.467. Hence, GRB 060927 is the second most
distant GRB with a spectroscopically measured redshift. The shape of the red
wing of the spectral break can be fitted by a damped Lyalpha profile with a
column density with log(N_HI/cm^-2) ~ 22.5. We discuss the implications of this
work for the use of GRBs as probes of the end of the dark ages and draw three
main conclusions: i) GRB afterglows originating from z>6 should be relatively
easy to detect from the ground, but rapid NIR monitoring is necessary to ensure
that they are found; ii) The presence of large HI column densities in some GRBs
host galaxies at z>5 makes the use of GRBs to probe the reionization epoch via
spectroscopy of the red damping wing challenging; iii) GRBs appear crucial to
locate typical star-forming galaxies at z>5 and therefore the type of galaxies
responsible for the reionization of the universe.

Ultra-High-Energy (UHE) ($E>100 $TeV) Extensive Air Showers (EASs) have been
monitored for a period of five years (1997-2003), using a small array of
scintillation detectors in Tehran, Iran. The data have been analyzed to take in
to account of the dependence of source counts on zenith angle. Because of
varying thickness of the overlaying atmosphere, the shower count rate is
extremely dependent on zenith angle. During a calendar year different sources
come in the field of view of the array at varying zenith angles and have
different effective observation time equivalent to zenith in a day. High energy
gamma-ray sources from the EGRET third catalogue where observed and the data
were analyzed using an excess method. Upper limits were obtained for 10 EGRET
sources. Then we investigated the EAS event rates for these 10 sources and
obtained a flux for each of them using parameters of our experiment results and
simulations. Finally we investigated the gamma-ray spectrum in the UHE range
using these fluxes with reported fluxes of the EGRET sources.

In this work, we explore the intersection of mathematical concepts related to bodies and cosine transforms. We aim to provide a deeper understanding of these concepts by developing a more generalized approach to the study of their intersections.

Our key idea is to extend the concepts of center and radius of a body to higher dimensions, and to introduce an analog for these concepts in the context of cosine transforms. This allows us to connect the two ideas in a more fundamental way, and provides a new way to study the geometry of bodies and the properties of cosine transforms.

Our methodology involves a careful study of the existing literature on bodies and cosine transforms, as well as a detailed analysis of the mathematical structures underlying these concepts. We then use these insights to develop new theories and techniques that allow us to explore the intersections of these concepts in greater depth.

The motivation for our research comes from the desire to understand the deep connections between different areas of mathematics. By developing a more generalized approach to the study of bodies and cosine transforms, we hope to gain new insights into the nature of these concepts and their fundamental properties. This, in turn, could lead to new applications in fields such as computer science, physics, and engineering.

In this work, we investigate the formation of globular cluster systems (GCSs) in massive elliptical galaxies by studying their multimodality from radial variations of their stellar populations. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that GCSs are invaluable probes for studying the assembly histories of their host galaxies, as well as the processes driving galaxy formation and evolution. Furthermore, understanding the formation and evolution of GCSs is crucial for enabling a deeper understanding of the hierarchical growth of massive galaxies. 

Using data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey, we analyzed the radial color and spectral properties of GCSs in a sample of 10 massive elliptical galaxies. We found that the majority of these galaxies exhibited a clear bimodality in their GCSs, with a blue and red subpopulation, as well as a minority with more complex multimodality. Furthermore, our analysis revealed that the radial distributions of the blue and red subpopulations were significantly different, with red clusters being more centrally concentrated.

The results obtained in this work shed light on the mechanisms responsible for the formation of GCSs, and their relationship with the larger-scale properties of their host galaxies. Additionally, our work sets the foundation for future studies on the detailed kinematics and chemical abundances of GCSs, which are essential for fully understanding their formation and evolution. Ultimately, our research aims to contribute to a deeper understanding of the complex processes driving galaxy formation and evolution in the Universe.

In this paper, we present the measurement of the top-quark mass using missing $E_T$+jets events with secondary vertex $b$-tagging at the CDF II particle collider. The top-quark is the heaviest known elementary particle and its mass is a fundamental parameter in the Standard Model of particle physics. Precise measurement of the top-quark mass is crucial to test the consistency of the Standard Model and to search for new physics beyond it.

Our goal was to measure the top-quark mass using a novel method consisting of selecting events that have missing transverse energy (MET) and at least one jet that contains a secondary vertex identified as a $b$-jet. We analyzed data collected in proton-antiproton collisions at $\sqrt{s} = 1.96$ TeV by the CDF II detector, located at the Fermilab Tevatron accelerator.

The key idea of our analysis was to construct a template of the top-quark mass distribution by fitting the MET distribution in data and comparing it to the prediction from simulation. We used a Bayesian approach to extract the top-quark mass and its uncertainty. Our method has the advantage of being less sensitive to the jet energy scale and resolution uncertainties, compared to traditional top-quark mass measurements.

Our result for the top-quark mass is $172.8 \pm 2.2 \mathrm{(stat.)} \pm 1.5 \mathrm{(syst.)}$ GeV/$c^2$, which is one of the most precise measurements to date. This measurement contributes to the global effort to improve the knowledge of the top-quark mass and to search for new physics phenomena.

Context: Cool stars, companions to compact objects, are known to show Li
abundances which are high compared to field stars of the same spectral type,
which are heavily Li depleted. This may be due either to Li production or Li
preservation in these systems. Aims: To measure the lithium isotopic ratio in
the companion star of the neutron star X-ray binary Cen X-4. Method: We use
UVES spectra obtained in years 2000 and 2004 around the orbital quadratures.
The spectra are analysed with spectrum synthesis techniques and the errors
estimated with Monte Carlo simulations. Results: We measure A(Li)=2.87+-0.20
and 6Li/7Li = 0.12+0.08-0.05 at 68% confidence level. We also present updated
system parameters with a refined determination of the orbital period and
component masses i.e. 1.14+-0.45 Msun and 0.23+-0.10 Msun for the neutron star
and companion, respectively. Conclusions: In our view the low level of 6Li
favours Li preservation scenarios, although Li production mechanisms cannot be
ruled out. In the case of preservation, no Li is freshly created in the binary,
but the tidally-locked companion has preserved its original Li by some
mechanism, possibly inhibited destruction due to its fast rotation.

In this work, we present a cross-layer optimization framework for multi-input multi-output (MIMO)-based mesh networks with Gaussian vector broadcast channels. The motivation for this research stems from the need to improve network capacity and transmission efficiency in wireless mesh networks that are subject to interference and fading. 

The proposed optimization framework is based on a joint design of physical layer and network layer parameters, which exploit the MIMO gain to enhance the network performance. The aim of the optimization is to maximize the network throughput while satisfying the quality of service requirements of users and minimizing the interference between nodes. 

Our analysis shows that the proposed framework outperforms existing solutions in terms of network throughput, packet delivery ratio, and energy efficiency. In particular, we demonstrate that by using MIMO techniques, the network can achieve higher throughput and lower transmit power compared to non-MIMO based networks. Furthermore, the proposed optimization algorithm can adaptively adjust the transmission rate of each node to improve overall network performance.

Overall, our results highlight the effectiveness of cross-layer optimization and MIMO technologies for improving the performance of wireless mesh networks in the presence of interference and fading. This work has significant implications for real-world wireless mesh networks, particularly in urban settings where interference is a significant challenge.

In this work, we present K-band imaging of host galaxies of strong CaII absorbers at redshifts around 1. Our primary motivation for this research is to understand the nature and origin of these absorbers, which are believed to arise from neutral gas clouds along the line of sight to background quasars. Previous studies have shown that a significant fraction of these absorbers are associated with galaxies, and may therefore provide insight into galaxy formation and evolution. 

Our imaging data show that the CaII absorber galaxies have a range of sizes, luminosities, and morphologies, consistent with a diverse population of galaxies. However, we find that the majority of them are relatively compact, with effective radii of only a few kpc. This is in contrast to typical star-forming galaxies at similar redshifts, which are more extended. 

These results suggest that the CaII absorber galaxies may be an earlier stage of galaxy formation, perhaps still in the process of assembling their stellar populations. Alternatively, they may represent a population of more compact galaxies that is not commonly observed in the general galaxy population. Our work contributes to a broader understanding of galaxy formation and evolution, and highlights the importance of studying the host galaxies of strong absorption systems.

Motivated by recent experiments on BaCuSi2O6, we investigate magnetic
excitations and quantum phase transitions of layered dimer magnets with
inter-layer frustration. We consider two scenarios, (A) a lattice with one
dimer per unit cell and perfect inter-layer frustration, and (B) an enlarged
unit cell with inequivalent layers, with and without perfect frustration. In
all situations, the critical behavior at asymptotically low temperatures is
three-dimensional, but the corresponding crossover scale may be tiny. Magnetic
ordering in case (B) can be discussed in terms of two condensates; remarkably,
perfect frustration renders the proximity effect ineffective. Then, the
ordering transition will be generically split, with clear signatures in
measurable properties. Using a generalized bond-operator method, we calculate
the low-temperature magnetic properties in the paramagnetic and
antiferromagnetic phases. Based on the available experimental data on
BaCuSi2O6, we propose that scenario (B) with inequivalent layers and imperfect
frustration is realized in this material, likely with an additional modulation
of the inter-layer couling along the c axis.

In our research, we investigated the infrared environments of masers associated with star formation, with an aim to understand the mechanisms of star formation and the relationship between masers and star-forming regions. Our study was motivated by the fact that masers, which are regions of intense and coherent radiation, are commonly found in star-forming regions, and can provide information about the physical conditions of these regions. However, the exact nature of this relationship is not well understood, and the infrared environments of masers have not been extensively studied.

To address this problem, we analyzed infrared data from the Spitzer Space Telescope for a sample of masers associated with star-forming regions, and used statistical and modeling techniques to relate the masers to their surrounding environments. Our results show that masers are often associated with warm dust and gas in star-forming regions, and that the properties of the masers are related to the strength and temperature of the surrounding radiation field. This suggests that masers can provide a probe of the physical conditions in star-forming regions, and may play an important role in the process of star formation.

Our study provides new insights into the relationship between masers and star-forming regions, and highlights the potential of masers as a tool for investigating the physical processes of star formation.

In our work, we investigated the phenomenon of entanglement in a Jaynes-Cummings model with two atoms and two photon modes. The problem this work attempts to solve is to understand the dynamics of entanglement in a complex quantum system. This is an important area of research as entanglement is a key feature of quantum mechanics and plays a crucial role in quantum computation and communication. 

Our approach was to use the Jaynes-Cummings model, which describes the interaction between a two-level atom and a single mode of the electromagnetic field, in combination with two identical atoms and two photon modes. We then used the concept of the concurrence to quantify the amount of entanglement in the system. 

The key idea behind our work was to explore the role that different parameters, such as the atomic detuning and the coupling strength between the atoms, play in the entanglement dynamics. Our methodology involved theoretical analysis and numerical simulations of the model.

Our results show that entanglement can be sustained in the system for a long time, provided the atoms are initially prepared in an entangled state. We found that the degree of entanglement is highly sensitive to the initial conditions and the parameters of the model. Our work provides insights into the complex dynamics of entanglement in multi-atom Jaynes-Cummings models, which could be useful in the development of future quantum technologies.

We selected a mass-limited sample of 4048 objects from the VIMOS VLT Deep
Survey in the redshift interval 0.5<z<1.3. We used the amplitude of the 4000
Balmer break (Dn4000) to separate the galaxy population and the EW[OII]3727
line as proxy for the star formation activity. We discuss to what extent
stellar mass drives galaxy evolution, showing for the first time the interplay
between stellar ages and stellar masses over the past 8Gyr. Low-mass galaxies
have small Dn4000 and at increasing stellar mass, the galaxy distribution moves
to higher Dn4000 values as observed in the local Universe. As cosmic time goes
by, we witness an increasing abundance of massive spectroscopically ET systems
at the expense of the LT systems. This spectral transformation is a process
started at early epochs and continuing efficiently down to the local Universe.
This is confirmed by the evolution of our type-dependent stellar mass function.
The underlying stellar ages of LT galaxies apparently do not show evolution,
likely as a result of a continuous formation of new stars. All star formation
activity indicators consistently point towards a star formation history peaked
in the past for massive galaxies, with little or no residual star formation
taking place in the most recent epochs. The activity and efficiency of forming
stars are mechanisms that depend on stellar mass, and the mass assembly becomes
progressively less efficient in massive systems as time elapses. The concepts
of star formation downsizing and mass assembly downsizing describe a single
scenario that has a top-down evolutionary pattern. The role of (dry) merging
events seems to be only marginal at z<1.3, as our estimated efficiency in
stellar mass assembly can possibly account for the progressive accumulation of
passively evolving galaxies.

In this research work, we investigate the validity of $\Lambda \neq 0$ FLRW models by using the Angular-Diameter-Distance-Maximum (ADDM) and its redshift as constraints. The motivation for this research is to understand the behavior of our universe, which requires the correct theoretical models. The $\Lambda$CDM model has been successful in describing the large-scale structure of the universe, but it assumes a cosmological constant that introduces a number of inconsistencies in our current understanding of astrophysics. Therefore, it is important to investigate alternative models that do not include a cosmological constant.

Our investigation involves analyzing Type Ia supernovae data along with BAO and CMB data to constrain the parameters of $\Lambda \neq 0$ FLRW models. We obtain a value of 70.6 km/s/Mpc for the Hubble parameter, which is consistent with previous studies. Additionally, we find that our results favor a non-zero value for the cosmological constant, but with a smaller magnitude than that predicted by the $\Lambda$CDM model.

The main problem that this work attempts to solve is to provide an alternative cosmological model that is consistent with observational data and does not require a cosmological constant. Our results provide support for $\Lambda \neq 0$ FLRW models, and future studies could further investigate the implications of these models. Overall, this work contributes to our understanding of the nature of our universe and its evolution over time.

Recently, the collisionless expansion of spherical nanoplasmas has been
analyzed with a new ergodic model, clarifying the transition from
hydrodynamic-like to Coulomb-explosion regimes, and providing accurate laws for
the relevant features of the phenomenon. A complete derivation of the model is
here presented. The important issue of the self-consistent initial conditions
is addressed by analyzing the initial charging transient due to the electron
expansion, in the approximation of immobile ions. A comparison among different
kinetic models for the expansion is presented, showing that the ergodic model
provides a simplified description, which retains the essential information on
the electron distribution, in particular, the energy spectrum. Results are
presented for a wide range of initial conditions (determined from a single
dimensionless parameter), in excellent agreement with calculations from the
exact Vlasov-Poisson theory, thus providing a complete and detailed
characterization of all the stages of the expansion.

In this work, we investigate the effective drift velocity of large-scale magnetic fields in a two-dimensional magnetohydrodynamic turbulence with a focus on nonlinear turbulent magnetic diffusion. The motivation for this research stems from the importance of magnetic fields in astrophysical and plasma systems. For instance, magnetic fields play a key role in the dynamics of galaxies and the transport of energy in the solar wind.

To solve this problem, we employ a theoretical framework that accounts for the interaction between the magnetic and velocity fields in the turbulence. Our analytical approach provides a comprehensive understanding of the underlying physical mechanisms driving the evolution of the magnetic fields and their associated effective drift velocity. In particular, we find that nonlinear turbulent magnetic diffusion significantly affects the effective drift velocity of the magnetic field, which has important implications for the transport of energy and matter in astrophysical systems.

Our study sheds light on the complex interplay between magnetic fields and turbulence and provides valuable insights into the dynamics of astrophysical and plasma systems. The results obtained have important implications for the interpretation of observational data and the development of predictive models for the evolution of magnetic fields in such systems.

In this manuscript we investigate the capabilities of the Discrete Dipole
Approximation (DDA) to simulate scattering from particles that are much larger
than the wavelength of the incident light, and describe an optimized publicly
available DDA computer program that processes the large number of dipoles
required for such simulations. Numerical simulations of light scattering by
spheres with size parameters x up to 160 and 40 for refractive index m=1.05 and
2 respectively are presented and compared with exact results of the Mie theory.
Errors of both integral and angle-resolved scattering quantities generally
increase with m and show no systematic dependence on x. Computational times
increase steeply with both x and m, reaching values of more than 2 weeks on a
cluster of 64 processors. The main distinctive feature of the computer program
is the ability to parallelize a single DDA simulation over a cluster of
computers, which allows it to simulate light scattering by very large
particles, like the ones that are considered in this manuscript. Current
limitations and possible ways for improvement are discussed.

We study the hydrodynamic limit for some conservative particle systems with
degenerate rates, namely with nearest neighbor exchange rates which vanish for
certain configurations. These models belong to the class of {\sl kinetically
constrained lattice gases} (KCLG) which have been introduced and intensively
studied in physics literature as simple models for the liquid/glass transition.
Due to the degeneracy of rates for KCLG there exists {\sl blocked
configurations} which do not evolve under the dynamics and in general the
hyperplanes of configurations with a fixed number of particles can be
decomposed into different irreducible sets. As a consequence, both the Entropy
and Relative Entropy method cannot be straightforwardly applied to prove the
hydrodynamic limit. In particular, some care should be put when proving the One
and Two block Lemmas which guarantee local convergence to equilibrium. We show
that, for initial profiles smooth enough and bounded away from zero and one,
the macroscopic density profile for our KCLG evolves under the diffusive time
scaling according to the porous medium equation. Then we prove the same result
for more general profiles for a slightly perturbed dynamics obtained by adding
jumps of the Symmetric Simple Exclusion. The role of the latter is to remove
the degeneracy of rates and at the same time they are properly slowed down in
order not to change the macroscopic behavior. The equilibrium fluctuations and
the magnitude of the spectral gap for this perturbed model are also obtained.

We estimate the dust temperatures of the clumps in the rho Oph main cloud
taking into account the 3D geometry of the region, and external heating from
the interstellar radiation field and from HD147879,a nearby luminous B2V star,
which is believed to dominate the radiation field in the region. We find that
the regions where prestellar cores are observed (i.e. at optical visual
extinctions >7 mag) are colder than ~10-11K. These dust temperatures are
smaller than those which previous studies of the same region have assumed. We
use the new dust temperatures to estimate the masses of the prestellar cores in
the rho Oph main cloud from mm observations, and we find core masses that are
larger than previous estimates by a factor of ~2-3. This affects the core mass
function (CMF) of the region; we find that the mass at which the core mass
spectrum steepens from a slope alpha~1.5 to a slope alpha~2.5 has moved from
~0.5 Msun to ~1Msun. In contrast with the CMF in other star forming regions
(e.g. Orion), there is no indication for a turnover down to the completeness
limit (~0.2Msun), but the CMF may flatten at around ~0.4Msun. We generalize our
results to the prestellar cores in Taurus and in Orion. In Taurus the ambient
radiation field heating the prestellar cores is believed to be weaker than than
that in rho Oph. Hence, the dust temperatures of the cores in Taurus are
expected to be below ~ 10-11 K.In Orion the radiation field is believed to be
10^3 times stronger than the standard interstellar radiation field. Based on
this assumption we estimate that the dust temperatures of the prestellar cores
in Orion are around ~20-30K.

In spatially distributed multiuser antenna systems, the received signal
contains multiple carrier-frequency offsets (CFOs) arising from mismatch
between the oscillators of transmitters and receivers. This results in a
time-varying rotation of the data constellation, which needs to be compensated
at the receiver before symbol recovery. In this paper, a new approach for blind
CFO estimation and symbol recovery is proposed. The received base-band signal
is over-sampled, and its polyphase components are used to formulate a virtual
Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) problem. By applying blind MIMO system
estimation techniques, the system response can be estimated and decoupled
versions of the user symbols can be recovered, each one of which contains a
distinct CFO. By applying a decision feedback Phase Lock Loop (PLL), the CFO
can be mitigated and the transmitted symbols can be recovered. The estimated
MIMO system response provides information about the CFOs that can be used to
initialize the PLL, speed up its convergence, and avoid ambiguities usually
linked with PLL.

Gravitational waves from the inspiral and coalescence of supermassive
black-hole (SMBH) binaries with masses ~10^6 Msun are likely to be among the
strongest sources for the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA). We
describe a three-stage data-analysis pipeline designed to search for and
measure the parameters of SMBH binaries in LISA data. The first stage uses a
time-frequency track-search method to search for inspiral signals and provide a
coarse estimate of the black-hole masses m_1, m_2 and of the coalescence time
of the binary t_c. The second stage uses a sequence of matched-filter template
banks, seeded by the first stage, to improve the measurement accuracy of the
masses and coalescence time. Finally, a Markov Chain Monte Carlo search is used
to estimate all nine physical parameters of the binary. Using results from the
second stage substantially shortens the Markov Chain burn-in time and allows us
to determine the number of SMBH-binary signals in the data before starting
parameter estimation. We demonstrate our analysis pipeline using simulated data
from the first LISA Mock Data Challenge. We discuss our plan for improving this
pipeline and the challenges that will be faced in real LISA data analysis.

In this research work, we present the measurement of the aerosol phase function using the fluorescence detector of the Pierre Auger Observatory. The main objective of this study was to improve our understanding of the atmosphere's optical properties and to characterize the aerosol scattering properties, which directly impact the reliability and accuracy of the fluorescence technique.

Our methodology involved collecting fluorescence data from atmospheric nitrogen molecules excited by cosmic ray showers. We analyzed the spectral dependency of the fluorescence light intensity for different zenith angles, and using that information, we extracted the aerosol phase function. Our measurement of the aerosol phase function was found to be consistent with the modified Gamma distribution model within the confidence intervals, and we computed the aerosol optical depth as well.

Our findings offer valuable insights into the aerosol scattering properties for the fluorescence technique used in the Pierre Auger Observatory, which will help in understanding and reducing the systematic uncertainties in the energy estimation of ultra-high energy cosmic ray events detected by the observatory. This research thus makes a significant contribution towards improving the accuracy and reliability of cosmic ray measurements, ultimately advancing our understanding of the universe.

In this study, we present a comprehensive spectral analysis of long gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) from the Swift satellite with known redshift values. GRBs are among the most energetic phenomena in the universe and are believed to be associated with the death of massive stars. One of the main objectives of this work is to investigate the nature of the prompt emission and its underlying physical processes in the most energetic events in the universe.

We analyzed the spectral properties of 35 long GRBs using the Band function and the empirical models of the photon spectra, including the cutoff power-law model and the Band+blackbody model. We found that the majority of the spectra can be well described by the Band function, and some bursts show a significant deviation from this model, indicating the possible existence of alternative radiative mechanisms.

Our results also reveal some correlations between the spectral parameters, like the peak energy and the fluence or luminosity, which suggest an intrinsic link between the spectral properties and the physics of GRBs. Our study provides valuable insights into the underlying mechanisms of GRB radiation and helps understand the sources' physical properties. It also highlights the importance of studying the prompt emission of GRBs as a powerful tool for investigating fundamental physics and astrophysical phenomena.

In this work, we present constraints on the heavy element abundance in the solar interior through the analysis of frequency separation ratios of low-degree p modes. The heavy element abundance is important for understanding the formation and the evolution of stars, as well as the nucleosynthesis of elements. Previous studies have shown that the solar heavy element abundance is difficult to determine, as it is affected by uncertainties in the input physics used in the solar models and by the limited accuracy of solar observations. In this work, we use the frequency separation ratios of low-degree p modes to reduce the uncertainties in the input physics of the solar models, and to probe the solar heavy element abundance with greater precision.

We analyze p modes that are observed by the Solar Dynamics Observatory, and we use an asteroseismic approach to determine the frequency separation ratios. We then use a Bayesian analysis to infer the heavy element abundance from the frequency separation ratios. Our analysis suggests that the solar heavy element abundance is lower than what is predicted by the standard solar model. Our results are consistent with recent studies that indicate a lower heavy element abundance in the solar atmosphere. The methodology presented in this work can be applied to other solar-like stars to improve our understanding of their internal structure and chemical properties.

Our work investigates the complex phenomena of pulsar radiation belts and transient radio emission. The main focus is on the detection and characterization of pulsar radio emission and the understanding of the physical processes that produce these emissions. Pulsars are rapidly rotating, highly magnetized neutron stars that emit beams of radio waves. The radio waves can travel millions of light-years and are useful for studying cosmic objects and their physical properties.

We use data from the Low-Frequency Array (LOFAR) radio telescope to study the pulsar radiation belts and transient radio emission. Our methodology involves analyzing the signal data to identify the different emission components and their temporal and spectral characteristics. By analyzing the pulsar radiation belts and transient radio emission properties, we can study the interactions between the pulsar magnetosphere and the surrounding medium.

The motivation for this research is to gain a better understanding of the astrophysical phenomena associated with pulsars, which are crucial probes of the properties of ultra-dense matter. The problem we attempt to solve is to better understand how these processes produce radio emissions and how they interact with pulsar magnetospheres.

Our results provide a better understanding of the physical processes that are involved in pulsar radiation belts and transient radio emission. This research contributes to the development of a more complete picture of the universe's evolution and its physical processes.

We present a five (u*,g',r',i',z') band optical photometry catalog of the
sources in the North Ecliptic Pole (NEP) region based on deep observations made
with MegaCam at CFHT. The source catalog covers about 2 square degree area
centered at the NEP and reaches depths of about 26 mag for u*, g', r' bands,
about 25 mag for i' band, and about 24 mag for z' band (4 sigma detection over
an 1 arcsec aperture). The total number of cataloged sources brighter than r'=
23 mag is about 56,000 including both point sources and extended sources. From
the investigation of photometric properties using the color-magnitude diagrams
and color-color diagrams, we have found that the colors of extended sources are
mostly (u*-r') < 3.0 and (g'-z') > 0.5. This can be used to separate the
extended sources from the point sources reliably, even for the faint source
domain where typical morphological classification schemes hardly work
efficiently. We have derived an empirical color-redshift relation of the red
sequence galaxies using the Sloan Digital Sky Survey data. By applying this
relation to our photometry catalog and searching for any spatial overdensities,
we have found two galaxy clusters and one nearby galaxy group.

We analyze the local field of stellar tangential velocities for a sample of
$42 339$ non-binary Hipparcos stars with accurate parallaxes, using a vector
spherical harmonic formalism. We derive simple relations between the parameters
of the classical linear model (Ogorodnikov-Milne) of the local systemic field
and low-degree terms of the general vector harmonic decomposition. Taking
advantage of these relationships we determine the solar velocity with respect
to the local stars of $(V_X,V_Y,V_Z)=(10.5, 18.5, 7.3)\pm 0.1$ \kms. The Oort's
parameters determined by a straightforward least-squares adjustment in vector
spherical harmonics, are $A=14.0\pm 1.4$, $B=-13.1\pm 1.2$, $K=1.1\pm 1.8$, and
$C=-2.9\pm 1.4$ \kmspc. We find a few statistically significant higher degree
harmonic terms, which do not correspond to any parameters in the classical
linear model. One of them, a third-degree electric harmonic, is tentatively
explained as the response to a negative linear gradient of rotation velocity
with distance from the Galactic plane, which we estimate at $\sim -20$ \kmspc.
The most unexpected and unexplained term within the Ogorodnikov-Milne model is
the first-degree magnetic harmonic representing a rigid rotation of the stellar
field about the axis $-Y$ pointing opposite to the direction of rotation. This
harmonic comes out with a statistically robust coefficient $6.2 \pm 0.9$
\kmspc, and is also present in the velocity field of more distant stars. The
ensuing upward vertical motion of stars in the general direction of the
Galactic center and the downward motion in the anticenter direction are
opposite to the vector field expected from the stationary Galactic warp model.

In this paper, we present our investigation into the highly variable intra-day variation (IDV) source J1128+592. Our study aimed to characterize the variability of this source at centimeter wavelengths and explain the physical mechanism behind it. We used data from the Very Large Array (VLA) archives at 4.9 GHz and 8.4 GHz to analyze the source's variability. We assessed the temporal variations of J1128+592 by using statistical analysis tools. We identified a clear dominant timescale in the IDV time series, which allowed us to conclude that the variability is due to scintillation in the interstellar medium. We also found that the scintillation timescale changes over time, leading to variations in the observed variability amplitude.

Our results underline the importance of studying the variability of radio sources, particularly of those that exhibit strong IDV, in providing insights into the interstellar medium structure and its turbulence. This research adds to the understanding of the mechanisms of IDV variability in radio sources and provides further basis for exploring the physics of the interstellar medium. Our methodology can also be extended to studying other sources with similar variability characteristics.

In this paper, we investigate the future projections of the local large-scale structure of the universe, with an emphasis on the roles of dark matter and dark energy. The motivation behind this research is to explore the current state and potential evolution of the local universe in light of recent observational and theoretical advancements. Specifically, we aim to provide insights into the fate of our local group of galaxies, which is the Milky Way, Andromeda, and Triangulum galaxies. 

The problem this work attempts to solve is the lack of knowledge about the ultimate fate of the local large-scale structure and how it is influenced by dark matter and dark energy. To address this problem, we analyze data from large cosmological simulations and examine the effects of gravitational interactions between galaxies, dark matter, and dark energy on the evolution of the structure.

The key ideas of our work are first, that the gravitational dynamics of dark matter and galaxies play a crucial role in shaping the large-scale structure of the universe. Second, dark energy has a repulsive effect that acts against gravity. Thus, the combination of dark matter and dark energy determines the ultimate fate of the universe on larger scales. Our methodology involves running simulations with advanced computational techniques to model the evolution of the local universe over billions of years.

In conclusion, our work provides a comprehensive perspective on the future of the local large-scale structure of the universe, with an emphasis on the roles of dark matter and dark energy. The integration of theoretical advances and observational data provides unique insights into the complex interplay between dark matter, galaxies, and dark energy, shedding light on the ultimate fate of our local universe.

We have presented a detailed study of twist-2 and twist-3 light-cone
distribution amplitudes of $1^3P_1$ and $1^1P_1$ axial-vector mesons, based on
QCD conformal partial wave expansion. Applying equations of motion, the
twist-three two-parton light-cone distribution amplitudes can be expressed in
terms of leading-twist and twist-three three-parton light-cone distribution
amplitudes. The relevant G-parity invariant and violating parameters,
containing the corrections due to the SU(3) breaking effects, are evaluated
from the QCD sum rule method. The results for axial-vector decay constants of
$1^3P_1$ states are presented. The values of tensor decay constants and
Gegenbauer moments of the leading twist distribution amplitudes for $1^1P_1$
states are updated. Using Gell-Mann-Okubo mass formula, the mixing angle for
the $f_8$ and $f_1$ of $1^3P_1$ states is $\theta_{^3P_1}\sim 38^\circ$, and
that for $h_8$ and $h_1$ of $1^1P_1$ states is $\theta_{^1P_1}\sim 10^\circ$.
The detailed properties for physical states $f_1(1285), f_1(1420), h_1(1170)$,
and $h_1(1380)$ are given. Assuming the mixing angle between $K_{1A}$ and
$K_{1B}$ to be $\theta_K=45^\circ$ or $-45^\circ$, we also give the detailed
study for $K_1(1270)$ and $K_1(1400)$. Using the conformal partial wave
expansion, we obtain the models for light-cone distribution amplitudes,
containing contributions up to conformal spin 9/2. It is interesting to note
that some distribution amplitudes have significant asymmetric behaviors, which
should be phenomenologically attractive.

We consider a system of d non-linear stochastic heat equations in spatial
dimension 1 driven by d-dimensional space-time white noise. The non-linearities
appear both as additive drift terms and as multipliers of the noise. Using
techniques of Malliavin calculus, we establish upper and lower bounds on the
one-point density of the solution u(t,x), and upper bounds of Gaussian-type on
the two-point density of (u(s,y),u(t,x)). In particular, this estimate
quantifies how this density degenerates as (s,y) converges to (t,x). From these
results, we deduce upper and lower bounds on hitting probabilities of the
process {u(t,x)}_{t \in \mathbb{R}_+, x \in [0,1]}, in terms of respectively
Hausdorff measure and Newtonian capacity. These estimates make it possible to
show that points are polar when d >6 and are not polar when d<6. We also show
that the Hausdorff dimension of the range of the process is 6 when d>6, and
give analogous results for the processes t \mapsto u(t,x) and x \mapsto u(t,x).
Finally, we obtain the values of the Hausdorff dimensions of the level sets of
these processes.

Security of information has become a major issue during the last decades. New
algorithms based on chaotic maps were suggested for protection of different
types of multimedia data, especially digital images and videos in this period.
However, many of them fundamentally were flawed by a lack of robustness and
security. For getting higher security and higher complexity, in the current
paper, we introduce a new kind of symmetric key block cipher algorithm that is
based on \emph{tripled chaotic maps}. In this algorithm, the utilization of two
coupling parameters, as well as the increased complexity of the cryptosystem,
make a contribution to the development of cryptosystem with higher security. In
order to increase the security of the proposed algorithm, the size of key space
and the computational complexity of the coupling parameters should be increased
as well. Both the theoretical and experimental results state that the proposed
algorithm has many capabilities such as acceptable speed and complexity in the
algorithm due to the existence of two coupling parameter and high security.
Note that the ciphertext has a flat distribution and has the same size as the
plaintext. Therefore, it is suitable for practical use in secure
communications.

The luminosity and the spectral distribution of the afterglow of GRB 031203
have been presented within our theoretical framework, which envisages the GRB
structure as composed by a proper-GRB, emitted at the transparency of an
electron-positron plasma with suitable baryon loading, and an afterglow
comprising the "prompt emission" as due to external shocks. In addition to the
GRB emission, there appears to be a prolonged soft X-Ray emission lasting for
10^6-10^7 seconds followed by an exponential decay. This additional source has
been called by us URCA-3. It is urgent to establish if this component is
related to the GRB or to the Supernova (SN). In this second case, there are two
possibilities: either the interaction of the SN ejecta with the interstellar
medium or, possibly, the cooling of a young neutron star formed in the SN
2003lw process. The analogies and the differences between this triptych GRB
031203 / SN 2003lw / URCA-3 and the corresponding ones GRB 980425 / SN 1998bw /
URCA-1 and GRB 030329 / SN 2003dh / URCA-2, as well as GRB 060218 / SN 2006aj
are discussed.

We investigate a lattice model of polymers where the nearest-neighbour
monomer-monomer interaction strengths differ according to whether the local
configurations have so-called ``hydrogen-like'' formations or not. If the
interaction strengths are all the same then the classical $\theta$-point
collapse transition occurs on lowering the temperature, and the polymer enters
the isotropic liquid-drop phase known as the collapsed globule. On the other
hand, strongly favouring the hydrogen-like interactions give rise to an
anisotropic folded (solid-like) phase on lowering the temperature. We use Monte
Carlo simulations up to a length of 256 to map out the phase diagram in the
plane of parameters and determine the order of the associated phase
transitions. We discuss the connections to semi-flexible polymers and other
polymer models. Importantly, we demonstrate that for a range of energy
parameters two phase transitions occur on lowering the temperature, the second
being a transition from the globule state to the crystal state. We argue from
our data that this globule-to-crystal transition is continuous in two
dimensions in accord with field-theory arguments concerning Hamiltonian walks,
but is first order in three dimensions.

In this review, we explore the concept of dynamical 3-space, its key ideas, and methodology. At its core, dynamical 3-space aims to unify Einstein's theory of relativity with quantum mechanics by positing that a third spatial dimension exists, which is dynamic and constantly changing. We evaluate the theoretical framework underlying this idea, reviewing its implications for key physical phenomena, such as gravity, and explore its potential applications for understanding fundamental aspects of the universe, including dark matter and dark energy.

The motivation for this research lies in the fact that our current understanding of the universe's physical laws remains incomplete, particularly in the context of the fundamental forces of nature and their relationship with space and time. There are important problems such as the issue of quantum gravity that continues to elude physicists. The dynamical 3-space conception offers a promising framework that may help bridge some of these gaps and provide a more coherent, fundamental theoretical picture of the universe.

We evaluate various proponents of dynamical 3-space, examining their methodological approaches and presenting their key insights. Additionally, we highlight the challenges and limitations associated with this framework, such as the difficulties in constructing accurate predictive models or experimental tests. Nonetheless, we argue that dynamical 3-space remains an intriguing and important avenue for further research, with the potential to expand our understanding of the universe's fundamental nature in exciting and meaningful ways.

We outline a dynamical dark energy scenario whose signatures may be
simultaneously tested by astronomical observations and laboratory experiments.
The dark energy is a field with slightly sub-gravitational couplings to matter,
a logarithmic self-interaction potential with a scale tuned to $\sim 10^{-3}
{\rm eV}$, as is usual in quintessence models, and an effective mass $m_\phi$
influenced by the environmental energy density. Its forces may be suppressed
just below the current bounds by the chameleon-like mimicry, whereby only outer
layers of mass distributions, of thickness $1/m_\phi$, give off appreciable
long range forces. After inflation and reheating, the field is relativistic,
and attains a Planckian expectation value before Hubble friction freezes it.
This can make gravity in space slightly stronger than on Earth. During the
matter era, interactions with nonrelativistic matter dig a minimum close to the
Planck scale. However, due to its sub-gravitational matter couplings the field
will linger away from this minimum until the matter energy density dips below
$\sim 10^{-12} {\rm eV}^4$. Then it starts to roll to the minimum, driving a
period of cosmic acceleration. Among the signatures of this scenario may be
dark energy equation of state $w \ne -1$, stronger gravity in dilute mediums,
that may influence BBN and appear as an excess of dark matter, and
sub-millimeter corrections to Newton's law, close to the present laboratory
limits.

In this paper, we present a comprehensive analysis of the local stellar velocity field via vector spherical harmonics. The problem that this work attempts to solve is the characterization of the kinematical properties of stars in the Galactic neighborhood. The motivation for this research stems from the need to obtain a better understanding of the dynamical structure and evolution of the Milky Way, including the distribution of dark matter.

We analyzed the data from the Gaia-ESO survey and applied a vector spherical harmonics decomposition to the velocity field in the Solar neighborhood. Our approach allowed us to separate the velocity field into its radial, azimuthal, and polar components and study their properties. We found that the velocity field exhibits both coherent motion and random fluctuations, with clear signatures of bulk rotation and possible streaming motions on smaller scales. The amplitudes of the modes of the vector harmonics are in agreement with expectations from theoretical models.

Our results provide new insights into the kinematical properties of stars in the Galactic neighborhood and contribute to our understanding of the large-scale structure and evolution of the Milky Way. This work demonstrates the powerful tools that can be applied to study the velocity field of nearby stars and paves the way for future studies of Galactic dynamics using vector spherical harmonics.

In this paper, we investigate the use of antenna combining techniques for multi-input multi-output (MIMO) downlink channels in wireless communication systems. The main thrust of our work is to investigate the impact of spatial correlation and combined diversity on the achievable system capacity and error rate performance of MIMO systems.
To achieve this goal, we first describe the basic principles of antenna combining and present a theoretical framework for its application in MIMO downlink channels. We then propose a simple but effective antenna combining scheme that incorporates the use of multiple antennas at the receiver end. The proposed scheme maximizes the signal-to-noise ratio and reduces the level of interference, resulting in improved system performance.
We validate the efficacy of our proposed technique by comparing its performance against traditional MIMO schemes. Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed antenna combining technique is capable of achieving significant gains in terms of system capacity and error rate performance, particularly when using a small number of antennas. These results suggest that our technique can be used to boost the performance of wireless communication systems operating in MIMO downlink channels and pave the way for more robust and efficient wireless communications in the future.

In this study, we aimed to model the energy dependencies of High-Frequency Quasi-Periodic Oscillations (HFQPOs) observed in Black Hole X-ray Binaries (BHBs). The key idea behind this research was to explore the dependence of HFQPO frequency on the energy of X-ray photons emitted by the BHBs. We attempted to investigate how the QPO frequency changes with varying energy of emitted photons and attempted to understand the physics behind this effect. 

The methodology used was to obtain data through the NICER (Neutron star Interior Composition Explorer) X-ray telescope, which is specially designed to observe high-energy X-ray radiation emitted by Black Holes. We analyzed these data using wavelet and Fourier analysis methods to study the dependence of the QPO frequency on X-ray energy. The problem we aimed to solve was to understand the fundamental physical origin of HFQPOs in BHBs and the factors that affect their properties.

The motivation behind this research was to gain insights into the structure and dynamics of BH accretion disks, which play an essential role in the mechanism behind X-ray emission. By understanding the energy dependencies of HFQPOs, we aim to strengthen our knowledge of the accretion disk physics and gain insights into the dynamics of the disk region, where oscillations often occur. The results of this research can have implications for astrophysics, gravitational-wave astronomy and fundamental physics.

In this paper, we present our investigation on the role of GRB 031203 in clarifying the astrophysical scenario of Gamma Ray Bursts (GRBs). We used a comprehensive approach combining observational data, data analysis techniques and modeling to address key issues surrounding this celestial event. Our results show that GRB 031203 is a unique discovery that has given us vital information about the nature of GRBs and their associated phenomena. We demonstrate that the spectral index of this GRB is consistent with the curvature effect model providing further validation of this physical model of Gamma Ray Bursts. 

Additionally, our study provides evidence that the X-ray radiation from this event supports the importance of reverse shock emission in the overall emission mechanism of GRBs. Crucially, we highlight the potential of GRB 031203 as a diagnostic tool for studying the structure of the progenitor star and binary system. Our work seeks to address persistent scientific concerns around the nature of GRBs, their environment, and their association with astrophysical systems. This study lays a solid foundation for further research that can provide more insight into the complex phenomenon of GRBs.

A model for charge transfer mechanism in YBa2Cu3O6+x high-Tc cuprate based on
critical chain length concept is proposed to account for 60K and 90K plateaus
in Tc(x) dependence. It has been shown, when the statistics of CuO chain
formation was described in terms of two dimensional asymmetric next-to-nearest
neighbor Ising (ASYNNNI) model, that at any constant temperature below the top
of OII phase there exists a uniquely defined value of critical chain length
lcr(T) that yields a constant doping p(x)=const over the regime of OII phase
(related to 60K plateau of Tc(x)), while 90K plateau coincides with the
monotonously increasing p(x) over optimal doping level p=0.16 in the regime of
OI phase. Short length chains (l<lcr(T)), together with the first lcr(T)-2
holes in longer chains (l>lcr(T)), are taken as not capable of attracting
electrons from CuO2 planes.. It is shown that only a part (41%) of the
remaining l-lcr(T)+1 holes in the long chains can capture electrons. The
results obtained indicate that the ASYNNNI model and two-plateaus-like behavior
of Tc(x) in YBa2Cu3O6+x are closely connected.

We continue our program of single-site observations of pulsating subdwarf B
(sdB) stars and present the results of extensive time series photometry of HS
0039+4302 and HS 0444+0458. Both were observed at MDM Observatory during the
fall of 2005. We extend the number of known frequencies for HS 0039+4302 from 4
to 14 and discover one additional frequency for HS 0444+0458, bringing the
total to three. We perform standard tests to search for multiplet structure,
measure amplitude variations, and examine the frequency density to constrain
the mode degree $\ell$.
  Including the two stars in this paper, 23 pulsating sdB stars have received
follow-up observations designed to decipher their pulsation spectra. It is
worth an examination of what has been detected. We compare and contrast the
frequency content in terms of richness and range and the amplitudes with
regards to variability and diversity. We use this information to examine
observational correlations with the proposed $\kappa$ pulsation mechanism as
well as alternative theories.

In this paper, we present a novel approach towards connecting the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) and the International Linear Collider (ILC) with quintessence. Our work focused on investigating the possibility of using quintessence to explain the nature and properties of dark energy, and to bridge the gap between the LHC and ILC experiments. 

Our study was motivated by the lack of a comprehensive understanding of the fundamental nature of dark energy. Despite its significant contribution to the expansion of the universe, the presence and behavior of dark energy remains a mystery. We propose that quintessence offers a promising alternative explanation.

The key idea behind our work is to reconcile the apparent differences between the LHC and ILC experiments by invoking the presence of quintessence. Our approach is grounded in the principles of symmetry breaking and the Higgs mechanism, and we utilize a combination of numerical simulations and theoretical models to investigate the potential connections between these phenomena.

Our findings demonstrate that quintessence can serve as a unifying force that connects the LHC and ILC experiments. We show that quintessence can provide a natural explanation for the observed cosmic acceleration, and that it has the potential to resolve some of the most pressing problems in modern physics. Our work represents a significant step forward in the quest for a universal theory of physics, and we hope that it will inspire further investigation into the nature of quintessence and its role in the universe.

Vortices are pervasive in nature, representing the breakdown of laminar fluid
flow and hence playing a key role in turbulence. The fluid rotation associated
with a vortex can be parameterized by the circulation $\Gamma=\oint {\rm d}{\bf
r}\cdot{\bf v}({\bf r})$ about the vortex, where ${\bf v}({\bf r})$ is the
fluid velocity field. While classical vortices can take any value of
circulation, superfluids are irrotational, and any rotation or angular momentum
is constrained to occur through vortices with quantized circulation. Quantized
vortices also play a key role in the dissipation of transport in superfluids.
In BECs quantized vortices have been observed in several forms, including
single vortices, vortex lattices, and vortex pairs and rings. The recent
observation of quantized vortices in a fermionic gas was taken as a clear
signature of the underlying condensation and superfluidity of fermion pairs. In
addition to BECs, quantized vortices also occur in superfluid Helium, nonlinear
optics, and type-II superconductors.

We present the results of REM near-infrared and optical multiband observations of the blazar PKS2155-304 conducted during 2005. The main aim of this work is to investigate the spectral variability of PKS2155-304 over a wide range of wavelengths and to study how the spectral energy distribution (SED) changes over time. The observed variability in SED is a crucial indicator for physical models of blazars and can provide insight into the emission mechanisms in these objects. 

Our observations were conducted with the REM telescope located at La Silla Observatory in Chile. We obtained simultaneous near-infrared and optical data, which allowed us to construct the SED of PKS2155-304. We find that the SED of PKS2155-304 varies significantly on timescales of days, with the near-infrared and optical emission showing a correlated behavior. We also find that the SED of PKS2155-304 is well-described by a standard one-zone leptonic model that assumes that the synchrotron emission from relativistic electrons is responsible for the observed radiation. 

Our results provide new insights into the nature of blazars and how their emission varies over time. The study of blazars is of great importance for astrophysics and cosmology, as these objects can be used to probe the properties of the intergalactic medium and to study the large-scale structure of the universe.

In this work, we present a unified projection formalism for describing the Al-Pd-Mn quasicrystal and its Xi-approximants with metadislocations. Our aim is to address the challenge of understanding the structural properties of quasicrystals, which exhibit long-range order without periodicity. The complexity and lack of symmetry in quasicrystal structures make it difficult to describe them using conventional crystallographic methods. 
The key idea in our proposed formalism is to project the quasicrystal onto a higher-dimensional space and represent it as a regular crystal with a higher symmetry than the original system. Metadislocations, which are a characteristic feature of quasicrystals and play an important role in their mechanical properties, are also included in this formalism. 
The methodology involves constructing a mathematical model that incorporates the projection of the quasicrystal, the representation of its symmetry in the higher-dimensional space, and the description of metadislocations using topological methods. 
The motivation for this research is twofold. Firstly, it contributes to the fundamental understanding of the Al-Pd-Mn quasicrystalline system, which has been of interest in both materials science and solid-state physics. Secondly, our formalism has practical applications in the design and engineering of quasicrystalline materials, particularly in the development of new alloys with enhanced mechanical properties. Overall, our approach provides a powerful and unified framework for describing quasicrystal structures, opening up avenues for future research in this field.

In this study, we investigate the impact of trehalose, maltose, and sucrose on the structural and dynamical properties of lysozyme using molecular dynamics simulations. The objective of the research is to gain insight into how these three disaccharides interact with lysozyme, a well-known antibacterial enzyme. The study aims to contribute to a better understanding of the molecular mechanisms underlying the stabilization effects of disaccharides on proteins.

We used all-atom molecular dynamics simulations to study lysozyme in the presence of trehalose, maltose, and sucrose molecules. The conformational stability, hydrogen bonding pattern, and flexibility of lysozyme were analyzed in the presence and absence of the three disaccharides.

Our results show that trehalose and sucrose form stronger hydrogen bonds with lysozyme than maltose. Additionally, the presence of trehalose and sucrose increases the conformational stability of lysozyme, while the flexibility of lysozyme decreases. On the other hand, maltose does not affect the structural stability of lysozyme, indicating a weaker interaction.

Our findings provide valuable insights into the stabilizing effects of trehalose, maltose, and sucrose on lysozyme and how they potentially enhance protein stability. This research can serve as a basis for designing novel protein stabilizers and therapeutic agents that exploit the stabilizing effects of disaccharides on biomolecules.

In our work titled "Dirichlet or Potts?", we explore the problem of image segmentation, which involves partitioning an image into several distinct regions. This is a fundamental problem in computer vision and has significant applications in medical imaging, object recognition, and autonomous driving systems. The work attempts to address the question of which model, Dirichlet or Potts, is better suited for image segmentation tasks. 

We first introduce the Dirichlet model, which assumes that the image regions have independent Gaussian distributions. We then present the Potts model, which models neighboring regions as a group with a shared distribution. We compared the performance of these two models on the Berkeley Segmentation Dataset, consisting of natural, real-world images. Our analysis reveals that the Potts model outperforms the Dirichlet model in most cases in terms of the segmentation accuracy.

The key idea behind our work is to evaluate the effectiveness of two widely-used models for image segmentation. Our methodology involves training both models on a set of images and comparing their performance in terms of their ability to accurately segment images. We believe that our results have significant implications for researchers and practitioners in the field of computer vision, who can use these findings to improve their segmentation algorithms. 

In summary, our work "Dirichlet or Potts?" investigates the problem of image segmentation, with the aim of comparing the performance of two popular models. Our results demonstrate that the Potts model outperforms the Dirichlet model, and we anticipate that this work will enable more accurate and efficient segmentation of real-world images in future applications.

We present a detailed analysis of the properties of (65489) Ceto/Phorcys, a binary Centaur orbiting in the outer Solar System. Our study aims to determine the orbit, mass, size, albedo, and density of the system using a combination of observational data and numerical simulations. The motivation for this research lies in the need to understand the physical properties and evolutionary history of Centaurs, a population of small bodies that have been dynamically scattered into their current orbits from the Kuiper belt by the giant planets.

We use observations from the Hubble Space Telescope and ground-based facilities to calculate the system's orbit, while numerical simulations are used to model the binary's tidal evolution and estimate its properties. Our results suggest that (65489) Ceto/Phorcys has a mass of 188±19 km, a size of 168±8 km, and a density of 1.14±0.12 g/cm³. The albedo of the system is found to be 0.059±0.011, consistent with the values found for other Centaurs.

This work provides new insights into the properties of Centaurs, such as the tidal evolution of binary systems and the physical characteristics of these primitive Solar System bodies. Our study demonstrates the importance of combining observational data and numerical simulations to understand the formation and evolution of small celestial bodies in the outer Solar System.

In this work, we present a linear analysis of the order of epitaxial self-assembled quantum dots (QDs) by using a mathematical model based on the reaction-diffusion equation. The problem that we aim to solve is to understand the pattern formation and ordering process of QDs during the epitaxial growth process. We investigate the effects of various factors, such as the surface diffusion coefficient, the nucleation rate, and the growth rate, on the evolution of the QD pattern. In addition, we investigate the influence of the surface material on the ordering of QDs.

The key idea behind our methodology is to use a mathematical model to describe the process of QD formation and ordering. We use numerical simulations to solve the resulting equations and analyze the QD ordering patterns. Our study reveals that the surface material, growth rate, and the ratio of the nucleation rate to diffusion constant greatly affect the ordering behavior of QDs during growth. Additionally, we find that an increase in the growth rate leads to a decrease in the inter-dot spacing. Our findings indicate that the proposed mathematical model can be used to predict the order and spacing of QDs during the epitaxial growth process, which is essential for the development of quantum dot-based devices.

This paper investigates the many-to-one throughput capacity (and by symmetry,
one-to-many throughput capacity) of IEEE 802.11 multi-hop networks. It has
generally been assumed in prior studies that the many-to-one throughput
capacity is upper-bounded by the link capacity L. Throughput capacity L is not
achievable under 802.11. This paper introduces the notion of "canonical
networks", which is a class of regularly-structured networks whose capacities
can be analyzed more easily than unstructured networks. We show that the
throughput capacity of canonical networks under 802.11 has an analytical upper
bound of 3L/4 when the source nodes are two or more hops away from the sink;
and simulated throughputs of 0.690L (0.740L) when the source nodes are many
hops away. We conjecture that 3L/4 is also the upper bound for general
networks. When all links have equal length, 2L/3 can be shown to be the upper
bound for general networks. Our simulations show that 802.11 networks with
random topologies operated with AODV routing can only achieve throughputs far
below the upper bounds. Fortunately, by properly selecting routes near the
gateway (or by properly positioning the relay nodes leading to the gateway) to
fashion after the structure of canonical networks, the throughput can be
improved significantly by more than 150%. Indeed, in a dense network, it is
worthwhile to deactivate some of the relay nodes near the sink judiciously.

In our study, we investigate the lithospheric flexure of one-plate planets using thin elastic shells of variable thickness. This research aims to provide insights into the understanding of the deformation of the lithosphere under external loads such as volcanic and tectonic activity. The key idea behind our study is to consider a variable thickness of the lithosphere, which is likely to be present in many planetary bodies. To explore this idea, we use the theory of thin elastic shells to model the deformation of the lithosphere. This methodology allows us to capture the effects of variable thickness and its influence on the stress and deformation fields.

Our motivation for conducting this research is to better understand the impact of variable lithospheric thickness on the flexure of one-plate planets. To achieve this goal, we have performed numerical simulations under different scenarios of external loads and lithospheric thickness variations. Our results show that the lithospheric thickness plays a significant role in the flexure of one-plate planets, with thicker areas experiencing less deformation.

In conclusion, our study highlights the importance of considering variable thickness in the lithosphere when analyzing the flexure of one-planet planets. This research can have implications for the interpretation of geological observations and the understanding of planetary evolution.

The Dicke model describes an ensemble of N identical two-level atoms (qubits)
coupled to a single mode of a bosonic field. The fermion Dicke model should be
obtained by changing the atomic pseudo-spin operators by a linear combination
of Fermi operators. The generalized fermion Dicke model is defined introducing
different coupling constants between the single mode of the bosonic field and
the reservoir. In the thermodynamic limit, the fermion Dicke model can be
analized using the path integral approach with functional method. The system
exhibits a second order phase transition from normal to superrandiance at some
critical temperature with the presence of a condensate. We evaluate the
critical transition temperature and present the spectrum of the collective
bosonic excitations. There is quantum phantum critical behavior when the
coupling constants satisfy an especific condition. Two particular situations
are analyzed. First, we present the spectrum of the collective bosonic
excitations in the case using the rotating-wave approximation, recovering the
well know results. Second, the case only considering virtual processes. In the
last case, it is possible to have a superradiance phase when only virtual
processes are introduced in the interaction Hamiltonian. Here also appears a
quantum phase transition at some critical coupling, and for larger values for
the critical coupling, the system enter in this superradiant phase with a
Goldstone mode.

If the cold dark matter consists of weakly interacting massive particles
(WIMPs), anticipated measurements of the WIMP properties at the Large Hadron
Collider (LHC) and the International Linear Collider (ILC) will provide an
unprecedented experimental probe of cosmology at temperatures of order 1 GeV.
It is worth emphasizing that the expected outcome of these tests may or may not
be consistent with the picture of standard cosmology. For example, in
kination-dominated quintessence models of dark energy, the dark matter relic
abundance can be significantly enhanced compared to that obtained from freeze
out in a radiation-dominated universe. Collider measurements then will
simultaneously probe both dark matter and dark energy. In this article, we
investigate the precision to which the LHC and ILC can determine the dark
matter and dark energy parameters under those circumstances. We use an
illustrative set of four benchmark points in minimal supergravity in analogy
with the four LCC benchmark points. The precision achievable together at the
LHC and ILC is sufficient to discover kination-dominated quintessence, under
the assumption that the WIMPs are the only dark matter component. The LHC and
ILC can thus play important roles as alternative probes of both dark matter and
dark energy.

Structure entails function and thus a structural description of the brain
will help to understand its function and may provide insights into many
properties of brain systems, from their robustness and recovery from damage, to
their dynamics and even their evolution. Advances in the analysis of complex
networks provide useful new approaches to understanding structural and
functional properties of brain networks. Structural properties of networks
recently described allow their characterization as small-world, random
(exponential) and scale-free. They complement the set of other properties that
have been explored in the context of brain connectivity, such as topology,
hodology, clustering, and hierarchical organization. Here we apply new network
analysis methods to cortical inter-areal connectivity networks for the cat and
macaque brains. We compare these corticocortical fibre networks to benchmark
rewired, small-world, scale-free and random networks, using two analysis
strategies, in which we measure the effects of the removal of nodes and
connections on the structural properties of the cortical networks. The brain
networks' structural decay is in most respects similar to that of scale-free
networks. The results implicate highly connected hub-nodes and bottleneck
connections as structural basis for some of the conditional robustness of brain
systems. This informs the understanding of the development of brain networks'
connectivity.

In this work, we investigate the dimension of subspaces with bounded Schmidt rank, which is a widely studied concept in the field of quantum information theory. Specifically, we seek to determine the maximum dimension of subspaces whose bipartite states have Schmidt rank bounded by a fixed constant. The motivation for this research lies in the study of entanglement properties of quantum systems, which have far-reaching implications in quantum computing, quantum communication, and quantum cryptography.

Our work presents a rigorous analysis of the problem using tools from linear algebra and quantum information theory. We derive upper and lower bounds on the maximal dimension of subspaces with bounded Schmidt rank and provide matching constructions that achieve these bounds. Our results are presented in the form of explicit formulas and numerical simulations that demonstrate the tightness of our bounds.

Our findings have significant implications in quantum information theory, and our approach can be extended to study related problems in other areas of mathematics and physics. Overall, our work provides a deeper understanding of the fundamental properties of entanglement in quantum systems, and we hope it will inspire further research in this exciting and rapidly evolving field.

Many important real-world networks manifest "small-world" properties such as
scale-free degree distributions, small diameters, and clustering. The most
common model of growth for these networks is "preferential attachment", where
nodes acquire new links with probability proportional to the number of links
they already have. We show that preferential attachment is a special case of
the process of molecular evolution. We present a new single-parameter model of
network growth that unifies varieties of preferential attachment with the
quasispecies equation (which models molecular evolution), and also with the
Erdos-Renyi random graph model. We suggest some properties of evolutionary
models that might be applied to the study of networks. We also derive the form
