of the degree distribution resulting from our algorithm, and we show through
simulations that the process also models aspects of network growth. The
unification allows mathematical machinery developed for evolutionary dynamics
to be applied in the study of network dynamics, and vice versa.

In this study, we investigated the relationship between chaos and symmetry in string cosmology. The motivation for this research stemmed from the fact that, despite the progress made in string theory, there are still several unresolved issues in cosmology that need attention. We aimed to provide a deeper understanding of the underlying principles that govern the behavior of the early universe and the formation of structures within it.

We found that chaos and symmetry are closely interconnected in string cosmology. Specifically, we explored the role of the chaotic dynamics of the early universe in determining the symmetry breaking patterns that emerge later on. Our analysis revealed that there are certain symmetry groups that are particularly sensitive to the initial conditions of the universe, leading to a rich and complex spectrum of symmetry breaking patterns.

The key methodology behind our study involved numerical simulations and analytical calculations of the dynamics of the early universe. We used tools from string theory and the theory of dynamical systems to investigate how the interplay between chaos and symmetry affects the evolution of the universe.

Overall, our results shed light on the intricate interplay between dynamics and symmetry in the early universe. Our findings have important implications for our understanding of both string theory and cosmology, and could potentially inform the development of new models that better describe the behavior of the universe in its earliest stages.

In this paper, we present our work on the applications of tripled chaotic maps in cryptography. The main motivation for our research is the need to develop efficient and secure encryption schemes that can resist attacks from determined malicious actors. The tripled chaotic maps have attracted significant attention because of their pseudo-randomness, unpredictability, and sensitivity to initial conditions, making them suitable for cryptographic purposes.
 
Our results indicate that the tripled chaotic maps can be used for various cryptographic tasks, such as generating random numbers, key exchange, data encryption, and authentication. We propose a novel chaotic block cipher that uses the chaotic maps to produce the cipher key and confuse the plaintext, resulting in a high level of security and efficiency. In addition, we employ the tripled chaotic maps to develop an authentication protocol that deters attackers from accessing a network without permission.

The problem we intend to solve with this work is the vulnerability of traditional cryptosystems to sophisticated attacks such as brute-force attacks and differential attacks. Our proposed scheme offers robustness against these attacks by leveraging the properties of tripled chaotic maps to generate a secure cipher key and encrypt the plaintext. Our findings highlight the potential of tripled chaotic maps in modern cryptography and pave the way for further research in this field.

One of the central goals of the Spitzer Legacy Project ``From Molecular Cores
to Planet-forming Disks'' (c2d) is to determine the frequency of remnant
circumstellar disks around weak-line T Tauri stars (wTTs) and to study the
properties and evolutionary status of these disks. Here we present a census of
disks for a sample of over 230 spectroscopically identified wTTs located in the
c2d IRAC (3.6, 4.5, 4.8, and 8.0 um) and MIPS (24 um) maps of the Ophiuchus,
Lupus, and Perseus Molecular Clouds. We find that ~20% of the wTTs in a
magnitude limited subsample have noticeable IR-excesses at IRAC wavelengths
indicating the presence of a circumstellar disk. The disk frequencies we find
in these 3 regions are ~3-6 times larger than that recently found for a sample
of 83 relatively isolated wTTs located, for the most part, outside the highest
extinction regions covered by the c2d IRAC and MIPS maps. The disk fractions we
find are more consistent with those obtained in recent Spitzer studies of wTTs
in young clusters such as IC 348 and Tr 37. From their location in the H-R
diagram, we find that, in our sample, the wTTs with excesses are among the
younger part of the age distribution. Still, up to ~50% of the apparently
youngest stars in the sample show no evidence of IR excess, suggesting that the
circumstellar disks of a sizable fraction of pre-main-sequence stars dissipate
in a timescale of ~1 Myr. We also find that none of the stars in our sample
apparently older than ~10 Myrs have detectable circumstellar disks at
wavelengths < 24 um. Also, we find that the wTTs disks in our sample exhibit a
wide range of properties (SED morphology, inner radius, L_DISK/L*, etc) which
bridge the gaps observed between the cTTs and the debris disk regimes.

In our work, we investigate the quantum spin liquid states in the two-dimensional kagome antiferromagnets, ZnxCu4-x(OD)6Cl2. The kagome lattice is a highly debated system in condensed matter physics, which is gaining prominence due to its interesting and complex behavior. Kagome antiferromagnets have been previously shown to possess strong frustration, leading to various exotic states of matter. Our aim is to study the quantum spin liquid behavior in these materials as we believe that this can help us gain a better understanding of the underlying physics of the system.

Our research has been motivated by the fact that despite extensive studies on kagome antiferromagnets, the nature of the ground state and the possible existence of a quantum spin liquid state remain elusive. We utilized neutron scattering measurements and performed extensive theoretical analysis, which helped us to unveil various features of these materials' quantum behavior. We discovered that we had observed the most conclusive evidence of a quantum spin liquid in ZnxCu4-x(OD)6Cl2 compounds to date.

Our results show that there is a profound and exciting relationship between the magnetic properties and structural changes in these materials. This research marks a significant step towards further understanding quantum spin liquids and their associated behavior in highly complex systems. The insights offered by our work are likely to play a vital role in the development of future spin-based technologies, with possible applications in quantum computing.

Galaxy cluster merger statistics are an important component in understanding
the formation of large-scale structure. Unfortunately, it is difficult to study
merger properties and evolution directly because the identification of cluster
mergers in observations is problematic. We use large N-body simulations to
study the statistical properties of massive halo mergers, specifically
investigating the utility of close halo pairs as proxies for mergers. We
examine the relationship between pairs and mergers for a wide range of merger
timescales, halo masses, and redshifts (0<z<1). We also quantify the utility of
pairs in measuring merger bias. While pairs at very small separations will
reliably merge, these constitute a small fraction of the total merger
population. Thus, pairs do not provide a reliable direct proxy to the total
merger population. We do find an intriguing universality in the relation
between close pairs and mergers, which in principle could allow for an estimate
of the statistical merger rate from the pair fraction within a scaled
separation, but including the effects of redshift space distortions strongly
degrades this relation. We find similar behavior for galaxy-mass halos, making
our results applicable to field galaxy mergers at high redshift. We investigate
how the halo merger rate can be statistically described by the halo mass
function via the merger kernel (coagulation), finding an interesting
environmental dependence of merging: halos within the mass resolution of our
simulations merge less efficiently in overdense environments. Specifically,
halo pairs with separations less than a few Mpc/h are more likely to merge in
underdense environments; at larger separations, pairs are more likely to merge
in overdense environments.

In our work, we study the sharp $L^p$-estimates for maximal operators associated to hypersurfaces in $\bR^3$ for $p>2$. The problem we attempt to solve is the lack of estimates for these maximal operators. Despite numerous studies in the field, these operators remain elusive and difficult to estimate. Our research strives to provide a comprehensive analysis of the operators and establish sharp estimates.

Our results indicate that these maximal operators can be estimated by establishing an appropriate setup and utilizing known estimates. Specifically, we show that a combination of the endpoint theorem and a weighted norm inequality leads to the sharp $L^p$-estimates. We use this methodology to establish estimates for various maximal operators, including those associated with Legendrian submanifolds, real analytic hypersurfaces, and curves in $\bR^3$.

The key idea behind our work is to bridge the gap between the existing research and the establishment of sharp estimates for maximal operators associated to hypersurfaces. We employ techniques such as the endpoint theorem, weighted norm inequalities, and geometric analysis to provide a comprehensive analysis of these operators. Our approach provides a powerful methodology for future researchers in the field and paves the way for further investigation of these elusive operators.

We present Spitzer observations of the dark globule CB190 (L771). We observe
a roughly circular 24 micron shadow with a 70 arcsec radius. The extinction
profile of this shadow matches the profile derived from 2MASS photometry at the
outer edges of the globule and reaches a maximum of ~32 visual magnitudes at
the center. The corresponding mass of CB190 is ~10 Msun. Our 12CO and 13CO J =
2-1 data over a 10 arcmin X 10 arcmin region centered on the shadow show a
temperature ~10 K. The thermal continuum indicates a similar temperature for
the dust. The molecular data also show evidence of freezeout onto dust grains.
We estimate a distance to CB190 of 400 pc using the spectroscopic parallax of a
star associated with the globule. Bonnor-Ebert fits to the density profile, in
conjunction with this distance, yield xi_max = 7.2, indicating that CB190 may
be unstable. The high temperature (56 K) of the best fit Bonnor-Ebert model is
in contradiction with the CO and thermal continuum data, leading to the
conclusion that the thermal pressure is not enough to prevent free-fall
collapse. We also find that the turbulence in the cloud is inadequate to
support it. However, the cloud may be supported by the magnetic field, if this
field is at the average level for dark globules. Since the magnetic field will
eventually leak out through ambipolar diffusion, it is likely that CB190 is
collapsing or in a late pre-collapse stage.

In this paper, we investigate the spectral fluctuations of tridiagonal random matrices from the beta-Hermite ensemble. Our motivation for this research is to understand the statistical properties of random matrices, which have applications in various areas of physics, such as quantum mechanics, statistical mechanics, and condensed matter physics. In particular, the beta-Hermite ensemble is a well-known model for studying the eigenvalue statistics of random matrices.

Using the method of orthogonal polynomials and Toeplitz determinants, we derive a set of recurrence relations for the moments of the eigenvalues of the tridiagonal random matrix. From these recurrence relations, we obtain explicit expressions for the spectral two-point correlation function and the number variance. We also find that the spectral fluctuations of the tridiagonal random matrix depend on the choice of the parameter in the beta-Hermite ensemble.

Our key idea is to use the techniques of random matrix theory to study the statistical properties of tridiagonal random matrices. Our methodology involves deriving recurrence relations for the moments of the eigenvalues and using them to obtain explicit expressions for the spectral two-point correlation function and the number variance.

In conclusion, our work provides new insights into the spectral fluctuations of tridiagonal random matrices from the beta-Hermite ensemble. This research contributes to the understanding of random matrix theory and has potential applications in physics and engineering.

Current experimental data allow the zero value for one neutrino mass, either
m_1 = 0 or m_3 = 0. This observation implies that a realistic neutrino mass
texture can be established by starting from the limit (a) m_1 = m_2 = 0 and m_3
\neq 0 or (b) m_1 = m_2 \neq 0 and m_3 = 0. In both cases, we may introduce a
particular perturbation which ensures the resultant neutrino mixing matrix to
be the tri-bimaximal mixing pattern or its viable variations with all entries
being formed from small integers and their square roots. We find that it is
natural to incorporate this kind of neutrino mass matrix in the minimal Type-II
seesaw model with only one heavy right-handed Majorana neutrino N in addition
to the SU(2)_L Higgs triplet \Delta_L. We show that it is possible to account
for the cosmological baryon number asymmetry in the m_3 =0 case via thermal
leptogenesis, in which the one-loop vertex correction to N decays is mediated
by \Delta_L and the CP-violating asymmetry of N decays is attributed to the
electron flavor.

In this paper, we present the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) Quasar Catalog IV, which includes data from the fifth data release (DR5). With this catalog, we aim to provide a comprehensive and up-to-date dataset of quasars since the DR1 release of the SDSS Quasar Catalog. 

The methodology involves using the SDSS spectroscopic observations and photometric data to identify and measure quasars, which are defined as luminous galactic nuclei that emit intense radiation. The catalog includes 77,429 quasars that span a redshift range of 0.08 to 5.41, with an average magnitude of i=19.

The motivation for this research stems from the important role quasars play in understanding the formation and evolution of galaxies, as well as their potential as cosmological probes. With this catalog, researchers can investigate the spatial distribution, correlation function, and clustering properties of quasars, among other things. Additionally, the catalog can aid in the identification of rare quasar populations and the study of their physical properties.

Overall, this work represents a significant contribution to the field of extragalactic astronomy and cosmology, offering a valuable resource for future research into the nature and evolution of quasars.

Most cool core clusters of galaxies possess active galactic nuclei (AGN) in
their centers. These AGN inflate buoyant bubbles containing non-thermal radio
emitting particles. If such bubbles efficiently confine cosmic rays (CR) then
this could explain ``radio ghosts'' seen far from cluster centers. We simulate
the diffusion of cosmic rays from buoyant bubbles inflated by AGN. Our
simulations include the effects of the anisotropic particle diffusion
introduced by magnetic fields. Our models are consistent with the X-ray
morphology of AGN bubbles, with disruption being suppressed by the magnetic
draping effect. We conclude that for such magnetic field topologies, a
substantial fraction of cosmic rays can be confined inside the bubbles on
buoyant rise timescales even when the parallel diffusivity coefficient is very
large. For isotropic diffusion at a comparable level, cosmic rays would leak
out of the bubbles too rapidly to be consistent with radio observations. Thus,
the long confinement times associated with the magnetic suppression of CR
diffusion can explain the presence of radio ghosts. We show that the partial
escape of cosmic rays is mostly confined to the wake of the rising bubbles, and
speculate that this effect could: (1) account for the excitation of the
H$\alpha$ filaments trailing behind the bubbles in the Perseus cluster, (2)
inject entropy into the metal enriched material being lifted by the bubbles
and, thus, help to displace it permanently from the cluster center and (3)
produce observable $\gamma$-rays via the interaction of the diffusing cosmic
rays with the thermal intracluster medium (ICM).

Sequences that are defined by multisums of hypergeometric terms with compact
support occur frequently in enumeration problems of combinatorics, algebraic
geometry and perturbative quantum field theory. The standard recipe to study
the asymptotic expansion of such sequences is to find a recurrence satisfied by
them, convert it into a differential equation satisfied by their generating
series, and analyze the singulatiries in the complex plane. We propose a
shortcut by constructing directly from the structure of the hypergeometric term
a finite set, for which we conjecture (and in some cases prove) that it
contains all the singularities of the generating series. Our construction of
this finite set is given by the solution set of a balanced system of polynomial
equations of a rather special form, reminiscent of the Bethe ansatz. The finite
set can also be identified with the set of critical values of a potential
function, as well as with the evaluation of elements of an additive $K$-theory
group by a regulator function. We give a proof of our conjecture in some
special cases, and we illustrate our results with numerous examples.

In this study, we investigate the high-frequency quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs) observed in the microquasar GRS 1915+105 by proposing a possible explanation for their origin. Observations have shown that QPOs have a periodicity ranging from a few tens to hundreds of Hz. Our investigation is motivated by the fact that the origin of these oscillations has remained elusive, despite extensive studies. 

We propose that the QPOs arise due to the existence of LNRF-velocity hump-induced oscillations in a Keplerian accretion disc orbiting near an extreme Kerr black hole. Our simulations reveal that the Keplerian disc oscillates radially as it moves towards the black hole, resulting in a feedback mechanism that contributes to the QPOs observed. We used numerical simulations to validate our proposed model, where we investigate the disc oscillation properties under various parameter settings. 

Our study provides a new avenue for investigating the origin of high-frequency QPOs observed in microquasars. We conclude that the QPOs are likely to arise from the existence of LNRF-velocity hump-induced oscillations in a Keplerian accretion disc orbiting near-extreme Kerr black holes. Notably, our proposed explanation can also be extended to other systems exhibiting similar QPOs, making it a significant contribution to our understanding of accretion physics.

In this work, we conducted a detailed analysis of the isotopic ratio of lithium in the neutron star binary system Cen X-4, aiming to shed light on the origin of lithium in X-ray binaries. The motivation behind this research lies in the fact that the lithium abundance in some X-ray binaries has been found to be significantly higher than expected from standard stellar evolution models. This poses a challenge to our understanding of the formation and evolution of X-ray binaries, which are important probes of fundamental astrophysical processes such as accretion and nuclear processes in extreme environments.

We analyzed a Chandra High Energy Transmission Grating Spectrum of Cen X-4, using state-of-the-art spectral modeling techniques to obtain robust measurements of the isotopic 6Li/7Li ratio. Our analysis revealed a lithium isotopic ratio of 0.08±0.07, consistent with the expected value for the Milky Way disk. This result rules out the possibility of a primordial origin for the high lithium abundance in some X-ray binaries, as had been previously suggested.

Our work thus provides important insights into the origin of lithium in X-ray binaries, thereby opening up new avenues for understanding the formation and evolution of these important astrophysical systems.

We present a study of the in-medium effects on particle production in heavy ion collisions. Our research is motivated by the fact that the properties of nuclear matter are modified in the hot and dense environment created in these collisions. It is well known that particle production depends on the collision energy and the type of nucleus. However, the effect of the medium created in these collisions on the production rate is not well understood. 

We use the transport model approach to study particle production in heavy ion collisions. Our simulations include the dynamics of the produced medium, the propagation of the particles through the medium, and their scattering with the medium constituents. We consider different types of medium, including a hadronic gas, a quark-gluon plasma, and a mixed phase. We analyze the dependence of the particle production rate on the medium density, temperature, and composition. 

Our results show that the in-medium effects can significantly modify the particle production rates, especially for low transverse momenta and high multiplicities. Furthermore, we observe that the sensitivity of the particle production to the medium properties is enhanced for lighter nuclei and higher collision energies. Our study provides valuable insights into the understanding of the properties of the medium created in heavy ion collisions and its impact on the particle production.

We present the first systematic investigation of the [NeV] (14um/24um) and
[SIII] (18um/33um) infrared line flux ratios, traditionally used to estimate
the density of the ionized gas, in a sample of 41 Type 1 and Type 2 active
galactic nuclei (AGNs) observed with the Infrared Spectrograph on board
Spitzer. The majority of galaxies with both [NeV] lines detected have observed
[NeV] line flux ratios consistent with or below the theoretical low density
limit, based on calculations using currently available collision strengths and
ignoring absorption and stimulated emission. We find that Type 2 AGNs have
lower line flux ratios than Type 1 AGNs and that all of the galaxies with line
flux ratios below the low density limit are Type 2 AGNs. We argue that
differential infrared extinction to the [NeV] emitting region due to dust in
the obscuring torus is responsible for the ratios below the low density limit
and we suggest that the ratio may be a tracer of the inclination angle of the
torus to our line of sight. Because the temperature of the gas, the amount of
extinction, and the effect of absorption and stimulated emission on the line
ratios are all unknown, we are not able to determine the electron densities
associated with the [NeV] line flux ratios for the objects in our sample. We
also find that the [SIII] emission from the galaxies in our sample is extended
and originates primarily in star forming regions. Since the emission from
low-ionization species is extended, any analysis using line flux ratios from
such species obtained from slits of different sizes is invalid for most nearby
galaxies.

In this study, we aimed to experimentally test non-local realism, which is a theoretical concept that states that physical systems cannot have instantaneous interactions over vast distances. This concept is crucial in understanding the foundations of quantum mechanics and its relevance in explaining the strange quantum phenomena.  Our experiment aimed to investigate whether these non-local interactions exist or whether there are alternative explanations for these phenomena, as proposed by local realism. 

The methodology involved the use of Bell's inequality test, which states that local realism imposes a limit on the correlations between the measurements of two physically distant systems. By generating entangled photon pairs and measuring their polarization properties, we tested the Bell inequality. Our results showed that the correlations between the measurements are non-local, inconsistent with the predictions of local realism, and in line with the predictions of quantum mechanics.

These results have significant implications for our understanding of the nature of reality and the limitations of classical physics in explaining quantum phenomena. By further developing such experiments, we can shed more light on the mysterious and complex nature of quantum mechanics, and potentially pave the way for new quantum technologies.

We present results of a photometric survey whose aim was to derive structural
and astrophysical parameters for 42 open clusters. While our sample is
definitively not representative of the total open cluster sample in the Galaxy,
it does cover a wide range of cluster parameters and is uniform enough to allow
for simple statistical considerations. BV wide-field CCD photometry was
obtained for open clusters for which photometric, structural, and dynamical
evolution parameters were determined. The limiting and core radii were
determined by analyzing radial density profiles. The ages, reddenings, and
distances were obtained from the solar metallicity isochrone fitting. The mass
function was used to study the dynamical state of the systems, mass segregation
effect and to estimate the total mass and number of cluster members. This study
reports on the first determination of basic parameters for 11 out of 42
observed open clusters. The angular sizes for the majority of the observed
clusters appear to be several times larger than the catalogue data indicate.
The core and limiting cluster radii are correlated and the latter parameter is
3.2 times larger on average. The limiting radius increases with the cluster's
mass, and both the limiting and core radii decrease in the course of dynamical
evolution. For dynamically not advanced clusters, the mass function slope is
similar to the universal IMF slope. For more evolved systems, the effect of
evaporation of low-mass members is clearly visible. The initial mass
segregation is present in all the observed young clusters, whereas the
dynamical mass segregation appears in clusters older than about log(age)=8.
Low-mass stars are deficient in the cores of clusters older than log(age)=8.5
and not younger than one relaxation time.

GEMS and GOODS fields were examined to z~1.4 for galaxy interactions and
mergers. The basic morphologies are familiar: antennae with long tidal tails,
tidal dwarfs, and merged cores; M51-type galaxies with disk spirals and tidal
arm companions; early-type galaxies with diffuse plumes; equal-mass
grazing-collisions; and thick J-shaped tails beaded with star formation and
double cores. One type is not common locally and is apparently a loose
assemblage of smaller galaxies. Photometric measurements were made of the tails
and clumps, and physical sizes were determined assuming photometric redshifts.
Antennae tails are a factor of ~3 smaller in GEMS and GOODS systems compared to
local antennae; their disks are a factor of ~2 smaller than locally. Collisions
among early type galaxies generally show no fine structure in their tails,
indicating that stellar debris is usually not unstable. One exception has a
5x10**9 Msun smooth red clump that could be a pure stellar condensation. Most
tidal dwarfs are blue and probably form by gravitational instabilities in the
gas. One tidal dwarf looks like it existed previously and was incorporated into
the arm tip by tidal forces. The star-forming regions in tidal arms are 10 to
1000 times more massive than star complexes in local galaxies, although their
separations are about the same. If they all form by gravitational
instabilities, then the gaseous velocity dispersions in interacting galaxies
have to be larger than in local galaxies by a factor of ~5 or more; the gas
column densities have to be larger by the square of this factor.

We present a study in which we investigate the use of close galaxy pairs as proxies for galaxy cluster mergers. The motivation of our research is to develop efficient techniques for identifying galaxy cluster mergers, which are important events in the evolution of galaxies and clusters. However, detecting these mergers is not an easy task, as they often involve significant dynamics and complex gravitational interactions. Our approach here is to use the proximity of galaxies as an indicator of gravitational perturbations in the cluster environment, which could be evidence of an ongoing merger. Specifically, we analyze a sample of galaxy clusters from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey and search for pairs of galaxies separated by distances smaller than a given threshold. We then compare the properties of these pairs with those of clusters identified as mergers by other means. Our results indicate that galaxy pairs can indeed serve as useful proxies for cluster mergers, with pairs in merging clusters showing distinctive properties such as higher galaxy masses and larger velocity dispersions. We also find that the effectiveness of our method depends on the choice of pair distance threshold and the dynamical state of the clusters. This study provides an alternative and complementary method for identifying galaxy cluster mergers, with potential implications for a wide range of astrophysical research.

In this paper, we investigate the many-to-one throughput capacity of IEEE 802.11 multi-hop wireless networks. Our research is motivated by the increasing demand for high-performance wireless networks with reliable connectivity and throughput. Multi-hop wireless networks have been proposed as a solution for providing wireless coverage in areas with limited infrastructure and for supporting high-density environments, such as stadiums, shopping centers, and industrial sites.

The main challenge we address in this work is to determine the maximum achievable throughput capacity of many-to-one traffic in multi-hop wireless networks. We propose a novel methodology that combines network modeling and analysis with experimental measurements of real-world network deployments. Our approach takes into account the effects of interference, channel fading, and network topology on the network performance.

We present extensive simulation and experimental results to demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach and to evaluate the throughput capacity of IEEE 802.11 multi-hop wireless networks under different scenarios and network configurations. Our results show that the many-to-one throughput capacity of multi-hop wireless networks strongly depends on the network topology, the packet transmission rate, and the interference level. We also identify the key factors that impact the network performance and provide insights for improving the network design and operation.

Overall, our work provides valuable insights into the performance of multi-hop wireless networks and lays the foundation for further research in this area.

In this combined extended rejoinder, we respond to the criticisms raised by I. I. Guseinov in his comment on our paper "One-Range Addition Theorems for Coulomb Interaction Potential and Its Derivatives." Our work focuses on deriving addition theorems for the Coulomb potential and its derivatives using the one-range technique. The key idea behind our approach is to exploit the symmetry properties of the Coulomb potential to simplify the computations involved in deriving the one-range addition theorems. This approach offers several advantages over other existing methods for deriving addition theorems, such as the use of complex variables or Fourier transforms.

In his comment, Guseinov argued that our results are incorrect and that our methodology is flawed. However, we believe that Guseinov's criticisms are misguided and based on a misunderstanding of our approach. We provide a detailed rebuttal of Guseinov's criticisms and show that our results are indeed correct and that our methodology is sound.

Overall, our work contributes to the ongoing effort to derive addition theorems for the Coulomb potential and its derivatives. Our approach offers a simpler and more elegant solution to this problem, and we believe that our results will be of interest to researchers in the field of chemical physics.

Cosmic acceleration is investigated through a kink-like expression for the
deceleration parameter (q). The new parametrization depends on the initial
(q_i) and final (q_f) values of q, on the redshift of the transition from
deceleration to acceleration (z_{t}) and the width of such transition (\tau).
We show that although supernovae (SN) observations (Gold182 and SNLS data
samples) indicate, at high confidence, that a transition occurred in the past
(z_{t}>0) they do not, by themselves, impose strong constraints on the maximum
value of z_{t}. However, when we combine SN with the measurements of the ratio
between the comoving distance to the last scattering surface and the
SDSS+2dfGRS BAO distance scale (S_{k}/D_{v}) we obtain, at 95.4% confidence
level, z_{t}=0.84+{0.17}-{0.13} and \tau =0.51-{0.17}+{0.23} for
(S_{k}/D_{v}+Gold182), and z_{t}=0.88-{0.10}+{0.12} and \tau
=0.35-{0.10}+{0.12} for (S_{k}/D_{v} + SNLS), assuming q_i=0.5 and q_f=-1. We
also analyze the general case, q_f\in(-\infty,0) finding the constraints that
the combined tests (S_{k}/D_{v} + SNLS) impose on the present value of the
deceleration parameter (q_0).

We report observations of the dust cloud L1204 with the Onsala 25-m telescope
in the 6 cm (1$_{11}-1_{10}$) transition of \htco. The observed region includes
the
  S140 H${\alpha}$ arc. This spectral line is seen here in absorption against
the cosmic microwave background, indicating the presence of widespread warm
molecular gas at intermediate densities. Overall, the distributions of H$_2$CO
and CO (taken from the literature) are fairly similar, though significant
differences exist at small scales. Most notably, while the CO peak is nearly
coincident with the S140 H${\alpha}$ arc, the maximum H$_2$CO absorption is
clearly separated from it by a full 10$'$ beam ($\sim$ 3 pc). We argue that
these differences result from differing abundances and excitation requirements.
The CO(1-0) line is more optically thick and more biased towards warm gas than
the H$_2$CO 6 cm line. On the other hand, formaldehyde is more easily
photodissociated and is, therefore, a poorer tracer of the molecular gas
located immediately behind Photon Dominated Regions.

We study spontaneous symmetry breaking in a system of two parallel
quasi-one-dimensional traps, equipped with optical lattices (OLs) and filled
with a Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC). The cores are linearly coupled by
tunneling. Analysis of the corresponding system of linearly coupled
Gross-Pitaevskii equations (GPEs) reveals that spectral bandgaps of the single
GPE split into subgaps. Symmetry breaking in two-component BEC solitons is
studied in cases of the attractive (AA) and repulsive (RR) nonlinearity in both
traps; the mixed situation, with repulsion in one trap and attraction in the
other (RA), is considered too. In all the cases, stable asymmetric solitons are
found, bifurcating from symmetric or antisymmetric ones (and destabilizing
them), in the AA and RR systems, respectively. In either case, bi-stability is
predicted, with a nonbifurcating stable branch, either antisymmetric or
symmetric, coexisting with asymmetric ones. Solitons destabilized by the
bifurcation tend to rearrange themselves into their stable asymmetric
counterparts. The impact of a phase mismatch, between the OLs in the two cores
is also studied. Also considered is a related model, for a binary BEC in a
single-core trap with the OL, assuming that the two species (representing
different spin states of the same atom) are coupled by linear interconversion.
In that case, the symmetry-breaking bifurcations in the AA and RR models switch
their character, if the inter-species nonlinear interaction becomes stronger
than the intra-species nonlinearity.

In our work, we aimed to calculate the second post-Newtonian approximation of the scalar-tensor theory of gravity. This was motivated by the need to develop a better understanding of the gravitational interactions between massive bodies. The post-Newtonian approximation technique involves expanding Einstein's field equations in powers of 1/c2, where c is the speed of light. This approximation is valid when gravitational fields are weak and velocities are small compared to the speed of light.

Our approach involved constructing the Lagrangian of the scalar-tensor theory and solving the corresponding field equations to obtain the gravitational potential at the second post-Newtonian approximation. We found that the scalar-tensor theory yields a weaker gravitational force compared to general relativity, which is consistent with previous works.

Our research serves as a step forward in the study of scalar-tensor theories and their applications in astrophysics and cosmology. One of the problems our work attempts to solve is to understand the contribution of scalar fields to gravitational interactions. Our results provide a theoretical framework for future studies, which could include testing the scalar-tensor theory against observational data and exploring its implications for the behavior of gravity in extreme environments, such as black holes and neutron stars.

In this study, we present submillimeter imaging of the gas and dust disk around the young Herbig Ae star HD 163296, obtained using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA). Our findings provide insight into the disk structure and kinematics, thereby enriching our understanding of star formation and planet formation. 

The main problem this research attempts to solve is our lack of knowledge about the detailed structure of the disk around HD 163296, which is a key target for studying protoplanetary disks and a well-known example of a transitional disk, where planet formation may be occurring. Furthermore, the disk has a brightness asymmetry that could originate from either dust depletion or a biased pressure distribution, both of which have important implications for planet formation. 

Our research was motivated by the need to examine HD 163296's disk at high spatial resolution and sensitivity, and our analysis of the ALMA data revealed several key findings. We uncovered a tilted inner disk and a possible spiral structure in the outer disk, indicating the presence of a protoplanet or planetesimal. We also measured a temperature gradient across the disk, which is important for understanding planet formation and migration. These results help to shed light on the formation and evolution of protoplanetary disks and the creation of planetary systems.

We present results of the analysis of cometary X-ray spectra with an extended
version of our charge exchange emission model (Bodewits et al. 2006). We have
applied this model to the sample of 8 comets thus far observed with the Chandra
X-ray observatory and ACIS spectrometer in the 300-1000 eV range. The surveyed
comets are C/1999 S4 (LINEAR), C/1999 T1 (McNaught-Hartley), C/2000 WM1
(LINEAR), 153P/2002 (Ikeya-Zhang), 2P/2003 (Encke), C/2001 Q4 (NEAT), 9P/2005
(Tempel 1) and 73P/2006-B (Schwassmann-Wachmann 3) and the observations include
a broad variety of comets, solar wind environments and observational
conditions. The interaction model is based on state selective, velocity
dependent charge exchange cross sections and is used to explore how cometary
X-ray emission depend on cometary, observational and solar wind
characteristics. It is further demonstrated that cometary X-ray spectra mainly
reflect the state of the local solar wind. The current sample of Chandra
observations was fit using the constrains of the charge exchange model, and
relative solar wind abundances were derived from the X-ray spectra. Our
analysis showed that spectral differences can be ascribed to different solar
wind states, as such identifying comets interacting with (I) fast, cold wind,
(II), slow, warm wind and (III) disturbed, fast, hot winds associated with
interplanetary coronal mass ejections. We furthermore predict the existence of
a fourth spectral class, associated with the cool, fast high latitude wind.

In this paper, we present new results on superstring isometries and hidden dimensions. The motivation for this research stems from the need for a deeper understanding of the connections between string theory, which is one of the most promising candidates for a theory of everything, and the geometry of spacetime. We focus on the study of isometries, which are transformations that preserve distances, and which play a crucial role in the formulation of string theory. More specifically, we investigate the existence of new isometries that are compatible with the constraints of superstring theory and the presence of hidden dimensions beyond the usual four. 

Our approach is based on a combination of mathematical tools from differential geometry and physics, including Lie algebras, group theory, and the use of supersymmetric structures. In particular, we develop a new method for constructing superstring isometry algebras that take into account the interaction between the different types of superstring modes and the hidden dimensions. We provide explicit examples of such algebras and examine their properties, including their relation to existing symmetries in superstring theory.

Overall, our results contribute to a better understanding of the structure of superstring theory and the role of hidden dimensions in its formulation. They open up new avenues for future research and have potential implications for the search for a unified theory of fundamental physics.

Recently, Berry phase in the semiclassical dynamical of Bloch electrons has
been found to make a correction to the phase-space density of states and a
general multi-band formula for finite-temperature orbital magnetization has
been given [Phys. Rev. Lett. \textbf{97}, 026603 (2006)], where the orbital
magnetization $\mathcal{M}$ consists of two parts, i.e., the conventional part
$M_{c}$ and the Berry-phase correction part $M_{\Omega}$. Using this general
formula, we theoretically investigate the orbital magnetization and its effects
on thermoelectric transport and magnetic susceptibility properties of the
two-dimensional \textit{kagom\'{e}} lattice with spin anisotropies included.
The study in this paper is highly interesting by the occurrence of nonzero
Chern number in the lattice. The spin chirality parameter $\phi$ (see text)
results in profound effects on the orbital magnetization properties. It is
found that the two parts in orbital magnetization opposite each other. In
particular, we show that $M_{c}$ and $M_{\Omega}$ yield the paramagnetic and
diamagnetic responses, respectively. It is further shown that the orbital
magnetization displays fully different behavior in the metallic and insulating
regions, which is due to the different roles $M_{c}$ and $M_{\Omega}$ play in
these two regions. The anomalous Nernst conductivity is also calculated, which
displays a peak-valley structure as a function of the electron Fermi energy.

In this study, we explored the subjective evaluation of different forms in an immersive environment using virtual reality technology. Our main objective was to understand how different shapes and designs affect the users' emotional states and overall experience. The methodology involved creating three virtual reality environments with different forms: a circular room, a rectangular room, and a hexagonal room. A total of 50 participants were exposed to each environment and were asked to evaluate their experience using a questionnaire that included questions about their emotional state, spatial perception, and overall satisfaction.

The results of our study showed that the participants' emotional states varied depending on the forms presented to them. Interestingly, participants reported feeling a sense of calmness and tranquility in the circular and hexagonal environments, while the rectangular environment elicited feelings of unease and discomfort. Furthermore, participants reported a stronger sense of spatial perception in the circular and hexagonal environments compared to the rectangular one. 

Our study offers insight into the potential of virtual reality technologies in shaping our emotional states and subjective experiences. The findings from this study could help designers and architects create more engaging and immersive environments that cater to the emotional needs of their users. Ultimately, this work attempts to solve the problem of how best to design immersive environments that can enhance people's emotional states and overall wellbeing.

Planetary topography can either be modeled as a load supported by the
lithosphere, or as a dynamical effect due to lithospheric flexure caused by
mantle convection. In both cases the response of the lithosphere to external
forces can be calculated with the theory of thin elastic plates or shells. On
one-plate planets the spherical geometry of the lithospheric shell plays an
important role in the flexure mechanism. So far the equations governing the
deformations and stresses of a spherical shell have only been derived under the
assumption of a shell of constant thickness. However local studies of gravity
and topography data suggest large variations in the thickness of the
lithosphere. In this article we obtain the scalar flexure equations governing
the deformations of a thin spherical shell with variable thickness or variable
Young's modulus. The resulting equations can be solved in succession, except
for a system of two simultaneous equations, the solutions of which are the
transverse deflection and an associated stress function. In order to include
bottom loading generated by mantle convection, we extend the method of stress
functions to include loads with a toroidal tangential component. We further
show that toroidal tangential displacement always occurs if the shell thickness
varies, even in the absence of toroidal loads. We finally prove that the
degree-one harmonic components of the transverse deflection and of the toroidal
tangential displacement are independent of the elastic properties of the shell
and are associated with translational and rotational freedom. The flexure
equations for a shell of variable thickness are useful not only for the
prediction of the gravity signal in local admittance studies, but also for the
construction of stress maps in tectonic analysis.

We explore the hierarchy of hidden space-time symmetries of noncritical
strings in RNS formalism, realized nonlinearly. Under these symmetry
transformations the variation of the matter part of the RNS action is cancelled
by that of the ghost part. These symmetries, referred to as the
$\alpha$-symmetries, are induced by special space-time generators, violating
the equivalence of ghost pictures. We classify the $\alpha$-symmetry generators
in terms of superconformal ghost cohomologies $H_{n}\sim{H_{-n-2}}(n\geq{0})$
and associate these generators with a chain of hidden space-time dimensions,
with each ghost cohomology $H_{n}\sim{H_{-n-2}}$ ``contributing'' an extra
dimension. Namely, we show that each ghost cohomology $H_{n}\sim{H_{-n-2}}$ of
non-critical superstring theory in $d$-dimensions contains $d+n+1$
$\alpha$-symmetry generators and the generators from
$H_{k}\sim{H_{-k-2}},1\leq{k}\leq{n}$, combined together, extend the space-time
isometry group from the naive $SO(d,2)$ to $SO(d+n,2)$. In the simplest case of
$n=1$ the $\alpha$-generators are identified with the extra symmetries of the
$2T$-physics formalism, also known to originate from a hidden space-time
dimension.

In this work, we propose a spectral method for matching exterior and interior elliptic problems. Our motivation for this research is to address the challenge of finding accurate solutions to boundary value problems that arise in various fields of science and engineering. The method can be applied to problems such as fluid flow, acoustics, and electromagnetic fields. Specifically, we consider a domain containing two disconnected regions, one interior and one exterior.

The main challenge is to match the solution near the boundary between the two regions. The proposed method is based on using a spectral decomposition that matches the solution on the boundary and ensures continuity across it. We show that our approach is efficient and accurate, producing solutions that converge rapidly with increasing spectral resolution. Moreover, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method by applying it to various test problems with different boundary conditions. The results obtained indicate that the proposed method achieves high accuracy and is computationally efficient. 

In conclusion, the spectral method developed in this work provides an effective and reliable technique for matching exterior and interior elliptic problems. It advances the state-of-the-art in numerical methods for boundary value problems and has the potential to find widespread use in different areas of science and engineering.

We present our work on exploring the use of strings as sigma models and their behavior in the tensionless limit. Our motivation for this research lies in the fact that string theory has proven to be one of the most promising candidates for a theory of everything, combining quantum mechanics and general relativity. Studying strings as sigma models allows us to understand the dynamics of string theory more thoroughly, which could aid in making predictions and testing the theory.

We use techniques from conformal field theory to study sigma models for strings. We then look at the behavior of the strings as the tension is decreased to the limit of zero. Our results show that the tensionless limit introduces new symmetries which can be used to model certain phenomena in string theory.

Furthermore, we attempt to solve the problem of understanding how the tensionless limit affects the dynamics of strings. We found that the tensionless limit introduces new conformal symmetries that provide new insights into the behavior of strings in this limit.

In conclusion, our work sheds light on the behavior of strings as sigma models and their properties in the tensionless limit. This research contributes to the ongoing effort of understanding and developing string theory as a viable theory of everything.

In this work, we present the results of the SAURON project, an observational survey of 24 early-type spiral galaxies that were studied using IFU spectroscopy at the William Herschel Telescope. We reconstruct the stellar populations of these galaxies using absorption line strength maps for a wide range of spectral features, including Mg and Fe. The key idea behind our work is to use a spatially resolved approach to probe the age, metallicity, and alpha-element abundance of the stellar populations in these galaxies. By extracting this information, we aim to investigate the formation and evolution of galaxies and the role of different physical processes in shaping their stellar content.

Our methodology involves using a combination of spectral synthesis models and pixel-fitting techniques to derive the relevant physical parameters from the observed spectra. We examine the radial variation of these parameters as a function of the galaxy's structural properties, such as its bulge-to-disk ratio and the presence of bars. We find a diverse range of stellar population gradients among the galaxies, with some showing a clear age or metallicity gradient, while others show no significant variation.

Thus, the main problem that our work attempts to solve is to provide a comprehensive and spatially resolved analysis of the stellar populations in early-type spiral galaxies. Our results shed light on the formation and evolution of these galaxies and highlight the importance of spatially resolved spectroscopy in understanding the physical mechanisms that drive their evolution.

A three-dimensional system of interacting spins typically develops static
long-range order when it is cooled. If the spins are quantum (S = 1/2),
however, novel quantum paramagnetic states may appear. The most highly sought
state among them is the resonating valence bond (RVB) state in which every pair
of neighboring quantum spins form entangled spin singlets (valence bonds) and
the singlets are quantum mechanically resonating amongst all the possible
highly degenerate pairing states. Here we provide experimental evidence for
such quantum paramagnetic states existing in frustrated antiferromagnets,
ZnxCu4-x(OD)6Cl2, where the S = 1/2 magnetic Cu2+ moments form layers of a
two-dimensional kagome lattice. We find that in Cu4(OD)6Cl2, where distorted
kagome planes are weakly coupled to each other, a dispersionless excitation
mode appears in the magnetic excitation spectrum below ~ 20 K, whose
characteristics resemble those of quantum spin singlets in a solid state, known
as a valence bond solid (VBS), that breaks translational symmetry. Doping
nonmagnetic Zn2+ ions reduces the distortion of the kagome lattice, and weakens
the interplane coupling but also dilutes the magnetic occupancy of the kagome
lattice. The VBS state is suppressed and for ZnCu3(OD)6Cl2 where the kagome
planes are undistorted and 90% occupied by the Cu2+ ions, the low energy spin
fluctuations in the spin liquid phase become featureless.

In this article we calculate the surface phase diagram of a two-dimensional
hard-rod fluid confined between two hard lines. In a first stage we study the
semi-infinite system consisting of an isotropic fluid in contact with a single
hard line. We have found complete wetting by the columnar phase at the
wall-isotropic fluid interface. When the fluid is confined between two hard
walls, capillary columnar ordering occurs via a first-order phase transition.
For higher chemical potentials the system exhibits layering transitions even
for very narrow slits (near the one-dimensional limit). The theoretical model
used was a density-functional theory based on the Fundamental-Measure
Functional applied to a fluid of hard rectangles in the restricted-orientation
approximation (Zwanzig model). The results presented here can be checked
experimentally in two-dimensional granular media made of rods, where vertical
motions induced by an external source and excluded volume interactions between
the grains allow the system to explore those stationary states which
entropically maximize packing configurations. We claim that some of the surface
phenomena found here can be present in two-dimensional granular-media fluids.

(ABRIDGED) Star-forming small galaxies made out of collisional debris have
been found in a variety of merging systems. So far only a few of them are known
in ULIRGs although they show clear signs of interactions. Whether external star
formation may take place in such objects is an open question. The aim of this
paper is to identify and characterise the physical and kinematic properties of
the external star forming regions in a sample of ULIRGs, including TDG
candidates, using optical IFS and high angular resolution HST imaging. We have
found that the presence of external star-forming regions is common with 12
objects being identified in 5 ULIRGs. These regions show a large range of
dynamical mass up to 1x10^{10} M_sun, with average sizes of ~750 pc. In
addition, the line ratios, metallicities and H\alpha equivalent widths are
typical of young bursts of star formation (age ~ 5-8 Myr), and similar to those
of other TDG candidates. Their extinction corrected H\alpha luminosities lead
to masses for the young stellar component of ~2x10^6 - 7x10^8 M_sun. The
likelihood of survival of these regions as TDGs is discussed based on their
structural and kinematic properties. Most of these systems follow the relation
between effective radius and velocity dispersion found for globular clusters
and Ellipticals, which suggests they are stable against internal motions. The
stability against forces from the parent galaxy have been studied and a
comparison of the data with the predictions of dynamical evolutionary models is
also performed. Five regions out of twelve show High-Medium or High likelihood
of survival. Our best candidate, which satisfy all the utilized criteria, is
located in the advanced merger IRAS15250+3609 and presents a velocity field
decoupled from the relatively distant parent galaxy.

Thermal inertia determines the temperature distribution over the surface of
an asteroid and therefore governs the magnitude the Yarkovsky effect. The
latter causes gradual drifting of the orbits of km-sized asteroids and plays an
important role in the delivery of near-Earth asteroids (NEAs) from the main
belt and in the dynamical spreading of asteroid families. At present, very
little is known about the thermal inertia of asteroids in the km size range.
Here we show that the average thermal inertia of a sample of NEAs in the
km-size range is 200 $\pm$ 40 J m−2 s−0.5 K−1. Furthermore,
we identify a trend of increasing thermal inertia with decreasing asteroid
diameter, D. This indicates that the dependence of the drift rate of the
orbital semimajor axis on the size of asteroids due to the Yarkovsky effect is
a more complex function than the generally adopted D^(−1) dependence, and
that the size distribution of objects injected by Yarkovsky-driven orbital
mobility into the NEA source regions is less skewed to smaller sizes than
generally assumed. We discuss how this fact may help to explain the small
difference in the slope of the size distribution of km-sized NEAs and main-belt
asteroids.

The temperature variation effect of shape anisotropy on the coercivity,
HC(T), for the aligned Stoner-Wohlfarth (SW) soft ferromagnets, such as fcc Ni,
fcc Co and bcc Fe, are investigated within the framework of Neel-Brown (N-B)
analysis. An extended N-B equation is thus proposed,by introducing a single
dimensionless correction function, the reduced magnetization, m(\tao) =
MS(T)/MS(0), in which \tao = T/TC is the reduced temperature, MS(T) is the
saturation magnetization, and TC is the Curie temperature. The factor, m(\tao),
accounts for the temperature-dependent effect of the shape anisotropy. The
constants, H0 and E0, are for the switching field at zero temperature and the
potential barrier at zero field, respectively. According to this newly derived
equation, the blocking temperature above which the properties of
superparamagnetism show up is described by the expression, TB =
E0m^2(\tao)/[kBln(t/t0)], with the extra correction factor m^2(\tao). The
possible effect on HC(T) and the blocking temperature, TB, attributed to the
downshift of TC resulting from the finite size effect has been discussed also.

We present detailed results from performing general relativistic (GR)
simulations of stellar core collapse to a proto-neutron star, using a
microphysical equation of state (EoS) as well as an approximate description of
deleptonization during the collapse phase. We show that for a wide variety of
rotation rates and profiles the gravitational wave (GW) burst signals from the
core bounce are of a generic type, already known as Type I in the literature.
In addition, for most models the characteristic frequency of the GW burst
signal lies in a narrow range around approximately 718 Hz. In our systematic
study, using both GR and Newtonian gravity, we identify, individually quantify,
and discuss in detail the micro- and macrophysical mechanisms leading to this
result, i.e. the effects of rotation, the EoS, and deleptonization. We also
discuss the detectability prospects of such GW burst signals by GW detectors,
and infer that such a generic type of signal templates will likely facilitate a
more efficient search in current and future detectors of both interferometric
and resonant type.

In particular cases of stationary and stationary axially symmetric space-time
passage to non-relativistic limit of Einstein equation is completed. For this
end the notions of absolute space and absolute time are introduced due to
stationarity of the space-time under consideration. In this construction
absolute time is defined as a function $t$ on the space-time such that $\prt_t$
is exactly the Killing vector and the space at different moments is presented
by the surfaces $t=\con $. The space-time metric is expressed in terms of
metric of the 3-space and two potentials one of which is exactly Newtonian
gravitational potential $\Phi$, another is vector potential $\vec A$ which,
however, differs from vector potential known in classical electrodynamics. In
the first-order approximation on $\Phi/c^2$, $|\vec A|/c$ Einstein equation is
reduced to a system for these functions in which left-hand sides contain
Laplacian of the Newtonian potential, derivatives of the vector potential and
curvature of the space and the right-hand sides do 3-dimensional stress tensor
and densities of mass and energy. Subj-class: Classical Physics

Our work focuses on the multi-agent approach to the self-organization of networks. The problem we attempt to solve is the creation of efficient and self-sustaining networks that can adapt to change while preserving their effectiveness. The traditional approach of network design and management fails to address the complexity of modern networks, which operate in dynamic environments that require constant adaptation. Our research aims to provide a novel approach to network design and management, using multi-agent systems as a means of achieving self-organization.

We have obtained encouraging results using the proposed approach, where networks implemented by the multi-agent systems (MAS) approach are capable of optimizing their operations, adapting to changes and growing in an organic manner. The MAS approach allows for agent interactions, enabling the network to learn and evolve over time while remaining effective. Our results suggest that this approach offers a fundamental shift in the management of complex networks, providing a more robust and viable solution than current network design techniques.

The motivation for our research is driven by the need to develop more responsive, adaptive, and efficient network management techniques. This work offers a contribution to the area of distributed computing, multi-agent systems, and network design, with the potential to impact various domains, including transportation, energy, and communication. The proposed approach of multi-agent systems for the self-organization of networks provides a novel and practical solution to the complex challenges of modern networks.

We present the results of the analysis of neutrino observations by the
Antarctic Muon and Neutrino Detector Array (AMANDA) correlated with photon
observations of more than 400 gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) in the Northern
Hemisphere from 1997 to 2003. During this time period, AMANDA's effective
collection area for muon neutrinos was larger than that of any other existing
detector. Based on our observations of zero neutrinos during and immediately
prior to the GRBs in the dataset, we set the most stringent upper limit on muon
neutrino emission correlated with gamma-ray bursts. Assuming a Waxman-Bahcall
spectrum and incorporating all systematic uncertainties, our flux upper limit
has a normalization at 1 PeV of
  E^2{\Phi}_{\nu} {\leq} 6.0 \times 10^{-9} GeV cm^{-2} s^{-1} sr^{-1}, with
90% of the events expected within the energy range of ~10 TeV to ~3 PeV. The
impact of this limit on several theoretical models of GRBs is discussed, as
well as the future potential for detection of GRBs by next generation neutrino
telescopes. Finally, we briefly describe several modifications to this analysis
in order to apply it to other types of transient point sources.

In this present study, we explore the universality of mixed action extrapolation formulae in the context of lattice QCD simulations. Our main focus is to understand the effectiveness of mixed action simulations in obtaining reliable estimates of physical observables. The methodology involves using partially quenched theories with different actions for the valence and sea quarks, with the aim of taking the continuum limit while keeping the quark masses physical. This allows us to study nonperturbative effects and perform extrapolation procedures, and we verify our results using three different sets of gauge ensembles generated by the MILC collaboration. 

The motivation behind this research stems from the need to obtain accurate results for observables that are poorly known experimentally, such as the proton charge radius or the CKM matrix elements. The results obtained from these simulations can provide valuable information about the underlying physics and test the validity of the Standard Model. In our study, we investigate mixed action simulations with different lattice spacings, quark masses and volumes, and show that the mixed action extrapolation formulae can be used reliably to estimate the physical values of the observables under investigation. 

The main outcomes of our study highlight the importance of mixed action simulations for the precise determination of hadronic observables. The universality of the extrapolation formulae confirms their robustness and applicability to different types of gauge ensembles, thus making them valuable tools for current and future lattice QCD calculations. These results can potentially provide additional insights into the physics of the strong interaction.

In this study, we present the results of a deep X-ray observation of the core of the Perseus galaxy cluster, which highlights the importance of sound waves in regulating the distribution of metals and cosmic rays in the intracluster medium (ICM). Our goal was to explore the interplay between the physical processes that drive the evolution of galaxy clusters, such as turbulence, heating, and cooling, and their impact on the ICM properties. 

Using the Chandra X-ray observatory, we obtained high-resolution images of the Perseus cluster, which allowed us to measure the spatial distribution of various ICM components, such as gas density, temperature, metallicity, and cosmic ray pressure. We found that the sound waves generated by active galactic nuclei (AGN) outbursts play a crucial role in redistributing metals and cosmic rays within the cluster core, suppressing their concentration in the central regions and spreading them over larger scales.

Our findings provide a new perspective on the complex dynamics of galaxy clusters, revealing a rich network of physical processes that interact with each other in a nonlinear way. Our work sheds light on the role of AGN feedback and sound waves as key drivers of the evolution of galaxy clusters, and paves the way for further investigations of the intracluster medium using multi-wavelength observations and numerical simulations.

We present absorption line strength maps of a sample of 24 representative
early-type spiral galaxies, mostly of type Sa, obtained as part of the SAURON
survey of nearby galaxies using our custom-built integral-field spectrograph.
Using high-quality spectra, spatially binned to a constant signal-to-noise, we
measure several key age, metallicity and abundance ratio sensitive indices from
the Lick/IDS system over a contiguous two-dimensional field including bulge and
inner disc. We present maps of H beta, Fe 5015 and Mg b, for each galaxy
  The absorption line maps show that many galaxies contain some younger
populations (<= 1 Gyr), distributed in small or large inner discs, or in
circumnuclear star forming rings. In many cases these young stars are formed in
circumnuclear mini-starbursts, which are dominating the light in the centres of
some of the early-type spirals. These mini-starburst cause a considerable
scatter in index-index diagrams such as Mg b- H beta and Mg b -Fe 5015, more
than is measured for early-type galaxies. We find that the central regions of
Sa galaxies display a wide range in ages, even within the galaxies. 50% of the
sample show velocity dispersion drops in their centres.
  All of the galaxies of our sample lie on or below the Mg b- $\sigma$ relation
for elliptical galaxies in the Coma cluster, and above the H beta absorption
line - $\sigma$ relation for elliptical galaxies. If those relations are
considered to be relations for the oldest local galaxies we see that our sample
of spirals has a considerable scatter in age, with the largest scatter at the
lowest $\sigma$. This is in disagreement with highly inclined samples, in which
generally only old stellar populations are found in the central regions.
  All this can be understood if ... (see paper for rest of abstract)

In this work, we investigate the dramatic variability of X-ray absorption lines in the black hole candidate Cygnus X-1. Our research aims to understand the origin and nature of these variability phenomena, which hold important clues to the inner workings of black holes in general. The problem we attempt to solve is the lack of a clear explanation for the observed variability, which has complicated the interpretation of the available data and theoretical models.

To tackle this challenge, we first collected and analyzed a large amount of X-ray spectral data from several observing campaigns of Cygnus X-1. We then performed a detailed statistical analysis of the data, focusing on the variability characteristics of the absorption lines, their energy dependence, and their time evolution patterns. Our key ideas center around the concept of clumpy accretion flows, which could generate the observed variability by modulating the absorption properties of dense gas clouds that surround the black hole.

Our methodology involved constructing and fitting various physical models to the observed X-ray spectra, including Monte Carlo simulations of clumpy accretion flows and hydrodynamic simulations of the gas clouds. Our results suggest that the variability of the X-ray absorption lines is consistent with a clumpy accretion scenario, with the absorption properties modulated by the geometrical arrangement and motion of the clouds. The implications of our findings for black hole accretion and feedback processes are discussed.

The Hubble Ultra Deep Field source JD2 presented in Mobasher et al. (2005) is
an unusual galaxy that is very faint at all wavelengths shortward of 1.1
micron. Photometric redshift fits to data at 0.4 to 8 microns yield a
significant probability that it is an extremely massive galaxy at z~6.5. In
this paper we present new photometry at 16 microns and 22 micron from Spitzer
Infrared Spectrograph (IRS) peak-up imaging of the Great Observatories Origins
Deep Survey (GOODS) fields. We find that the spectral energy distribution shows
a factor of ~4 rise in flux density between the 16 micron and 22 micron
bandpass which is most likely due to the entrance of polycyclic aromatic
hydrocarbon emission features into the 22 micron and 24 micron passbands. The
flux ratio between these bandpasses can be best fit by a z~1.7 luminous
infrared galaxy with a bolometric luminosity of (2-6)\times10^{11} L_sun
corresponding to a star-formation rate of 80 M_sun/yr. The predicted flux
density values at other longer wavelengths are below the detection limits of
current instrumentation but such sources could potentially be detected in
lensed submillimeter surveys. Re-evaluation of the optical/near-infrared
photometry continues to favor z>6 photometric redshift solutions, but we argue
that the consistency of the multiwavelength parameters of this galaxy with
other dusty starbursts favor the z~2 mid-infrared photometric redshift. The
data presented here provide evidence that optically undetected near-infrared
sources which are detected at 24 microns are most likely dusty, starburst
galaxies at a redshift of z~2 with stellar masses >10^{10} M_sun.

In this study, we investigated the effect of temperature-dependent shape anisotropy on coercivity with aligned soft ferromagnets using a Stoner-Wohlfarth model. The motivation for this research is the need to understand the behavior of magnetic materials at different temperatures and to develop more effective ways of controlling magnetic properties. Our analysis shows that the coercivity of the aligned soft ferromagnet has a strong dependence on temperature due to changes in the shape anisotropy energy. We found that when the temperature is increased, the shape anisotropy energy decreases which results in a decrease in coercivity. Our results also indicate that the orientation of the easy axis of magnetization, with respect to the direction of anisotropy, plays a crucial role in determining the coercivity as well as the shape anisotropy. 

The methodology used involved developing a mathematical model based on the Stoner-Wohlfarth theory and solving the associated equations numerically using the Runge-Kutta method. We varied the temperature and the orientation of the easy axis of magnetization to study the impact on the coercivity. Our study provides insights into the behavior of magnetic materials at different temperatures, which could be useful in designing and optimizing magnetic devices for a wide range of applications.

A review of CP violation from the Standard Model to strings is given which
includes a broad landscape of particle physics models, encompassing the
non-supersymmetric 4D extensions of the standard model, and models based on
supersymmetry, on extra dimensions, on strings and on branes. The
supersymmetric models discussed include complex mSUGRA and its extensions,
while the models based on extra dimensions include 5D models including models
based on warped geometry. CP violation beyond the standard model is central to
achieving the desired amount of baryon asymmetry in the universe via
baryogenesis and leptogenesis. They also affect a variety of particle physics
phenomena: electric dipole moments, $g-2$, relic density and detection rates
for neutralino dark matter in supersymmetric theories, Yukawa unification in
grand unified and string based models, and sparticle production cross sections,
and their decays patterns and signatures at hadron colliders. Additionally CP
violations can generate CP even-CP odd Higgs mixings, affect the neutral Higgs
spectrum and lead to phenomena detectable at colliders. Prominent among these
are the CP violation effects in decays of neutral and charged Higgs bosons.
Neutrino masses introduce new sources of CP violation which will be explored in
neutrino factories in the future. Such phases can also enter in proton
stability in unified models of particle interactions. The current experimental
status of CP violation is discussed and possibilities for the future outlined.

In our work entitled "Sterile Neutrinos at the CNGS", we aim to investigate the possible existence of sterile neutrinos at the CERN Neutrinos to Gran Sasso (CNGS) beamline. Sterile neutrinos are hypothesized particles that do not interact via the weak force, making them impossible to detect using conventional means. However, they can be inferred from oscillation data through their impact on the active neutrino propagation.

Our methodology involves analyzing the data from a series of muon- and electron-neutrino disappearance experiments performed using the CNGS beamline. We employ a statistical analysis technique known as Bayesian inference to constrain the allowed parameter space for sterile neutrino oscillations. We also compare our results with those obtained from other experiments, including the Liquid Scintillator Neutrino Detector (LSND), the MiniBooNE experiment, and the Daya Bay reactor neutrino experiment.

The problem our work aims to solve is to address the persistent and inconclusive observations of a "sterile neutrino signal" in various neutrino experiments, which challenges the current understanding of neutrino oscillations and particle physics in general. Our results indicate that the data from the CNGS experiments are consistent with the hypothesis of sterile neutrino oscillations, with a significance level of 2.4σ. However, this result remains inconclusive, and further analysis and experiments are necessary to confirm or reject the existence of sterile neutrinos.

For some 100 years physics has modelled space and time via the spacetime
concept, with space being merely an observer dependent perspective effect of
that spacetime - space itself had no observer independent existence - it had no
ontological status, and it certainly had no dynamical description. In recent
years this has all changed. In 2002 it was discovered that a dynamical 3-space
had been detected many times, including the Michelson-Morley 1887 light-speed
anisotropy experiment. Here we review the dynamics of this 3-space, tracing its
evolution from that of an emergent phenomena in the information-theoretic
Process Physics to the phenomenological description in terms of a velocity
field describing the relative internal motion of the structured 3-space. The
new physics of the dynamical 3-space is extensively tested against experimental
and astronomical observations, including the necessary generalisation of the
Maxwell, Schrodinger and Dirac equations, leading to a derivation and
explanation of gravity as a refraction effect of quantum matter waves. The flat
and curved spacetime formalisms are derived from the new physics, so explaining
their apparent many successes.

We present an analysis of the transverse proximity effect on the spectral hardness observed along the line of sight towards the quasar HE 2347-4342. The transverse proximity effect refers to the influence that a nearby galaxy has on the absorption properties of gas at large distances, along the same line of sight towards a background quasar. It has been previously observed, but little is known about its influence on the spectral hardness of the radiation. 

Using data from the Faint Object Spectrograph on the Hubble Space Telescope, we studied the spectral shape and the transverse distribution of Lyα absorption along the line of sight towards HE 2347-4342 in the presence of a bright galaxy at a distance of 46 kpc. We found that the spectral hardness of the radiation is systematically different on either side of the galaxy, indicating that the transverse proximity effect alters the physical properties of the intervening gas. 

Our results suggest that the transverse proximity effect may be a powerful tool to study the gas in the intergalactic medium and the influence of nearby galaxies on its evolution. More detailed studies, using a larger sample of quasars and galaxies, will be required to fully understand the physical properties involved and the systematic effects that may be present.

In this paper, we present the results of our attempt to develop a new theoretical framework to study the solar-interior equation of state using the path-integral formalism. We explore the domain of validity of the framework proposed in our previous work and aim to identify the limitations of this approach. Our analysis shows that the path-integral formalism provides a viable alternative to traditional methods for the study of the equation of state, and can be used to gain insights into various physical phenomena related to the interior of the sun.

Our work builds on recent advances in the path-integral formalism, and takes inspiration from the rich history of research in this area.
