The purpose of our study was to contribute to the growing body of knowledge regarding the diversity of exoplanets and provide insight into the formation and evolution of planetary systems. The study also aimed to demonstrate the importance of combining different observing methods to discover and confirm exoplanets. 

Our methodology involved the use of the Transit Analysis Package, which employs MCMC algorithms to model the transit events and estimate the parameters of the exoplanets, such as their radii and periods. We also conducted a statistical analysis to compare the properties of the confirmed exoplanets with those of other known exoplanets. 

In conclusion, our investigation of transit-selected exoplanets from the MACHO survey has resulted in the confirmation of six exoplanet candidates, providing valuable information about the diversity of exoplanets and the methods used to detect them. Our study highlights the importance of utilizing multiple observational techniques to expand our understanding of exoplanetary systems and their properties.

In this research, we explore the discrete dipole approximation (DDA) for the simulation of light scattering by particles that are much larger than the wavelength. The motivation for this work stems from the need to better understand light interaction with complex particles in a variety of natural and man-made settings, such as atmospheric science, environmental science, and materials science. Such understanding can provide critical insights into the behavior and properties of these systems, which are important for applications ranging from remote sensing to biomedical imaging.

To achieve our goal, we first review the theoretical basis of DDA, which is a numerical method for solving the Maxwell equations for the interaction of light with particles. We then demonstrate the implementation of DDA in practical simulations of light scattering, including several examples of particles that are much larger than the wavelength of the incident light. Our results show good agreement with previous experimental and theoretical studies, indicating that DDA is a promising tool for studying light scattering by complex particles.

Overall, our work addresses the problem of simulating light scattering by particles that are much larger than the wavelength, which is a challenging problem in many scientific and engineering fields. We hope that our findings will contribute to the development of new applications and understanding in these areas.

In this study, we investigate the stretching behavior of homopolymers to understand the relationship between molecular details and macroscopic properties of polymers. The motivation of this research is to obtain a better understanding of the fundamental aspects of polymer physics, which may pave the way towards developing novel materials.

Our results show that the stretching behavior of homopolymers can be analyzed based on two different regimes, namely the linear and non-linear response. Additionally, we found that the elasticity of homopolymers is mainly controlled by the intrinsic properties of the chain, such as the degree of polymerization and the bond angle.

The key ideas behind our work are based on the application of statistical mechanics and continuum mechanics principles, as well as the use of computer simulations to analyze the stretching behavior of polymers. The methodology of our study involves the application of well-validated force fields to simulate the behavior of homopolymers under different stretching conditions.

Overall, our results provide insights into the fundamental properties of homopolymers, which can be leveraged in designing novel materials with tailored elasticity and strength. This work also paves the way towards a deeper understanding of the complex behavior of polymeric materials.

In this work, we investigate the concept of fuzzy extra dimensions, which offer a potential solution to some of the shortcomings of the standard model of particle physics. We explore the phenomenon of dimensional reduction in the context of fuzzy extra dimensions, which can provide a method for resolving the hierarchy problem in quantum field theory. Additionally, we investigate the possibility of dynamical generation of fuzzy extra dimensions, which could offer an explanation for the observed hierarchy of particle masses.

Furthermore, we examine the renormalizability of the model, which is a crucial aspect of any viable theory of particle physics. Specifically, we utilize a method known as the heat kernel technique to demonstrate the renormalizability of the theory and to calculate quantum corrections to the scalar field propagator.

The key idea behind the research is to introduce a novel model of extra dimensions that simultaneously addresses some of the outstanding issues in particle physics, such as the hierarchy problem and the nature of dark matter. The methodology involves a combination of analytical and numerical techniques to explore the behavior of the fuzzy extra dimensions in different scenarios.

The motivation for the research stems from the desire to understand the fundamental nature of the universe and to provide a theoretical framework that can explain a wide range of observations in particle physics and cosmology. This work represents a step towards achieving this goal by proposing a new theoretical concept and investigating its properties.

One of the major problems in computational biology is the inability of
existing classification models to incorporate expanding and new domain
knowledge. This problem of static classification models is addressed in this
paper by the introduction of incremental learning for problems in
bioinformatics. Many machine learning tools have been applied to this problem
using static machine learning structures such as neural networks or support
vector machines that are unable to accommodate new information into their
existing models. We utilize the fuzzy ARTMAP as an alternate machine learning
system that has the ability of incrementally learning new data as it becomes
available. The fuzzy ARTMAP is found to be comparable to many of the widespread
machine learning systems. The use of an evolutionary strategy in the selection
and combination of individual classifiers into an ensemble system, coupled with
the incremental learning ability of the fuzzy ARTMAP is proven to be suitable
as a pattern classifier. The algorithm presented is tested using data from the
G-Coupled Protein Receptors Database and shows good accuracy of 83%. The system
presented is also generally applicable, and can be used in problems in genomics
and proteomics.

In this study, we analyze the stability and formation of the HD 73526 resonant system, taking into account the effects of secular and resonant perturbations. The goal of this research is to understand the mechanisms that shape the orbital architectures of exoplanetary systems. The observed HD 73526 system consists of two Jupiter-like planets in an exact 2:1 mean-motion resonance, orbiting around a near-solar type star. 

We perform numerical simulations of the system's evolution over 10 million years, considering various initial conditions and perturbation strengths. Our simulations show that the HD 73526 system is dynamically stable and robust to secular and resonant perturbations. We find that the resonant commensurability between the two planets helps maintain their orbits stable over long periods of time. 

We also investigate the formation of the HD 73526 system using the N-body simulations with a disk of planetesimals as the initial condition. Our simulations suggest that the 2:1 resonance may have been formed as a result of the interaction between the planets and the disk of planetesimals. 

In conclusion, our study sheds light on the mechanisms that drive the formation and evolution of planetary resonant systems such as HD 73526. Our results contribute to the broader understanding of exoplanet systems, and can be useful for future observations and theories of planetary system formation.

In this work, we present a three-stage search methodology for detecting supermassive black hole binaries (SMBHBs) in data from the upcoming Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA) mission. The detection of SMBHBs is of great interest as they are believed to be key sources of gravitational waves in the universe. Our study aims to identify reliable and efficient methods for detecting SMBHBs in LISA data, which will help in precisely characterizing their properties and evolution.

First, we use a data-driven binary search approach to identify promising candidate SMBHBs. Next, we refine our search using a matched filtering method that identifies waveforms that fit more closely to the expected signals from SMBHBs. Finally, we utilize a Bayesian model selection process to evaluate the plausibility of each candidate and determine the most probable SMBHB signals.

Using simulations of noise and different SMBHB systems, we validate the effectiveness of our methodology. We demonstrate that our three-stage search approach can accurately and efficiently detect SMBHBs with varying properties in LISA data, and highlight the importance of incorporating Bayesian model selection into search pipelines. We believe that our methodology will be useful in the upcoming LISA mission to unravel the mysteries of SMBHBs and their role in the universe.

Air fluorescence detectors measure the energy of ultra-high energy cosmic
rays by collecting fluorescence light emitted from nitrogen molecules along the
extensive air shower cascade. To ensure a reliable energy determination, the
light signal needs to be corrected for atmospheric effects, which not only
attenuate the signal, but also produce a non-negligible background component
due to scattered Cherenkov light and multiple-scattered light. The correction
requires regular measurements of the aerosol attenuation length and the aerosol
phase function, defined as the probability of light scattered in a given
direction. At the Pierre Auger Observatory in Malargue, Argentina, the phase
function is measured on an hourly basis using two Aerosol Phase Function (APF)
light sources. These sources direct a UV light beam across the field of view of
the fluorescence detectors; the phase function can be extracted from the image
of the shots in the fluorescence detector cameras. This paper describes the
design, current status, standard operation procedure, and performance of the
APF system at the Pierre Auger Observatory.

In this paper, we present a novel and comprehensive approach to studying the (co)cyclic (co)homology of bialgebroids via (co)monads. Bialgebroids are algebras with two compatible structures, an associative algebra and a coalgebra. Understanding their (co)cyclic (co)homology is crucial for developing algebraic tools to study noncommutative geometry and quantum field theory. 

Our approach involves constructing a category of (co)monads over bialgebroids and studying their homology and cohomology. We show that (co)monads naturally encode (co)cyclic (co)homology data, and provide a detailed calculation of this data for a specific class of bialgebroids. 

Our methodology is based on a combination of algebraic and homological techniques, including the use of differential graded bicomplexes, spectral sequences, and categorical language. We also make use of the recent theory of twisted (co)homology to better understand the global structure of the (co)cyclic (co)homology data.

Our main result is the construction of a spectral sequence that calculates the (co)cyclic (co)homology of any bialgebroid in terms of the homology and cohomology of the category of (co)monads over that bialgebroid. This provides a powerful and versatile tool for calculating (co)cyclic (co)homology in a broad range of applications. We conclude by discussing some potential future directions for this line of research.

In this work, we present the kinematic design of a three-degrees-of-freedom hybrid manipulator consisting of a revolute joint, a prismatic joint, and a spherical joint. The main objective of this work is to develop a manipulator that can perform complex tasks which require high dexterity and precision. To achieve this objective, we employed the Denavit-Hartenberg convention to derive the kinematic equations of the manipulator that relate the joint variables to the end-effector position and orientation.

We then utilized the inverse kinematics approach to solve for the joint variables that will achieve a desired end-effector position and orientation. We evaluated the manipulator's workspace and analyzed its singularities and deviations to ensure that it can perform the desired task while avoiding collisions and joint limit violation. 

This work's primary contribution is to provide a comprehensive solution to the kinematic design of a hybrid manipulator that can be used in applications such as robotic surgery, manufacturing, and inspection. The motivation behind this research is the need for robots that can perform delicate and complex tasks that are challenging for humans to execute. 

In conclusion, our proposed design has the potential to improve precision and productivity while reducing the risk of human error in various domains. The results of this work can serve as a basis for future developments in the field of robotics.

The properties of galaxies are strongly correlated with their environment,
with red galaxies dominating galaxy clusters and blue galaxies dominating the
general field. However, not all field galaxies are young: studies of the
colors, line strengths, and M/L ratios of massive early-type galaxies at
0<z<1.3 show that the most massive galaxies do not seem to care about their
surroundings, and have very similar ages irrespective of their environment.
There is good evidence that the growth of these galaxies does continue longer
in the field than in clusters, via (nearly) dissipationless mergers of already
old galaxies. These results are consistent with predictions of recent galaxy
formation models, which incorporate AGN feedback to suppress star formation in
the most massive halos. Systematic studies of the relation of galaxies with
their environment beyond z=1 are difficult, and still somewhat contradictory.
Intriguingly both the DEEP2 and VVDS surveys find that the color-density
relation disappears at z~1.3, unfortunately just at the point where both
surveys become highly incomplete. On the other hand, clustering studies at
z~2.5 have shown that red galaxies cluster more strongly than blue galaxies,
implying that the color-density relation was already in place at that redshift.

This paper presents the implementation and performance analysis of Block Locally Optimal Preconditioned Eigenvalue Xolvers (BLOPEX) in hypre and PETSc libraries. The motivation for this work stems from the need to solve large and sparse eigenvalue problems arising in scientific and engineering applications. These problems are computationally intensive and often require the solution of a large number of eigenvalues and vectors. The traditional approach to solving these problems relies on iterative solvers, and the choice of a suitable preconditioner is critical for their convergence and efficiency. BLOPEX offers a flexible and effective framework for constructing preconditioners that take advantage of the spectral properties of the underlying matrix. 

The main objective of this work is to evaluate the performance of BLOPEX preconditioners in the context of the hypre and PETSc libraries, two widely used software packages for solving linear systems and eigenvalue problems. We assess the scalability, robustness, and accuracy of BLOPEX, as well as its impact on the overall solution time. Our experiments show that BLOPEX can significantly improve the convergence and efficiency of iterative solvers for a wide range of eigenvalue problems. The key ideas behind BLOPEX are to split the preconditioner construction into small, tractable problems based on local matrix blocks and to exploit the Krylov subspace framework for solving the resulting eigenvalue problems. Overall, our work highlights the potential of BLOPEX as a powerful tool for solving large-scale eigenvalue problems.

The approximants xi, xi' and xi'_n of the quasicrystal Al-Mn-Pd display most
interesting plastic properties as for example phason-induced deformation
processes (Klein, H., Audier, M., Boudard, M., de Boissieu, M., Beraha, L., and
Duneau, M., 1996, Phil. Mag. A, 73, 309.) or metadislocations (Klein, H.,
Feuerbacher, M., Schall, P., and Urban, K., 1999, Phys. Rev. Lett., 82, 3468.).
Here we demonstrate that the phases and their deformed or defected states can
be described by a simple projection formalism in three-dimensional space - not
as usual in four to six dimensions. With the method we can interpret
microstructures observed with electron microscopy as phasonic phase boundaries.
Furthermore we determine the metadislocations of lowest energy and relate them
uniquely to experimentally observed ones. Since moving metadislocations in the
xi'-phase can create new phason-planes, we suggest a dislocation induced phase
transition from xi' to xi'_n. The methods developed in this paper can as well
be used for various other complex metallic alloys.

In this study, we investigate the existence and properties of stationary symmetric and asymmetric solitons, in nonlinear linearly coupled Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs) held in optical lattices. Our motivation is the potential significance of such solitons in the development of controllable light-based devices and nonlinear optics, as well as the emergence of interesting phenomena, such as self-trapping and quantum tunneling, in BECs. The research problem we are attempting to solve is to locate the critical parameters that govern the stability of the solitons, as well as to map out their evolution and interaction. We solve the coupled Gross-Pitaevskii equations (CGPE) that govern the condensates, using complex numerical simulations. Our results reveal that the symmetric and asymmetric solitons can be generated and stabilized, with their sizes and shapes determined by the strengths and nature of the coupling and the lattice potential. Our analysis shows that the solitons exhibit different behaviors, depending on the degree of the coupling. In particular, an intermediate coupling strength may generate a transition from stable to unstable solitons, leading to anti-phase oscillations and periodic collapses. Our findings thus illustrate the richness and complexity of the dynamics of coupled BECs in lattices, and may inform the design of novel nonlinear photonic devices.

In this paper, we deal with a generalization of the geometry of
parallelizable manifolds, or the absolute parallelism (AP-) geometry, in the
context of generalized Lagrange spaces. All geometric objects defined in this
geometry are not only functions of the positional argument $x$, but also depend
on the directional argument $y$. In other words, instead of dealing with
geometric objects defined on the manifold $M$, as in the case of classical
AP-geometry, we are dealing with geometric objects in the pullback bundle
$\pi^{-1}(TM)$ (the pullback of the tangent bundle $TM$ by $ \pi: T
M\longrightarrow M$). Many new geometric objects, which have no counterpart in
the classical AP-geometry, emerge in this more general context. We refer to
such a geometry as generalized AP-geometry (GAP-geometry). In analogy to
AP-geometry, we define a $d$-connection in $\pi^{-1}(TM)$ having remarkable
properties, which we call the canonical $d$-connection, in terms of the unique
torsion-free Riemannian $d$-connection. In addition to these two
$d$-connections, two more $d$-connections are defined, the dual and the
symmetric $d$-connections. Our space, therefore, admits twelve curvature
tensors (corresponding to the four defined $d$-connections), three of which
vanish identically. Simple formulae for the nine non-vanishing curvatures
tensors are obtained, in terms of the torsion tensors of the canonical
$d$-connection. The different $W$-tensors admitted by the space are also
calculated. All contractions of the $h$- and $v$-curvature tensors and the
$W$-tensors are derived. Second rank symmetric and skew-symmetric tensors,
which prove useful in physical applications, are singled out.

This article is a thorough critique to the Weniger's comments made to our
papers published in prestigious journals in the recent years. A detailed and
critical examination of the arguments that led to the suggested comment by
Weniger reveals some serious flaws. In our published papers we have shown that
the unsymmetrical and symmetrical one-range addition theorems for Slater type
orbitals, Coulomb-Yukawa like correlated interaction potentials (CIPs) and
their derivatives are derived from the expansions in terms of -ETOs that are
complete and orthonormal sets of exponential type orbitals in corresponding
Hilbert spaces, where The concrete criticism raised in Weniger's comment
against our papers actually touches a very minor aspect of the works that are
not relevant at all for the conclusions, which are made. As can be seen from
our papers, all of the formulas for different kinds of multicenter integrals
over Slater type orbitals with integer and noninteger principal quantum numbers
obtained by the use of unsymmetrical and symmetrical one-range addition
theorems were tested by computer calculations. We reject the Weniger's personal
views about papers published by Guseinov and his coworkers from 1978 to 2006
and respectable referees on one-range addition theorems and multicenter
integrals. All claims of inconsistencies and flaws in the theoretical framework
are rejected as unfounded. This rejoinder paper contains all of the answers to
Weniger's comments.

Current status of TAMA and CLIO detectors in Japan is reported in this
article. These two interferometric gravitational-wave detectors are being
developed for the large cryogenic gravitational wave telescope (LCGT) which is
a future plan for detecting gravitational wave signals at least once per year.
  TAMA300 is being upgraded to improve the sensitivity in low frequency region
after the last observation experiment in 2004. To reduce the seismic noises, we
are installing new seismic isolation system, which is called TAMA Seismic
Attenuation System, for the four test masses. We confirmed stable mass locks of
a cavity and improvements of length and angular fluctuations by using two SASs.
We are currently optimizing the performance of the third and fourth SASs. We
continue TAMA300 operation and R&D studies for LCGT. Next data taking in the
summer of 2007 is planned.
  CLIO is a 100-m baseline length prototype detector for LCGT to investigate
interferometer performance in cryogenic condition. The key features of CLIO are
that it locates Kamioka underground site for low seismic noise level, and
adopts cryogenic Sapphire mirrors for low thermal noise level. The first
operation of the cryogenic interferometer was successfully demonstrated in
February of 2006. Current sensitivity at room temperature is close to the
target sensitivity within a factor of 4. Several observation experiments at
room temperature have been done. Once the displacement noise reaches at thermal
noise level of room temperature, its improvement by cooling test mass mirrors
should be demonstrated.

At least four high-frequency quasiperiodic oscillations (QPOs) at frequencies
41Hz, 67Hz, 113Hz, and 167Hz were reported in a binary system GRS 1915+105
hosting near-extreme Kerr black hole with a dimensionless spin a>0.98. We use
the idea of oscillations induced by the hump of the orbital velocity profile
(related to locally non-rotating frames - LNRF) in discs orbiting near-extreme
Kerr black holes, which are characterized by a "humpy frequency" f_h, that
could excite the radial and vertical epicyclic oscillations with frequencies
f_r, f_v. Due to non-linear resonant phenomena the combinational frequencies
are allowed as well. Assuming mass M=14.8M_sun and spin a=0.9998 for the GRS
1915+105 Kerr black hole, the model predicts frequencies f_h=41Hz, f_r=67Hz,
(f_h+f_r)=108Hz, (f_v-f_r)=170Hz corresponding quite well to the observed ones.
For black-hole parameters being in good agreement with those given
observationally, the forced resonant phenomena in non-linear oscillations,
excited by the "hump-induced" oscillations in a Keplerian disc, can explain
high-frequency QPOs in GRS 1915+105 within the range of observational errors.

In this study, we investigate the gravitational acceleration of quantum vacuum energy, specifically the Casimir energy. While there have been previous studies on the Casimir energy, there has been little exploration of how it falls under the influence of gravity. We focus on analyzing the behavior of a perfect conductor plate system under different gravitational conditions using the Dirac equation. We observe that the Casimir energy falls with gravitational acceleration, contrary to the usual behavior of energy in free fall where there is no clear direction.

Our motivation for this research stems from the theoretical implications of understanding the behavior of energy in the strong gravitational field. Moreover, this study further aids in developing our understanding of the Casimir effect, which has a range of practical applications, from efficient energy harvesting to building nanoscale machines. By studying the interaction between the Casimir energy and gravity, we can also gain insight into the more fundamental mechanisms behind gravity and possibly develop a better understanding of the gravitational force.

Our results indicate that there is indeed a gravitational acceleration of the Casimir energy, and this effect can be understood through the behavior of the solutions to the Dirac equation in a gravitational field. This study provides new and important insights into both the behavior of quantum vacuum energy in the presence of gravity and the more fundamental mechanisms behind the laws of gravitation.

In this work, we investigate the connections between self-dual vertex operator super algebras (SDVOSAs) and the Baby Monster, which is the largest sporadic simple group in mathematics. SDVOSAs are algebraic structures that play a significant role in the study of mathematical physics, while the Baby Monster has profound implications in group theory and algebraic geometry. We aim to establish a correspondence between these two seemingly disparate areas of research.

To achieve our goal, we use techniques from group theory, representation theory, and vertex operator algebras. We show that there exists a one-to-one correspondence between certain types of SDVOSAs and certain subgroups of the Baby Monster. Moreover, we develop an algorithm to construct SDVOSAs from given subgroups of the Baby Monster and vice versa.

Our results have implications not only for the study of SDVOSAs and the Baby Monster but also for their applications in other fields of mathematics and physics. The key idea behind our work is to understand the relationship between algebraic structures and groups by establishing a correspondence between them. This approach allows us to leverage the tools and techniques developed in one area to shed light on problems in another area.

Overall, our work contributes to the understanding of the deep connections between seemingly disparate areas of mathematics, and we hope that it will inspire further research in this direction.

In this paper, it is shown that the cosmological model that was introduced in
a sequence of three earlier papers under the title, A Dust Universe Solution to
the Dark Energy Problem can be used to analyse and solve the Cosmological
Coincidence Problem. The generic coincidence problem that appears in the
original Einstein universe model is shown to arise from a misunderstanding
about the magnitude of dark energy density and the epoch time governing the
appearance of the integer relation between dark energy and normal energy
density. The solution to the generic case then clearly points to the source of
the time coincidence integer problem in the Friedman dust universe model. It is
then possible to eliminate this coincidence by removing a degeneracy between
different measurement epoch times. In this paper's first appendix, a
fundamental time dependent relation between dark mass and dark energy is
derived with suggestions how this relation could explain cosmological voids and
the clumping of dark mass to become visible matter. In this paper's second
appendix, it is shown that that dark energy is a conserved with time substance
that is everywhere and for all time permeable to the dark mass and visible mass
of which the contracting or expanding universe is composed. The last two
appendices involve detailed studies of cosmology, quantum dark energy related
issues. There are more detailed abstracts given with all four appendices.

In our research, we investigate the Exact Floquet States (EFS) of a driven condensate in an optical lattice. Our motivation is to study the properties of out-of-equilibrium quantum systems driven by an external periodic force. We aim to provide a new approach to understanding the stability of EFS, which are states in the driven system that preserve the periodicity of the driving force. These states can play a crucial role in the dynamics of the system and provide a useful tool for studying quantum systems out of equilibrium.

The key idea of our approach is to use a Floquet-Magnus expansion to calculate the stability properties of the EFS. We then use a variational method to approximate the EFS solutions in the strong driving regime. Our methodology combines analytical and numerical methods to obtain solutions for the unstable EFS.

Our research attempts to solve the problem of the stability of EFS, which is a crucial issue in the study of quantum systems driven by external forces. Our results reveal the existence of unstable EFS and provide a new approach for studying the dynamics of driven quantum systems beyond linear response theory. The insights gained from our research can potentially guide the design of experiments on driven condensates and pave the way for developing new applications in quantum information processing and quantum simulation.

In this research, we explore the concept of the Dynamical Discrete Web (DDW), a powerful mathematical tool that has been shown to accurately model a wide range of dynamical systems. Our motivation stems from the limitations of traditional continuous models that can break down in certain situations, leading to inaccuracies and errors in predictions. The DDW, on the other hand, is capable of describing complex dynamical processes that exhibit non-linear behavior with high precision and accuracy. 

The main problem that this work seeks to address is the ability to represent dynamical systems using discrete webs while preserving their key properties. We employ topological methods in order to construct a DDW that is faithful to the underlying system, and we introduce a novel approach to compute invariant vectors using the Laplacian matrix. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of the DDW in modeling complex dynamical systems and highlight its potential to improve the accuracy of predictions in a wide range of fields, from climate modeling to finance and biochemistry. Overall, this research represents a significant step forward in our understanding of the interplay between topology and dynamics, and opens up new avenues for future investigations in this area.

In this work, we explore the imprint of distortions in the Oort cloud on the cosmic microwave background (CMB) anisotropies. It is known that the Oort cloud is a dynamic region of the outer solar system that contains a large number of comets and icy debris. This region is affected by gravitational perturbations from large, distant objects such as other stars and galactic tides. As a result, the Oort cloud can undergo deformations in its spherical symmetry.

Our methodology consists of analyzing the potential impact of the Oort cloud's shape and orientation on the CMB anisotropies. We consider different models of distortion, such as oblateness, prolateness, and axial misalignment, and assess their effects on the CMB temperature and polarization maps.

Our analysis reveals that distortions in the Oort cloud can induce non-Gaussian features, such as localized hot and cold spots, in the CMB maps. These features can be potentially observed and used to constrain the properties of the Oort cloud. We show that future CMB experiments, such as the Simons Observatory and CMB-S4, can provide significant improvements in the detection and characterization of these distortions.

Our work addresses the problem of understanding the impact of small-scale structures in the solar system on the large-scale structure of the universe. By examining the imprint of the Oort cloud on the CMB anisotropies, we provide a new avenue to probe the properties and dynamics of this remote region.

In this work, we investigate the in-plane structure and ordering at liquid sodium surfaces and interfaces using ab initio molecular dynamics simulations. The motivation for this research is rooted in the lack of knowledge regarding the local organization of liquid sodium at its surfaces and interfaces, despite the abundance of experimental and theoretical studies on bulk sodium. Understanding the structural and ordering properties of liquid sodium is critical for the development of sodium-based energy systems, such as sodium-ion batteries.

Our simulations reveal that the local structure of liquid sodium at its surface is different from that of the bulk, with a preference for the formation of compact atomic clusters. Moreover, we find that the structure of the interface between liquid sodium and a graphite surface is determined by the mutual influence of surface reconstruction and ionic ordering, resulting in a periodic stacking of sodium layers on graphite. Our findings provide valuable insights into the behavior of liquid sodium at its surfaces and interfaces, with implications for the design and optimization of sodium-based energy systems.

The key ideas and methodology in our research involve the application of ab initio molecular dynamics, which provides a first-principles understanding of complex systems without the need for experimental data. Our simulations were performed using density functional theory, and the resulting electronic and atomic structures were analyzed using visualization and statistical tools. Our approach provides a powerful and efficient means of exploring complex liquid-solid interfaces for a broad range of materials.

In this work, we investigate the clustering features of $^9$Be, $^{14}$N, $^7$Be, and $^8$B nuclei in relativistic fragmentation. The motivation for this research lies in the fact that such nuclei exhibit interesting nuclear structure properties, such as enhanced clustering and exotic decay patterns. Our aim is to map out the clustering patterns of these nuclei in relativistic fragmentation, which is a complex process involving the breakup of highly excited nuclei in high energy collisions.

To achieve this aim, we use the event generator UrQMD (Ultra-relativistic Quantum Molecular Dynamics), which is designed to model the entire collision dynamics from the initial overlap of two nuclei to the final-state fragmentation products. We focus on central collisions of $^{197}$Au+$^{197}$Au at beam energies of 400 and 800 MeV/nucleon. We analyze the final-state fragment spectra and identify the clustering patterns using the statistical tool of principal component analysis (PCA).

Our results show that the PCA method is a powerful tool for identifying clustering patterns, even in highly complex systems such as relativistic fragmentation. We find that the $^9$Be and $^{14}$N nuclei exhibit clear clustering patterns, whereas the $^7$Be and $^8$B nuclei show weaker clustering tendencies. Overall, our work contributes to a deeper understanding of nuclear clustering and fragmentation in high energy collisions and provides insights into the nuclear forces that drive these processes.

In our work, we investigate a problem that has remained unsolved in the field of theoretical physics: the existence of forbidden territories in the string landscape. The string landscape encompasses all possible configurations of strings within a theoretical space. Our research aims to identify areas within this space where the laws of physics break down, rendering them forbidden to explore. 

To tackle this issue, we have employed mathematical models that allow us to map the landscape with greater accuracy. Through careful analysis of these models, we were able to identify potential forbidden territories that had not previously been detected by other researchers. Our findings confirm that such territories do indeed exist, and suggest that they may be more prevalent than previously believed.

The motivation for our research stems from the desire to gain a deeper understanding of the underlying principles that govern the universe. We believe that by identifying and exploring these forbidden territories, we may be able to uncover new insights into the nature of the universe and the fundamental laws that govern it. Ultimately, our work has the potential to contribute to the ongoing quest to develop a unified theory of everything in the world of theoretical physics.

In this work, we investigate the structural relaxation around substitutional Cr3+ in MgAl2O4 by means of Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations. The problem we are trying to solve is understanding how the substitution of chromium atoms for aluminum atoms in the spinel structure of MgAl2O4 affects the structure and the electronic properties of the material. Since Cr3+ is known to be a promising candidate for various technological applications, such as catalysis and superconductivity, it is essential to have a deep understanding of its behavior in a spinel structure. 

Our results show that the substitution of Cr3+ for Al3+ induces a considerable relaxation of the neighboring atoms, leading to a significant decrease in the bond lengths between the Cr3+ and its nearest-neighbor oxygen atoms. The substitution also leads to the formation of local distortions in the crystal structure that can affect the magnetic and electronic properties of the material. We have identified the key features of the relaxation behavior, and our study provides insights into how the relaxation process affects the stability and properties of the Cr3+ dopant. Our methodology involves DFT calculations on periodic supercells using the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package, and we have employed various visualization tools to explain the results. Our study paves the way for a better understanding of the interaction between substitutional dopants and their host materials in spinel structures.

We study scaling relations of compressible strongly magnetized turbulence. We
find a good correspondence of our results with the Fleck (1996) model of
compressible hydrodynamic turbulence. In particular, we find that the
density-weighted velocity, i.e. $u \equiv \rho^{1/3} v$, proposed in Kritsuk et
al. (2007) obeys the Kolmogorov scaling, i.e. $E_{u}(k)\sim k^{-5/3}$ for the
high Mach number turbulence. Similarly, we find that the exponents of the third
order structure functions for $u$ stay equal to unity for the all the Mach
numbers studied. The scaling of higher order correlations obeys the She-Leveque
(1994) scalings corresponding to the two-dimensional dissipative structures,
and this result does not change with the Mach number either. In contrast to $v$
which exhibits different scaling parallel and perpendicular to the local
magnetic field, the scaling of $u$ is similar in both directions. In addition,
we find that the peaks of density create a hierarchy in which both physical and
column densities decrease with the scale in accordance to the Fleck (1996)
predictions. This hierarchy can be related ubiquitous small ionized and neutral
structures (SINS) in the interstellar gas. We believe that studies of
statistics of the column density peaks can provide both consistency check for
the turbulence velocity studies and insight into supersonic turbulence, when
the velocity information is not available.

In this study, we aim to investigate the enrichment of the interstellar medium (ISM) by metal-rich droplets and its effect on the abundance bias in HII regions. The motivation for this research stems from the fact that the presence of metal-rich droplets can significantly impact the chemical evolution of the ISM and the formation of stars and galaxies. 

We used a multidisciplinary approach that involved hydrodynamic simulations and analytical modeling to study the impact of metal-rich droplets on the ISM. Our results show that the presence of these droplets can lead to the enrichment of the ISM with heavy elements and play a critical role in shaping the chemical abundance patterns in HII regions. Interestingly, we found that the abundance bias in HII regions can be significantly altered by metal-rich droplets. 

Our study also shed light on the formation and evolution of metal-rich droplets and their association with supernova remnants and other astrophysical phenomena. The key idea behind our research is to provide an improved understanding of the chemical evolution of the ISM and the formation of stars and galaxies. 

Our findings have significant implications for the study of galaxy evolution and the role of metal-rich droplets in shaping the chemical diversity of the Universe. Overall, our study provides a novel perspective on the enrichment of the ISM by metal-rich droplets, and its impact on the chemical evolution of the Universe.

Previous LHC forecasts for the constrained minimal supersymmetric standard
model (CMSSM), based on current astrophysical and laboratory measurements, have
used priors that are flat in the parameter tan beta, while being constrained to
postdict the central experimental value of MZ. We construct a different, new
and more natural prior with a measure in mu and B (the more fundamental MSSM
parameters from which tan beta and MZ are actually derived). We find that as a
consequence this choice leads to a well defined fine-tuning measure in the
parameter space. We investigate the effect of such on global CMSSM fits to
indirect constraints, providing posterior probability distributions for Large
Hadron Collider (LHC) sparticle production cross sections. The change in priors
has a significant effect, strongly suppressing the pseudoscalar Higgs boson
dark matter annihilation region, and diminishing the probable values of
sparticle masses. We also show how to interpret fit information from a Markov
Chain Monte Carlo in a frequentist fashion; namely by using the profile
likelihood. Bayesian and frequentist interpretations of CMSSM fits are compared
and contrasted.

In this study, we present the chemical analysis based on high-resolution optical spectra of two post-Asymptotic Giant Branch (AGB) stars, IRAS08281-4850 and IRAS14325-6428, with a spectral type of A and an unusual s-process enrichment. We aimed to investigate the chemical abundances of these two stars and to understand how they were enriched with s-process elements. Our methodology involved obtaining spectra from the Anglo-Australian Telescope and analysing them with spectral synthesis programs. We found that IRAS08281-4850 has a metallicity of [Fe/H]=−0.61±0.13 and a strong s-process enrichment, especially for elements with an atomic number greater than 56. IRAS14325-6428, on the other hand, has a lower metallicity of [Fe/H]=−1.75±0.14 and a relatively weaker s-process enrichment than IRAS08281-4850. Our analysis revealed that the chemical abundances of both stars were consistent with a scenario in which s-process elements were produced in an intermediate-mass AGB star. This study provides additional evidence for the complex and diverse chemical evolution of post-AGB stars and contributes to the understanding of their formation and evolution.

We present a study focused on the mass and motion of the Local Volume through a constrained simulation that aims to reproduce the observed properties of the galaxy distribution. The Local Volume (LV) is a region of the universe that contains about thirty galaxies within a radius of approximately 10 Mpc around the Milky Way. Available observations in the LV have provided insights into the large-scale structure of the universe, including the location and motion of galaxies and the amount of matter that contributes to their gravitational fields. Our work uses a constrained simulation technique that imposes observational constraints on the simulation to produce a more realistic description of the local universe. By adjusting the initial conditions of the simulation, we ensure that it reproduces important observables, such as the distribution of matter and the velocities of the galaxies in the LV. The main aim of this research is to achieve a better understanding of the mechanisms that shape the structure of the LV and the broader universe. Our findings present a significant step forward in constraining theoretical models of galaxy formation and evolution.

The Katz-Sarnak density conjecture states that, in the limit as the
conductors tend to infinity, the behavior of normalized zeros near the central
point of families of L-functions agree with the N -> oo scaling limits of
eigenvalues near 1 of subgroups of U(N). Evidence for this has been found for
many families by studying the n-level densities; for suitably restricted test
functions the main terms agree with random matrix theory. In particular, all
one-parameter families of elliptic curves with rank r over Q(T) and the same
distribution of signs of functional equations have the same limiting behavior.
We break this universality and find family dependent lower order correction
terms in many cases; these lower order terms have applications ranging from
excess rank to modeling the behavior of zeros near the central point, and
depend on the arithmetic of the family. We derive an alternate form of the
explicit formula for GL(2) L-functions which simplifies comparisons, replacing
sums over powers of Satake parameters by sums of the moments of the Fourier
coefficients lambda_f(p). Our formula highlights the differences that we expect
to exist from families whose Fourier coefficients obey different laws (for
example, we expect Sato-Tate to hold only for non-CM families of elliptic
curves). Further, by the work of Rosen and Silverman we expect lower order
biases to the Fourier coefficients in families of elliptic curves with rank
over Q(T); these biases can be seen in our expansions. We analyze several
families of elliptic curves and see different lower order corrections,
depending on whether or not the family has complex multiplication, a forced
torsion point, or non-zero rank over Q(T).

We present analysis of archival data from multiple XMM-Newton observations of
the Seyfert 1 galaxy NGC 4151. Spectral data from the RGS instruments reveal
several strong soft X-ray emission lines, chiefly from hydrogen-like and
helium-like oxygen, nitrogen, neon and carbon. Radiative recombination continua
(RRC) from oxygen and carbon are also detected. Our analysis suggests that the
emission data are consistent with photoionization. Using the CLOUDY
photoionization code, we found that, while a two-component, high column density
model (10e23 cm-2) with low covering factor proved adequate in reproducing all
detected Lyman series lines, it proved insufficient in modeling He-like
triplets observed (neon, oxygen, and nitrogen). If resonance line data were
ignored, the two-component model was sufficient to match flux from
intercombination and forbidden lines. However, with the inclusion of resonance
line data, He-like triplets could no longer be modeled with only two
components. We found that observed oxygen G and R ratios especially were
anomalous in parameter space investigated. We investigated, and were forced to
dismiss, the possibility that a third purely collisional component could be
responsible for enhanced resonance line contributions. We succeeded in modeling
the observed spectrum with the addition of a third, lower column density
(10e20.5 cm-2) component with non-zero microturbulence and high covering
factor. While sufficient to reproduce observed soft X-ray flux, our model faces
certain shortcomings, particularly in a less-than-ideal visual fit to the line
profile. Two of the three emission model components bear similarities to
components determined by Kraemer et al. (2005) in their study of NGC 4151
absorption spectra.

We explore the cosmological content of Salam-Sezgin six dimensional
supergravity, and find a solution to the field equations in qualitative
agreement with observation of distant supernovae, primordial nucleosynthesis
abundances, and recent measurements of the cosmic microwave background. The
carrier of the acceleration in the present de Sitter epoch is a quintessence
field slowly rolling down its exponential potential. Intrinsic to this model is
a second modulus which is automatically stabilized and acts as a source of cold
dark matter with a mass proportional to an exponential function of the
quintessence field (hence realizing VAMP models within a String context).
However, any attempt to saturate the present cold dark matter component in this
manner leads to unacceptable deviations from cosmological data -- a numerical
study reveals that this source can account for up to about 7% of the total cold
dark matter budget. We also show that (1) the model will support a de Sitter
energy in agreement with observation at the expense of a miniscule breaking of
supersymmetry in the compact space; (2) variations in the fine structure
constant are controlled by the stabilized modulus and are negligible; (3)
``fifth''forces are carried by the stabilized modulus and are short range; (4)
the long time behavior of the model in four dimensions is that of a
Robertson-Walker universe with a constant expansion rate (w = -1/3). Finally,
we present a String theory background by lifting our six dimensional
cosmological solution to ten dimensions.

We present an analysis of high resolution VLT-FLAMES spectra of 61 B-type
stars with relatively narrow-lined spectra located in 4 fields centered on the
Milky Way clusters; NGC3293 & NGC4755 and the Large and Small Magellanic cloud
clusters; NGC2004 and NGC330. For each object a quantitative analysis was
carried out using the non-LTE model atmosphere code TLUSTY; resulting in the
determination of their atmospheric parameters and photospheric abundances of
the dominant metal species (C, N, O, Mg, Si, Fe). The results are discussed in
relation to our earlier work on 3 younger clusters in these galaxies; NGC6611,
N11 and NGC346 paying particular attention to the nitrogen abundances which are
an important probe of the role of rotation in the evolution of stars. This work
along with that of the younger clusters provides a consistent dataset of
abundances and atmospheric parameters for over 100 B-type stars in the three
galaxies. We provide effective temperature scales for B-type dwarfs in all
three galaxies and for giants and supergiants in the SMC and LMC. In each
galaxy a dependence on luminosity is found between the three classes with the
unevolved dwarf objects having significantly higher effective temperatures. A
metallicity dependence is present between the SMC and Galactic dwarf objects,
and whilst the LMC stars are only slightly cooler than the SMC stars, they are
significantly hotter than their Galactic counterparts.

In our work, we explore the new functionalities of microlasers and optoelectronic components through the creation and study of photonic molecules made of matched and mismatched microcavities. Our motivation lies in the potential to achieve enhanced control over light-matter interactions, leading to greater design flexibility and increased device performance. 

Our approach involves using semiconductor-based microlasers and microcavities, with a variety of shapes and sizes, to construct larger structures with novel optical properties. By carefully controlling the spacing and coupling between the individual microcavities, we form photonic molecules that exhibit novel resonance effects, including anticrossing and supermode splitting. 

Through experimental and theoretical analysis, we aim to understand the underlying physics of these new structures and the mechanisms that give rise to their unique optical behavior. Furthermore, we investigate the potential applications of these photonic molecules, such as ultra-compact sensors, integrated photonic circuits, and advanced optical signal processing. 

Our work represents a significant step towards the development of highly functional and efficient optoelectronic devices, with potential uses ranging from telecommunications to medical imaging. By leveraging the power of photonic molecules, we unlock new possibilities for manipulating light at the nanoscale.

We explore the initial conditions for cosmological N-body simulations
suitable for calculating the skewness and kurtosis of the density field. In
general, the initial conditions based on the perturbation theory (PT) provide
incorrect second-order and higher-order growth. These errors implied by the use
of the perturbation theory to set up the initial conditions in N-body
simulations are called transients. Unless these transients are completely
suppressed compared with the dominant growing mode, we can not reproduce the
correct evolution of cumulants with orders higher than two, even though there
is no problem with the numerical scheme. We investigate the impact of
transients on the observable statistical quantities by performing $N$-body
simulations with initial conditions based on Lagrangian perturbation theory
(LPT). We show that the effects of transients on the kurtosis from the initial
conditions, based on second-order Lagrangian perturbation theory (2LPT) have
almost disappeared by $z\sim5$, as long as the initial conditions are set at $z
> 30$. This means that for practical purposes, the initial conditions based on
2LPT are accurate enough for numerical calculations of skewness and kurtosis.

In this work, we present the results obtained from the observation of the South-West extension of the hard X-ray emission from the Coma cluster. The motivation for this research was to study the non-thermal processes associated with the large-scale structure of the Coma cluster, which is one of the largest known clusters of galaxies in the local Universe. The emission mechanism of this non-thermal X-ray radiation has been attributed to the inverse Compton scattering of cosmic microwave background photons by relativistic electrons in the intracluster medium (ICM). 

By analyzing the X-ray data obtained by the INTEGRAL satellite, we discovered a significant extension of the hard X-ray emission of the Coma cluster towards the South-West direction. We found that the spatial distribution of the emission aligned well with the radio relics, which suggests that the same population of relativistic electrons that produce the radio emission are also responsible for the X-ray emission. Additionally, we found that the X-ray spectrum of the extension was significantly harder than the spectra of the other X-ray features in the cluster.

Our results shed light on the physics of the intracluster medium, the origin of the relativistic electrons, and the mechanisms of injection, acceleration, and propagation of high-energy particles in the Coma cluster. This work attempts to solve the problem of the origin of the hard X-ray emission in the Coma cluster, which has been poorly understood so far.

In this work, we explore the implications of a newly predicted value for the branching ratio of the decay B -> Xs gamma, also known as b to s gamma, on the Constrained Minimal Supersymmetric Standard Model (CMSSM). The CMSSM is a popular extension of the Standard Model of particle physics that seeks to explain the existence of dark matter and other phenomena not accounted for by the Standard Model. However, experiments have failed to find evidence for supersymmetry at energy scales accessible to current accelerators. Nevertheless, it remains an active area of research since it is an elegant and theoretically attractive extension of the Standard Model.

We find that the new predicted value for the branching ratio of B -> Xs gamma conflicts with the previously favored CMSSM parameter space, leading to a need for extensions to the CMSSM or new physics. We use a combination of theoretical calculations and numerical simulations to obtain our results. Our methodology involves computing the precise impact of the new b to s gamma prediction on various observables, such as Higgs boson masses and the dark matter abundance, within the CMSSM. We also carefully consider systematic uncertainties in our calculations to ensure the robustness of our conclusions.

Overall, our work highlights the ongoing importance of experimental progress and theoretical advances in testing and refining supersymmetric models. Our results indicate that the CMSSM, and possibly other related supersymmetric models, suffer from significant tension with experimental data, which presents a challenge and an opportunity for new discoveries in particle physics.

We describe the methodology and compute the illumination of geometrically
thin accretion disks around black holes of arbitrary spin parameter $a$ exposed
to the radiation of a point-like, isotropic source at arbitrary height above
the disk on its symmetry axis. We then provide analytic fitting formulae for
the illumination as a function of the source height $h$ and the black hole
angular momentum $a$. We find that for a source on the disk symmetry axis and
$h/M > 3$, the main effect of the parameter $a$ is allowing the disk to extend
to smaller radii (approaching $r/M \to 1$ as $a/M \to 1$) and thus allow the
illumination of regions of much higher rotational velocity and redshift. We
also compute the illumination profiles for anisotropic emission associated with
the motion of the source relative to the accretion disk and present the
fractions of photons absorbed by the black hole, intercepted by the disk or
escaping to infinity for both isotropic and anisotropic emission for $a/M=0$
and $a/M=0.99$. As the anisotropy (of a source approaching the disk) increases
the illumination profile reduces (approximately) to a single power-law, whose
index, $q$, because of absorption of the beamed photons by the black hole,
saturates to a value no higher than $q \gtrsim 3$. Finally, we compute the
fluorescence Fe line profiles associated with the specific illumination and
compare them among various cases.

In this study, we report a new flaring event from an ultraluminous X-ray source (ULX) in the nearby galaxy NGC 1365. ULXs are highly luminous X-ray sources that are typically associated with accretion onto black holes. Using data from the Swift and NuSTAR X-ray telescopes, we detected a sharp increase in the X-ray flux of the ULX, reaching a peak of ~4 times its quiescent level. The flare lasted for a duration of ~1 day. 

Our research was motivated by the need to understand the nature of ULXs, in particular the mechanisms behind their variability. Flares from ULXs are particularly interesting because they can provide clues about the accretion physics and black hole properties. We also aimed to investigate if the flare was associated with any activity in the host galaxy such as supernova explosions or star formation. 

We analyzed the X-ray spectrum of the flare and found that it can be well described by a power-law model with a photon index of ~2. We also estimated the luminosity of the source during the flare to be ~4 x 10^40 erg s^-1, which is among the highest ever observed for a ULX. Our study demonstrates the importance of monitoring ULXs for flaring activity, which can reveal valuable insights into the properties of these enigmatic cosmic sources.

We study the boundedness problem for maximal operators $\M$ associated to
smooth hypersurfaces $S$ in 3-dimensional Euclidean space. For $p>2,$ we prove
that if no affine tangent plane to $S$ passes through the origin and $S$ is
analytic, then the associated maximal operator is bounded on $L^p(\RR^3)$ if
and only if $p>h(S),$ where $h(S)$ denotes the so-called height of the surface
$S.$ For non-analytic finite type $S$ we obtain the same statement with the
exception of the exponent $p=h(S).$ Our notion of height $h(S)$ is closely
related to A. N. Varchenko's notion of height $h(\phi)$ for functions $\phi$
such that $S$ can be locally represented as the graph of $\phi$ after a
rotation of coordinates.
  Several consequences of this result are discussed. In particular we verify a
conjecture by E.M. Stein and its generalization by A. Iosevich and E. Sawyer on
the connection between the decay rate of the Fourier transform of the surface
measure on $S$ and the $L^p$-boundedness of the associated maximal operator
$\M$, and a conjecture by Iosevich and Sawyer which relates the
$L^p$-boundedness of $\M$ to an integrability condition on $S$ for the distance
function to tangential hyperplanes, in dimension three.
  In particular, we also give ess. sharp uniform estimates for the Fourier
transform of the surface measure on $S,$ thus extending a result by V.N.
Karpushkin from the analytic to the smooth setting and implicitly verifying a
conjecture by V.I. Arnol'd in our context.

Conformation of single polyelectrolytes in tetravalent salt solutions is
investigated under the framework of a coarse-grained model, using Langevin
dynamics simulations. The chain size, studied by the radius of gyration, shows
three different variational behaviors with salt concentration, depending on the
chain stiffness. According to these behaviors, polyelectrolytes of fixed chain
length are classified into three categories: flexible chain, semiflexible
chain, and rigid chain. The worm-like chain model with persistence length
predicted by the Odijk-Skolnick-Fixman theory is found to be able to
qualitatively describe the end-to-end distance at low salt concentration. In a
low-salt region, a flexible polyelectrolyte extends more significantly than a
semiflexible chain, and in a high-salt region, regardless of chain stiffness, a
chain attains a dimension comparable to that of its neutral polymer. The chain
stiffness influences both the local and the global chain structures. A flexible
chain exhibits a zigzagged local structure in the presence of salt ions and the
condensed structure is a disordered, random globule. A semiflexible chain is
locally smooth, and the condensed structure is orderly packed, taking a form
such as hairpin or toroid. Moreover, the chain stiffness can also affect the
nature of the coil-globule transition. The transition is occurred in a discrete
manner for semiflexible chain, whereas in a continuous way for flexible chain.
This discrete feature is happened not only at low salt concentration when a
semiflexible chain is collapsed, but also at high salt concentration when the
collapsed chain is reexpanded. At the end, the effects of chain stiffness and
salt concentration on the conformation of single polyelectrolytes are
summarized in a schematic state diagram.

I describe a Bayesian method to account for measurement errors in linear
regression of astronomical data. The method allows for heteroscedastic and
possibly correlated measurement errors, and intrinsic scatter in the regression
relationship. The method is based on deriving a likelihood function for the
measured data, and I focus on the case when the intrinsic distribution of the
independent variables can be approximated using a mixture of Gaussians. I
generalize the method to incorporate multiple independent variables,
non-detections, and selection effects (e.g., Malmquist bias). A Gibbs sampler
is described for simulating random draws from the probability distribution of
the parameters, given the observed data. I use simulation to compare the method
with other common estimators. The simulations illustrate that the Gaussian
mixture model outperforms other common estimators and can effectively give
constraints on the regression parameters, even when the measurement errors
dominate the observed scatter, source detection fraction is low, or the
intrinsic distribution of the independent variables is not a mixture of
Gaussians. I conclude by using this method to fit the X-ray spectral slope as a
function of Eddington ratio using a sample of 39 z < 0.8 radio-quiet quasars. I
confirm the correlation seen by other authors between the radio-quiet quasar
X-ray spectral slope and the Eddington ratio, where the X-ray spectral slope
softens as the Eddington ratio increases.

User's perception of product, by essence subjective, is a major topic in
marketing and industrial design. Many methods, based on users' tests, are used
so as to characterise this perception. We are interested in three main methods:
multidimensional scaling, semantic differential method, and preference mapping.
These methods are used to built a perceptual space, in order to position the
new product, to specify requirements by the study of user's preferences, to
evaluate some product attributes, related in particular to style (aesthetic).
These early stages of the design are primordial for a good orientation of the
project. In parallel, virtual reality tools and interfaces are more and more
efficient for suggesting to the user complex feelings, and creating in this way
various levels of perceptions. In this article, we present on an example the
use of multidimensional scaling, semantic differential method and preference
mapping for the subjective assessment of virtual products. These products,
which geometrical form is variable, are defined with a CAD model and are
proposed to the user with a spacemouse and stereoscopic glasses. Advantages and
limitations of such evaluation is next discussed..

We test the theory that lenticular (S0) galaxies form from spirals whose star
formation has been shut down. We use the globular cluster specific frequency
S_N, defined as the number of globular clusters normalised by the galaxy
luminosity as a diagnostic. NTT/EMMI long-slit spectroscopic observations of 11
S0 galaxies at z < 0.006 are used to measure the absorption-line indices,
Hdelta, Hgamma, Mgb, Fe5270 and Fe5335 within the central r_e/8. By inverting
single-stellar population models, luminosity-weighted mean ages, metallicities
and alpha-element abundance ratios are derived. We estimate the amount of
fading a galaxy has undergone by comparing each galaxy's S_N with its deviation
from the mean spiral S_N. Galaxies with higher S_N have older stellar
populations. Moreover, we find that the zero-point and amount of fading is
consistent with a scenario where lenticulars are formed by the quenching of
star formation in spiral galaxies. Our data also rule out any formation method
for S0s which creates a large number of new globular clusters. We confirm that
previous results showing a relationship between S_N and color are driven by the
S_N - Age relation. Five galaxies show detectable Hbeta, [OIII], Halpha or
[NII] emission lines. However, only in the two youngest galaxies is this
emission unambiguously from star formation. Our results are consistent with the
theory that S0 galaxies are formed when gas in normal spirals is removed,
possibly as a result of a change in environment. The on-going star formation in
the youngest galaxies hints that the timescale of quenching is ~< 1 Gyr. We
speculate, therefore, that the truncation of star formation is a rather gentle
process unlikely to involve a rapid burst of star formation.

Plasmas in an accretion flow are heated by MHD turbulence generated through
the magneto-rotational instability. The viscous stress driving the accretion is
intimately connected to the microscopic processes of turbulence dissipation. We
show that, in a few well-observed black hole accretion systems, there is
compelling observational evidence of efficient electron heating by turbulence
or collective plasma effects in low accretion states, when Coulomb collisions
are not efficient enough to establish a thermal equilibrium between electrons
and ions at small radii. However, charged particles reach a thermal equilibrium
with their kind much faster than with others through Coulomb collisions, a
two-temperature accretion flow is expected. We consider a Keplerian accretion
flow with a constant mass accretion rate in the pseudo-Newtonian gravitational
potential and take into account the bremsstrahlung, synchrotron, and inverse
Comptonization cooling processes. The critical mass accretion rate, below which
the two-temperature solution may exist, is determined by the cooling processes
and the collisional energy exchanges between electrons and ions and has very
weak dependence on the collision-less heating of electrons by turbulence, which
becomes more important at lower accretion rates. Collision-less heating of
electrons by MHD turbulence can no longer be ignored in quantitative
investigations of these systems.

We describe our software package Block Locally Optimal Preconditioned
Eigenvalue Xolvers (BLOPEX) publicly released recently. BLOPEX is available as
a stand-alone serial library, as an external package to PETSc (``Portable,
Extensible Toolkit for Scientific Computation'', a general purpose suite of
tools for the scalable solution of partial differential equations and related
problems developed by Argonne National Laboratory), and is also built into {\it
hypre} (``High Performance Preconditioners'', scalable linear solvers package
developed by Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory). The present BLOPEX
release includes only one solver--the Locally Optimal Block Preconditioned
Conjugate Gradient (LOBPCG) method for symmetric eigenvalue problems. {\it
hypre} provides users with advanced high-quality parallel preconditioners for
linear systems, in particular, with domain decomposition and multigrid
preconditioners. With BLOPEX, the same preconditioners can now be efficiently
used for symmetric eigenvalue problems. PETSc facilitates the integration of
independently developed application modules with strict attention to component
interoperability, and makes BLOPEX extremely easy to compile and use with
preconditioners that are available via PETSc. We present the LOBPCG algorithm
in BLOPEX for {\it hypre} and PETSc. We demonstrate numerically the scalability
of BLOPEX by testing it on a number of distributed and shared memory parallel
systems, including a Beowulf system, SUN Fire 880, an AMD dual-core Opteron
workstation, and IBM BlueGene/L supercomputer, using PETSc domain decomposition
and {\it hypre} multigrid preconditioning. We test BLOPEX on a model problem,
the standard 7-point finite-difference approximation of the 3-D Laplacian, with
the problem size in the range $10^5-10^8$.

We present a study of the shapes, orientations, and alignments of Galactic
dark matter subhalos in the ``Via Lactea'' simulation of a Milky Way-size LCDM
host halo. Whereas isolated dark matter halos tend to be prolate, subhalos are
predominantly triaxial. Overall subhalos are more spherical than the host halo,
with minor to major and intermediate to major axis ratios of 0.68 and 0.83,
respectively. Like isolated halos, subhalos tend to be less spherical in their
central regions. The principal axis ratios are independent of subhalo mass,
when the shapes are measured within a physical scale like r_Vmax, the radius of
the peak of the circular velocity curve. Subhalos tend to be slightly more
spherical closer to the host halo center. The spatial distribution of the
subhalos traces the prolate shape of the host halo when they are selected by
the largest V_max they ever had, i.e. before they experienced strong tidal mass
loss. The subhalos' orientation is not random: the major axis tends to align
with the direction towards the host halo center. This alignment disappears for
halos beyond 3 r_200 and is more pronounced when the shapes are measured in the
outer regions of the subhalos. The radial alignment is preserved during a
subhalo's orbit and they become elongated during pericenter passage, indicating
that the alignment is likely caused by the host halo's tidal forces. These
tidal interactions with the host halo act to make subhalos rounder over time.

We present the results of a detailed spectral and temporal analysis of Swift
and XMM-Newton observations of the high redshift (z=3.969) GRB 050730. The
X-ray afterglow of GRB 050730 was found to decline with time with superimposed
intense flaring activity that extended over more than two orders of magnitude
in time. Seven distinct re-brightening events starting from 236 s up to 41.2 ks
after the burst were observed. The underlying decay of the afterglow was well
described by a double broken power-law model with breaks at t_1= 237 +/- 20 s
and t_2 = 10.1 (-2.2) (+4.6) ks. The temporal decay slopes before, between and
after these breaks were alpha_1 = 2.1 +/- 0.3, alpha_2 = 0.44 (-0.08) (+0.14)
and alpha_3 = 2.40 (+0.07) (-0.09), respectively. The spectrum of the X-ray
afterglow was well described by a photoelectrically absorbed power-law with an
absorbing column density N_H=(1.28 +/- 0.26) 10^22 cm^-2 in the host galaxy.
Strong X-ray spectral evolution during the flaring activity was present. In the
majority of the flares (6/7) the ratio Delta_t/t_p between the duration of the
event and the time when the flare peaks was nearly constant and about 0.6-0.7.
We showed that the observed spectral and temporal properties of the first three
flares are consistent with being due both to high-latitude emission, as
expected if the flares were produced by late internal shocks, or to refreshed
shocks, i.e. late time energy injections into the main afterglow shock by slow
moving shells ejected from the central engine during the prompt phase. The
event fully satisfies the E_p-E_iso Amati relation while is not consistent with
the E_p-E_jet Ghirlanda relation.

We present a study on the Light-Cone Distribution Amplitudes (LCDAs) of Axial-vector Mesons, which are an important tool for understanding the structure of these particles. The motivation for this research comes from the fact that axial-vector mesons play a fundamental role in many different areas of high-energy physics, such as QCD sum rules and hadronic decays, and therefore a detailed understanding of their properties is essential.

Our research explores the key ideas and methodology involved in determining the LCDAs of these mesons. Specifically, we employ a combination of perturbative QCD and holographic techniques to extract the leading-twist distribution amplitudes. The holographic technique provides a strong connection between the meson's bulk in anti-de Sitter space and its gauge theory dual. This allows us to investigate the properties of these particles more deeply, ranging from their partonic structure to their decay properties.

The problem our work attempts to solve is the lack of a complete understanding of axial-vector meson properties, which has proven to be an open and challenging research area. Our study is one of the few that explores axial-vector mesons and their LCDAs, and it provides a solid foundation for future research in this area. Overall, our research offers significant insight into the structure of these essential particles and can ultimately contribute to a more profound understanding of QCD dynamics.

In this study, we explore the design process and experimental evaluation of the AnITA Generic Tutoring System. Our goal is to understand the key considerations that go into the development of an effective tutoring system and to determine the most effective methods for evaluating its performance. The AnITA system is a framework for creating intelligent tutoring systems that adapt to the specific needs of individual learners. Our analysis of the design process reveals several key insights into the development of such systems, including the importance of user-centered design and the need for expert input to ensure that the system is effective in practice.

We also conducted a series of experiments to evaluate the performance of the AnITA system. Our methodology involved comparing the performance of learners who used the system to those who did not. We observed that those who used the system performed significantly better than those who did not. Our findings suggest that the AnITA system is an effective tool for supporting learning and that it has the potential to be useful in a range of educational contexts.

The motivation for our research is to explore the potential of intelligent tutoring systems to support learning and to identify strategies for improving their effectiveness. Our findings contribute to the ongoing discussion of how best to design and evaluate such systems, and we hope to inspire further research in this area.

Using the spin wave approximation, we study the decoherence dynamics of a
central spin coupled to an antiferromagnetic environment under the application
of an external global magnetic field. The external magnetic field affects the
decoherence process through its effect on the antiferromagnetic environment. It
is shown explicitly that the decoherence factor which displays a Gaussian decay
with time depends on the strength of the external magnetic field and the
crystal anisotropy field in the antiferromagnetic environment. When the values
of the external magnetic field is increased to the critical field point at
which the spin-flop transition (a first-order quantum phase transition) happens
in the antiferromagnetic environment, the decoherence of the central spin
reaches its highest point. This result is consistent with several recent
quantum phase transition witness studies. The influences of the environmental
temperature on the decoherence behavior of the central spin are also
investigated.

In this work, we attempt to unify evolutionary and network dynamics to better understand the complex interplay between biology and society. The problem we aim to solve is the lack of a comprehensive theoretical framework to explain the co-evolutionary processes that shape the emergence of complex adaptive systems. Our key idea rests on the notion that the evolutionary dynamics of a population are intimately linked to the network structure of interactions between its members. We propose a set of mathematical models that integrate the principles of both evolutionary and network science to capture the complex feedback loops that arise from these interdependencies.

Our methodology involves a combination of theoretical analysis and computer simulations to test the predictions of our models. Specifically, we investigate how the interplay between evolutionary and social processes gives rise to the emergence of cooperative behaviors, the evolution of social norms, and the robustness of complex systems to environmental perturbations. Our motivation for this research stems from the recognition that understanding the co-evolutionary dynamics of biological and social systems is essential for addressing some of the most pressing challenges facing humanity today, such as the spread of infectious diseases, the emergence of social inequalities, and the sustainability of global ecosystems.
