Our results show a clear correlation between the oxygen content and the onset of the 60K plateau in the superconducting transition temperature. We also observe changes in the magnetization and electrical resistivity of the samples as the oxygen content is varied. These findings suggest that oxygen doping plays a critical role in the superconducting properties of YBa_2Cu_3O_6+x, and that further research is needed to fully understand the underlying physics. Overall, this work provides valuable insights into the design and optimization of high-temperature superconducting materials for practical applications in energy generation and storage.

We study the effect of a close encounter of a passing star on the shape of
the inner Oort Cloud, using the impulse approximation. The deviation of the
perturbed Oort Cloud from sphericity adds angular fluctuations to the
brightness of the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) due to thermal emission by
the comets. An encounter with a solar-mass star at an impact parameter of $1750
\au$, as expected based on the abundance and velocity dispersion of stars in
the solar neighborhood, leads to a quadrupole moment in the square of the
fractional CMB intensity fluctuation of $C_2 = 4.5 \times 10^{-15}, 6.7 \times
10^{-12}, 1.1 \times 10^{-9}$ at $\nu = 30, 353, 545 \GHz$ (these being the
frequency bands of the upcoming Planck satellite). We also quantify the
quadrupole spectral distortions produced by the Scattered Disc, which will
exist regardless of any perturbation and the subsequent shape of the Oort
Cloud. For comparison, the square fractional temperature fluctuation quadrupole
moment predicted by the current cosmological model is $C_2 = 1.76 \times
10^{-10}$, which corresponds to fluctuations in the CMB intensity of $C_2 = 2.9
\times 10^{-10}, 6.8 \times 10^{-9}, 1.6 \times 10^{-8}$ at $\nu = 30, 353, 545
\GHz$. Finally, we discuss how a measurement of the anisotropic spectral
distortions could be used to constrain the trajectory of the closest stellar
fly-by.

In this article we study a controllability problem for a parabolic and a
hyperbolic partial differential equations in which the control is the shape of
the domain where the equation holds. The quantity to be controlled is the trace
of the solution into an open subdomain and at a given time, when the right hand
side source term is known. The mapping that associates this trace to the shape
of the domain is nonlinear. We show (i) an approximate controllability property
for the linearized parabolic problem and (ii) an exact local controllability
property for the linearized and the nonlinear equations in the hyperbolic case.
We then address the same questions in the context of a finite difference
spatial semi-discretization in both the parabolic and hyperbolic problems. In
this discretized case again we prove a local controllability result for the
parabolic problem, and an exact controllability for the hyperbolic case,
applying a local surjectivity theorem together with a unique continuation
property of the underlying adjoint discrete system.

The most massive elliptical galaxies show a prominent multi-modality in their
globular cluster system color distributions. Understanding the mechanisms which
lead to multiple globular cluster sub-populations is essential for a complete
picture of massive galaxy formation. By assuming that globular cluster
formation traces the total star formation and taking into account the radial
variations in the composite stellar populations predicted by the Pipino &
Matteucci (2004) multi-zone photo-chemical evolution code, we compute the
distribution of globular cluster properties as a function of galactocentric
radius. We compare our results to the spectroscopic measurements of globular
clusters in nearby early-type galaxies by Puzia et al. (2006) and show that the
observed multi-modality in globular cluster systems of massive ellipticals can
be, at least partly, ascribed to the radial variation in the mix of stellar
populations. Our model predicts the presence of a super-metal-rich population
of globular clusters in the most massive elliptical galaxies, which is in very
good agreement with the spectroscopic observations. Furthermore, we investigate
the impact of other non-linear mechanisms that shape the metallicity
distribution of globular cluster systems, in particular the role of
merger-induced globular cluster formation and a non-linear color-metallicity
transformation, and discuss their influence in the context of our model
(abridged)

It is now an accepted fact that the size at which dunes form from a flat sand
bed as well as their `minimal size' scales on the flux saturation length. This
length is by definition the relaxation length of the slowest mode toward
equilibrium transport. The model presented by Parteli, Duran and Herrmann
[Phys. Rev. E 75, 011301 (2007)] predicts that the saturation length decreases
to zero as the inverse of the wind shear stress far from the threshold. We
first show that their model is not self-consistent: even under large wind, the
relaxation rate is limited by grain inertia and thus can not decrease to zero.
A key argument presented by these authors comes from the discussion of the
typical dune wavelength on Mars (650 m) on the basis of which they refute the
scaling of the dune size with the drag length evidenced by Claudin and
Andreotti [Earth Pla. Sci. Lett. 252, 30 (2006)]. They instead propose that
Martian dunes, composed of large grains (500 micrometers), were formed in the
past under very strong winds. We show that this saltating grain size, estimated
from thermal diffusion measurements, is not reliable. Moreover, the microscopic
photographs taken by the rovers on Martian aeolian bedforms show a grain size
of 87 plus or minus 25 micrometers together with hematite spherules at
millimetre scale. As those so-called ``blueberries'' can not be entrained by
reasonable winds, we conclude that the saltating grains on Mars are the small
ones, which gives a second strong argument against the model of Parteli et al.

We provide the perturbative and non-perturbative arguments showing that
theories with large number of species of the quantum fields, imply an
inevitable hierarchy between the masses of the species and the Planck scale,
shedding a different light on the hierarchy problem. In particular, using the
black hole physics, we prove that any consistent theory that includes N number
of the Z_2-conserved species of the quantum fields of mass \Lambda, puts a
lower bound on the Planck mass, which in large N limit is given by N\Lambda^2.
An useful byproduct of this proof is that any exactly conserved quantum charge,
not associated with a long-range classical field, must be defined maximum
modulo N, bounded by the the ratio of the Planck to the unit charge masses
squared. For example, a continuous global U(1) `baryon number' symmetry, must
be explicitly broken by gravity, at least down to a Z_N subgroup, with N
bounded by the ratio of the Planck to baryon masses squared. The same
constraint applies to any discrete gauge symmetry, as well as to other
quantum-mechanically detectable black hole charges that are associated with the
massive quantum hair of the black hole. We show that the
gravitationally-coupled N-species sector that solves the gauge hirearchy
problem, should be probed by LHC.

We investigate the effects of relatively rapid variations of the boundaries
of an overmoded cavity on the stochastic properties of its interior acoustic or
electromagnetic field. For quasi-static variations, this field can be
represented as an ideal incoherent and statistically homogeneous isotropic
random scalar or vector field, respectively. A physical model is constructed
showing that the field dynamics can be characterized as a generalized diffusion
process. The Langevin--It\^{o} and Fokker--Planck equations are derived and
their associated statistics and distributions for the complex analytic field,
its magnitude and energy density are computed. The energy diffusion parameter
is found to be proportional to the square of the ratio of the standard
deviation of the source field to the characteristic time constant of the
dynamic process, but is independent of the initial energy density, to first
order. The energy drift vanishes in the asymptotic limit. The time-energy
probability distribution is in general not separable, as a result of
nonstationarity. A general solution of the Fokker--Planck equation is obtained
in integral form, together with explicit closed-form solutions for several
asymptotic cases. The findings extend known results on statistics and
distributions of quasi-stationary ideal random fields (pure diffusions), which
are retrieved as special cases.

Mixed action theories with chirally symmetric valence fermions exhibit very
desirable features both at the level of the lattice calculations as well as in
the construction and implementation of the low energy mixed action effective
field theory. In this work we show that when such a mixed action effective
field theory is projected onto the valence sector, both the Lagrangian and the
extrapolation formulae become universal in form through next to leading order,
for all variants of discretization methods used for the sea fermions. Our
conclusion relies on the chiral nature of the valence quarks. The result
implies that for all sea quark methods which are in the same universality class
as QCD, the numerical values of the physical coefficients in the various mixed
action chiral Lagrangians will be the same up to lattice spacing dependent
corrections. This allows us to construct a prescription to determine the mixed
action extrapolation formulae for a large class of hadronic correlation
functions computed in partially quenched chiral perturbation theory at the
one-loop level. For specific examples, we apply this prescription to the
nucleon twist--2 matrix elements and the nucleon--nucleon system. In addition,
we determine the mixed action extrapolation formula for the neutron EDM as this
provides a nice example of a theta-dependent observable; these observables are
exceptions to our prescription.

The early Solar System contained short-lived radionuclides such as 60Fe (t1/2
= 1.5 Myr) whose most likely source was a nearby supernova. Previous models of
Solar System formation considered a supernova shock that triggered the collapse
of the Sun's nascent molecular cloud. We advocate an alternative hypothesis,
that the Solar System's protoplanetary disk had already formed when a very
close (< 1 pc) supernova injected radioactive material directly into the disk.
We conduct the first numerical simulations designed to answer two questions
related to this hypothesis: will the disk be destroyed by such a close
supernova; and will any of the ejecta be mixed into the disk? Our simulations
demonstrate that the disk does not absorb enough momentum from the shock to
escape the protostar to which it is bound. Only low amounts (< 1%) of mass loss
occur, due to stripping by Kelvin-Helmholtz instabilities across the top of the
disk, which also mix into the disk about 1% of the intercepted ejecta. These
low efficiencies of destruction and injectation are due to the fact that the
high disk pressures prevent the ejecta from penetrating far into the disk
before stalling. Injection of gas-phase ejecta is too inefficient to be
consistent with the abundances of radionuclides inferred from meteorites. On
the other hand, the radionuclides found in meteorites would have condensed into
dust grains in the supernova ejecta, and we argue that such grains will be
injected directly into the disk with nearly 100% efficiency. The meteoritic
abundances of the short-lived radionuclides such as 60Fe therefore are
consistent with injection of grains condensed from the ejecta of a nearby (< 1
pc) supernova, into an already-formed protoplanetary disk.

In this work, we explore the behavior of Bose-Einstein-condensed gases in arbitrarily strong random potentials. Our goal was to investigate the interplay between quantum fluctuations and disorder in these systems and understand how it affects their properties. To that end, we performed numerical simulations of the Gross-Pitaevskii equation for different values of disorder strength, interaction strength, and temperature.

Our results reveal several interesting features of the system. First, we find that as disorder strength increases, the condensate becomes more fragmented and spreads out over a large region, indicating a breakdown of coherence. Second, we observe the emergence of rare regions that are nearly free of disorder, which act as nucleation sites for the condensate. Third, we find that the presence of repulsive interactions among particles enhances the fragmentation of the condensate, whereas attractive interactions lead to its reformation.

Our findings have important implications for the study of disordered quantum systems, as they shed light on the complex interplay between coherence, fluctuations, and interaction effects. Moreover, they provide valuable insights into the behavior of Bose-Einstein-condensed gases in environments where disorder is the dominant factor. Overall, this work contributes to a better understanding of the fundamental laws governing quantum matter and opens up new avenues for future research.

We study conditions for the deterministic transformation
$\ket{\psi}\longrightarrow\ket{\phi}$ of a bipartite entangled state by a
separable operation. If the separable operation is a local operation with
classical communication (LOCC), Nielsen's majorization theorem provides
necessary and sufficient conditions. For the general case we derive a necessary
condition in terms of products of Schmidt coefficients, which is equivalent to
the Nielsen condition when either of the two factor spaces is of dimension 2,
but is otherwise weaker. One implication is that no separable operation can
reverse a deterministic map produced by another separable operation, if one
excludes the case where the Schmidt coefficients of $\ket{\psi}$ and are the
same as those of $\ket{\phi}$. The question of sufficient conditions in the
general separable case remains open. When the Schmidt coefficients of
$\ket{\psi}$ are the same as those of $\ket{\phi}$, we show that the Kraus
operators of the separable transformation restricted to the supports of
$\ket{\psi}$ on the factor spaces are proportional to unitaries. When that
proportionality holds and the factor spaces have equal dimension, we find
conditions for the deterministic transformation of a collection of several full
Schmidt rank pure states $\ket{\psi_j}$ to pure states $\ket{\phi_j}$.

In this paper, we present a novel wavelet-based algorithm, TRUFAS (Transit Real-time Unsuper-vised Frequency Analysis System), for the fast detection of planetary transits. The search for exoplanets by transit methods is crucial, but the current state-of-the-art algorithms for transit detection are computationally expensive, and their performance decreases with the amount of data analyzed. Our proposed algorithm overcomes this problem by processing large amounts of data in a short time, without sacrificing detection efficiency. 

The TRUFAS algorithm uses a wavelet-based technique to identify transit-like signals in the time series data. It works in the frequency domain to remove strong systematic errors during the search process. Based on tests done on simulated data and real data, we confirmed that TRUFAS performs better or as well as other algorithms, including the Box Least-Squares (BLS) algorithm. We compared the computed ranks of all algorithms on the same datasets and applied a reliability test to quantify their detection capabilities. The TRUFAS algorithm obtained a high RMS improvement percentage compared to other algorithms, signifying its superior transit detection efficiency.

In conclusion, TRUFAS is a powerful tool for the large-scale transit search in the era of big data astronomy. The proposed algorithm overcomes computational limitations of traditional transit detection methods while being efficient and accurate. Furthermore, with its built-in frequency-based error correction and detection reliability assessment, TRUFAS can accelerate the discovery of new exoplanets.

One of the puzzling findings in the study of the chemical evolution of
(post-)AGB stars is why very similar stars (in terms of metallicity, spectral
type, infrared properties, etc...) show a very different photospheric
composition. We aim at extending the still limited sample of s-process enriched
post-AGB stars, in order to obtain a statistically large enough sample that
allows us to formulate conclusions concerning the 3rd dredge-up occurrence. We
selected two post-AGB stars on the basis of IR colours indicative of a past
history of heavy mass loss: IRAS08281-4850 and IRAS14325-6428. They are cool
sources in the locus of the Planetary Nebulae (PNe) in the IRAS colour-colour
diagram. Abundances of both objects were derived for the first time on the
basis of high-quality UVES and EMMI spectra, using a critically compiled line
list with accurate log(gf) values, together with the latest Kurucz model
atmospheres. Both objects have very similar spectroscopically defined effective
temperatures of 7750-8000K. They are strongly carbon and s-process enriched,
with a C/O ratio of 1.9 and 1.6, and an [ls/Fe] of +1.7 and +1.2, for IRAS08281
and IRAS14325 resp. Moreover, the spectral energy distributions (SEDs) point to
heavy mass-loss during the preceding AGB phase. IRAS08281 and IRAS14325 are
prototypical post-AGB objects in the sense that they show strong post 3rd
dredge-up chemical enrichments. The neutron irradiation has been extremely
efficient, despite the only mild sub-solar metallicity. This is not conform
with the recent chemical models. The existence of very similar post-AGB stars
without any enrichment emphasizes our poor knowledge of the details of the AGB
nucleosynthesis and dredge-up phenomena. We call for a very systematic chemical
study of all cool sources in the PN region of the IRAS colour-colour diagram.

In this study, we explore the survival of millimeter to centimeter-sized grains in protoplanetary disks. The problem we aim to solve pertains to the seemingly contradictory observations of grain growth and limited grain retention in such disks. While protoplanetary disks are known to be the birthplaces of planets, it remains unclear how the growth of dust grains from micron to millimeter sizes occurs and how these grains are ultimately retained or lost from their natal disks. Our motivation for this research is to gain a better understanding of the formation of planets and planetary systems, which is vital in understanding our own solar system and its history. 

To achieve this, we utilize a high-resolution numerical model to simulate dust dynamics in protoplanetary disks. This allows us to investigate the growth and survival of grain populations of different sizes under varying conditions. We find that while larger grains are more likely to be retained, the survival of smaller grains is imperative for the growth of larger grains. Our key finding is that turbulence plays a crucial role in both the evolution of dust and the retention of grains. Our methodology involves a combination of numerical simulations, analytical calculations, and comparison with observational data. Our results have significant implications for the formation and evolution of planets and contribute to our understanding of the processes that shape protoplanetary disks.

We present a study on the self-similar evolution of cosmic-ray-modified quasi-parallel plane shocks. Our work aims to shed light on the properties of collisionless shocks in interstellar space, where cosmic rays play a crucial role in accelerating particles to high energies. Using a one-dimensional hydrodynamical numerical simulation, we investigate the time-dependent behavior of the shock front as it evolves to a steady-state solution. We assume a power-law distribution for the cosmic-ray particles and solve the corresponding transport equation to obtain the cosmic-ray pressure.

Our analysis indicates that the presence of cosmic rays modifies the shock structure, producing a shock front that is wider and less steep than its gas-only counterpart. The cosmic-ray pressure contributes significantly to the total pressure, facilitating faster shock propagation. Furthermore, we find that the shock acceleration efficiency is enhanced by the concerted action of the cosmic rays and the background plasma. The self-similar nature of the shock evolution provides a useful framework for understanding the underlying physics and facilitates comparisons with analytical models. Our work contributes to the ongoing effort to understand the complex dynamics of astrophysical shocks and their impact on the interstellar medium.

In this paper, we present a novel model for time-dependent variance incorporating long-term memory. Our research is motivated by the need to capture non-stationarity and long-range dependence in financial time series, which violate assumptions of traditional models. Such features have also been observed in fields outside of finance, such as environmental and medical data. 

Our proposed framework, called the Generalised Long-term Memory Variance (GLMV) model, extends the popular Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity (ARCH) model by incorporating a long-memory component using Fractionally Integrated Autoregressive Moving Average (ARFIMA) models. We derive the moment conditions and asymptotic properties of the data-driven estimators, which we show perform well in simulations. 

Applying the GLMV model to real-world datasets, we demonstrate its superior performance in capturing variance dynamics compared to existing models. Specifically, the GLMV model is able to capture both short- and long-term persistence, and accurately estimates the Hurst exponent of the series. These results are particularly promising for applications in risk management, portfolio optimization, and pricing of financial derivatives. 

Overall, our study presents an innovative approach to modelling time-dependent variance incorporating long-term memory, and offers new insights into the dynamics of complex systems.

Based on radial velocity measurements it has been found recently that the two
giant planets detected around the star HD 73526 are in 2:1 resonance. However,
as our numerical integration shows, the derived orbital data for this system
result in chaotic behavior of the giant planets, which is uncommon among the
resonant extrasolar planetary systems.
  We intend to present regular (non-chaotic) orbital solutions for the giant
planets in the system HD 73526 and offer formation scenarios based on combining
planetary migration and sudden perturbative effects such as planet-planet
scattering or rapid dispersal of the protoplanetary disk. A comparison with the
already studied resonant system HD 128311, exhibiting similar behavior, is also
done.
  The new sets of orbital solutions have been derived by the Systemic Console
(www.oklo.org). The stability of these solutions has been investigated by the
Relative Lyapunov indicator, while the migration and scattering effects are
studied by gravitational N-body simulations applying non-conservative forces as
well. Additionally, hydrodynamic simulations of embedded planets in
protoplanetary disks are performed to follow the capture into resonance.
  For the system HD 73526 we demonstrate that the observational radial velocity
data are consistent with a coplanar planetary system engaged in a stable 2:1
resonance exhibiting apsidal corotation. We have shown that, similarly to the
system HD 128311, the present dynamical state of HD 73526 could be the result
of a mixed evolutionary process melting together planetary migration and a
perturbative event.

We examine gauge theories defined in higher dimensions where theextra
dimensions form a fuzzy (finite matrix) manifold. First we reinterpret these
gauge theories as four-dimensional theories with Kaluza-Klein modes and then we
perform a generalized \`a la Forgacs-Manton dimensional reduction. We emphasize
some striking features emerging in the later case such as (i) the appearance of
non-abelian gauge theories in four dimensions starting from an abelian gauge
theory in higher dimensions, (ii) the fact that the spontaneous symmetry
breaking of the theory takes place entirely in the extra dimensions and (iii)
the renormalizability of the theory both in higher as well as in four
dimensions. Then reversing the above approach we present a renormalizable four
dimensional SU(N) gauge theory with a suitable multiplet of scalar fields,
which via spontaneous symmetry breaking dynamically develops extra dimensions
in the form of a fuzzy sphere. We explicitly find the tower of massive
Kaluza-Klein modes consistent with an interpretation as gauge theory on $M^4
\times S^2$, the scalars being interpreted as gauge fields on $S^2$. Depending
on the parameters of the model the low-energy gauge group can be of the form
$SU(n_1) \times SU(n_2) \times U(1)$.

Recent studies of clustering in light nuclei with an initial energy above 1 A
GeV in nuclear treack emulsion are overviewed. The results of investigations of
the relativistic $^9$Be nuclei fragmentation in emulsion, which entails the
production of He fragments, are presented. It is shown that most precise
angular measurements provided by this technique play a crucial role in the
restoration of the excitation spectrum of the $\alpha$ particle sysytem. In
peripheral interactions $^9$Be nuclei are dissociated practically totally
through the 0$^+$ and 2$^+$ states of the $^8$Be nucleus.
  The results of investigations of the dissociation of a $^{14}$N nucleus of
momentum 2.86 A GeV/c in emulsion are presented as example of more complicated
system. The momentum and correlation characteristics of $\alpha$ particles for
the $^{14}$N$\to3\alpha+X$ channel in the laboratory system and the rest
systems of 3$\alpha$ particles were considered in detail.
  Topology of charged fragments produced in peripheral relativistic
dissociation of radioactive $^8$B, $^7$Be nuclei in emulsion is studied.

In this paper, we present diagnostic tools for analyzing 3D unstructured oceanographic data. The motivation for this research stems from the increasing availability of large-scale oceanographic data and the need to extract meaningful information from these datasets. However, the complex nature of oceanography presents a significant challenge, and traditional 2D visualization methods are inadequate for representing the data. 

To address this challenge, we developed several diagnostic tools, including level set methods, contour mapping, and vector visualization techniques. These tools enable us to identify important dynamic features of the ocean and quantify their properties. Additionally, these tools allow us to analyze complex datasets with ease and gain deeper insight into the complex physical processes that occur in the ocean.

Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of these diagnostic tools in analyzing oceanographic data. We show that these tools can highlight important areas of interest, identify areas of high or low concentration of various properties, and track the evolution of complex physical processes over time. Furthermore, we demonstrate the potential of these tools in aiding in the development of predictive models, helping us to better understand and manage ocean ecosystems.

Overall, this work aims to solve the problem of how to effectively analyze 3D unstructured oceanographic data, ultimately providing valuable insights into the complex ocean systems and processes.

In this work, we investigate the initial conditions of star formation in the Ophiuchus main cloud by studying the kinematics of protocluster condensations. We identify ten protocluster condensations based on molecular line observations with the Nobeyama 45 m telescope. By analyzing the kinematics of the condensations, we find that they are gravitationally bound and have supersonic internal motions. These findings suggest that the protocluster condensations are the sites of future star formation in the Ophiuchus main cloud. 

The motivation for this research lies in the importance of understanding the early stages of star formation, which are poorly understood. Protocluster condensations are thought to be the precursors to star clusters, which are common objects in the universe. Studying the properties of protocluster condensations, such as their kinematics, can provide insight into the processes that lead to the formation of star clusters. Additionally, the Ophiuchus main cloud is a nearby star-forming region that has been extensively studied, making it an ideal target for understanding the initial conditions of star formation. 

This work attempts to solve the problem of determining the initial conditions of star formation in the Ophiuchus main cloud by studying the kinematics of protocluster condensations. Our results provide important information about the properties of protocluster condensations and suggest that they are the sites of future star formation in this region. The identification of these protocluster condensations also provides targets for future observations, which can help to further our understanding of the early stages of star formation.

We present a systematic theory of Coulomb-induced correlation effects in the
nonlinear optical processes within the strong-coupling regime. In this paper we
shall set a dynamics controlled truncation scheme \cite{Axt Stahl} microscopic
treatment of nonlinear parametric processes in SMCs including the
electromagnetic field quantization. It represents the starting point for the
microscopic approach to quantum optics experiments in the strong coupling
regime without any assumption on the quantum statistics of electronic
excitations (excitons) involved. We exploit a previous technique, used in the
semiclassical context, which, once applied to four-wave mixing in quantum
wells, allowed to understand a wide range of observed phenomena \cite{Sham
PRL95}. We end up with dynamical equations for exciton and photon operators
which extend the usual semiclassical description of Coulomb interaction
effects, in terms of a mean-field term plus a genuine non-instantaneous
four-particle correlation, to quantum optical effects.

In this paper, we investigate the global well-posedness of the Klein-Gordon-Hartree equation below energy space. This equation models the evolution of charged particles interacting through long-range Coulomb forces and has important applications in quantum mechanics and plasma physics. Our motivation for studying this problem stems from its fundamental importance in understanding the behavior of many-body systems in the presence of long-range interactions. Moreover, the energy space is a natural framework to study the long-time behavior of solutions to this equation, and it allows us to understand the possible blow-up phenomena.

To prove the global well-posedness of this equation, we apply a combination of energy estimates, frequency localization techniques, and Strichartz estimates. In particular, we use a new bilinear estimate that was recently introduced in the literature to overcome the difficulty arising from the presence of the Coulomb interaction. Additionally, we establish a key a priori bound on the charge density that plays a crucial role throughout the analysis.

Our main result shows that for initial data in the energy space, the solutions exist globally in time and enjoy the expected decay rates at infinity. This result complements previous works on the local well-posedness of this equation and provides a complete understanding of its global dynamics.

In this paper, we present a study of the non-linear period-luminosity (P-L) relation of LMC Cepheids. We utilize two powerful statistical tools, Testimator and Schwarz information criterion (SIC), to investigate the deviation from linearity in the P-L relation. Our motivation is to improve the accuracy of distance measurements using Cepheids and ultimately shed light on the cosmic distance ladder and the expansion history of the Universe. 

We employ the OGLE-III LMC Cepheid data set as our sample, spanning a period range of 1 to 100 days. We fit the P-L relation with a linear model and a quadratic model using maximum likelihood estimation. We then apply Testimator, a model selection criterion that compares the log-likelihoods of the models to identify the best-fit model. Additionally, we use SIC to penalize overfitting and select the model that provides the best trade-off between goodness-of-fit and complexity.

Our analysis reveals significant evidence for non-linearity in the P-L relation of LMC Cepheids, with the quadratic model providing a superior fit over the linear model. The results indicate that the non-linearity is not merely a result of statistical fluctuation but reflects the underlying physics of the Cepheids. Our study highlights the importance of utilizing non-linear models in the analysis of Cepheid data and provides valuable insights into the astrophysical properties of these important standard candles.

Galaxy Evolution and Environment is a study that aims to explore the relationship between the evolution of galaxies and their environment. The leading problem the research attempts to solve is how the evolution and structure of galaxies are influenced by their environment. Our motivation behind conducting this research is to understand the interplay between galaxies and their surrounding environment, which is crucial for predicting the behavior of galaxies and stargazing.

The research involved analyzing a sample of galaxies at different distances from clusters using data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey. The results demonstrate that interactions between galaxies and their environment, such as mergers, tidal forces, and gas stripping, play a significant role in shaping the evolution and structure of galaxies. We further found that the environmental impact on galaxy evolution depends on various factors such as galaxy mass, distance from the galaxy cluster, and the distribution of matter in the cluster.

Our findings suggest that the environment plays a critical role in shaping the evolution of galaxies. This research offers insights into the growth and formation of galaxies in different environments, making it a vital contribution to our understanding of the universe. Understanding the complex relationship between galaxies and their environment is the key to unlocking the mysteries of the universe.

Max-product belief propagation is a local, iterative algorithm to find the
mode/MAP estimate of a probability distribution. While it has been successfully
employed in a wide variety of applications, there are relatively few
theoretical guarantees of convergence and correctness for general loopy graphs
that may have many short cycles. Of these, even fewer provide exact ``necessary
and sufficient'' characterizations.
  In this paper we investigate the problem of using max-product to find the
maximum weight matching in an arbitrary graph with edge weights. This is done
by first constructing a probability distribution whose mode corresponds to the
optimal matching, and then running max-product. Weighted matching can also be
posed as an integer program, for which there is an LP relaxation. This
relaxation is not always tight. In this paper we show that \begin{enumerate}
\item If the LP relaxation is tight, then max-product always converges, and
that too to the correct answer. \item If the LP relaxation is loose, then
max-product does not converge. \end{enumerate} This provides an exact,
data-dependent characterization of max-product performance, and a precise
connection to LP relaxation, which is a well-studied optimization technique.
Also, since LP relaxation is known to be tight for bipartite graphs, our
results generalize other recent results on using max-product to find weighted
matchings in bipartite graphs.

In this work, we investigate the cosmology of modified Gauss-Bonnet gravity, which is an extension of General Relativity. The key idea behind this theory is that the Gauss-Bonnet term, which is a curvature invariant, is added to the Einstein-Hilbert action, giving rise to an additional contribution to the gravitational field equations. We study the cosmological implications of this modification, focusing on the early universe and the inflationary era.

Our methodology involves the numerical integration of the cosmological equations for a variety of initial conditions, exploring the parameter space of the theory. We use the Planck data on the cosmic microwave background radiation to constrain the parameters of the model, and compare the theoretical predictions with observations. We find that the modified Gauss-Bonnet gravity is capable of providing a viable alternative to the standard inflationary paradigm, with several interesting features such as the possibility of a graceful exit from inflation and a reduction in the tensor-to-scalar ratio.

The main motivation for this research is to explore the consequences of modifying gravity at the cosmological scale, in the light of the current observational data. Our results indicate that the modified Gauss-Bonnet gravity can provide a compelling cosmological model, worth further investigation in the search for a complete theory of gravity.

To understand the whole set of positive and null data on the
Theta+(1530)-production, we suggest the hypothesis that multiquark hadrons are
mainly generated from many-quark states, which emerge either as short-term
hadron fluctuations, or as hadron remnants in hard processes. This approach
allows us to describe both non-observation of the Theta+ in current null
experiments and peculiar features of its production in positive experiments.
Further, we are able to propose new experiments that might be decisive for the
problem of the Theta+ existence. Distributions of the Theta+ in such
experiments can give important information both on higher Fock components of
conventional hadrons and about structure and hadronization properties of hadron
remnants produced in hard processes. We also explain that description of
multiquark hadrons may require a modified form of the constituent quark model,
with quark masses and couplings being intermediate between their values for the
familiar constituent quarks and the current ones.

One of the most notorious non-Fermi liquid properties of both archetypal
heavy-fermion systems [1-4] and the high-Tc copper oxide superconductors [5] is
an electrical resistivity that evolves linearly with temperature, T. In the
heavy-fermion superconductor CeCoIn5 [5], this linear behaviour was one of the
first indications of the presence of a zero-temperature instability, or quantum
critical point. Here, we report the observation of a unique control parameter
of T-linear scattering in CeCoIn5, found through systematic chemical
substitutions of both magnetic and non-magnetic rare-earth, R, ions into the Ce
sub-lattice. We find that the evolution of inelastic scattering in Ce1-xRxCoIn5
is strongly dependent on the f-electron configuration of the R ion, whereas two
other key properties -- Cooper-pair breaking and Kondo-lattice coherence -- are
not. Thus, T-linear resistivity in CeCoIn5 is intimately related to the nature
of incoherent scattering centers in the Kondo lattice, which provides insight
into the anomalous scattering rate synonymous with quantum criticality [7].

We analyze a probabilistic cellular automaton describing the dynamics of
coexistence of a predator-prey system. The individuals of each species are
localized over the sites of a lattice and the local stochastic updating rules
are inspired on the processes of the Lotka-Volterra model. Two levels of
mean-field approximations are set up. The simple approximation is equivalent to
an extended patch model, a simple metapopulation model with patches colonized
by prey, patches colonized by predators and empty patches. This approximation
is capable of describing the limited available space for species occupancy. The
pair approximation is moreover able to describe two types of coexistence of
prey and predators: one where population densities are constant in time and
another displaying self-sustained time-oscillations of the population
densities. The oscillations are associated with limit cycles and arise through
a Hopf bifurcation. They are stable against changes in the initial conditions
and, in this sense, they differ from the Lotka-Volterra cycles which depend on
initial conditions. In this respect, the present model is biologically more
realistic than the Lotka-Volterra model.

In this paper, we analyze the use of separable operations for entanglement transformations. The problem we attempt to solve is the characterization of the set of entanglement transformations that can be achieved without the use of entangling operations. We propose a methodology that is focused on the modification of the Schmidt decomposition of the initial and target states, by means of local operations. Our key idea is that entanglement transformations can be achieved by modifying the weights and/or the structure of the Schmidt decomposition while preserving its nonnegativity. By using this methodology, we provide a complete characterization of the set of entanglement transformations that can be achieved using separable operations in different scenarios, including bipartite pure states, multipartite pure states, and mixed states. Moreover, we derive the conditions for the existence of a separable entanglement transformation in terms of the Schmidt rank and the Schmidt coefficients of the initial and target states. Our results provide new insights into the understanding of entanglement transformations and the role of separable operations.

In this work, we present a new approach to the Generalized Twistor Transform and Dualities in quantum field theory, aimed at providing a fresh perspective on the description of particles with spin, beyond the cases of free and massless fields. Specifically, we propose a novel formalism based on the introduction of a spinorial wave function, which encapsulates the relevant geometric and algebraic properties of spacetime and spin degrees of freedom. Our approach allows us to naturally incorporate both chiral and spinor structures, and enables us to establish new connections between different classes of theories, such as conformal field theory, super Yang-Mills, and gravity.

The motivation for this research lies in the need for a deeper understanding of the fundamental aspects of particle physics and their relation to the underlying geometry of spacetime. In particular, our work provides insights into the algebraic and geometric structures that govern the behavior of particles with spin in various theories, and sheds light on the role of duality symmetries in their description. Moreover, our results have potential applications in the study of high-energy physics and the unification of the fundamental forces of nature. Overall, our work provides a novel formalism for describing particles with spin beyond the free and massless cases, and opens new avenues for exploring the interplay between geometry, algebra, and quantum mechanics.

In this work, we investigate the redshift and geometrical aspect of photons by studying their trajectories in the vicinity of a massive object. Our motivation for this research is to deepen our understanding of the effects of gravity on the properties of light, which is a fundamental aspect of modern astrophysics. We approach this problem by using the framework of general relativity, which provides a powerful tool for describing the curvature of spacetime due to the presence of matter. In particular, we focus on the Schwarzschild metric, which describes the geometry around a non-rotating and spherically symmetric object. 

Our key result is the derivation of an analytical expression for the redshift of a photon as it moves along a radial trajectory near a massive object. This redshift is found to depend on both the gravitational potential and the velocity of the photon, which leads to interesting consequences such as the existence of a critical velocity beyond which the redshift becomes infinite. Additionally, we analyze the bending of a photon's trajectory due to the curvature of spacetime, which is related to the deflection angle predicted by general relativity. We confirm the validity of our results by comparing them with existing observations of astronomical objects in the vicinity of massive objects, such as black holes and neutron stars. 

Our methodology uses a combination of analytical calculations, numerical simulations, and physical intuition to obtain a comprehensive understanding of the effects of gravity on photons. Our findings provide valuable insight into the behavior of light in extreme conditions, and can be applied to a wide range of astrophysical phenomena, from the dynamics of stars in galaxy clusters to the structure of the cosmic microwave background radiation.

A generalized geometric method is developed for constructing exact solutions
of gravitational field equations in Einstein theory and generalizations. First,
we apply the formalism of nonholonomic frame deformations (formally considered
for nonholonomic manifolds and Finsler spaces) when the gravitational field
equations transform into systems of nonlinear partial differential equations
which can be integrated in general form. The new classes of solutions are
defined by generic off-diagonal metrics depending on integration functions on
one, two and three (or three and four) variables if we consider four (or five)
dimensional spacetimes. Second, we use a general scheme when one (two)
parameter families of exact solutions are defined by any source-free solutions
of Einstein's equations with one (two) Killing vector field(s). A successive
iteration procedure results in new classes of solutions characterized by an
infinite number of parameters for a non-Abelian group involving arbitrary
functions on one variable. Five classes of exact off-diagonal solutions are
constructed in vacuum Einstein and in string gravity describing solitonic
pp-wave interactions. We explore possible physical consequences of such
solutions derived from primary Schwarzschild or pp-wave metrics.

We investigate the plasma flow properties inside a Supergranular (SG) cell,
in particular its interaction with small scale magnetic field structures. The
SG cell has been identified using the magnetic network (CaII wing brightness)
as proxy, applying the Two-Level Structure Tracking (TST) to high spatial,
spectral and temporal resolution observations obtained by IBIS. The full 3D
velocity vector field for the SG has been reconstructed at two different
photospheric heights. In order to strengthen our findings, we also computed the
mean radial flow of the SG by means of cork tracing. We also studied the
behaviour of the horizontal and Line of Sight plasma flow cospatial with
cluster of bright CaII structures of magnetic origin to better understand the
interaction between photospheric convection and small scale magnetic features.
The SG cell we investigated seems to be organized with an almost radial flow
from its centre to the border. The large scale divergence structure is probably
created by a compact region of constant up-flow close to the cell centre. On
the edge of the SG, isolated regions of strong convergent flow are nearby or
cospatial with extended clusters of bright CaII wing features forming the knots
of the magnetic network.

A significant fraction of nearby elliptical galaxies are known to have high
density gas disks in their circumnuclear (CN) region (0.1 to a few kpc). Yet,
ellipticals, especially luminous ones, show little signs of recent star
formation (SF). To investigate the possible cause of the dearth of SF in these
systems, we study the gravitational stability of CN gas disks embedded within
the potentials of both the stellar bulge and the central massive black hole
(BH) in ellipticals. We find that CN disks in higher mass galaxies are
generally more stable than those in lower mass galaxies, because higher mass
galaxies tend to have more massive BHs and more centrally concentrated stellar
density profiles. We also consider the case in which the central stellar
density profile has a core, which is often observed for ellipticals whose total
stellar mass is higher than about 10^11 Msun. Such a cored stellar density
profile leads to more unstable CN disks than the power-law density profile
characteristic of less massive galaxies. However, the more massive BHs in
high-mass galaxies act to stabilize the CN disk. Our results demonstrate that
the gravitational potentials of both the central BH and the stellar component
should be taken into account when studying the properties of CN disks, as their
stability is sensitive to both the BH mass and the stellar density profile. Our
results could explain the observed trend that less luminous ellipticals have a
greater tendency to exhibit ongoing SF than giant ellipticals.

In this work, we present a detailed analysis of generic gravitational wave signals produced by the collapse of rotating stellar cores. The motivation behind this research is twofold. Firstly, rotating stellar cores are expected to be the progenitors of many astrophysical events that produce detectable gravitational waves, such as core-collapse supernovae and black hole formation. Secondly, existing models for predicting these signals are limited in their applicability and accuracy, and there is a need to develop more robust and general models. 

To address this, we use numerical simulations to study the collapse of rotating polytropic stars, varying the equation of state, initial conditions, and rotation rates. We use the spectral Einstein code to compute the gravitational wave signals produced by the collapse, identifying key features in the waveforms such as the presence of multiple oscillations, quasi-normal modes, and frequency modulations. Additionally, we investigate the impact of different parameters on the signal properties, such as the rotational energy and the equation of state.

Our results demonstrate a clear connection between the properties of the collapsing star and the resulting gravitational wave signals, highlighting the importance of considering rotating stellar cores as a source of generic gravitational wave signals. This study provides a new framework for understanding and predicting the gravitational wave signals produced by core-collapse events, with potential applications in the field of gravitational wave astronomy.

We present the results from a study of the long-term optical spectral
variations of BL Lacertae, using the long and well-sampled B and R-band light
curves of the Whole Earth Blazar Telescope (WEBT) collaboration, binned on time
intervals of 1 day. The relation between spectral slope and flux (the spectrum
gets bluer as the source flux increases) is well described by a power-law
model, although there is significant scatter around the best-fitting model
line. To some extent, this is due to the spectral evolution of the source
(along well-defined loop-like structures) during low-amplitude events, which
are superimposed on the major optical flares, and evolve on time scales of a
few days. The "bluer-when-brighter" mild chromatism of the long-term variations
of the source can be explained if the flux increases/decreases faster in the B
than in the R band. The B and R-band variations are well correlated, with no
significant, measurable delays larger than a few days. On the other hand, we
find that the spectral variations lead those in the flux light curves by ~ 4
days. Our results can be explained in terms of Doppler factor variations due to
changes in the viewing angle of a curved and inhomogeneous emitting jet.

We investigate the age constraints that can be placed on the double pulsar
system using models for the spin-down of the first-born 22.7-ms pulsar A and
the 2.77-s pulsar B with characteristic ages of 210 and 50 Myr respectively.
Standard models assuming dipolar spin-down of both pulsars suggest that the
time since the formation of B is ~50 Myr, i.e. close to B's characteristic age.
However, adopting models which account for the impact of A's relativistic wind
on B's spin-down we find that the formation of B took place either 80 or 180
Myr ago, depending the interaction mechanism. Formation 80 Myr ago, closer to
B's characteristic age, would result in the contribution from J0737-3039 to the
inferred coalescence rates for double neutron star binaries increasing by 40%.
The 180 Myr age is closer to A's characteristic age and would be consistent
with the most recent estimates of the coalescence rate. The new age constraints
do not significantly impact recent estimates of the kick velocity, tilt angle
between pre and post-supernova orbital planes or pre-supernova mass of B's
progenitor.

In this work, we investigate capillary ordering and layering transitions in two-dimensional hard-rod fluids. The problem we attempt to solve is understanding the fundamental behavior of hard rods in the presence of attractive forces. Specifically, we investigate how the hard rods self-assemble into ordered structures due to capillary interactions and layering transitions. Our research is motivated by the importance of understanding the behavior of simple model systems in fundamental physics and engineering applications.

The key idea of our study is that we utilize molecular dynamics simulations to explore the systematics of capillary ordering in two-dimensional systems of hard rods with long-range attractive forces. We investigate the behavior of the system as the density and temperature are varied, and we analyze the resulting structures using statistical measures such as radial distribution functions and orientational correlation functions. Additionally, we investigate the role of the size distribution of the rods on the formation of the capillary ordering transition.

Our methodology involves a combination of simulations and numerical analysis to probe the behavior of the system. We utilize a self-consistent field approach to verify the results of our simulations and to elucidate the role of the attractive forces in the ordering behavior. Our findings suggest that the capillary ordering transition is sensitive to the size distribution of rods, and that this ordering can be tuned by adjusting the attractive forces. Overall, our work provides a detailed understanding of capillary ordering and layering transitions in two-dimensional hard-rod fluids and could have implications in fields such as materials science and soft matter physics.

In this work, we model the spectral energy distribution (SED) and variability of 3C 66A during the Whole Earth Blazar Telescope (WEBT) campaign of 2003 -- 2004. The main problem that we aim to solve is the understanding of the nature of variability of 3C 66A. This source has been studied extensively in the past, but the nature of its variability is still not completely understood. The motivation for our research is to provide a more complete understanding of this source, which will aid in the development of models for other similar objects.

Our methodology involves collecting data from various ground-based and space-based telescopes, which are fitted with a variety of instruments, including photometers, spectrometers, and polarimeters. We construct the SED using the data obtained from these instruments and use it to model the variability of 3C 66A. We also use a statistical analysis to determine the significance of the variability detected in the source. Our key idea involves the use of multiple instruments to obtain a complete dataset and the construction of SED models that are capable of representing the variability in the source.

In conclusion, our work aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of the variability of 3C 66A as observed during the WEBT campaign. The combination of our multi-instrument approach and statistical analysis provides a robust and rigorous methodology for studying the variability of blazars, which will aid in the development of models for these sources.

Atoms at liquid metal surfaces are known to form layers parallel to the
surface. We analyze the two-dimensional arrangement of atoms within such layers
at the surface of liquid sodium, using ab initio molecular dynamics (MD)
simulations based on density functional theory. Nearest neighbor distributions
at the surface indicate mostly 5-fold coordination, though there are noticeable
fractions of 4-fold and 6-fold coordinated atoms. Bond angle distributions
suggest a movement toward the angles corresponding to a six-fold coordinated
hexagonal arrangement of the atoms as the temperature is decreased towards the
solidification point. We rationalize these results with a distorted hexagonal
order at the surface, showing a mixture of regions of five and six-fold
coordination. The liquid surface results are compared with classical MD
simulations of the liquid surface, with similar effects appearing, and with ab
initio MD simulations for a model solid-liquid interface, where a pronounced
shift towards hexagonal ordering is observed as the temperature is lowered.

In this work, we investigate dynamical traps and chaotic advection in a meandering jet flow by using numerical simulations. The main objective of this research is to understand the complex transport phenomena in the meandering jet flow and to explore the possibility of trapping particles in the flow. Particle trajectories are computed using Lagrangian analysis, and the chaotic advection is characterized using the finite-time Lyapunov exponent (FTLE) analysis. Our results demonstrate that the meandering jet flow is capable of trapping particles and creating dynamical traps. The FTLE analysis reveals the formation of several unstable and stable manifolds, which provide further insight into the chaotic advection and trapping mechanism in the flow. We also investigate the impact of varying the jet amplitude, frequency, and Reynolds number on trapping and advection phenomena. Our findings indicate that the amplitude and frequency of the jet play a crucial role in determining the trapping behavior, while the Reynolds number mainly affects the chaotic advection in the flow. Overall, our work contributes to the understanding of complex transport phenomena and may have implications in various applications, such as microfluidics and environmental modeling.

The present work presents the ASTROD I Charging Simulation and Disturbances that explores the charging behavior and disturbances caused to the spacecraft. The motivation behind this research is to understand the charging phenomena and its impact, which has been identified as an essential aspect of the space environment. The methodology involved in this work includes the simulation of the charging phenomenon using the semi-implicit particle-in-cell (PIC) method. We consider cases of a spacecraft in geosynchronous orbit encountering the electron flux and investigate the associated potential distribution, and current and power densities. Our rigorous analysis of spacecraft charging and its impact on the performance revealed that the charging potential can become sufficiently large to disrupt the satellite's on-board electronics. Moreover, charging transient-induced electrostatic discharge deteriorates the satellite's performances. The obtained results highlight that the charging behavior depends on the spacecraft's design, materials, and orbit, and its effect should be considered from the beginning of the mission planning phase. This study opens new avenues for the understanding of charging behavior, and further research is needed to mitigate the risks associated with the charging.

Our work titled “Characterization of P2P IPTV Traffic: Scaling Analysis” explores the fundamental understanding of Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Internet Protocol Television (IPTV) traffic. P2P networks are increasingly becoming popular, and IPTV, in particular, generates a considerable amount of traffic, which can affect network performance. Therefore, the primary aim of this study is to characterize P2P IPTV traffic and analyze its scaling properties to improve network performance.

Our methodology involves analyzing P2P IPTV traffic measurements obtained from a real-world trace, collected during the broadcast of the 2010 FIFA World Cup. We focused on identifying the scaling properties of P2P IPTV traffic using a statistical approach to provide an understanding of the traffic generation process.

Our work attempts to solve the problem of the lack of understanding of P2P IPTV traffic's scaling properties, which is critical in managing network resources effectively. This research aims to contribute towards the development of efficient network management strategies to enhance network performance and user experience.

This work's motivation is to provide insights into the characteristics of P2P IPTV traffic, which can assist in mitigating the challenges posed by such traffic on network performance. This research also contributes to the area of network traffic engineering and offers a practical approach to analyze and manage P2P IPTV traffic.

In this work, we aim to investigate the hitting probabilities for a class of non-linear stochastic heat equations under the influence of multiplicative noise. The problem we attempt to solve is to determine the probability of a stochastic heat equation reaching a desired level or, alternatively, remaining in a particular region of its state space. To achieve this goal, we develop a novel methodology, which involves constructing upper and lower bounds for the probabilities of interest using the Feynman-Kac formula. We then use the stochastic maximum principle to derive necessary and sufficient conditions for the hitting probabilities.

Our motivation for this research stems from the wide-ranging applications of stochastic heat equations in several fields including physics, finance, biology and engineering. Accurately estimating the hitting probabilities is essential to correctly predict the behavior of the system and make informed decisions. In addition, our analysis sheds light on the underlying dynamics of the system and its sensitivity to external forces.

Overall, our work contributes towards advancing the theoretical understanding of non-linear stochastic heat equations and provides a powerful tool for practitioners to analyze and manage risk in real-world situations.

The Infrared Camera (IRC) is one of two focal-plane instruments on the AKARI
satellite. It is designed for wide-field deep imaging and low-resolution
spectroscopy in the near- to mid-infrared (1.8--26.5um) in the pointed
observation mode of AKARI. IRC is also operated in the survey mode to make an
all-sky survey at 9 and 18um. It comprises three channels. The NIR channel
(1.8--5.5um) employs a 512 x 412 InSb array, whereas both the MIR-S
(4.6--13.4um) and MIR-L (12.6--26.5um) channels use 256 x 256 Si:As impurity
band conduction arrays. Each of the three channels has a field-of-view of about
10' x 10' and are operated simultaneously. The NIR and MIR-S share the same
field-of-view by virtue of a beam splitter. The MIR-L observes the sky about
$25' away from the NIR/MIR-S field-of-view. IRC gives us deep insights into the
formation and evolution of galaxies, the evolution of planetary disks, the
process of star-formation, the properties of interstellar matter under various
physical conditions, and the nature and evolution of solar system objects. The
in-flight performance of IRC has been confirmed to be in agreement with the
pre-flight expectation. This paper summarizes the design and the in-flight
operation and imaging performance of IRC.

We reanalyse leptogenesis via the out-of-equilibrium decay of the lightest
right-handed neutrino in type II seesaw scenarios, taking into account
flavour-dependent effects. In the type II seesaw mechanism, in addition to the
type I seesaw contribution, an additional direct mass term for the light
neutrinos is present. We consider type II seesaw scenarios where this
additional contribution arises from the vacuum expectation value of a Higgs
triplet, and furthermore an effective model-independent approach. We
investigate bounds on the flavour-specific decay asymmetries, on the mass of
the lightest right-handed neutrino and on the reheat temperature of the early
universe, and compare them to the corresponding bounds in the type I seesaw
framework. We show that while flavour-dependent thermal type II leptogenesis
becomes more efficient for larger mass scale of the light neutrinos, and the
bounds become relaxed, the type I seesaw scenario for leptogenesis becomes more
constrained. We also argue that in general, flavour-dependent effects cannot be
ignored when dealing with leptogenesis in type II seesaw models.

In this paper, we aim to establish the equivalence between the Linear Programming (LP) relaxation and Max-Product for solving the problem of weighted matching in general graphs. The primary motivation for this research is the need for efficient methods of solving combinatorial optimization problems. The weighted matching problem is a fundamental problem in graph theory and has many real-world applications, such as matching patients to organ donors in the field of medical science and matching tutors with students in educational domains.

Our work shows that the Max-Product and LP relaxation algorithms are equivalent for the weighted matching problem in general graphs. The proof we present is based on the duality theory of Linear Programming and exhibits the gap between the two approaches. The equivalence we establish between the LP relaxation and Max-Product algorithms is significant for the theoretical aspects of combinatorial optimization and contributes to the existing literature in this field.

The problem we attempt to solve is to find a more efficient algorithm for solving the weighted matching problem. Our research provides insights into the optimality of the Max-Product algorithm and demonstrates the effectiveness of LP relaxation in solving this problem. Therefore, our work is useful for researchers and practitioners working in the field of combinatorial optimization, algorithm design, and graph theory.

In this work, we investigate the scaling laws and intermittency in highly compressible turbulence, which is a fundamental phenomenon that occurs in various physical systems. The problem addressed in this research is to understand the mechanisms of energy transfer in highly compressible turbulence, which have been a long-standing issue due to the complex nature of the flow. 

Our approach is based on numerical simulations of highly compressible turbulence, where we use a computational fluid dynamics solver coupled with a sophisticated compressible flow solver. We analyze the results of our simulations using statistical tools such as structure functions and probability density functions. 

Our results show that highly compressible turbulence exhibits a self-similar behavior, which is characterized by universal scaling laws as a function of the compressibility parameter. Moreover, we find that the intermittency of the flow is also dependent on the compressibility parameter, indicating the presence of anomalous scaling in the energy transfer cascade. 

The motivation for this research is to provide insights into the fundamental mechanisms of energy transfer in highly compressible turbulence, which has important applications in various fields such as astrophysics, atmospheric science, and aerospace engineering. Our work contributes to the understanding of the complex nature of highly compressible flows, which is essential for the advancement of science and technology.

In this paper, we investigate the linearity versus non-linearity of the Large
Magellanic Cloud (LMC) Cepheid period-luminosity (P-L) relation using two
statistical approaches not previously applied to this problem: the testimator
method and the Schwarz Information Criterion (SIC). The testimator method is
extended to multiple stages for the first time, shown to be unbiased and the
variance of the estimated slope can be proved to be smaller than the standard
slope estimated from linear regression theory. The Schwarz Information
Criterion (also known as the Bayesian Information Criterion) is more
conservative than the Akaike Information Criterion and tends to choose lower
order models. By using simulated data sets, we verify that these statistical
techniques can be used to detect intrinsically linear and/or non-linear P-L
relations. These methods are then applied to independent LMC Cepheid data sets
from the OGLE project and the MACHO project, respectively. Our results imply
that there is a change of slope in longer period ranges for all of the data
sets. This strongly supports previous results, obtained from independent
statistical tests, that the observed LMC P-L relation is non-linear with a
break period at/around 10 days.

In this work, we present a distributed implementation of standard oracle operators. The motivation for this research stems from the growing need for efficient and scalable query processing in modern data-intensive applications. Traditional centralized query processing systems often face serious limitations in terms of scalability and fault tolerance, making them unsuitable for large-scale data processing. In order to overcome these limitations, we propose a distributed approach to oracle operator implementation that is optimized for large-scale data processing environments.

The problem that our work attempts to solve is to achieve efficient and scalable query processing in distributed systems while ensuring fault tolerance and high performance. We propose a distributed implementation of standard oracle operators such as filtering, projection, aggregation, and join operations that can be executed in a fault-tolerant and scalable manner on distributed clusters.

The results obtained show that our approach to distributed implementation of standard oracle operators provides efficient and scalable query processing while maintaining high performance and fault tolerance. Our experiments show the superiority of our distributed implementation over traditional centralized approaches in terms of scalability and response time. Overall, our work contributes to the development of efficient and scalable query processing systems capable of handling large-scale data processing challenges.

Recent results from the HESS gamma ray telescope have shown the presence of
both a diffuse, extended, flux of gamma rays above ~0.4 TeV and discrete
sources in and near the Galactic Centre. Here, we put forward a possible
explanation in terms of the diffusion of cosmic ray protons from a succession
of supernova remnants (SNR) in the SgrA* region of the Galaxy plus a
contribution from SNR in the rest of the Galactic Centre Region, to be called
the Galactic Centre Ridge (GCR). Protons are favoured over electrons because
the mG magnetic fields in the Region will attenuate energetic electrons
severely. Prominent features are the need for 'anomalous diffusion' of the
protons in the whole region and the adoption of low efficiency for SNR
acceleration in the high density regions. The latter is related by us to the
well-known low 'cosmic ray gradient' in the Galaxy. A corroborating feature is
the close correlation of inferred cosmic ray intensity with the smoothed
intensity of 5 GHZ radio radiation. We attribute this to the presence of the
SNR in the GCR.

Because of its proximity the Virgo Cluster is an excellent target for
studying interactions of galaxies with the cluster environment. Both the
high-velocity tidal interactions and effects of ram pressure stripping by the
intracluster gas can be investigated. Optical and/or \ion{H}{i} observations do
not always show effects of weak interactions between galaxies and their
encounters with the cluster medium. For this reason we searched for possible
anomalies in the magnetic field structure in Virgo Cluster spirals which could
be attributed to perturbations in their gas distribution and kinematics. Five
angularly large Virgo Cluster spiral galaxies (NGC 4501, NGC 4438, NGC 4535,
NGC 4548 and NGC 4654) were the targets for a sensitive total power and
polarization study using the 100-m radio telescope in Effelsberg at 4.85 GHz.
For two objects polarization data at higher frequencies have been obtained
allowing Faraday rotation analysis. Distorted magnetic field structures were
identified in all galaxies. Interaction-induced magnetized outflows were found
in NGC 4438 (due to nuclear activity) and NGC 4654 (a combination of tidal
tails and ram pressure effects). Almost all objects (except the anaemic NGC
4548) exhibit distortions in polarized radio continuum attributable to
influence of the ambient gas. For some galaxies they agree with observations of
other species, but sometimes (NGC 4535) the magnetic field is the only tracer
of the interaction with the cluster environment. The cluster environment
clearly affects the evolution of the galaxies due to ram pressure and tidal
effects. Magnetic fields provide a very long-lasting memory of past
interactions. Therefore, they are a good tracer of weak interactions which are
difficult to detect by other observations.

We present first results of our simulations of magnetic fields in the
formation of single and binary stars using a recently developed method for
incorporating Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) into the Smoothed Particle
Hydrodynamics (SPH) method. An overview of the method is presented before
discussing the effect of magnetic fields on the formation of circumstellar
discs around young stars. We find that the presence of magnetic fields during
the disc formation process can lead to significantly smaller and less massive
discs which are much less prone to gravitational instability. Similarly in the
case of binary star formation we find that magnetic fields, overall, suppress
fragmentation. However these effects are found to be largely driven by magnetic
pressure. The relative importance of magnetic tension is dependent on the
orientation of the field with respect to the rotation axis, but can, with the
right orientation, lead to a dilution of the magnetic pressure-driven
suppression of fragmentation.

A scale invariant model containing dilaton $\phi$ and dust (as a model of
matter) is studied where the shift symmetry $\phi\to\phi +const.$ is
spontaneously broken at the classical level due to intrinsic features of the
model. The dilaton to matter coupling "constant" $f$ appears to be dependent of
the matter density. In normal conditions, i.e. when the matter energy density
is many orders of magnitude larger than the dilaton contribution to the dark
energy density, $f$ becomes less than the ratio of the "mass of the vacuum" in
the volume occupied by the matter to the Planck mass. The model yields this
kind of "Archimedes law" without any especial (intended for this) choice of the
underlying action and without fine tuning of the parameters. The model not only
explains why all attempts to discover a scalar force correction to Newtonian
gravity were unsuccessful so far but also predicts that in the near future
there is no chance to detect such corrections in the astronomical measurements
as well as in the specially designed fifth force experiments on intermediate,
short (like millimeter) and even ultrashort (a few nanometer) ranges. This
prediction is alternative to predictions of other known models.

We study the distinct effects of Dark Matter and Dark Energy on the future
evolution of nearby large scale structures using constrained N-body
simulations. We contrast a model of Cold Dark Matter and a Cosmological
Constant (LCDM) with an Open CDM (OCDM) model with the same matter density
Omega_m =0.3 and the same Hubble constant h=0.7. Already by the time the scale
factor increased by a factor of 6 (29 Gyr from now in LCDM; 78 Gyr from now in
OCDM) the comoving position of the Local Group is frozen. Well before that
epoch the two most massive members of the Local Group, the Milky Way and
Andromeda, will merge. However, as the expansion rates of the scale factor in
the two models are different, the Local Group will be receding in physical
coordinates from Virgo exponentially in a LCDM model and at a roughly constant
velocity in an OCDM model. More generally, in comoving coordinates the future
large scale structure will look like a sharpened image of the present
structure: the skeleton of the cosmic web will remain the same, but clusters
will be more `isolated' and the filaments will become thinner. This implies
that the long-term fate of large scale structure as seen in comoving
coordinates is determined primarily by the matter density. We conclude that
although the LCDM model is accelerating at present due to its Dark Energy
component while the OCDM model is non accelerating, their large scale structure
in the future will look very similar in comoving coordinates.

Two approaches to the study of cosmological density perturbations in modified
theories of Palatini gravity have recently been discussed. These utilise,
respectively, a generalisation of Birkhoff's theorem and a direct linearization
of the gravitational field equations. In this paper these approaches are
compared and contrasted. The general form of the gravitational lagrangian for
which the two frameworks yield identical results in the long-wavelength limit
is derived. This class of models includes the case where the lagrangian is a
power-law of the Ricci curvature scalar. The evolution of density perturbations
in theories of the type $f(R)=R-c /R^ b$ is investigated numerically. It is
found that the results obtained by the two methods are in good agreement on
sufficiently large scales when the values of the parameters (b,c) are
consistent with current observational constraints. However, this agreement
becomes progressively poorer for models that differ significantly from the
standard concordance model and as smaller scales are considered.

When modeling the distribution of a set of data by a mixture of Gaussians,
there are two possibilities: i) the classical one is using a set of parameters
which are the proportions, the means and the variances; ii) the second is to
consider the proportions as the probabilities of a discrete valued hidden
variable. In the first case a usual prior distribution for the proportions is
the Dirichlet which accounts for the fact that they have to sum up to one. In
the second case, to each data is associated a hidden variable for which we
consider two possibilities: a) assuming those variables to be i.i.d. We show
then that this scheme is equivalent to the classical mixture model with
Dirichlet prior; b) assuming a Markovian structure. Then we choose the simplest
markovian model which is the Potts distribution. As we will see this model is
more appropriate for the case where the data represents the pixels of an image
for which the hidden variables represent a segmentation of that image. The main
object of this paper is to give some details on these models and different
algorithms used for their simulation and the estimation of their parameters.
  Key Words: Mixture of Gaussians, Dirichlet, Potts, Classification,
Segmentation.
