Medvedev and Melott (2007) have suggested that periodicity in fossil
biodiversity may be induced by cosmic rays which vary as the Solar System
oscillates normal to the galactic disk. We re-examine the evidence for a 62
million year (Myr) periodicity in biodiversity throughout the Phanerozoic
history of animal life reported by Rohde & Mueller (2005), as well as related
questions of periodicity in origination and extinction. We find that the signal
is robust against variations in methods of analysis, and is based on
fluctuations in the Paleozoic and a substantial part of the Mesozoic.
Examination of origination and extinction is somewhat ambiguous, with results
depending upon procedure. Origination and extinction intensity as defined by RM
may be affected by an artifact at 27 Myr in the duration of stratigraphic
intervals. Nevertheless, when a procedure free of this artifact is implemented,
the 27 Myr periodicity appears in origination, suggesting that the artifact may
ultimately be based on a signal in the data. A 62 Myr feature appears in
extinction, when this same procedure is used. We conclude that evidence for a
periodicity at 62 Myr is robust, and evidence for periodicity at approximately
27 Myr is also present, albeit more ambiguous.

We report results from a 30 ks observation of Cygnus X-1 with the High Energy
Transmission Grating Spectrometer (HETGS) on board the {\em Chandra X-ray
Observatory}. Numerous absorption lines were detected in the HETGS spectrum.
The lines are associated with highly ionized Ne, Na, Mg, Al, Si, S, and Fe,
some of which have been seen in earlier HETGS observations. Surprisingly,
however, we discovered dramatic variability of the lines over the duration of
the present observation. For instance, the flux of the Ne X line at 12.14 \AA\
was about $5 \times 10^{-3}$ photons cm$^{-2}$ s$^{-1}$ in the early part of
the observation but became subsequently undetectable, with a 99% upper limit of
$0.06 \times 10^{-3}$ photons cm$^{-2}$ s$^{-1}$ on the flux of the line. This
implies that the line weakened by nearly two orders of magnitude on a timescale
of hours. The overall X-ray flux of the source did also vary during the
observation but only by 20--30%. For Cyg X-1, the absorption lines are
generally attributed to the absorption of X-rays by ionized stellar wind in the
binary system. Therefore, they may provide valuable diagnostics on the physical
condition of the wind. We discuss the implications of the results.

We present tests of Bayesian model selection techniques applied to gravitational wave astronomy. Our goal is to compare the effectiveness of different Bayesian algorithms in detecting gravitational wave sources and recovering their parameters, such as masses and spins of the merging compact objects. In particular, we test the performance of nested sampling, thermodynamic integration, and reversible jump Markov chain Monte Carlo (RJMCMC) algorithms. Our analysis is based on simulated gravitational wave signals corresponding to binary black hole and neutron star mergers, and we use several different noise configurations to quantify the impact of the detector's sensitivity on the algorithms' performance. 

Our results show that RJMCMC and nested sampling provide accurate results in recovering the source parameters even in challenging noise conditions. In comparison, thermodynamic integration suffers from convergence issues, and its performance is highly dependent on the chosen annealing scheme. We also find that the choice of prior and likelihood functions can significantly affect the accuracy of the results. Finally, we demonstrate the impact of model selection on the scientific interpretation of the data by comparing the inferred probability of different models for the same data set. 

Our work highlights the importance of choosing appropriate Bayesian algorithms and carefully analyzing the impact of assumptions made on the prior and likelihood functions. Our results and methodology can be useful for future gravitational wave detections, where advanced Bayesian model selection techniques will play a crucial role in uncovering the physics of compact object mergers.

ASTROD I is planned as a single spacecraft mission. It will use
interferometric and pulse ranging techniques between the spacecraft and ground
stations, to make high precision measurements of the parameters that describe
the solar system, and to test relativistic gravity with improved accuracy. At
the heart of the spacecraft is a test mass, which the spacecraft will follow
using a drag-free control system. The mission critically depends on maintaining
the geodesic motion of the test mass. Charging of the test mass due to cosmic
rays and solar particles will disturb its geodesic motion. We have modelled the
charging process using the GEANT4 toolkit and a simplified, geometrical model
and estimate that the ASTROD I test mass will charge positively, at a rate of
24 +/-7 e+/s, due to cosmic ray protons and alpha particles (3He and 4He) at
solar minimum. We have used the results of this simulation to estimate the
magnitude of disturbances associated with test mass charging, for the
worst-case scenario, taking into account uncertainties in the model and
potential charging contributions from minor cosmic-ray components.

In this work, we propose a scheme for continuous quantum error correction (CQEC) in the presence of non-Markovian decoherence. Our main goal is to improve the resilience of quantum systems to environmental fluctuations, minimizing the likelihood of error propagation and maximizing the fidelity of quantum information processing. Our approach is based on a hybrid approach combining Hamiltonian evolution and quantum measurement, where the evolution is driven by a feedback control that monitors the environment and corrects the quantum state accordingly. 

We first motivate our research by highlighting the need to overcome the limitations of traditional quantum error correction (QEC) methods, which are based on discrete measurements and short time intervals. In contrast, CQEC provides a continuous monitoring that enhances the stability and prolongs the coherence time of the quantum system, enabling real-time error detection and correction with minimal disruption of the computational process. However, the non-Markovian nature of decoherence poses a challenge due to the indefinite memory of the environment and the complex correlations between the system and its surroundings.

Our methodology employs a master equation approach, where we derive a stochastic differential equation for the system in the presence of non-Markovian noise. We then use a feedback control loop that acts on the system based on the information provided by the measurement outcomes to actively correct the quantum errors. Our results show that the proposed CQEC scheme can achieve better error suppression rates and higher fidelity compared to standard QEC methods, especially in the case of non-Markovian decoherence. This has important implications for practical quantum computing and other quantum information processing tasks, where reliability and longevity are crucial.

Using an improved version of the previously introduced CRASH (Cosmic Ray
Acceleration SHock) code, we have calculated the time evolution of cosmic-ray
(CR) modified quasi-parallel plane shocks for Bohm-like diffusion, including
self-consistent models of Alfven wave drift and dissipation, along with thermal
leakage injection of CRs. The new simulations follow evolution of the CR
distribution to much higher energies than our previous study, providing a
better examination of evolutionary and asymptotic behaviors. The postshock CR
pressure becomes constant after quick initial adjustment, since the evolution
of the CR partial pressure expressed in terms of a momentum similarity variable
is self-similar. The shock precursor, which scales as the diffusion length of
the highest energy CRs, subsequently broadens approximately linearly with time,
independent of diffusion model, so long as CRs continue to be accelerated to
ever-higher energies. This means the nonlinear shock structure can be described
approximately in terms of the similarity variable, x/(u_s t), where u_s is the
shock speed once the postshock pressure reaches an approximate time asymptotic
state. As before, the shock Mach number is the key parameter determining the
evolution and the CR acceleration efficiency, although finite Alfven wave drift
and wave energy dissipation in the shock precursor reduce the effective
velocity change experienced by CRs, so reduce acceleration efficiency
noticeably, thus, providing a second important parameter at low and moderate
Mach numbers.

This work investigates new kinematic features of parallel manipulators. It is
well known that parallel manipulators admit generally several direct kinematic
solutions for a given set of input joint values. The aim of this paper is to
characterize the uniqueness domains in the workspace of parallel manipulators,
as well as their image in the joint space. The study focuses on the most usual
case of parallel manipulators with only one inverse kinematic solution. The
notion of aspect introduced for serial manipulators in [Borrel 86] is redefined
for such parallel manipulators. Then, it is shown that it is possible to link
several solutions to the forward kinematic problem without meeting a
singularity, thus meaning that the aspects are not uniqueness domains. An
additional set of surfaces, namely the characteristic surfaces, are
characterized which divide the workspace into basic regions and yield new
uniqueness domains. This study is illustrated all along the paper with a 3-RPR
planar parallel manipulator. An octree model of spaces is used to compute the
joint space, the workspace and all other newly defined sets.

In this research, we investigated the competition between hydrogen-like and isotropic interactions on polymer collapse. We aimed to understand the driving forces behind the collapse of polymer chains in various solvents and to determine the dominance of each interaction type on this process. To achieve this goal, we employed theoretical calculations and simulations based on a coarse-grained model of polymers. 

Our findings suggest that the strength and orientation of both hydrogen-like and isotropic interactions can significantly affect the collapse behavior of a polymer chain. In particular, we found that hydrogen-like interactions can enhance the collapse, while isotropic interactions can either stabilize or destabilize the collapsed state, depending on their strength and orientation relative to the polymer backbone. 

The importance of this work lies in providing a deeper understanding of the fundamental physics of polymer collapse and in providing a theoretical basis for designing polymer-based materials with desired properties. Our results can also shed light on the behavior of biological macromolecules, such as proteins and nucleic acids, which are strongly affected by hydrogen-like and isotropic interactions in their native environments.

In this work, we present an extended optical model analysis of the elastic scattering and fusion cross-section data for the 7Li+208Pb system at near-Coulomb-barrier energies using the folding potential. The main problem that our work attempts to solve is the lack of understanding of the reaction dynamics for this system at these energies. We use the folding potential in our calculations, which is based on the nuclear matter density distribution of the interacting nuclei and provides a more accurate representation of the nuclear potential. 

We analyze the elastic scattering and fusion cross-section data using the optical model, and the extended optical model including the breakup process. We also investigate the effect of the neutron distribution of 7Li on the reaction dynamics. Our analysis shows that the extended optical model provides a better description of the elastic scattering and fusion cross-section data than the standard optical model at near-Coulomb-barrier energies. We observe that the neutron distribution of 7Li plays a significant role in the reaction dynamics. 

In conclusion, our work provides new insights into the reaction dynamics of the 7Li+208Pb system at near-Coulomb-barrier energies using the folding potential and the extended optical model. Our findings are crucial for understanding the role of the neutron distribution of 7Li in the reaction dynamics, and will help to improve the accuracy of future predictions for this system.

In this study, we aimed to simulate heme using the Density Functional Theory plus Hubbard U correction (DFT+U) and investigate the accuracy of spin-state energetics. Heme is an essential protein in many biological processes, including oxygen transport and electron transfer. The accurate determination of its spin-state energetics is crucial for understanding its functional mechanism. However, the spin-state energetics of heme are a challenging problem because of the strong electron correlation effects. In this study, we first validated the DFT+U approach by comparing our results with experimental data. We then compared our calculations with those from the DFT approach, which neglects electron correlation effects, and found that the DFT+U approach consistently gave a lower energy for the high-spin state than the DFT approach. Furthermore, we demonstrated that the inclusion of U significantly improves the accuracy of the energy predictions by taking into account the electron correlation effects. These findings reveal the importance of including electron correlation effects in simulating spin-state energetics of heme, and the DFT+U approach provides a reliable method for such simulations. This work paves the way for more accurate simulations of heme and other systems with strong electron correlation effects, and thus contributes to the advancement of computational chemistry and materials science.

We study the spectral and energetics properties of 47 long-duration gamma-ray
bursts (GRBs) with known redshift, all of them detected by the Swift satellite.
Due to the narrow energy range (15-150 keV) of the Swift-BAT detector, the
spectral fitting is reliable only for fitting models with 2 or 3 parameters. As
high uncertainty and correlation among the errors is expected, a careful
analysis of the errors is necessary. We fit both the power law (PL, 2
parameters) and cut--off power law (CPL, 3 parameters) models to the
time-integrated spectra of the 47 bursts, and present the corresponding
parameters, their uncertainties, and the correlations among the uncertainties.
The CPL model is reliable only for 29 bursts for which we estimate the nuf_nu
peak energy Epk. For these GRBs, we calculate the energy fluence and the rest-
frame isotropic-equivalent radiated energy, Eiso, as well as the propagated
uncertainties and correlations among them. We explore the distribution of our
homogeneous sample of GRBs on the rest-frame diagram E'pk vs Eiso. We confirm a
significant correlation between these two quantities (the "Amati" relation) and
we verify that, within the uncertainty limits, no outliers are present. We also
fit the spectra to a Band model with the high energy power law index frozen to
-2.3, obtaining a rather good agreement with the "Amati" relation of non-Swift
GRBs.

In this work, we explore the notion of uniqueness domains in the workspace of parallel manipulators. These domains are defined as the portions of the workspace where no two end-effector poses can be achieved by different sets of actuator configurations. We aim to identify these domains and provide a systematic approach to their analysis, which could have important implications for the design and control of parallel manipulators.

Our methodology involves applying mathematical tools from algebraic geometry and singularity theory to the study of parallel manipulators. We first establish a general framework for the analysis of uniqueness domains, which enables us to identify them algebraically. We then apply this framework to a number of specific manipulator architectures, including the Stewart platform and the 3-PRRR robot. Through numerical simulations and analysis of the resulting algebraic equations, we are able to identify and describe the geometry and topology of uniqueness domains in these models.

Our motivation for this research arises from the need for a better understanding of the kinematics and dynamics of parallel manipulators. By identifying and characterizing uniqueness domains, we can improve the design and control of these systems, enabling them to achieve more precise and reliable motion. We believe that the development of this methodology and its application to specific manipulator architectures represent a significant contribution to the field of robotics and engineering.

In this study, we investigate the dust temperatures of prestellar cores within the rho Oph main cloud and other star-forming regions and explore the implications for the core mass function. Prestellar cores are dense, collapsing structures that form protostars and ultimately, young stellar objects. Understanding their properties and distribution is crucial for advancing our knowledge of star formation. We use infrared observations from the Herschel Space Observatory to estimate the dust temperatures of prestellar cores and compare them to theoretical predictions. Our analysis reveals that the dust temperatures of prestellar cores in the rho Oph main cloud are colder than those in other star-forming regions. We show that this has important consequences for the inferred properties of these structures, particularly their masses. Specifically, colder cores have higher masses than previously estimated, leading to a shift in the shape of the core mass function towards higher masses. Our findings have implications for our understanding of star formation and the origin of the initial mass function of stars.

In our study, we explore the celestial pole offset observations in the free core nutation frequency band to gain a better understanding of Earth's rotational dynamics. The motivation for the research stems from the importance of accurate knowledge of the Earth's rotation for various applications, including navigation, geodesy, and astronomy. 

We analyze observations collected from various space and ground-based techniques and use state-of-the-art statistical techniques to separate the free core nutation frequencies from other signals. Our analysis yields a clear detection of the free core nutation signals, indicating the existence of a free inner core. 

To further investigate the rotational dynamics, we also analyze the temporal evolution of the observed offsets and compare them to theoretical predictions. Our results demonstrate good agreement between observations and theoretical models, providing greater confidence in our understanding of the Earth's rotational dynamics in the free core nutation frequency band. 

Overall, our study provides valuable insights into the Earth's rotational dynamics and has implications for various geodetic and astronomical applications. The key ideas and methodology involve rigorous statistical analysis of celestial pole offset observations to isolate the free core nutation signals and compare them to theoretical models.

We present the second installment of the Spitzer c2d survey of weak-line T Tauri stars, concentrating on characterizing the infrared spectral energy distributions (SEDs) for a sample of 105 objects. Our aim is to gain insight into the timescale and mechanisms of planet formation in these young stars. We find that 80% of the sources in our sample display excess emission longward of 4.5 μm, indicating the presence of circumstellar disks. We use radiative transfer models to fit the SEDs and estimate the disk properties, such as the inner edge radius and masses. We find that our sample of disks appear to have relatively little mass in the inner disk, which suggest that rupured inner disks may be relatively common. Finally, we perform a statistical analysis to set upper limits on the occurrence of Jupiter-mass planets in our disk sample. Our results suggest that fewer than 10% of circumstellar disks produce Jupiter-mass planets within three astronomical units, consistent with other surveys of young stars. Our findings thus provide important constraints for theories of planet formation.

Most working scientists hold fast to the concept of 'realism' - a viewpoint
according to which an external reality exists independent of observation. But
quantum physics has shattered some of our cornerstone beliefs. According to
Bell's theorem, any theory that is based on the joint assumption of realism and
locality (meaning that local events cannot be affected by actions in space-like
separated regions) is at variance with certain quantum predictions. Experiments
with entangled pairs of particles have amply confirmed these quantum
predictions, thus rendering local realistic theories untenable. Maintaining
realism as a fundamental concept would therefore necessitate the introduction
of 'spooky' actions that defy locality. Here we show by both theory and
experiment that a broad and rather reasonable class of such non-local realistic
theories is incompatible with experimentally observable quantum correlations.
In the experiment, we measure previously untested correlations between two
entangled photons, and show that these correlations violate an inequality
proposed by Leggett for non-local realistic theories. Our result suggests that
giving up the concept of locality is not sufficient to be consistent with
quantum experiments, unless certain intuitive features of realism are
abandoned.

In this paper, we explore the geometry of parallelizable manifolds in the context of generalized Lagrange spaces. The motivation for the research arises from the need to understand the interplay between parallelizable manifolds and the symplectic geometry of Lagrange spaces. The study of parallelizable manifolds has applications in many fields, including physics and engineering.

We develop a methodology that exploits the structure of generalized Lagrange spaces to study parallelizable manifolds. In particular, we show that the existence of certain specific forms on the Lagrange spaces yields additional constraints on the geometry of parallelizable manifolds. Using this methodology, we prove new and interesting results about the geometry of parallelizable manifolds, including the existence of a canonical almost complex structure and the non-existence of certain classes of parallelizable manifolds.

Our approach is rigorously mathematical and makes use of various tools from differential geometry and symplectic geometry. Our results extend and generalize existing results in the field, making contributions to the theory of parallelizable manifolds and of generalized Lagrange spaces.

Overall, our work sheds new light on the interplay between parallelizable manifolds and symplectic geometry, and provides a foundation for future studies in this area.

In this work, we examine the electron heating mechanisms in black hole accretion disks during low states. The heating mechanisms are crucial for understanding the spectral energy distribution of the disk and the emission from the corona, both of which are important quantities for observing black holes. 

Our motivation for studying electron heating in low states is twofold. First, the observed spectra during low states of black hole binaries are different from those during high states, indicative of a different accretion geometry. Second, previous models did not provide a physical explanation for this difference. Therefore, we aim to understand the electron heating mechanism responsible for these spectral differences. 

We use magneto-hydrodynamic simulations to model the accretion disk in low state. Our simulations show that magnetic reconnection is the dominant heating mechanism in the corona and drives the observed differences in the spectral energy distribution between low and high states. 

The implications of our results suggest that magnetic reconnection is not only key to understanding electron heating in black hole accretion disks, but also provides insight into the energetics of the corona and its connection with the accretion disk.

In this research, we study the dynamical behavior of a predator-prey probabilistic cellular automaton through a mean-field approach. Our goal is to understand the emergence of stable oscillations in the system and the conditions that lead to their occurrence. We propose a theoretical framework that combines a mean-field approximation with a probabilistic model of interaction between predators and preys. This allows us to derive a set of differential equations that describe the evolution of the system in terms of the densities of each species.

Using numerical simulations and analytical calculations, we find that the system exhibits stable oscillations under certain conditions. These oscillations arise due to the interplay between predation, reproduction, and random fluctuations in the population densities. We show that the amplitude and period of the oscillations are determined by the strength of the predator-prey interaction and the initial densities of the species.

Our work contributes to the understanding of the self-organization of populations in ecological systems and provides insights into how stochasticity affects their dynamics. Our methodology combines analytical and numerical tools that may be useful for studying other complex systems. We expect that our findings will inspire further research on the role of randomness in the dynamics of biological populations.

In this paper, we present a Dynamics-Controlled Truncation (DCT) scheme for nonlinear dynamics in semiconductor microcavities. The motivation behind this research is the growing need for accurate and efficient modeling of the nonlinear dynamics in microcavity systems, which play an essential role in many optoelectronic applications. In particular, the DCT scheme is designed to address the challenge of controlling the growth of numerical errors resulting from the long simulation times required for studying the nonlinear dynamics of these systems.

The key idea behind the DCT scheme is to exploit the dynamic behavior of the system to make informed decisions on the level of details needed in the simulation at each time step. This is achieved by allowing the truncation of higher-order terms in the system's equations of motion at specific time intervals, guided by the behavior of the system's variables. The DCT scheme is implemented using a split-step Fourier method, which allows for efficient computation of the system's evolution.

The problem that this work attempts to solve is the challenge of efficiently modeling and analyzing the nonlinear dynamics in microcavity systems with high accuracy and long simulation times. We demonstrate the efficacy of the DCT scheme by applying it to the study of the nonlinear dynamics of a semiconductor microcavity system and comparing the results against those obtained using conventional numerical methods. Our results show that the DCT scheme can significantly reduce the computational cost of simulating nonlinear dynamics in semiconductor microcavities while retaining high accuracy.

We have investigated a closed system of equations for the quark propagator,
obtained earlier within our general approach to QCD at low energies. It implies
quark confinement (the quark propagator has no pole, indeed), as well as the
dynamical breakdown of chiral symmetry (a chiral symmetry preserving solution
is forbidded). This system can be solved exactly in the chiral limit. We have
established the space of the smooth test functions (consisting of the Green's
functions for the quark propagator and the corresponding quark-gluon vertex) in
which our generalized function (the confining gluon propagator) becomes a
continuous linear functional. It is a linear topological space $K(c)$ of the
infinitely differentiable functions (with respect to the dimensionless momentum
variable $x$), having compact support in the region $x \leq c$. We develop an
analytical formalism, the so-called chiral perturbtion theory at the
fundamental quark level, which allows one to find explicit solution for the
quark propagator in powers of the light quark masses. We also develop an
analytical formalism, which allows one to find the solution for the quark
propagator in the inverse powers of the heavy quark masses. It justifies the
use for the heavy quark propagator its free counterpart up to terms of the
order $1/m_Q^3$, where $m_Q$ is the heavy quark mass. So this solution
automatically possesses the heavy quark spin-flavor symmetry.

In this work we compare and characterize the behavior of Langevin and
Dissipative Particle Dynamics (DPD) thermostats in a broad range of
non-equilibrium simulations of polymeric systems. Polymer brushes in relative
sliding motion, polymeric liquids in Poiseuille and Couette flows, and
brush-melt interfaces are used as model systems to analyze the efficiency and
limitations of different Langevin and DPD thermostat implementations. Widely
used coarse-grained bead-spring models under good and poor solvent conditions
are employed to assess the effects of the thermostats. We considered
equilibrium, transient, and steady state examples for testing the ability of
the thermostats to maintain constant temperature and to reproduce the
underlying physical phenomena in non-equilibrium situations. The common
practice of switching-off the Langevin thermostat in the flow direction is also
critically revisited. The efficiency of different weight functions for the DPD
thermostat is quantitatively analyzed as a function of the solvent quality and
the non-equilibrium situation.

We critically examine a scenario for the enrichment of the interstellar
medium (ISM) in which supernova ejecta follow a long (10^8 yr) journey before
falling back onto the galactic disk in the form of metal-rich ``droplets'',
These droplets do not become fully mixed with the interstellar medium until
they become photoionized in HII regions. We investigate the hypothesis that the
photoionization of these highly metallic droplets can explain the observed
``abundance discrepancy factors'' (ADFs), which are found when comparing
abundances derived from recombination lines and from collisionally excited
lines, both in Galactic and extragalactic HII regions. We derive bounds of
10^{13}--10^{15} cm on the droplet sizes inside HII regions in order that (1)
they should not have already been detected by direct imaging of nearby nebulae,
and (2) they should not be too swiftly destroyed by diffusion in the ionized
gas. From photoionization modelling we find that, if this inhomogeneous
enrichment scenario holds, then the recombination lines strongly overestimate
the metallicities of the fully mixed HII regions. The abundances derived from
collisionally excited lines also suffer some bias, although to a much lesser
extent. In the absence of any recipe for correcting these biases, we recommend
the discarding of all objects showing large ADFs from studies of galactic
chemical evolution. These biases must also be kept in mind when comparing the
galactic abundance gradients for elements derived from recombination lines with
those derived from collisionally excited lines. Finally, we propose a set of
observations that could be undertaken to test our scenario and improve our
understanding of element mixing in the ISM.

The Spitzer Quasar and ULIRG Evolution Study (QUEST) aims to investigate the evolution of quasars and ultraluminous infrared galaxies (ULIRGs) by examining their spectral energy distributions (SEDs). In the QUEST II study, we obtained mid-infrared data using the Spitzer Space Telescope for a sample of 53 Palomar-Green (PG) quasars at redshifts 0.06 < z < 1.34. The key idea underlying this study is that the SEDs of active galaxies, including quasars and ULIRGs, can provide important insights into the physical mechanisms driving their evolution. 

The methodology employed in this study involves fitting the SEDs of the PG quasars with a set of models that account for various emission components including dust emission, synchrotron radiation, and AGN accretion disk emission. We find that the SEDs of the PG quasars are best described by including contributions from an AGN accretion disk and a dust torus component. Furthermore, we find a significant correlation between the strength of the silicate dust feature and the X-ray luminosity of the AGN. 

The results of this study provide new insights into the physical processes driving the evolution of quasars. The observation of a correlation between the silicate dust feature strength and the X-ray luminosity of the AGN suggests that the dust torus surrounding the AGN is directly affected by the activity of the accretion disk. This finding provides new clues for understanding the complex interplay between AGN and their host galaxies. The Spitzer QUEST project is a valuable tool for the study of active galactic nuclei, and these results represent a significant contribution to the field.

We investigate the origin of the GRB 060912A, which has observational
properties that make its classification as either a long or short burst
ambiguous. Short duration GRBs (SGRBs) are thought to have typically lower
energies than long duration bursts, can be found in galaxies with populations
of all ages and are likely to originate from different progenitors to the long
duration bursts. However, it has become clear that duration alone is
insufficient to make a distinction between the two populations in many cases,
leading to a desire to find additional discriminators of burst type. GRB
060912A had a duration of 6 s and occurred only ~10 arcsec from a bright, low
redshift ($z=0.0936$) elliptical galaxy, suggesting that this may have been the
host, which would favour it being a short-burst. However, our deep optical
imaging and spectroscopy of the location of GRB 060912A using the VLT shows
that GRB 060912A more likely originates in a distant star forming galaxy at
z=0.937, and is most likely a long burst. This demonstrates the risk in
identifying bright, nearby galaxies as the hosts of given GRBs without further
supporting evidence. Further, it implies that, in the absence of secure
identifications, "host" type, or more broadly discriminators which rely on
galaxy redshifts, may not be good indicators of the true nature of any given
GRB.

We study the Stokes-Einstein (SE) and the Stokes-Einstein-Debye (SED)
relations using molecular dynamics simulations of the extended simple point
charge model of water. We find that both the SE and SED relations break down at
low temperature. To explore the relationship between these breakdowns and
dynamical heterogeneities (DH), we also calculate the SE and SED relations for
subsets of the 7% ``fastest'' and 7% ``slowest'' molecules. We find that the SE
and SED relations break down in both subsets, and that the breakdowns occur on
all scales of mobility. Thus these breakdowns appear to be generalized
phenomena, in contrast with the view where only the most mobile molecules are
the origin of the breakdown of the SE and SED relations, embedded in an
inactive background where these relations hold. At low temperature, the SE and
SED relations in both subsets of molecules are replaced with ``fractional'' SE
and SED relations, $D_t\sim(\tau/T)^{-\xi_t}$ and $D_r\sim(\tau/T)^{-\xi_r}$
where $\xi_t\approx0.84<1$ and $\xi_r\approx0.75<1$. We also find that there is
a decoupling between rotational and translational motion, and that this
decoupling occurs in both fastest and slowest subsets of molecules. We also
find that when the decoupling increases, upon cooling, the probability of a
molecule being classified as both translationally and rotationally fastest also
increases. To study the effect of time scale for SE and SED breakdown and
decoupling, we introduce a time-dependent version of the SE and SED relations,
and a time-dependent function that measures the extent of decoupling. Our
results suggest that both the decoupling and SE and SED breakdowns are
originated at the time scale corresponding to the end of the cage regime, when
diffusion starts. This is also the time scale when the DH are more relevant.

Intersection bodies represent a remarkable class of geometric objects
associated with sections of star bodies and invoking
  Radon transforms, generalized cosine transforms, and the relevant Fourier
analysis. The main focus of this article is interrelation between generalized
cosine transforms of different kinds in the context of their application to
investigation of a certain family of intersection bodies, which we call
$\lam$-intersection bodies. The latter include $k$-intersection bodies (in the
sense of A. Koldobsky) and unit balls of finite-dimensional subspaces of
$L_p$-spaces. In particular, we show that restrictions onto lower dimensional
subspaces of the spherical Radon transforms and the generalized cosine
transforms preserve their integral-geometric structure. We apply this result to
the study of sections of $\lam$-intersection bodies. New characterizations of
this class of bodies are obtained and examples are given. We also review some
known facts and give them new proofs.

In this work, we investigate the uniqueness of $\bf C^*$- and $\bf C_+$-actions on Gizatullin surfaces, which are a particular class of complex surfaces. The main question we address is whether two such actions are necessarily equivalent, meaning that one can be obtained from the other by a change of coordinates. 

We prove that these actions are, in fact, unique up to certain transformations, which we describe explicitly in the paper. Our approach is based on the use of algebraic and geometric techniques, such as the study of linear systems of equations and the analysis of rational curves on the surface. 

Our main result is of great interest in several areas of mathematics, including algebraic geometry and group theory, where it has applications in the study of symmetries of complex surfaces and their classification. Furthermore, our work provides a new insight into the geometry of Gizatullin surfaces, which are an important and much-studied class of algebraic varieties. 

Overall, our investigation sheds light on the structure of $\bf C^*$- and $\bf C_+$-actions on Gizatullin surfaces and opens up new avenues for further research in this exciting area of mathematics.

Is it possible to link a set of nodes without using preexisting positional
information or any kind of long-range attraction of the nodes? Can the process
of generating positional information, i.e. the detection of ``unknown'' nodes
and the estabishment of chemical gradients, \emph{and} the process of network
formation, i.e. the establishment of links between nodes, occur in parallel, on
a comparable time scale, as a process of co-evolution?
  The paper discusses a model where the generation of relevant information for
establishing the links between nodes results from the interaction of many
\emph{agents}, i.e. subunits of the system that are capable of performing some
activities. Their collective interaction is based on (indirect) communication,
which also includes memory effects and the dissemination of information in the
system. The relevant (``pragmatic'') information that leads to the
establishment of the links then emerges from an evolutionary interplay of
selection and reamplification.

In this work, we investigate the fundamental problem of quark confinement in Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD). Our goal is to explore the mass gap, a quantity that defines the difference in energy between the ground state and the first excited state of a physical system. This is a crucial concept in the context of quark confinement, as it provides a mechanism for generating the mass of confined quarks. 

We employ a theoretical framework known as lattice QCD to simulate the strong interactions between quarks and gluons. Our approach involves discretizing spacetime and simulating the interactions on a computer, enabling us to calculate the mass gap with high accuracy. Our results indicate that the mass gap exists and is significant, which provides strong support for the idea of quark confinement. 

Additionally, our work proposes a new mechanism to explain the emergence of the mass gap. We demonstrate that the strong interactions between quarks and gluons lead to the formation of classical fields, which interact with the quantum fields of the quarks and generate their mass. This represents a significant advancement in our understanding of quark confinement in QCD. 

In summary, our work presents a novel solution to the problem of quark confinement in QCD, based on the concept of the mass gap and a new mechanism for its generation. These results represent a significant contribution to the field of particle physics and have important implications for the understanding of fundamental particles and their interactions.

In this research, we investigate the star formation rates (SFRs) of z=1 early-type galaxies through the use of the Very Deep GOODS-MIPS Imaging. These galaxies are believed to have low SFRs, but their origin and properties remain unclear. Therefore, our focus is to understand the implications of their SFRs for their optical/near-infrared spectral energy distributions. 

To achieve this, we analyze the data on a sample of 76 galaxies with various photometric tools, including spectral energy distribution modeling and morphological analysis. Our results reveal that early-type galaxies at z=1 have significantly lower SFRs compared to late-type galaxies but similar to modern-day early-type galaxies. Through the analysis of their optical/near-infrared spectral energy distributions, these galaxies show that they possess old stellar populations with low metallicity, implying that they formed early in the universe's history. 

The implications of our findings challenge the current galaxy formation models. They suggest that a combination of early star formation with subsequent passive evolution offers an explanation for the low SFRs of early-type galaxies, which were prevalent at that time. Furthermore, our data provide a vital reference for future studies of galaxy formation and evolution. 

Overall, this research fills a significant gap in our understanding of early-type galaxies at a redshift of z=1 and their properties of star formation and evolution.

In this work, we investigate the generalized Dicke model using functional methods. The generalized Dicke model is a quantum many-body physical system that describes the interactions between an ensemble of two-level systems and a bosonic field. We employ functional methods to study the model's ground state and low-lying excitations. In our approach, we map the many-body problem onto a functional theory of a classical field. This allows us to use standard variational methods to obtain a tractable and efficient solution.

Our results reveal the existence of a critical coupling strength, above which a quantum phase transition occurs. We identify the critical point and the nature of the phase transition through the behavior of the ground state energy and the fidelity susceptibility. Our findings also demonstrate that the functional method is a viable tool for studying the strongly correlated exponentially large Hilbert space of the generalized Dicke model.

Our work provides insight into the complex behavior of the generalized Dicke model and sheds light on the rich interplay between quantum many-body systems and bosonic fields. Our findings may prove useful in the development of quantum computing and quantum information processing. Overall, our work aims to contribute to the continued advancement of functional methods in the study of quantum many-body systems.

We present optical photometry and spectroscopy of the afterglow and host
galaxy of gamma-ray burst 040924. This GRB had a rather short duration of T90
~2.4s, and a well sampled optical afterglow light curve. We aim to use this
dataset to find further evidence that this burst is consistent with a massive
star core-collapse progenitor. We combine the afterglow data reported here with
data taken from the literature and compare the host properties with survey
data. We find that the global behaviour of the optical afterglow is well fit by
a broken power-law, with a break at ~0.03 days. We determine the redshift z =
0.858 +/- 0.001 from the detected emission lines in our spectrum. Using the
spectrum and photometry we derive global properties of the host, showing it to
have similar properties to long GRB hosts. We detect the [Ne III] emission line
in the spectrum, and compare the fluxes of this line of a sample of 15 long GRB
host galaxies with survey data, showing the long GRB hosts to be comparable to
local metal-poor emission line galaxies in their [Ne III] emission. We fit the
supernova bump accompanying this burst, and find that it is similar to other
long GRB supernova bumps, but fainter. All properties of GRB 040924 are
consistent with an origin in the core-collapse of a massive star: the
supernova, the spectrum and SED of the host and the afterglow.

We present new multi-wavelength millimeter interferometric observations of
the Herbig Ae star HD 163296 obtained with the IRAM/PBI, SMA and VLA arrays
both in continuum and in the 12CO, 13CO and C18O emission lines. Gas and dust
properties have been obtained comparing the observations with self-consistent
disk models for the dust and CO emission. The circumstellar disk is resolved
both in the continuum and in CO. We find strong evidence that the circumstellar
material is in Keplerian rotation around a central star of 2.6 Msun. The disk
inclination with respect to the line of sight is 46+-4 deg with a position
angle of 128+-4 deg. The slope of the dust opacity measured between 0.87 and 7
mm (beta=1) confirms the presence of mm/cm-size grains in the disk midplane.
The dust continuum emission is asymmetric and confined inside a radius of 200
AU while the CO emission extends up to 540 AU. The comparison between dust and
CO temperature indicates that CO is present only in the disk interior. Finally,
we obtain an increasing depletion of CO isotopomers from 12CO to 13CO and C18O.
We argue that these results support the idea that the disk of HD 163296 is
strongly evolved. In particular, we suggest that there is a strong depletion of
dust relative to gas outside 200 AU; this may be due to the inward migration of
large bodies that form in the outer disk or to clearing of a large gap in the
dust distribution by a low mass companion.

MIMO technology is one of the most significant advances in the past decade to
increase channel capacity and has a great potential to improve network capacity
for mesh networks. In a MIMO-based mesh network, the links outgoing from each
node sharing the common communication spectrum can be modeled as a Gaussian
vector broadcast channel. Recently, researchers showed that ``dirty paper
coding'' (DPC) is the optimal transmission strategy for Gaussian vector
broadcast channels. So far, there has been little study on how this fundamental
result will impact the cross-layer design for MIMO-based mesh networks. To fill
this gap, we consider the problem of jointly optimizing DPC power allocation in
the link layer at each node and multihop/multipath routing in a MIMO-based mesh
networks. It turns out that this optimization problem is a very challenging
non-convex problem. To address this difficulty, we transform the original
problem to an equivalent problem by exploiting the channel duality. For the
transformed problem, we develop an efficient solution procedure that integrates
Lagrangian dual decomposition method, conjugate gradient projection method
based on matrix differential calculus, cutting-plane method, and subgradient
method. In our numerical example, it is shown that we can achieve a network
performance gain of 34.4% by using DPC.

The standard oracle operator corresponding to a function f is a unitary
operator that computes this function coherently, i.e. it maintains
superpositions. This operator acts on a bipartite system, where the subsystems
are the input and output registers. In distributed quantum computation, these
subsystems may be spatially separated, in which case we will be interested in
its classical and entangling capacities. For an arbitrary function f, we show
that the unidirectional classical and entangling capacities of this operator
are log_{2}(n_{f}) bits/ebits, where n_{f} is the number of different values
this function can take. An optimal procedure for bidirectional classical
communication with a standard oracle operator corresponding to a permutation on
Z_{M} is given. The bidirectional classical capacity of such an operator is
found to be 2log_{2}(M) bits. The proofs of these capacities are facilitated by
an optimal distributed protocol for the implementation of an arbitrary standard
oracle operator.

"Einstein-aether" theory is a generally covariant theory of gravity
containing a dynamical preferred frame. This article continues an examination
of effects on the motion of binary pulsar systems in this theory, by
incorporating effects due to strong fields in the vicinity of neutron star
pulsars. These effects are included through an effective approach, by treating
the compact bodies as point particles with nonstandard, velocity dependent
interactions parametrized by dimensionless "sensitivities". Effective
post-Newtonian equations of motion for the bodies and the radiation damping
rate are determined. More work is needed to calculate values of the
sensitivities for a given fluid source, so precise constraints on the theory's
coupling constants cannot yet be stated. It is shown, however, that strong
field effects will be negligible given current observational uncertainties if
the dimensionless couplings are less than roughly 0.01 and two conditions that
match the PPN parameters to those of pure general relativity are imposed. In
this case, weak field results suffice and imply one further condition on the
couplings. Thus, there exists a one-parameter family of Einstein-aether
theories with "small-enough" couplings that passes all current observational
tests. No conclusion can yet be reached for large couplings.

A generalized twistor transform for spinning particles in 3+1 dimensions is
constructed that beautifully unifies many types of spinning systems by mapping
them to the same twistor, thus predicting an infinite set of duality relations
among spinning systems with different Hamiltonians. Usual 1T-physics is not
equipped to explain the duality relationships and unification between these
systems. We use 2T-physics in 4+2 dimensions to uncover new properties of
twistors, and expect that our approach will prove to be useful for practical
applications as well as for a deeper understanding of fundamental physics.
Unexpected structures for a new description of spinning particles emerge. A
unifying symmetry SU(2,3) that includes conformal symmetry SU(2,2)=SO(4,2) in
the massless case, turns out to be a fundamental property underlying the
dualities of a large set of spinning systems, including those that occur in
high spin theories. This may lead to new forms of string theory backgrounds as
well as to new methods for studying various corners of M theory. In this paper
we present the main concepts, and in a companion paper we give other details.

The ACS Survey of Galactic Globular Clusters, an HST Treasury Project, will
deliver high quality, homogeneous photometry of 65 globular clusters. This
paper introduces a new collection of stellar evolution tracks and isochrones
suitable for analyzing the ACS Survey data. Stellar evolution models were
computed at [Fe/H]= -2.5, -2.0, -1.5, -1.0, -0.5, and 0; [alpha/Fe]= -0.2, 0,
0.2, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8; and three initial He abundances for masses from 0.1 to
1.8 Msun and ages from 2 to 15 Gyr. Each isochrone spans a wide range in
luminosity from Mv~14 up to the tip of the red giant branch. These are
complemented by a set of He-burning tracks that extend from the zero age
horizontal branch to the onset of thermal pulsations on the asymptotic giant
branch. In addition, a set of computer programs are provided that make it
possible to interpolate the isochrones in [Fe/H], generate luminosity functions
from the isochrones, and create synthetic horizontal branch models. The tracks
and isochrones have been converted to the observational plane with two
different color-Teff transformations, one synthetic and one semi-empirical, in
ground-based B, V, and I, and F606W and F814W for both ACS-WFC and WFPC2
systems. All models and programs presented in this paper are available from
http://stellar.dartmouth.edu/~models/

In this work, we investigate the dynamical heterogeneities of water molecules and their role in breaking the Stokes-Einstein and Stokes-Einstein-Debye (SED) relations through molecular dynamics simulations. The SED relations connect the diffusion coefficient of a particle to its friction and the temperature of the solvent, and have been widely used to describe the transport properties of simple liquids such as water. However, recent experiments and simulations have shown that the SED relations break down in complex liquids such as supercooled water, and their underlying assumptions do not hold.

Our results show that the breakdown of the SED relations in water can be attributed to the dynamical heterogeneities of the liquid, where some regions of the liquid are more mobile than others. We find that the diffusion of water molecules in these heterogeneous regions is faster than in the bulk, leading to a non-linear relationship between the diffusion coefficient, viscosity, and temperature. Moreover, we observe a decoupling between the rotational and translational motion of water molecules, suggesting that the rotational and translation relaxation times depend on different factors.

Our study is motivated by the need for a better understanding of the transport properties of complex liquids such as water, and our work sheds light on the role of dynamical heterogeneities in breaking the SED relations. We adopt a molecular dynamics simulation approach with state-of-the-art force fields and statistical analysis to investigate the dynamical properties of water on a microscopic level.

For a (co)monad T_l on a category M, an object X in M, and a functor \Pi: M
\to C, there is a (co)simplex Z^*:=\Pi T_l^{* +1} X in C. Our aim is to find
criteria for para-(co)cyclicity of Z^*. Construction is built on a distributive
law of T_l with a second (co)monad T_r on M, a natural transformation i:\Pi T_l
\to \Pi T_r, and a morphism w: T_r X \to T_l X in M. The relations i and w need
to satisfy are categorical versions of Kaygun's axioms of a transposition map.
Motivation comes from the observation that a (co)ring T over an algebra R
determines a distributive law of two (co)monads T_l=T \otimes_R (-) and T_r =
(-)\otimes_R T on the category of R-bimodules. The functor \Pi can be chosen
such that Z^n= T\hat{\otimes}_R... \hat{\otimes}_R T \hat{\otimes}_R X is the
cyclic R-module tensor product. A natural transformation i:T \hat{\otimes}_R
(-) \to (-) \hat{\otimes}_R T is given by the flip map and a morphism w: X
\otimes_R T \to T\otimes_R X is constructed whenever T is a (co)module algebra
or coring of an R-bialgebroid. Stable anti Yetter-Drinfel'd modules over
certain bialgebroids, so called x_R-Hopf algebras, are introduced. In the
particular example when T is a module coring of a x_R-Hopf algebra B and X is a
stable anti Yetter-Drinfel'd B-module, the para-cyclic object Z_* is shown to
project to a cyclic structure on T^{\otimes_R *+1} \otimes_B X. For a B-Galois
extension S \to T, a stable anti Yetter-Drinfel'd B-module T_S is constructed,
such that the cyclic objects B^{\otimes_R *+1} \otimes_B T_S and T^
{\hat{\otimes}_S *+1} are isomorphic. As an application, we compute Hochschild
and cyclic homology of a groupoid with coefficients, by tracing it back to the
group case. In particular, we obtain explicit expressions for ordinary
Hochschild and cyclic homology of a groupoid.

In this work, we present a theoretical study of vortices in Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs). Our aim is to understand the formation, dynamics, and properties of vortex structures in these systems, which are of interest both from a fundamental perspective and for potential applications in quantum information processing and quantum metrology. We begin by reviewing the basic physics of BECs, including their mathematical description in terms of the Gross-Pitaevskii equation. We then discuss the formation of vortices in BECs and the properties of the resulting vortex structures, including their quantized core with a well-defined angular momentum. We analyze the dynamics of vortices, including their motion and interactions, both in the presence of external potentials and in homogeneous systems. Our results show that the dynamics of vortices are highly nonlinear and can lead to interesting and complex behavior, such as vortex reconnections and the formation of vortex lattices. Finally, we discuss the potential applications of vortex structures in BECs, including the generation of entangled states of atoms and the production of high-precision rotation sensors. Overall, our work provides a comprehensive theoretical treatment of vortices in BECs and sheds light on the fundamental physics of these fascinating quantum systems.

The nature of the coupling leading to superconductivity in layered materials
such as high-Tc superconductors and graphite intercalation compounds (GICs) is
still unresolved. In both systems, interactions of electrons with either
phonons or other electrons or both have been proposed to explain
superconductivity. In the high-Tc cuprates, the presence of a Van Hove
singularity (VHS) in the density of states near the Fermi level was long ago
proposed to enhance the many-body couplings and therefore may play a role in
superconductivity. Such a singularity can cause an anisotropic variation in the
coupling strength, which may partially explain the so-called nodal-antinodal
dichotomy in the cuprates. Here we show that the topology of the graphene band
structure at dopings comparable to the GICs is quite similar to that of the
cuprates and that the quasiparticle dynamics in graphene have a similar
dichotomy. Namely, the electron-phonon coupling is highly anisotropic,
diverging near a saddle point in the graphene electronic band structure. These
results support the important role of the VHS in layered materials and the
possible optimization of Tc by tuning the VHS with respect to the Fermi level.

We study the potential of the CNGS beam in constraining the parameter space
of a model with one sterile neutrino separated from three active ones by an
$\mathcal{O}(\eVq)$ mass-squared difference, $\Dmq_\Sbl$. We perform our
analysis using the OPERA detector as a reference (our analysis can be upgraded
including a detailed simulation of the ICARUS detector). We point out that the
channel with the largest potential to constrain the sterile neutrino parameter
space at the CNGS beam is $\nu_\mu \to \nu_\tau$. The reason for that is
twofold: first, the active-sterile mixing angle that governs this oscillation
is the less constrained by present experiments; second, this is the signal for
which both OPERA and ICARUS have been designed, and thus benefits from an
extremely low background. In our analysis we also took into account $\nu_\mu
\to \nu_e$ oscillations. We find that the CNGS potential to look for sterile
neutrinos is limited with nominal intensity of the beam, but it is
significantly enhanced with a factor 2 to 10 increase in the neutrino flux.
Data from both channels allow us, in this case, to constrain further the
four-neutrino model parameter space. Our results hold for any value of
$\Dmq_\Sbl \gtrsim 0.1 \eVq$, \textit{i.e.} when oscillations driven by this
mass-squared difference are averaged. We have also checked that the bound on
$\theta_{13}$ that can be put at the CNGS is not affected by the possible
existence of sterile neutrinos.

In this work, we present an analysis of the critical edge behavior in unitary random matrix ensembles and its connection with the thirty fourth Painleve transcendent. We show that the joint probability density function of the eigenvalues in these ensembles can be expressed as a Fredholm determinant involving a kernel function that is related to the Deift-Zhou steepest descent method for Riemann-Hilbert problems. From this, we derive a recurrence relation for the coefficients of the kernel function, which leads to an explicit expression for the probability density function.

We also demonstrate that the probability density function exhibits a critical edge behavior near the spectral edge, characterized by a logarithmic singularity. To understand this behavior, we employ the theory of Painleve transcendents and show that the logarithmic singularity is related to the behavior of the Painleve transcendent of type IV. We obtain a system of differential equations satisfied by the Painleve transcendent and derive an asymptotic expansion in the critical region.

Our analysis provides a deeper understanding of the critical edge behavior in random matrix ensembles and illuminates its connection with the theory of Painleve transcendents. The methodology presented here can be extended to other random matrix ensembles and offers a promising approach to study critical edge behavior in these systems.

We present optical spectroscopy of EX Hya during its 1991 outburst. This
outburst is characterised by strong irradiation of the front face of the
secondary star by the white dwarf, an overflowing stream which is seen strongly
in HeII and by a dip in the light curves, which extends from 0.1-0.6 in the
binary and spin phases. Strong irradiation of the accretion curtain and that of
the inner regions of the disc led to strong emission of HeII and to the
suppression of the Hg and Hb emission.
  Disc overflow was observed in quiescence in earlier studies, where the
overflow stream material was modulated at high velocities close to 1000 km/s.
In outburst, the overflowing material is modulated at even higher velocities
(~1500 km/s). These are streaming velocities down the field lines close to the
white dwarf. Evidence for material collecting near the outer edge of the disc
and corotating with the accretion curtain was observed. In decline, this
material and the accretion curtain obscured almost all the emission near binary
phase 0.4, causing a dip. The dip minimum nearly corresponds with spin pulse
minimum. This has provided additional evidence for an extended accretion
curtain, and for the corotation of material with the accretion curtain at the
outer edge of the disc. From these observations we suggest that a mechanism
similar to that of Spruit & Taam, where outbursts result due to the storage and
release of matter outside the magnetosphere, triggers the outbursts of EX Hya.
This is followed by the irradiation of the secondary star due to accretion
induced radiation.

We present a measurement of the top-quark mass in $p\bar p$ collisions at
\hbox{$\sqrt{s}=1.96$} TeV which uses events with an inclusive signature of
missing transverse energy and jets. % The event selection is sensitive to
$t\bar t\to W^+bW^-\bar b\to l\nu b q q^\prime \bar b$ independent of the
lepton flavor, and results in a large acceptance for $W\to\tau\nu$ decays. %
All-hadronic $t\bar t$ decays and events with identified electrons or muons are
vetoed to provide a statistically independent sample with respect to all
previous measurements. % The top-quark mass is inferred from the distribution
of the scalar sum of all jet transverse energies and the missing transverse
energy. Using 311 pb$^{-1}$ of integrated luminosity recorded by the Collider
Detector at Fermilab, we measure a top-quark mass $m_t = 172.3
^{+10.8}_{-9.6}$(stat.)$\pm 10.8$(syst.) GeV/$c^2$. While the uncertainty on
$m_t$ is larger than that of other measurements, the result is statistically
uncorrelated with those of other methods, and thus can help to reduce the
overall $m_t$ uncertainty when combined with other existing measurements.

In this work, we present a computational approach to exact Casimir force calculations by exploiting the properties of virtual photons in imaginary time. We aim to solve the problem of accurately determining the Casimir force between two parallel plates, which is known to have critical implications in micro-electromechanical systems and nanotechnologies. Our motivation for this research is rooted in the inadequacy of traditional methods, which fail to provide accurate results for complex geometries. Additionally, we aim to investigate the effect of non-local properties of imaginary photons on the Casimir force and to develop methods to include material properties. 

The results of our simulations show that the use of virtual photons in imaginary time can significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of traditional Casimir force calculations. Our approach demonstrates excellent agreement with analytical results for simple geometries and provides new insights for complex cases, including non-local and anisotropic materials. Furthermore, we present a method for estimating the effective Casimir force using an analytical model, which can provide a useful tool for design and verification in nanotechnology. Our work contributes to a deeper understanding of the Casimir effect and provides a powerful computational tool for further experimental and theoretical research.

In this paper, we present an investigation of the signatures of incoherence in a quantum information processor. Our aim is to understand the effects of decoherence in quantum information processing and to determine how best to mitigate it. We study a model composed of two qubits in a quantum dynamical system and examine its coherence properties using a set of coherence measures.

Our investigation reveals that the coherence properties of the quantum system are strongly influenced by the initial state and the coupling strength between the two qubits. Specifically, we observe a rapid decay of coherence at time scales proportional to the degree of coupling between the qubits, which indicates the presence of incoherent effects in the system. We also show that the coherence of the system can be enhanced by introducing an external driving field that drives the system toward a particular target state.

Our study has significant implications for the development of practical quantum information processing devices. By understanding the signatures of incoherence, we can design better quantum systems with improved coherence properties. Moreover, our results suggest that the application of external fields can be an effective approach to mitigate the effects of incoherence in quantum systems. Overall, our research advances the understanding of coherence in quantum systems and offers new avenues for the development of practical quantum information processing devices.

In this work, we explore the implications of natural priors in the context of the Constrained Minimal Supersymmetric Standard Model (CMSSM), also known as mSUGRA, by comparing theoretical expectations to experimental data from the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). The identification and use of natural priors as inputs to Bayesian analysis allows for a more robust prediction of the CMSSM's parameter space. 

Our results show that the CMSSM with natural priors remains viable in light of LHC data, with preference for low values of the supersymmetric spectrum. In particular, for the first time, we make a ten-year forecast for the sensitivity of the LHC to the CMSSM with natural priors and find a high degree of complementarity with direct dark matter detection experiments. By considering prior distributions that are motivated by naturalness arguments, we provide a new perspective on the relationship between the theory and experiment. 

The work attempts to solve the challenge of reconciling the standard model of particle physics with the observed phenomena at the LHC, and the question of whether the CMSSM, as a viable candidate for a supersymmetric extension of the standard model, can withstand experimental scrutiny. Our work advances the field of particle physics by demonstrating the importance of considering naturalness arguments in Bayesian analyses of supersymmetric models and by providing valuable insight into the future of experimental searches for supersymmetric particles at the LHC.

In this study, we present the results of an extensive survey of massive stars in the Milky Way and the Magellanic Clouds using the VLT-FLAMES instrument. Our focus was on investigating the evolution of surface N abundances and effective temperature scales in these galaxies. Massive stars play a crucial role in the evolution of galaxies and have a significant impact on the cosmic enrichment of chemical elements, such as nitrogen. The complex interplay of physical processes that govern the evolution of massive stars and the chemical enrichment of their surroundings make them vital objects of study. In this regard, the VLT-FLAMES survey is particularly relevant, as it provides a large sample of massive stars with high-quality data, allowing us to make statistically significant conclusions.

Our analysis reveals a robust correlation between surface N abundance and effective temperature, indicating that N enrichment is tied to the late stages of stellar evolution. We also found clear differences in the N abundance and effective temperature distributions between the Milky Way and the Magellanic Clouds, highlighting the importance of environment in shaping the properties of massive stars. These results provide essential insights into the mechanisms of chemical enrichment and the evolution of massive stars, shedding light on the broader aspects of galactic evolution.

This paper is an investigation of the stability of some ideal stars. It is
in- tended as a study in General Relativity, with emphasis on the coupling to
matter, eventually aimed at a better understanding of very strong gravitational
fields and Black Holes. The work is based on an action principle for the
complete system of metric and matter fields. We propose a complete revision of
the treatment of boundary conditions. An ideal star in our terminology has
spherical symmetry and an isentropic equation of state. In our first work on
this subject it was assumed that the density vanishes beyond a finite distance
from the origin. But it is difficult to decide what the proper boundary
conditions should be and we are consequently skeptical of the concept of a
fixed boundary. In this paper we investigate the double polytrope,
characterized by a polytropic index n less than 5 in the bulk of the star and a
value larger than 5 in an outer atmosphere that extends to infinity. It has no
fixed boundary but a region of critical density where the polytropic index
changes from a value that is appropriate for the bulk of the star to a value
that provides a crude model for the atmosphere. The existence of a relation
between mass and radius is confirmed, as well as an upper limit on the mass.
The principal conclusion is that all the static configurations are stable.

In this study, we investigate the role of strong field effects on binary systems in the framework of Einstein-aether theory. Strong field effects are known to be significant in the vicinity of compact objects such as neutron stars and black holes, and their understanding has been a subject of intense research in the field of gravitational physics. The Einstein-aether theory provides a covariant and robust framework to study the dynamics of strong gravitational fields.

The primary objective of this work is to explore the implications of strong field effects on binary systems, which are fundamental building blocks of the observable universe. We consider a binary system consisting of two neutron stars, and we calculate its phase evolution under the influence of strong field effects. We find that strong field effects have a significant impact on the phase evolution of the binary system. The phase evolution can be used to infer important astrophysical properties of the system, such as the masses and spins of the neutron stars.

Our approach is based on the post-Newtonian approximation and perturbative methods. We use the Hamiltonian formalism to derive the equations of motion and the corresponding phase evolution. The validity of our results is confirmed by comparing our predictions with observations of binary neutron star systems. Our study provides new insights into the dynamics of strong field effects in binary systems and highlights the importance of considering these effects in the analysis of binary neutron star data.

A multiple antenna downlink channel where limited channel feedback is
available to the transmitter is considered. In a vector downlink channel
(single antenna at each receiver), the transmit antenna array can be used to
transmit separate data streams to multiple receivers only if the transmitter
has very accurate channel knowledge, i.e., if there is high-rate channel
feedback from each receiver. In this work it is shown that channel feedback
requirements can be significantly reduced if each receiver has a small number
of antennas and appropriately combines its antenna outputs. A combining method
that minimizes channel quantization error at each receiver, and thereby
minimizes multi-user interference, is proposed and analyzed. This technique is
shown to outperform traditional techniques such as maximum-ratio combining
because minimization of interference power is more critical than maximization
of signal power in the multiple antenna downlink. Analysis is provided to
quantify the feedback savings, and the technique is seen to work well with user
selection and is also robust to receiver estimation error.

In this paper, we present a detailed analysis of GRB 060912A, a gamma ray burst (GRB) that has been classified as a long GRB despite its relatively short duration. We investigate whether this classification is accurate and what this might tell us about the long-short GRB divide, a fundamental and still unresolved issue in the study of GRBs.

Using a combination of data from both the Swift and Fermi satellites, we find that GRB 060912A exhibits several characteristics typically associated with short GRBs, such as a lack of extended emission and a relatively hard spectrum. However, we also find evidence that it may be associated with a supernova, a hallmark of long GRBs. Based on these results, we suggest that GRB 060912A may represent a new type of GRB that blurs the lines between long and short.

Our work contributes to the ongoing debate over the nature of the long-short GRB divide and highlights the need for more nuanced classifications of these events. We employ a rigorous and thorough methodology, including detailed analysis of both temporal and spectral data, to arrive at our conclusions. Overall, we hope that this case study will serve as a valuable contribution to the field and support further investigation into the diverse and complex phenomena associated with GRBs.

In this work, we present the results of our search for transiting exoplanets around the M dwarf debris disk host, AU Microscopii. We observed the star for 14 days with the MUSCAT instrument, installed on the Himalayan Chandra Telescope. Our main aim was to detect small exoplanets transiting the star, aiming at better understanding the population of planets around this type of star. 

We used differential photometry to detect the transit signals in the light curve of AU Mic. After analyzing the folded transit curves, we found no significant transit signals in the data. From the obtained results, we were able to place upper mass limits of about 1.6, 2.9, and 4.6 Earth masses (at 68% confidence level) on transiting planets with orbital periods of 1, 5, and 10 days, respectively. 

Our work aims to contribute to the ongoing efforts for planet hunting around M dwarf stars. The detection of transiting exoplanets around an M dwarf debris disk host, like AU Microscopii, would provide valuable information on the properties and formation of exoplanets. Our negative results will help to set constraints on the abundance and properties of planets that can be hosted by AU Mic, such as the maximum radius and mass of potentially transiting planets.

The ARCH process (R. F. Engle, 1982) constitutes a paradigmatic generator of
stochastic time series with time-dependent variance like it appears on a wide
broad of systems besides economics in which ARCH was born. Although the ARCH
process captures the so-called "volatility clustering" and the asymptotic
power-law probability density distribution of the random variable, it is not
capable to reproduce further statistical properties of many of these time
series such as: the strong persistence of the instantaneous variance
characterised by large values of the Hurst exponent (H > 0.8), and asymptotic
power-law decay of the absolute values self-correlation function. By means of
considering an effective return obtained from a correlation of past returns
that has a q-exponential form we are able to fix the limitations of the
original model. Moreover, this improvement can be obtained through the correct
choice of a sole additional parameter, $q_{m}$. The assessment of its validity
and usefulness is made by mimicking daily fluctuations of SP500 financial
index.

We study the dynamics and resulting score distribution of three-agent games
where after each competition a single agent wins and scores a point. A single
competition is described by a triplet of numbers $p$, $t$ and $q$ denoting the
probabilities that the team with the highest, middle or lowest accumulated
score wins. We study the full family of solutions in the regime, where the
number of agents and competitions is large, which can be regarded as a
hydrodynamic limit. Depending on the parameter values $(p,q,t)$, we find six
qualitatively different asymptotic score distributions and we also provide a
qualitative understanding of these results. We checked our analytical results
against numerical simulations of the microscopic model and find these to be in
excellent agreement. The three agent game can be regarded as a social model
where a player can be favored or disfavored for advancement, based on his/her
accumulated score. It is also possible to decide the outcome of a three agent
game through a mini tournament of two-a gent competitions among the
participating players and it turns out that the resulting possible score
distributions are a subset of those obtained for the general three agent-games.
We discuss how one can add a steady and democratic decline rate to the model
and present a simple geometric construction that allows one to write down the
corresponding score evolution equations for $n$-agent games.

Motivated by the Dobrushin uniqueness theorem in statistical mechanics, we
consider the following situation: Let \alpha be a nonnegative matrix over a
finite or countably infinite index set X, and define the "cleaning operators"
\beta_h = I_{1-h} + I_h \alpha for h: X \to [0,1] (here I_f denotes the
diagonal matrix with entries f). We ask: For which "cleaning sequences" h_1,
