h_2, ... do we have c \beta_{h_1} ... \beta_{h_n} \to 0 for a suitable class of
"dirt vectors" c? We show, under a modest condition on \alpha, that this occurs
whenever \sum_i h_i = \infty everywhere on X. More generally, we analyze the
cleaning of subsets \Lambda \subseteq X and the final distribution of dirt on
the complement of \Lambda. We show that when supp(h_i) \subseteq \Lambda with
\sum_i h_i = \infty everywhere on \Lambda, the operators \beta_{h_1} ...
\beta_{h_n} converge as n \to \infty to the "balayage operator" \Pi_\Lambda =
\sum_{k=0}^\infty (I_\Lambda \alpha)^k I_{\Lambda^c). These results are
obtained in two ways: by a fairly simple matrix formalism, and by a more
powerful tree formalism that corresponds to working with formal power series in
which the matrix elements of \alpha are treated as noncommuting indeterminates.

In this work, we present our investigations into the ground-state long-range order in quasi-one-dimensional Heisenberg quantum antiferromagnets. We have utilized high-order coupled-cluster calculations to understand the properties of these systems. Our motivation for this research is to better understand the properties of quasi-one-dimensional systems and to investigate their potential applications in quantum computing. 

Our methodology involves using the high-order coupled-cluster calculations technique to study the properties of quantum antiferromagnets. To this end, we have performed calculations on systems with up to 100 sites and studied the scaling of the ground-state energy gap and order parameters with the system size. We have also studied the stability of different types of long-range order in these systems such as spiral and dimer orders. 

Our results show that the ground-state energy gap in these systems scales exponentially with the system size, indicating the presence of long-range order in the system. We have also found that spiral and dimer orders are less stable than other types of long-range order in these systems. These findings can help to better understand the properties of quasi-one-dimensional systems and to inform the development of new quantum materials.

Simultaneous $\chi^{2}$ analyses previously made for elastic scattering and
fusion cross section data for the $^{6}$Li+$^{208}$Pb system is extended to the
$^{7}$Li+$^{208}$Pb system at near-Coulomb-barrier energies based on the
extended optical model approach, in which the polarization potential is
decomposed into direct reaction (DR) and fusion parts. Use is made of the
double folding potential as a bare potential. It is found that the experimental
elastic scattering and fusion data are well reproduced without introducing any
normalization factor for the double folding potential and that both the DR and
fusion parts of the polarization potential determined from the $\chi^{2}$
analyses satisfy separately the dispersion relation. Further, we find that the
real part of the fusion portion of the polarization potential is attractive
while that of the DR part is repulsive except at energies far below the Coulomb
barrier energy. A comparison is made of the present results with those obtained
from the Continuum Discretized Coupled Channel (CDCC) calculations and a
previous study based on the conventional optical model with a double folding
potential. We also compare the present results for the $^7$Li+$^{208}$Pb system
with the analysis previously made for the $^{6}$Li+$^{208}$Pb system.

We continue our study of chaotic mixing and transport of passive particles in
a simple model of a meandering jet flow [Prants, et al, Chaos {\bf 16}, 033117
(2006)]. In the present paper we study and explain phenomenologically a
connection between dynamical, topological, and statistical properties of
chaotic mixing and transport in the model flow in terms of dynamical traps,
singular zones in the phase space where particles may spend arbitrary long but
finite time [Zaslavsky, Phys. D {\bf 168--169}, 292 (2002)]. The transport of
passive particles is described in terms of lengths and durations of zonal
flights which are events between two successive changes of sign of zonal
velocity. Some peculiarities of the respective probability density functions
for short flights are proven to be caused by the so-called rotational-islands
traps connected with the boundaries of resonant islands (including those of the
vortex cores) filled with the particles moving in the same frame. Whereas, the
statistics of long flights can be explained by the influence of the so-called
ballistic-islands traps filled with the particles moving from a frame to frame.

In this study, we propose an adaptive strategy for the classification of G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs), which represent a vital class of transmembrane proteins that play an essential role in diverse physiological processes. Upon activation by an extracellular ligand, GPCRs activate intracellular signaling pathways mediated by G-proteins, which in turn, regulate various cellular functions. Due to their significant role in cellular signaling, GPCRs present potential targets for drug development. However, the vast number of GPCRs, coupled with their high structural diversity, pose a significant challenge in predicting their biological functions and designing drugs. 

Our work attempts to solve the problem of accurately classifying GPCRs based on their primary amino acid sequences. We used a combination of feature extraction techniques and machine learning algorithms to create an adaptive classifier capable of accommodating newly discovered GPCRs and ever-increasing sequence data. Our results show that our adaptive classifier outperformed existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity while requiring less computational resources. Overall, our adaptive strategy represents a valuable framework for the classification of GPCRs, which may lead to a better understanding of their biological functions and facilitate drug discovery efforts.

In this work, we present a reexamination of spin decoherence in semiconductor quantum dots from an equation-of-motion approach. Our aim is to further understand the fundamental physical processes causing spin decoherence in these systems in order to develop more efficient methods for controlling and manipulating quantum states in quantum dot-based devices. 

Our motivation for this research stems from the increasing interest in using quantum dot systems for quantum information processing, such as in quantum computation and communication. Spin-based qubits have shown particular promise in these applications due to their long coherence times and scalability. However, the coherence times of these qubits are limited by spin decoherence processes, which are not yet fully understood. 

Our key idea is to use an equation-of-motion approach to examine the dynamics of the system in more detail. This approach allows us to obtain a more complete picture of the various processes involved in spin decoherence, including the coupling between the spins and the environment, as well as the effects of spin-spin interactions. We then use this information to develop a theoretical model for the spin decoherence dynamics, which we validate against experimental data. 

Overall, our work provides a more comprehensive understanding of spin decoherence in semiconductor quantum dots and lays the groundwork for further advances in quantum dot-based quantum information processing.

In this work, we investigate the lower order terms in the 1-level density for families of holomorphic cuspidal newforms. This area of study is significant in understanding the distribution of zeros of L-functions, which is a fundamental problem in number theory. Our goal is to derive novel asymptotic formulas for the lower order terms of the 1-level density in terms of moments of central values of twisted L-functions associated with the family of newforms.

Our approach involves combining techniques from several different areas of number theory. We utilize the spectral theory of automorphic forms, which allows us to analyze the distribution of zeros of L-functions. We also use tools from the theory of random matrices to describe the distribution of eigenvalues of Hecke operators on the space of cusp forms. 

Our main result is an explicit formula for the lower order terms in the 1-level density in terms of the fourth moment of central values of twisted L-functions. We provide several examples to illustrate our formula in action, and we show that our formula is consistent with numerical experiments.

Overall, our work contributes to the understanding of the distribution of zeros of L-functions, and sheds light on the complex interplay between automorphic forms and random matrix theory.

Problems of stabilizing moduli of the type--IIB string theory on toroidal
orientifolds $\T^6/\Z_2$, in presence of worldvolume fluxes on various
D-branes, are considered. For $Z_2$ actions, introducing either O9 or O3
planes, we rule out the possibility of moduli stabilization in a wide class of
models with $\mathcal{N}=1$ supersymmetry, characterized by the type of fluxes
turned on along D-brane worldvolume. Our results, in particular, imply that
Abelian worldvolume fluxes can not by themselves stabilize closed string
moduli, in a consistent supersymmtric model, for above orientifold
compactifications. We also discuss other $Z_2$ orientifolds of $T^6$ and show
that certain other brane wrappings are also ruled out by similar consistency
requirements. In specific setups we consider examples with D9-branes wrapping
on a complex three-torus with its world-volume fluxes taken to be
semi-homogeneous bundles and D7-branes wrapping holomorphic four-cycles of the
complex three-torus carrying world-volume fluxes.

In this study, we present an investigation of the energy spectrum of gamma-ray sources detected by EGRET using data from an extensive air shower experiment. The motivation for this research lies in the need to understand the nature of the gamma-ray sources and the processes that produce them. We used data from the Tibet Air Shower array, which consists of 789 plastic scintillation detectors, to analyze the energy spectrum of 28 EGRET sources in the energy range of 100 MeV to 100 GeV.

Our methodology involved a detailed analysis of the data using Monte Carlo simulations and an unfolding technique. We found that the energy spectra of the EGRET sources were consistent with a power-law distribution, with the index ranging from 1.8 to 3.5. We also observed a correlation between the spectral index and the flux of the sources, indicating that brighter sources have harder spectra.

Overall, our results provide valuable insight into the energy spectra of gamma-ray sources at high energies. These findings have important implications for understanding the physical processes that produce gamma-rays and could inform future studies of gamma-ray sources.

We examine the question of whether the formal expressions of equilibrium
statistical mechanics can be applied to time independent non-dissipative
systems that are not in true thermodynamic equilibrium and are nonergodic. By
assuming the phase space may be divided into time independent, locally ergodic
domains, we argue that within such domains the relative probabilities of
microstates are given by the standard Boltzmann weights. In contrast to
previous energy landscape treatments, that have been developed specifically for
the glass transition, we do not impose an a priori knowledge of the
inter-domain population distribution. Assuming that these domains are robust
with respect to small changes in thermodynamic state variables we derive a
variety of fluctuation formulae for these systems. We verify our theoretical
results using molecular dynamics simulations on a model glass forming system.
Non-equilibrium Transient Fluctuation Relations are derived for the
fluctuations resulting from a sudden finite change to the system's temperature
or pressure and these are shown to be consistent with the simulation results.
The necessary and sufficient conditions for these relations to be valid are
that the domains are internally populated by Boltzmann statistics and that the
domains are robust. The Transient Fluctuation Relations thus provide an
independent quantitative justification for the assumptions used in our
statistical mechanical treatment of these systems.

In this study, we aimed to investigate the thermal inertia of near-Earth asteroids and its implication for the magnitude of the Yarkovsky effect. The Yarkovsky effect is a force that influences the orbit of asteroids by causing a drift in their trajectory due to the uneven heating and cooling of their surfaces. The thermal inertia, or the ability of an object to retain and release heat, is a crucial factor in determining the strength of the Yarkovsky effect. 

We utilized thermal emission measurements from the NEOWISE space telescope to calculate the thermal inertia values of 25 near-Earth asteroids. We then modeled the asteroid orbits and compared the observed deviations in their orbits to the predicted values based on the Yarkovsky effect. 

Our analysis revealed that the thermal inertia values of near-Earth asteroids vary widely, with some asteroids exhibiting significantly higher values than expected. This suggests that the Yarkovsky effect may be stronger in these asteroids than previously thought. We also found that the strength of the Yarkovsky effect is highly dependent on the asteroid size and surface properties. 

These findings will help us better understand the behavior of near-Earth asteroids and their potential impact on our planet. They also highlight the importance of accurately measuring the thermal inertia of asteroids in order to improve predictions of their orbits and potential hazards.

We present new results on dynamical instabilities in rapidly rotating
neutron-stars. In particular, using numerical simulations in full General
Relativity, we analyse the effects that the stellar compactness has on the
threshold for the onset of the dynamical bar-mode instability, as well as on
the appearance of other dynamical instabilities. By using an extrapolation
technique developed and tested in our previous study [1], we explicitly
determine the threshold for a wide range of compactnesses using four sequences
of models of constant baryonic mass comprising a total of 59 stellar models.
Our calculation of the threshold is in good agreement with the Newtonian
prediction and improves the previous post-Newtonian estimates. In addition, we
find that for stars with sufficiently large mass and compactness, the m=3
deformation is the fastest growing one. For all of the models considered, the
non-axisymmetric instability is suppressed on a dynamical timescale with an m=1
deformation dominating the final stages of the instability. These results,
together with those presented in [1], suggest that an m=1 deformation
represents a general and late-time feature of non-axisymmetric dynamical
instabilities both in full General Relativity and in Newtonian gravity.

In this study, we investigate the clustering properties of restframe UV selected galaxies and their evolution with cosmic time. We utilize the multi-wavelength data from the Galaxy Evolution Explorer (GALEX) and Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope Legacy Survey (CFHTLS) to construct a sample of over 40,000 UV-selected galaxies at redshifts 0.5 to 1.5. The key idea of this study is to examine the migration of star formation sites within these galaxies as they evolve over time. To achieve this, we utilize the Bayesian Photometric Redshift (BPZ) code to estimate the redshifts and stellar masses of these galaxies, and then employ the Voronoi Delaunay Method (VDM) to determine the clustering properties of the sample. 

Our results show that the clustering strength of restframe UV-selected galaxies increases with cosmic time, suggesting that these galaxies become more clustered as they evolve. We also find that there is a clear migration of star formation sites within galaxies, with star formation being more concentrated towards the central regions of galaxies at higher redshifts. This trend is most notable for the youngest and most star-forming galaxies in our sample. The methodology applied in this study provides insights into the properties and evolution of star-forming galaxies and can pave the way for further investigation into the physical processes that drive the observed migration of star formation sites.

We investigate the DFT+U approach as a viable solution to describe the
low-lying states of ligated and unligated iron heme complexes. Besides their
central role in organometallic chemistry, these compounds represent a
paradigmatic case where LDA, GGA, and common hybrid functionals fail to
reproduce the experimental magnetic splittings. In particular, the imidazole
pentacoordinated heme is incorrectly described as a triplet by all usual DFT
flavors. In this study we show that a U parameter close to 4 eV leads to spin
transitions and molecular geometries in quantitative agreement with
experiments, and that DFT+U represents an appealing tool in the description of
iron porphyrin complexes, at a much reduced cost compared to correlated
quantum-chemistry methods. The possibility of obtaining the U parameter from
first-principles is explored through a self-consistent linear-response
formulation. We find that this approach, which proved to be successful in other
iron systems, produces in this case some overestimation with respect to the
optimal values of U.

We use the Barnard 68 dark globule as a test case for a spherically symmetric
PDR model exposed to low-UV radiation fields. With a roughly spherical
morphology and an accurately determined density profile, Barnard 68 is ideal
for this purpose. The processes governing the energy balance in the cloud
surface are studied in detail. We compare the spherically symmetric PDR model
by Stoerzer, Stutzki & Sternberg (1996) to observations of the three lowest
rotational transitions of 12CO, 13CO J = 2-1 and J = 3-2 as well as the [CI]
3P_1-3P_0 fine structure transition. We study the role of Polycyclic Aromatic
Hydrocarbons (PAHs) in the chemical network of the PDR model and consider the
impact of depletion as well as of a variation of the external FUV field. We
find it difficult to simultaneously model the observed 12CO and 13CO emission.
The 12CO and [CI] emission can be explained by a PDR model with a external FUV
field of 1-0.75 chi_0, but this model fails to reproduce the observed 13CO by a
factor of ~2. Adding PAHs to the chemical network increases the [CI] emission
by 50% in our model but makes [CII] very faint. CO depletion only slightly
reduces the 12CO and 13CO line intensity (by <10% and <20%, respectively).
Predictions for the [CII] 2P_3/2-2P_1/2, [CI] 3P_2-3P_1 and 12CO J= 5-4 and 4-3
transitions are presented. This allows a test of our model with future
observations (APEX, NANTEN2, HERSCHEL, SOFIA).

The analysis of gravitational wave data involves many model selection
problems. The most important example is the detection problem of selecting
between the data being consistent with instrument noise alone, or instrument
noise and a gravitational wave signal. The analysis of data from ground based
gravitational wave detectors is mostly conducted using classical statistics,
and methods such as the Neyman-Pearson criteria are used for model selection.
Future space based detectors, such as the \emph{Laser Interferometer Space
Antenna} (LISA), are expected to produced rich data streams containing the
signals from many millions of sources. Determining the number of sources that
are resolvable, and the most appropriate description of each source poses a
challenging model selection problem that may best be addressed in a Bayesian
framework. An important class of LISA sources are the millions of low-mass
binary systems within our own galaxy, tens of thousands of which will be
detectable. Not only are the number of sources unknown, but so are the number
of parameters required to model the waveforms. For example, a significant
subset of the resolvable galactic binaries will exhibit orbital frequency
evolution, while a smaller number will have measurable eccentricity. In the
Bayesian approach to model selection one needs to compute the Bayes factor
between competing models. Here we explore various methods for computing Bayes
factors in the context of determining which galactic binaries have measurable
frequency evolution. The methods explored include a Reverse Jump Markov Chain
Monte Carlo (RJMCMC) algorithm, Savage-Dickie density ratios, the Schwarz-Bayes
Information Criterion (BIC), and the Laplace approximation to the model
evidence. We find good agreement between all of the approaches.

We present Spitzer observations of a 24 micron shadow in Bok Globule CB190. Our motivation for this research is to study the properties and structure of this molecular cloud, which is thought to be a potential site of future star formation. The 24 micron shadow is of particular interest because it is indicative of a dense, cold core within the globule. In this work, we attempt to determine the nature of this core, as well as the mass and temperature of the surrounding molecular gas.

We utilized mid-infrared imaging and spectroscopy from the Spitzer Space Telescope to map the distribution of dust emission within the globule. We also analyzed archival molecular line data from the FCRAO telescope to study the kinematics of the gas. By comparing the observed spectral energy distribution of the shadow to theoretical models, we determined that the core has a mass of around 11 solar masses and a temperature of approximately 13 K. Our results imply that CB190 is a relatively evolved molecular cloud, with the potential to form low-mass stars in the future.

Overall, our work provides valuable insights into the physical properties of Bok Globule CB190 and highlights the importance of using multi-wavelength observations to study the structure and evolution of molecular clouds.

We explore the morphology of hard (18-30 keV) X-ray emission from the Coma
cluster of galaxies. We analyze a deep (1.1 Ms) observation of the Coma cluster
with the ISGRI imager on board the \emph{INTEGRAL} satellite. We show that the
source extension in the North-East to South-West (SW) direction ($\sim 17'$)
significantly exceeds the size of the point spread function of ISGRI, and that
the centroid of the image of the source in the 18-30 keV band is displaced in
the SW direction compared to the centroid in the 1-10 keV band. To test the
nature of the SW extension we fit the data assuming different models of source
morphology. The best fit is achieved with a diffuse source of elliptical shape,
although an acceptable fit can be achieved assuming an additional point source
SW of the cluster core. In the case of an elliptical source, the direction of
extension of the source coincides with the direction toward the subcluster
falling onto the Coma cluster. If the SW excess is due to the presence of a
point source with a hard spectrum, we show that there is no obvious X-ray
counterpart for this additional source, and that the closest X-ray source is
the quasar EXO 1256+281, which is located $6.1'$ from the centroid of the
excess. The observed morphology of the hard X-ray emission clarifies the nature
of the hard X-ray "excess" emission from the Coma cluster, which is due to the
presence of an extended hard X-ray source SW of the cluster core.

We present the design and imaging performance of the Infrared Camera (IRC) on the AKARI satellite. The IRC was designed to cover the 1.7-26 μm wavelength range with three channels: NIR (1.7-5.5 μm), MIR-S (4-13 μm), and MIR-L (12-26 μm). The IRC has a 9.4'×10.1' field of view and uses Si:As IBC detector arrays with 40-mas pixels. We report on the sensitivity, stability, and spatial resolution of the IRC based on the on-orbit pointed observation data of standard stars and galaxies. The observations show that the IRC has a sensitivity of 0.056, 0.41, 3.8, and 310 mJy (5σ, 1 s integration) in the NIR, MIR-S, MIR-L, and FIR bands, respectively. The absolute photometric calibration of the IRC is accurate to 5%-10% depending on the band and processing method. The IRC also has a point source spatial resolution of 1.3-3.6'', depending on the band and the observing mode. The excellent imaging performance of the IRC enables us to study a wide range of astronomical objects, from nearby Solar System bodies to high-redshift quasars and galaxies. 

Our research was motivated by the need to advance our understanding of the universe through high-quality observations in the infrared regime. The IRC is an essential instrument for achieving this goal, providing unprecedented sensitivity, spatial resolution, and wavelength coverage. Our work provides a thorough characterization of the IRC design and performance, demonstrating its scientific potential and setting the foundation for future research in infrared astronomy.

In this work, we present a 3D velocity field of a Supergranular cell's photosphere. Supergranulation, which is the largest observed pattern of convective motion on the solar surface, is known to play a crucial role in the transport of heat and magnetic fields in the Sun's photosphere. However, it remains a challenging task to accurately determine the fundamental characteristics of Supergranular flows, such as their size, lifetime, and origin. 

To address this problem, we made use of a series of high-resolution full-disk Dopplergrams acquired by the Helioseismic and Magnetic Imager (HMI) onboard the Solar Dynamics Observatory, spanning over three consecutive days. Specifically, we applied a local correlation tracking technique to derive the horizontal velocity fields from the Dopplergrams, which were then used to reconstruct a 3D map of the Supergranular cell's velocity field.

We found that the flow structure of the Supergranular cell is characterized by a large-scale cyclonic circulation in its interior, as well as smaller-scale convective cells near its boundaries. Moreover, the average flow speed in the cell is found to be around 400 m s-1, consistent with previous studies. Our results provide important insights into the dynamics of Supergranulation and demonstrate the potential of 3D velocity field analysis for studying the Sun's photosphere.

In this paper, we study the dynamics of three agent games, particularly in terms of the behavior of players. The problem that this work attempts to solve is the identification of the equilibrium points of a three agent game and the various strategies employed by the players to reach those points. Through an iterative process that simulates the interactions of players, we investigate how the behavior of one agent affects the strategies of the other agents and how this interaction influences the overall outcome of the game.

We are motivated to study this problem because of the growing interest in multi-agent systems, which have become an important area of research in artificial intelligence and game theory. Moreover, understanding the dynamics of multi-agent systems can reveal insights into real-world situations, such as market competition, political negotiations, and social interactions.

Our results demonstrate that the dynamics of three agent games can be complex and unpredictable, with different players exhibiting different behaviors and strategies. Additionally, we find that the equilibrium points of the game can shift depending on the initial strategies of the players, and that certain strategies may be more effective in achieving equilibrium than others. Overall, our work contributes to a better understanding of the dynamics of three agent games and can provide insights into more complex multi-agent systems.

In this work, we revisit the outbursts of EX Hydrae, a cataclysmic variable system exhibiting frequent outbursts. Our study is motivated by the previous observations made by other researchers which led to the discovery of an unusual outburst pattern in this system. We hypothesize that the observed pattern can be explained by the existence of different accretion regimes that are triggered when the luminosity increases over a certain threshold. 
To test our hypothesis, we analyzed new observations obtained with the Chandra X-ray Observatory during the 2015/2016 outburst of EX Hydrae. We used spectral and timing analyses to study the evolution of the system during the outburst. Our results confirm the presence of two accretion regimes during the outburst, supporting our initial hypothesis. We find that the transition between the regimes occurs at a luminosity of ~10^34 erg/s. Moreover, we show that the properties of the two regimes are consistent with the disk instability model. We also report on the detection of a hard X-ray emission component in the system, which may be related to non-thermal plasma emission powered by magnetic fields. Our findings shed new light on the physical mechanisms underlying the outburst behaviour of cataclysmic variable systems.

Incoherent noise is manifest in measurements of expectation values when the
underlying ensemble evolves under a classical distribution of unitary
processes. While many incoherent processes appear decoherent, there are
important differences. The distribution functions underlying incoherent
processes are either static or slowly varying with respect to control
operations and so the errors introduced by these distributions are refocusable.
The observation and control of incoherence in small Hilbert spaces is well
known. Here we explore incoherence during an entangling operation, such as is
relevant in quantum information processing. As expected, it is more difficult
to separate incoherence and decoherence over such processes. However, by
studying the fidelity decay under a cyclic entangling map we are able to
identify distinctive experimental signatures of incoherence. This result is
demonstrated both through numerical simulations and experimentally in a three
qubit nuclear magnetic resonance implementation.

In this study, we present the results of a spectral analysis of the Chandra Comet Survey (CCS). The CCS is a long-term project aimed at characterizing comets through X-ray observations. Our analysis focused on the spectral properties of the seven comets included in the survey. We applied both phenomenological and physical models to extract information on the plasma composition, temperature, and emission mechanisms.

The motivation for this research is twofold: first, understanding the X-ray emission of comets is crucial for the interpretation of X-ray observations of other Solar System bodies and exoplanets; second, the CCS represents an unprecedented dataset with a high potential for discoveries. Our work attempts to address the puzzle of the X-ray emission from comets, which has been known for over two decades but is still not fully understood. The widely accepted scenario involves solar wind particles interacting with the coma gas, but the exact mechanisms at play are still the subject of debate.

Our analysis reveals that the cometary X-ray spectra are complex and can be best explained by a hybrid model that includes thermal and non-thermal components. This finding suggests that multiple processes are involved in producing the X-ray emission, and that the traditional scenario might not be sufficient to capture the full complexity of the problem. We discuss possible interpretations of our results and the implications for the understanding of the plasma environment around comets.

Overall, our work contributes to advancing the field of cometary X-ray astronomy and provides new insights into the physics of these enigmatic objects.

We present a purely theoretical study of the morphological evolution of
self-gravitating systems formed through the dissipationless collapse of N-point
sources. We explore the effects of resolution in mass and length on the growth
of triaxial structures formed by an instability triggered by an excess of
radial orbits. We point out that as resolution increases, the equilibria shift,
from mildly prolate, to oblate. A number of particles N ~= 100000 or larger is
required for convergence of axial aspect ratios. An upper bound for the
softening, e ~ 1/256, is also identified. We then study the properties of a set
of equilibria formed from scale-free cold initial mass distributions, ro ~ r^-g
with 0 <= g <= 2. Oblateness is enhanced for initially more peaked structures
(larger values of g). We map the run of density in space and find no evidence
for a power-law inner structure when g <= 3/2 down to a mass fraction <~0.1 per
cent of the total. However, when 3/2 < g <= 2, the mass profile in equilibrium
is well matched by a power law of index ~g out to a mass fraction ~ 10 per
cent. We interpret this in terms of less-effective violent relaxation for more
peaked profiles when more phase mixing takes place at the centre. We map out
the velocity field of the equilibria and note that at small radii the velocity
coarse-grained distribution function (DF) is Maxwellian to a very good
approximation.

In this work, we aimed to investigate the robustness of cortical networks against lesions. Lesions, caused by diseases or injuries, can disrupt the connectivity of the brain and lead to functional impairments. To address this problem, we utilized a simulation approach to examine the network performance under different lesion conditions. Specifically, we considered two types of lesions - targeted attack and random damage - and evaluated the impact on network efficiency, connectivity and modularity. Our findings indicate that the cortical network is capable of maintaining high efficiency and strong connectivity despite the presence of lesions. Moreover, we observed that modular structures in the network play a crucial role in preserving functionality following lesions. Our results provide insights into the underlying mechanisms of brain plasticity and offer potential applications for diagnosing and treating neurological disorders. The key idea of our methodology is to use a realistic computational model of cortical networks and simulate different types of lesion scenarios. We also used graph theory analysis to quantify the network metrics. This work contributes to the understanding of the resilience of cortical networks and offers a foundation for further research on the development of therapeutic interventions for brain lesions.

Up till now very few dying sources were known, presumably because the dying
phase is short at centimeter wavelengths. We therefore have tried to improve
the statistics on sources that have ceased to be active, or are intermittently
active. The latter sources would partly consist of a fossil radio plasma left
over from an earlier phase of activity, plus a recently restarted core and
radio jets. Improving the statistics of dying sources will give us a better
handle on the evolution of radio sources, in particular the frequency and time
scales of radio activity. We have used the WENSS and NVSS surveys, in order to
find sources with steep spectral indices, associated with nearby elliptical
galaxies. In the cross correlation we presently used only unresolved sources,
with flux densities at 1.4 GHz larger than 10 mJy. The eleven candidates thus
obtained were observed with the VLA in various configurations, in order to
confirm the steepness of the spectra, and to check whether active structures
like flat-spectrum cores and jets are present, perhaps at low levels. We
estimated the duration of the active and relic phases by modelling the
integrated radio spectra using the standard models of spectral evolution. We
have found six dying sources and three restarted sources, while the remaining
two candidates remain unresolved also with the new VLA data and may be Compact
Steep Spectrum sources, with an unusually steep spectrum. The typical age of
the active phase, as derived by spectral fits, is in the range 10^7 - 10^8
years. For our sample of dying sources, the age of the relic phase is on
average shorter by an order of magnitude than the active phase.

In the field of tutoring systems, investigations have shown that there are
many tutoring systems specific to a specific domain that, because of their
static architecture, cannot be adapted to other domains. As consequence, often
neither methods nor knowledge can be reused. In addition, the knowledge
engineer must have programming skills in order to enhance and evaluate the
system. One particular challenge is to tackle these problems with the
development of a generic tutoring system. AnITA, as a stand-alone application,
has been developed and implemented particularly for this purpose. However, in
the testing phase, we discovered that this architecture did not fully match the
user's intuitive understanding of the use of a learning tool. Therefore, AnITA
has been redesigned to exclusively work as a client/server application and
renamed to AnITA2. This paper discusses the evolvements made on the AnITA
tutoring system, the goal of which is to use generic principles for system
re-use in any domain. Two experiments were conducted, and the results are
presented in this paper.

In this research work, we investigate some aspects of measurement error in linear regression of astronomical data. The motivation for this research stems from the prevalence of measurement error in observational data, which can lead to misleading conclusions and inaccurate results. Our work aims to address this issue by examining the impact of measurement error on the regression coefficients and standard errors in linear regression models. 

We begin by conducting a simulation study, where we generate data with varying levels of measurement error and examine the resulting regression coefficients and standard errors. We also investigate the effect of sample size and signal-to-noise ratio on the accuracy of the regression results. In addition, we apply our methods to a real-world astronomical data set and compare the results to those obtained from traditional regression techniques. 

Our findings highlight the importance of accounting for measurement error in linear regression analysis of astronomical data. We demonstrate that ignoring measurement error can lead to biased estimates and incorrect inference, and that our proposed methods can improve the accuracy and reliability of regression results. Our work contributes to the development of statistical methods for handling measurement error in observational data, with potential applications in a wide range of scientific fields.

An investigation of the possible inflation of stimulated Brillouin
backscattering (SBS) due to ion kinetic effects is presented using
electromagnetic particle simulations and integrations of three-wave
coupled-mode equations with linear and nonlinear models of the nonlinear ion
physics. Electrostatic simulations of linear ion Landau damping in an ion
acoustic wave, nonlinear reduction of damping due to ion trapping, and
nonlinear frequency shifts due to ion trapping establish a baseline for
modeling the electromagnetic SBS simulations. Systematic scans of the laser
intensity have been undertaken with both one-dimensional particle simulations
and coupled-mode-equations integrations, and two values of the electron-to-ion
temperature ratio (to vary the linear ion Landau damping) are considered. Three
of the four intensity scans have evidence of SBS inflation as determined by
observing more reflectivity in the particle simulations than in the
corresponding three-wave mode-coupling integrations with a linear ion-wave
model, and the particle simulations show evidence of ion trapping.

We investigate the Gross-Pitaevskii equation for a classically chaotic
system, which describes an atomic Bose-Einstein condensate confined in an
optical lattice and driven by a spatiotemporal periodic laser field. It is
demonstrated that the exact Floquet states appear when the external
time-dependent potential is balanced by the nonlinear mean-field interaction.
The balance region of parameters is divided into a phase-continuing region and
a phase-jumping one. In the latter region, the Floquet states are
spatiotemporal vortices of nontrivial phase structures and zero-density cores.
Due to the velocity singularities of vortex cores and the blowing-up of
perturbed solutions, the spatiotemporal vortices are unstable periodic states
embedded in chaos. The stability and instability of these Floquet states are
numerically explored by the time evolution of fidelity between the exact and
numerical solutions. It is numerically illustrated that the stable Floquet
states could be prepared from the uniformly initial states by slow growth of
the external potential.

In this paper, we consider the Cauchy problem for Klein-Gordon equation with
a cubic convolution nonlinearity in $\R^3$. By making use of Bourgain's method
in conjunction with a precise Strichartz estimate of S.Klainerman and D.Tataru,
we establish the $H^s (s<1)$ global well-posedness of the Cauchy problem for
the cubic convolution defocusing Klein-Gordon-Hartree equation. Before arriving
at the previously discussed conclusion, we obtain global solution for this
non-scaling equation with small initial data in $H^{s_0}\times H^{s_0-1}$ where
$s_0=\frac\gamma 6$ but not $\frac\gamma2-1$, for this equation that we
consider is a subconformal equation in some sense. In doing so a number of
nonlinear prior estimates are already established by using Bony's
decomposition, flexibility of Klein-Gordon admissible pairs which are slightly
different from that of wave equation and a commutator estimate. We establish
this commutator estimate by exploiting cancellation property and utilizing
Coifman and Meyer multilinear multiplier theorem. As far as we know, it seems
that this is the first result on low regularity for this Klein-Gordon-Hartree
equation.

In our work titled "CP Violation From Standard Model to Strings", we delve deep into the concept of CP violation and their origins in the Standard Model of particle physics. The motivation behind our research stems from the fact that CP violation is crucial in understanding the asymmetry between matter and antimatter in the universe. The study of CP violation has been a significant topic of research since its initial discovery in the 60s, and it continues to remain a crucial issue in particle physics. Our research aims to provide a comprehensive overview of CP violation and the role it plays in the Standard Model of particle physics. 

We present a detailed analysis of several theoretical models, including the Kobayashi-Maskawa theory, which explains CP violation in the weak interaction of elementary particles. Additionally, we explore the possibility of explaining CP violation through string theory, which offers a broader framework of theoretical models. We have obtained several results that provide a new perspective on the origins and mechanisms of CP violation. Our work highlights the importance of exploring various theoretical models to understand the fundamental aspects of the universe. The methodology we employed involves a detailed analysis of existing literature and theoretical models to present a comprehensive overview of CP violation and its mechanisms. Our work contributes to the ongoing efforts in the field of particle physics in understanding the fundamental nature of the universe.

In our work, we explore the high-temperature limit for number density correlation functions in classical Coulomb fluids. This research was initiated by the need to better understand the behavior of charged fluids in the presence of high temperatures, which can lead to a range of interesting and complex phenomena. Our goal was to examine the properties of correlation functions in such conditions and to determine the nature of the underlying physics.

One of the key challenges we encountered in this research was the trickiness of the high-temperature limit. Because the electrons within Coulomb fluids exhibit both classical and quantum mechanical behavior, it can be difficult to develop a unified understanding of their correlations. In order to overcome this difficulty, we employed a combination of theoretical and computational methods to analyze the behavior of these fluids in detail.

Our results shed light on the fundamental physics of Coulomb fluids and contribute to our understanding of high-temperature behavior. Specifically, we find that the high-temperature limit of number density correlations depends on the underlying structure of the fluid. By providing a deeper understanding of these systems, our work has the potential to inform the development of future technologies and applications.

The longitudinal and transversal spin decoherence times, $T_1$ and $T_2$, in
semiconductor quantum dots are investigated from equation-of-motion approach
for different magnetic fields, quantum dot sizes, and temperatures. Various
mechanisms, such as the hyperfine interaction with the surrounding nuclei, the
Dresselhaus spin-orbit coupling together with the electron--bulk-phonon
interaction, the $g$-factor fluctuations, the direct spin-phonon coupling due
to the phonon-induced strain, and the coaction of the
electron--bulk/surface-phonon interaction together with the hyperfine
interaction are included. The relative contributions from these spin
decoherence mechanisms are compared in detail. In our calculation, the
spin-orbit coupling is included in each mechanism and is shown to have marked
effect in most cases. The equation-of-motion approach is applied in studying
both the spin relaxation time $T_1$ and the spin dephasing time $T_2$, either
in Markovian or in non-Markovian limit. When many levels are involved at finite
temperature, we demonstrate how to obtain the spin relaxation time from the
Fermi Golden rule in the limit of weak spin-orbit coupling. However, at high
temperature and/or for large spin-orbit coupling, one has to use the
equation-of-motion approach when many levels are involved. Moreover, spin
dephasing can be much more efficient than spin relaxation at high temperature,
though the two only differs by a factor of two at low temperature.

Our research investigates the interplay between frustration and inhomogeneous condensates in layered quantum dimer magnets with reduced dimensionality. The motivation for our work stems from the pursuit of a deeper understanding of the exotic magnetic behavior observed in these materials. These systems are characterized by strong quantum fluctuations and geometric frustration, which can lead to emergent phenomena such as topological phases and fractionalization of excitations.

Using numerical simulations based on a variational Monte Carlo method, we studied the ground state properties of a model system consisting of weakly coupled layers of quantum dimers. By systematically varying the strength of frustration and inhomogeneous condensates, we found that frustration suppresses the formation of inhomogeneous condensates, while inhomogeneous condensates can enhance frustration. Additionally, we observed the emergence of an inhomogeneous ground state structure when both frustration and inhomogeneous condensates were present, leading to the formation of domain walls and a rich phase diagram.

Our results shed light on the complex interplay between frustration and inhomogeneous condensates in reduced dimensional quantum magnets, providing new insights into the mechanisms of exotic magnetism in these materials. This work also has implications for the design and optimization of materials for future applications in quantum information processing and spintronics.

We present a comparison between anomalous 6-cm formaldehyde (H$_2$CO) absorption and CO(1-0) emission in the L1204/S140 region. The motivation behind this study is to investigate the relationship between molecular gas and dense cloud cores in this region, which is undergoing active star formation. Key methodology involved analyzing archival radio data from the Green Bank Telescope (GBT) and the Five College Radio Astronomy Observatory (FCRAO). We identified six H$_2$CO absorption features and found that the H$_2$CO absorption profiles and CO(1-0) emission have different shapes, peak velocities, and velocity dispersions. Our results suggest that the anomalous H$_2$CO absorption may be tracing different physical conditions compared to CO(1-0) emission, such as smaller dense cores associated with embedded protostars. This is supported by previous observations of compact H$_2$CO emission in other star-forming regions. Our findings highlight the importance of multi-wavelength studies to better understand the complex interplay between gas dynamics and star formation. Further studies are necessary to confirm these results and investigate the properties of the dense cores associated with the anomalous H$_2$CO absorption.

A Gizatullin surface is a normal affine surface $V$ over $\bf C$, which can
be completed by a zigzag; that is, by a linear chain of smooth rational curves.
In this paper we deal with the question of uniqueness of $\bf C^*$-actions and
$\bf A^1$-fibrations on such a surface $V$ up to automorphisms. The latter
fibrations are in one to one correspondence with $\bf C_+$-actions on $V$
considered up to a "speed change".
  Non-Gizatullin surfaces are known to admit at most one $\bf A^1$-fibration
$V\to S$ up to an isomorphism of the base $S$. Moreover an effective $\bf
C^{*}$-action on them, if it does exist, is unique up to conjugation and
inversion $t\mapsto t^{-1}$ of $\bf C^*$. Obviously uniqueness of $\bf
C^*$-actions fails for affine toric surfaces; however we show in this case that
there are at most two conjugacy classes of $\bf A^1$-fibrations. There is a
further interesting family of non-toric Gizatullin surfaces, called the
Danilov-Gizatullin surfaces, where there are in general several conjugacy
classes of $\bf C^*$-actions and $\bf A^1$-fibrations.
  In the present paper we obtain a criterion as to when $\bf A^1$-fibrations of
Gizatullin surfaces are conjugate up to an automorphism of $V$ and the base
$S$. We exhibit as well a large subclasses of Gizatullin $\bf C^{*}$-surfaces
for which a $\bf C^*$-action is essentially unique and for which there are at
most two conjugacy classes of $\bf A^1$-fibrations over $\bf A^1$.

In this work, we investigate the cosmological implications of string theory. We explore various string-inspired scenarios that describe the early universe, including inflationary models and those that involve the production of cosmic strings and branes. Through extensive analysis and numerical simulations, we show that these models can provide a robust and consistent framework for explaining the observed large-scale structure of the universe.

Our motivation for this research stems from the fact that string theory is currently one of the most promising candidates for a unified theory of physics that encompasses both quantum mechanics and general relativity. As such, it has the potential to address some of the most fundamental questions in cosmology, such as the nature of dark matter and the origin of the cosmic microwave background radiation.

The problem our work attempts to solve is how to bridge the gap between string theory and cosmology in a way that is both theoretically rigorous and empirically testable. We address this challenge by developing a systematic approach to studying the cosmological implications of string theory, which involves combining analytical and numerical techniques. Our results provide new insights into the connection between string theory and cosmology and open up new avenues for future investigations.

We present an optical source catalog of the North Ecliptic Pole (NEP) region obtained from the Subaru Strategic Program (SSP) observations. The NEP region is strategically important as it is located in a celestial region with a relatively low galactic dust extinction, making it an ideal target for deep surveys. Our catalog includes photometry and astrometry for over 17 million sources detected in deep imaging data consisting of 10 broadband filters with depths of at least $z \sim 26$. We achieved a positional accuracy of 30 mas and a photometric accuracy of 3\% for magnitudes brighter than $z = 23$, making our catalog one of the deepest and most accurate to date.

The motivation for this research stems from the need to accurately characterize the near-infrared extragalactic background light (EBL), which can provide insight into the evolution of galaxies and the history of star formation. To estimate the EBL, measurements of the resolved and unresolved sources in the NEP field are essential. Our catalog is a significant contribution towards this goal, as it allows for the identification and characterization of a large number of faint sources in the NEP region.

The problem our work attempts to solve is the lack of deep and comprehensive optical catalogs in the NEP field, which can limit the accuracy of both the measured EBL and individual studies on galaxy evolution. By providing a detailed catalog with high accuracy and depth, our work enables a wide range of astrophysical studies in the NEP region and beyond.

High-energy gamma-rays propagating in the intergalactic medium can interact
with background infrared photons to produce e+e- pairs, resulting in the
absorption of the intrinsic gamma-ray spectrum. TeV observations of the distant
blazar 1ES 1101-232 were thus recently used to put an upper limit on the
infrared extragalactic background light density. The created pairs can
upscatter background photons to high energies, which in turn may pair produce,
thereby initiating a cascade. The pairs diffuse on the extragalactic magnetic
field (EMF) and cascade emission has been suggested as a means for measuring
its intensity. Limits on the IR background and EMF are reconsidered taking into
account cascade emissions. The cascade equations are solved numerically.
Assuming a power-law intrinsic spectrum, the observed 100 MeV - 100 TeV
spectrum is found as a function of the intrinsic spectral index and the
intensity of the EMF. Cascades emit mainly at or below 100 GeV. The observed
TeV spectrum appears softer than for pure absorption when cascade emission is
taken into account. The upper limit on the IR photon background is found to be
robust. Inversely, the intrinsic spectra needed to fit the TeV data are
uncomfortably hard when cascade emission makes a significant contribution to
the observed spectrum. An EMF intensity around 1e-8 nG leads to a
characteristic spectral hump in the GLAST band. Higher EMF intensities divert
the pairs away from the line-of-sight and the cascade contribution to the
spectrum becomes negligible.

We investigate dynamical properties of bright solitons with a finite
background in the F=1 spinor Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC), based on an
integrable spinor model which is equivalent to the matrix nonlinear
Schr\"{o}dinger equation with a self-focusing nonlineality. We apply the
inverse scattering method formulated for nonvanishing boundary conditions. The
resulting soliton solutions can be regarded as a generalization of those under
vanishing boundary conditions. One-soliton solutions are derived in an explicit
manner. According to the behaviors at the infinity, they are classified into
two kinds, domain-wall (DW) type and phase-shift (PS) type. The DW-type implies
the ferromagnetic state with nonzero total spin and the PS-type implies the
polar state, where the total spin amounts to zero. We also discuss two-soliton
collisions. In particular, the spin-mixing phenomenon is confirmed in a
collision involving the DW-type. The results are consistent with those of the
previous studies for bright solitons under vanishing boundary conditions and
dark solitons. As a result, we establish the robustness and the usefulness of
the multiple matter-wave solitons in the spinor BECs.
